topics.title: Cellular Communication System: Need for Multi-Carrier System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cellular Communication System: Need for Multi-Carrier System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cellular Communication System — Formal Definition

A **Cellular Communication System** is a radio network architecture in which a geographical service area is divided into smaller, non-overlapping hexagonal regions called **cells**, each served by a low-power **Base Station (BS)**. The same set of radio frequencies can be reused in non-adjacent cells, dramatically increasing system capacity without requiring additional spectrum. This concept, known as **Frequency Reuse**, is the cornerstone of modern mobile communication standards such as **GSM, LTE, and 5G NR**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The cellular concept was first proposed by **Bell Labs (AT&T)** in **1947** and later commercialized in **1983 (AMPS)**. It solves the twin problems of **spectrum scarcity** and **user capacity**.

## 1.2 Multi-Carrier System — Formal Definition

A **Multi-Carrier (MC) System** is a modulation technique in which a high-rate serial data stream is split into several lower-rate parallel streams, each modulating its own **subcarrier**. The subcarriers are spaced such that they remain mutually **orthogonal** over the symbol duration, allowing their spectra to overlap without **Inter-Carrier Interference (ICI)**. The most successful realization of this principle is **Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** OFDM converts a **frequency-selective fading channel** into multiple **flat-fading sub-channels**, eliminating the need for complex time-domain equalizers.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Multi-Lane Highway

Imagine a single-lane road where every vehicle (data bit) must travel one after another. If the road has a deep pothole (frequency-selective fade), an entire convoy of vehicles may be delayed or damaged.

A **multi-carrier system** is like building a **20-lane highway** where traffic is distributed across lanes, and each lane uses a different frequency (color). Even if a pothole destroys one lane's cargo, the other 19 lanes deliver their data successfully. The information loss is minimal and correctable.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world link:** Every Wi-Fi router (IEEE 802.11a/g/n/ac/ax), 4G LTE, and 5G NR uses OFDM. The same principle keeps your video call smooth even in a moving car.

## 1.4 Why the "Need" Arises — The Core Motivation

Modern cellular users demand **high data rates (Mbps to Gbps)**, **low latency**, and **mobility**. Achieving these on a conventional **single-carrier** system is extremely difficult due to:

| Challenge | Effect on Single-Carrier System |
|-----------|--------------------------------|
| **Multipath propagation** | Causes severe **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)** |
| **Doppler spread** | Generates time-varying fading |
| **Frequency-selective fading** | Deep nulls across the signal bandwidth |
| **Complex equalization** | Requires high-order adaptive filters |
| **High symbol rate** | Symbol duration $\ll$ channel delay spread |

The solution is to transmit on **many parallel narrowband subcarriers**, each with a symbol duration much larger than the channel's maximum delay spread — a property known as **CP (Cyclic Prefix) absorption of ISI**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Evolution of Cellular Generations

1. **1G (1980s)** — Analog, FDMA, voice only.
2. **2G (1990s)** — Digital, TDMA/CDMA, GSM.
3. **3G (2000s)** — CDMA2000, WCDMA, mobile data.
4. **4G (2010s)** — **OFDM-based LTE**, broadband data.
5. **5G (2020s)** — **Scalable OFDM numerology**, eMBB, mMTC, URLLC.

> [!NOTE]
> From 4G onwards, **multi-carrier modulation** is the default physical layer choice.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Single-Carrier Transmission

A single-carrier system transmits:
$$ s(t) = \sum_{k} a_k \, g(t - kT_s) \cos(2\pi f_c t) $$

where $T_s$ is the **symbol period** and $B \approx 1/T_s$ is the bandwidth.

**Limitation:** As data rate $R_b$ increases, $T_s$ decreases. If $T_s < \sigma_\tau$ (RMS delay spread of the channel), severe ISI occurs because energy from one symbol spills into the next.

## 2.3 Multipath Channel Model

The **time-varying multipath channel** is described by:
$$ h(t, \tau) = \sum_{l=0}^{L-1} \alpha_l(t) \, \delta(\tau - \tau_l) $$

- $\alpha_l(t)$ — complex gain of the $l^{th}$ path.
- $\tau_l$ — propagation delay of the $l^{th}$ path.
- $L$ — total number of resolvable multipath components.

## 2.4 Key Channel Parameters

- **Coherence Bandwidth ($B_c$):** $\displaystyle B_c \approx \frac{1}{2\pi \sigma_\tau} \approx \frac{1}{5\sigma_\tau}$
- **Coherence Time ($T_c$):** $\displaystyle T_c \approx \frac{0.423}{f_D}$, where $f_D$ is the Doppler spread.
- **RMS Delay Spread ($\sigma_\tau$):** $\displaystyle \sigma_\tau = \sqrt{\overline{\tau^2} - (\overline{\tau})^2}$

## 2.5 Mathematical Foundation of Multi-Carrier Modulation

In an OFDM system with $N$ subcarriers:
$$ x(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \, e^{j 2\pi f_k t}, \quad 0 \le t \le T_{sym} $$

The subcarrier frequencies are chosen as:
$$ f_k = f_0 + k \cdot \Delta f, \quad \Delta f = \frac{1}{T_{sym}} $$

This **orthogonal spacing** ensures that the correlation between any two subcarriers is zero:
$$ \int_0^{T_{sym}} e^{j 2\pi f_m t} \cdot e^{-j 2\pi f_n t} \, dt = T_{sym} \, \delta_{mn} $$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit | Remarks |
|-------------------|------------|------|---------|
| Frequency Reuse Ratio | $q = \sqrt{3N}$ | — | $N$ = cluster size |
| Coherence Bandwidth | $B_c \approx \dfrac{1}{5\sigma_\tau}$ | Hz | $50\%$ correlation |
| RMS Delay Spread | $\sigma_\tau = \sqrt{\overline{\tau^2} - (\bar{\tau})^2}$ | seconds | Channel parameter |
| Symbol Period (MC) | $T_{sym} = N \cdot T_s$ | seconds | $N$ = no. of subcarriers |
| Subcarrier Spacing | $\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{T_{sym}}$ | Hz | Orthogonal spacing |
| CP Length | $T_{cp} \ge \tau_{max}$ | seconds | Eliminates ISI |
| Doppler Spread | $f_D = \dfrac{v}{\lambda}\cos\theta$ | Hz | $v$ = mobile speed |
| Coherence Time | $T_c \approx \dfrac{0.423}{f_D}$ | seconds | Channel time-invariant |
| PAPR (OFDM) | $\text{PAPR} = \dfrac{\max \vert x(t) \vert^2}{E[\vert x(t) \vert^2]}$ | dB | Major drawback |
| Bit Rate (OFDM) | $R_b = \dfrac{N \log_2 M}{T_{sym} + T_{cp}}$ | bps | $M$ = M-ary constellation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used by RF planning engineers in **cellular network design** (4G/5G deployment) and by chip designers in **DSP implementation of OFDM transceivers** (e.g., for Wi-Fi 6, 5G NR handsets).

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **4G LTE / 5G NR physical layer** — OFDM with scalable numerology ($\Delta f$ = 15, 30, 60, 120, 240 kHz).
- **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11ax)** — OFDMA uplink/downlink.
- **DVB-T / DVB-T2 digital TV broadcasting** — coded OFDM (COFDM).
- **ADSL / VDSL broadband** — Discrete Multi-Tone (DMT) modulation.
- **Power line communication** — multi-carrier modems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Why Single-Carrier Fails at High Data Rates

**Given:**
- Wireless channel with RMS delay spread $\sigma_\tau$.
- Data symbol period $T_s$.

**Step 1 — Define the time-dispersion criterion.**
A communication system is **ISI-free** if the symbol period is much larger than the delay spread:
$$ T_s \gg \sigma_\tau $$

**Step 2 — Express the bit rate.**
For binary signaling, the bit rate is $R_b = 1/T_s$. Combining with the ISI condition:
$$ R_b \ll \frac{1}{\sigma_\tau} $$

**Step 3 — Take a typical outdoor cellular value.**
Urban cellular channels have $\sigma_\tau \approx 4 \,\mu s$. Therefore:
$$ R_b \ll \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 250 \text{ kbps} $$

**Step 4 — Compare with modern data demands.**
Modern 4G/5G systems demand $R_b \approx 100$ Mbps or more. Substituting:
$$ 10^8 \ll 2.5 \times 10^5 \quad \text{(FALSE!)} $$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**
The single-carrier system **fails** because $R_b$ far exceeds $1/\sigma_\tau$. The fix is to **increase the symbol period** by using many parallel subcarriers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Multi-Carrier Symbol Period

**Given:** Total bit rate $R_b$, total number of subcarriers $N$, and constellation size $M$.

**Step 1 — Bits per OFDM symbol.**
Each OFDM symbol carries $\log_2 M$ bits per subcarrier, so total bits per symbol $= N \log_2 M$.

**Step 2 — Effective symbol period (without CP).**
$$ T_{sym} = \frac{N \log_2 M}{R_b} $$

**Step 3 — Compare with delay spread.**
For ISI-free transmission: $T_{sym} \gg \sigma_\tau$.

**Step 4 — Substituting $N = 64, M = 4, R_b = 10^8$ bps:**
$$ T_{sym} = \frac{64 \times 2}{10^8} = 1.28 \,\mu s $$

But with the **serial-to-parallel conversion**, each subcarrier's individual symbol duration is:
$$ T_{s,\text{parallel}} = N \cdot T_s = 1.28 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} $$

**Step 5 — Verdict.**
Since $1.28 \,\mu s$ is comparable to or larger than typical indoor $\sigma_\tau \approx 0.1 \,\mu s$, the multi-carrier system comfortably absorbs ISI after adding a cyclic prefix.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Orthogonality of Subcarriers

**Goal:** Prove that subcarriers $f_m$ and $f_n$ are orthogonal when $\Delta f = 1/T_{sym}$.

**Step 1 — Set up the inner product.**
$$ I = \int_0^{T_{sym}} e^{j 2\pi f_m t} \cdot e^{-j 2\pi f_n t} \, dt $$

**Step 2 — Substitute $f_m = f_0 + m\Delta f$ and $f_n = f_0 + n\Delta f$:**
$$ I = \int_0^{T_{sym}} e^{j 2\pi (m-n)\Delta f \, t} \, dt $$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration:**
$$ I = \left[ \frac{e^{j 2\pi (m-n)\Delta f \, t}}{j 2\pi (m-n)\Delta f} \right]_0^{T_{sym}} $$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\Delta f = 1/T_{sym}$:**
$$ I = \frac{e^{j 2\pi (m-n)} - 1}{j 2\pi (m-n) \cdot \tfrac{1}{T_{sym}}} $$

**Step 5 — Recognize periodicity.**
Since $e^{j 2\pi (m-n)} = 1$ for all integers $(m - n)$:
$$ I = \frac{1 - 1}{j 2\pi (m-n) \cdot \tfrac{1}{T_{sym}}} = 0 \quad \text{for } m \ne n $$

**Step 6 — Evaluate for $m = n$:**
$$ I = \int_0^{T_{sym}} 1 \, dt = T_{sym} $$

**Step 7 — Final orthogonality condition:**
$$ \int_0^{T_{sym}} e^{j 2\pi f_m t} \cdot e^{-j 2\pi f_n t} \, dt = T_{sym} \cdot \delta_{mn} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **cornerstone derivation** for OFDM. KTU frequently asks students to derive or state this result.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Single vs Multi-Carrier

| Performance Parameter | Single-Carrier (SC) | Multi-Carrier (OFDM) |
|------------------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Symbol period | $T_s$ (small) | $T_{sym} = N T_s$ (large) |
| Sensitivity to delay spread | High | Low (CP absorbs ISI) |
| Equalizer complexity | High-order adaptive FIR | One-tap per subcarrier |
| Spectral efficiency | Lower | Higher (overlapping spectra) |
| Robustness to narrowband fades | Poor (entire band affected) | Excellent (only some subcarriers fade) |
| PAPR | Low ($\approx 0$ dB for constant envelope) | High ($\approx 10$–$12$ dB) |
| Sensitivity to frequency offset | Low | Very high (breaks orthogonality) |
| Suitability for high data rates | Poor | Excellent |
| Implementation complexity | Moderate | High (FFT/IFFT required) |
| Used in | GSM, 1G/2G (with TDMA) | LTE, 5G, Wi-Fi, DVB |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Multi-Carrier (OFDM) Transmitter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Serial Bit Stream] --> B[Serial to Parallel Converter]
    B --> C1[Constellation Mapper Subcarrier 0]
    B --> C2[Constellation Mapper Subcarrier 1]
    B --> C3[Constellation Mapper Subcarrier N-1]
    C1 --> D1[IFFT Stage]
    C2 --> D2[IFFT Stage]
    C3 --> D3[IFFT Stage]
    D1 --> E[Add Cyclic Prefix]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Digital to Analog Converter]
    F --> G[RF Upconversion and Power Amplifier]
    G --> H[Multipath Wireless Channel]
    H --> I[Receiver RF Front End]
    I --> J[Analog to Digital Converter]
    J --> K[Remove Cyclic Prefix]
    K --> L[FFT Stage]
    L --> M[Parallel to Serial Converter]
    M --> N[Output Bit Stream]
```

## 4.2 Frequency-Domain View — Overlapping Orthogonal Subcarriers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sub[Orthogonal Subcarrier Spectrum Arrangement]
        S0["Subcarrier 0: Center f0, Bandwidth DeltaF"]
        S1["Subcarrier 1: Center f0+DeltaF, Bandwidth DeltaF"]
        S2["Subcarrier 2: Center f0+2DeltaF, Bandwidth DeltaF"]
        S3["Subcarrier 3: Center f0+3DeltaF, Bandwidth DeltaF"]
        S4["Subcarrier N-1: Center f0+(N-1)DeltaF, Bandwidth DeltaF"]
    end
    S0 -. Overlaps without ICI .- S1
    S1 -. Overlaps without ICI .- S2
    S2 -. Overlaps without ICI .- S3
    S3 -. Overlaps without ICI .- S4
```

## 4.3 Single-Carrier vs Multi-Carrier Time-Frequency Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SC[Single Carrier Wideband Transmission]
        SC1["Entire Bandwidth B Used for One Symbol at a Time"]
        SC2["Symbol Period Ts is Small"]
        SC3["Severe ISI on Frequency Selective Channels"]
    end
    subgraph MC[Multi Carrier OFDM Transmission]
        MC1["Bandwidth B Split into N Sub-bands"]
        MC2["N Parallel Low Rate Streams"]
        MC3["Long Symbol Period Tsym Absorbs Multipath"]
    end
    SC --> OutcomeA[High Equalizer Complexity]
    MC --> OutcomeB[One Tap Equalizer per Subcarrier]
```

## 4.4 Cellular Frequency Reuse Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CellA["Cell A: Frequency Set f1 f2 f3"]
    CellB["Cell B: Reuse Pattern with K offset from Cell A"]
    CellC["Cell C: Same Set as Cell A but Far Enough to Avoid Co-channel Interference"]
    CellD["Cell D: Reuse Pattern with K offset from Cell C"]
    CellA -->|Distance D greater than Reuse Distance| CellC
    CellB -->|Distance D greater than Reuse Distance| CellD
    CellA -->|Non adjacent cells| CellB
    CellA -->|Non adjacent cells| CellD
    CellC -->|Non adjacent cells| CellB
    CellC -->|Non adjacent cells| CellD
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the cellular concept. Why is frequency reuse essential in cellular systems?**

**Model Answer:**
The cellular concept divides a large service area into smaller hexagonal **cells**, each served by a low-power base station. **Frequency reuse** allows the same set of carrier frequencies to be assigned to multiple non-adjacent cells, separated by a minimum reuse distance $D$. This dramatically increases system capacity (number of users per MHz) without requiring new spectrum, which is a scarce and regulated natural resource. The **co-channel reuse ratio** is $q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$, where $N$ is the cluster size and $R$ is the cell radius.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three limitations of single-carrier transmission in high-data-rate wireless systems.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI):** Symbol period $T_s$ becomes smaller than the channel's RMS delay spread $\sigma_\tau$, causing severe ISI.
2. **Complex equalization:** A high-order adaptive FIR equalizer is needed at the receiver, increasing cost and power consumption.
3. **Vulnerability to frequency-selective fading:** A single deep fade can corrupt the entire signal bandwidth, making the link unreliable.
4. **Doppler sensitivity:** Mobile channels with high Doppler spread cause time-varying distortion, hard to track in real time.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 1]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
Explain the need for a multi-carrier system in modern cellular communication. Compare single-carrier and multi-carrier systems in terms of symbol period, equalizer complexity, and robustness to multipath.

**Model Solution:**

Modern cellular systems (4G LTE, 5G NR) require very high data rates (100 Mbps to several Gbps) to support multimedia, video streaming, and IoT. At such rates, the **symbol period** $T_s = 1/R_b$ becomes extremely small, on the order of nanoseconds.

When $T_s$ falls below the channel's **RMS delay spread** $\sigma_\tau$ (typically $0.1$ to $4 \,\mu s$ in outdoor environments), the received signal exhibits severe **ISI**. A single-carrier system must then employ:
- Complex **adaptive equalizers** (e.g., decision-feedback equalizers with 50+ taps).
- Sophisticated channel estimation algorithms.
- High power consumption for real-time DSP.

A **multi-carrier (OFDM) system** solves this elegantly by splitting the high-rate stream into $N$ parallel low-rate sub-streams. Each subcarrier's symbol period becomes $T_{sym} = N \cdot T_s$, which is now much larger than $\sigma_\tau$. A **cyclic prefix** of length $T_{cp} \ge \tau_{max}$ absorbs the multipath energy, completely eliminating ISI between OFDM symbols.

**Comparison Table (as required by the question):**

| Parameter | Single-Carrier | Multi-Carrier (OFDM) |
|-----------|----------------|----------------------|
| Symbol period | Very small (ns) | Large ($\mu$s range) |
| Equalizer complexity | High-order adaptive FIR | One complex tap per subcarrier |
| Multipath robustness | Poor | Excellent (CP absorption) |
| Implementation | Time-domain DSP | FFT/IFFT-based frequency-domain DSP |

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
Derive the **orthogonality condition** of subcarriers in an OFDM system and explain how it enables overlapping spectra without Inter-Carrier Interference (ICI).

**Model Solution:** *(Full derivation as shown in Section 3.3 above)*

The OFDM signal is:
$$ x(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \, e^{j 2\pi f_k t} $$

The subcarrier frequencies are $f_k = f_0 + k \Delta f$ with $\Delta f = 1/T_{sym}$. The cross-correlation between subcarriers $m$ and $n$ over one symbol period is:
$$ I_{mn} = \int_0^{T_{sym}} e^{j 2\pi f_m t} \cdot e^{-j 2\pi f_n t} \, dt $$

$$ I_{mn} = \int_0^{T_{sym}} e^{j 2\pi (m-n)\Delta f t} \, dt $$

$$ I_{mn} = \frac{e^{j 2\pi (m-n)} - 1}{j 2\pi (m-n)\Delta f} $$

Substituting $\Delta f = 1/T_{sym}$:
$$ I_{mn} = T_{sym} \cdot \delta_{mn} $$

This proves that each subcarrier is recovered perfectly at the receiver using a matched filter or FFT, **even though their spectra overlap**. The peak of one subcarrier's sinc-shaped spectrum coincides with the nulls of all others, eliminating ICI.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 1]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of an OFDM transmitter. Mention the role of the **IFFT**, **cyclic prefix**, and **PAPR**.

**Model Solution:**

An OFDM transmitter converts a serial bit stream into $N$ parallel streams, maps each onto a constellation (BPSK/QPSK/QAM), modulates them onto orthogonal subcarriers via an **N-point IFFT**, prepends a **cyclic prefix (CP)**, and finally upconverts to RF.

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.1 above):**
Serial bits $\to$ S/P converter $\to$ Constellation mapper $\to$ IFFT $\to$ Add CP $\to$ DAC $\to$ RF upconversion $\to$ Antenna.

**Role of IFFT:** Computationally efficient method to modulate data onto $N$ orthogonal subcarriers simultaneously. Each IFFT output sample represents the time-domain sum of all modulated subcarriers.

**Role of Cyclic Prefix:** A copy of the last $T_{cp}$ seconds of the OFDM symbol, prepended to its front. It absorbs the multipath delay spread, converting linear convolution with the channel into circular convolution. This makes the channel matrix diagonal in the frequency domain, enabling **one-tap equalization**.

**Role of PAPR (Peak-to-Average Power Ratio):** OFDM signals have a high PAPR (10–12 dB) because many subcarriers can add constructively. This forces the RF power amplifier to operate with large back-off, reducing efficiency. PAPR reduction techniques (clipping, SLM, PTS) are active research areas.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
A 4G LTE system uses OFDM with $N = 1024$ subcarriers, subcarrier spacing $\Delta f = 15$ kHz, and 64-QAM modulation. Calculate:
(i) Useful OFDM symbol duration $T_{sym}$.
(ii) Total OFDM symbol duration $T_{total}$ with a normal cyclic prefix of $4.7 \,\mu s$.
(iii) Achievable downlink data rate, assuming all subcarriers are used.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Useful OFDM symbol duration:**
$$ T_{sym} = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{15 \times 10^3} = 66.67 \,\mu s $$

**(ii) Total OFDM symbol duration:**
$$ T_{total} = T_{sym} + T_{cp} = 66.67 + 4.7 = 71.37 \,\mu s $$

**(iii) Achievable data rate:**
For 64-QAM, $\log_2 M = 6$ bits per subcarrier per symbol.
$$ R_b = \frac{N \log_2 M}{T_{total}} = \frac{1024 \times 6}{71.37 \times 10^{-6}} $$

$$ R_b = \frac{6144}{71.37 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 86.08 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} \approx 86.1 \text{ Mbps} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. Forgetting to include the cyclic prefix in $T_{total}$ — losing **2 marks**.
> 2. Confusing useful symbol period with sampling period — losing **2 marks**.
> 3. Using $\log_2 64 = 8$ instead of **6** — losing **2 marks**.
> 4. Not converting microseconds to seconds before dividing — losing **1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cellular Concept** = Divide service area into cells + reuse frequencies.
- **Co-channel Reuse Ratio:** $q = \sqrt{3N}$.
- **Single-carrier failure condition:** $T_s < \sigma_\tau \Rightarrow$ severe ISI.
- **Multi-carrier solution:** $T_{sym} = N \cdot T_s \gg \sigma_\tau$ (after adding CP).
- **Subcarrier spacing (OFDM):** $\Delta f = 1/T_{sym}$ — guarantees orthogonality.
- **Orthogonality integral:** $\int_0^{T_{sym}} e^{j 2\pi (m-n)\Delta f t} dt = T_{sym} \delta_{mn}$.
- **Cyclic Prefix length:** $T_{cp} \ge \tau_{max}$ — eliminates ISI between OFDM symbols.
- **Cyclic Prefix function:** Converts linear convolution to circular convolution, enabling **one-tap frequency-domain equalization**.
- **PAPR of OFDM:** Typically $10$–$12$ dB — major drawback; requires power amplifier back-off.
- **FFT Size (LTE):** $N = 1024$ (10 MHz), $2048$ (20 MHz).
- **Subcarrier Spacing (LTE):** $\Delta f = 15$ kHz. **(5G NR uses 15/30/60/120/240 kHz.)**
- **Useful OFDM symbol duration (LTE):** $T_{sym} = 1/15000 = 66.67 \,\mu s$.
- **Cyclic Prefix (LTE):** Normal CP $= 4.7 \,\mu s$ (first symbol $5.2 \,\mu s$); Extended CP $= 16.67 \,\mu s$.
- **Applications:** 4G LTE, 5G NR, Wi-Fi (802.11a/g/n/ac/ax), DVB-T, ADSL, Powerline.
- **Channel Parameters to Memorize:** Coherence bandwidth $B_c \approx 1/(5\sigma_\tau)$, Coherence time $T_c \approx 0.423/f_D$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM)

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM)** is a sophisticated **multicarrier modulation (MCM)** technique that divides a high-rate serial data stream into multiple low-rate parallel substreams, each transmitted simultaneously over a set of closely spaced, mutually orthogonal subcarriers. Each subcarrier is independently modulated using a conventional digital modulation scheme (such as **PSK** or **QAM**).

Mathematically, an OFDM signal in continuous time is expressed as:

$$s(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \cdot e^{j2\pi f_k t}, \quad 0 \le t \le T_u$$

where:
- $X_k$ = complex data symbol modulating the $k^{\text{th}}$ subcarrier
- $f_k$ = frequency of the $k^{\text{th}}$ subcarrier
- $N$ = total number of subcarriers
- $T_u$ = useful (data) symbol duration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The defining feature of OFDM is **orthogonality** — the subcarriers are mathematically arranged such that their spectra overlap in frequency, but the receiver can still perfectly separate them without inter-carrier interference (ICI).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Multi-Lane Highway:**
> Imagine a single-lane dirt road (a single-carrier system) trying to carry a thousand cars per minute. Every bump, pothole, and sudden slowdown causes a massive traffic jam. Now imagine a **10-lane expressway** (OFDM) where the same thousand cars are distributed across 10 lanes. Each lane carries only 100 cars per minute — much smoother, much more resilient to individual disruptions. Even if one lane has a pothole (frequency-selective fading), only 10% of the data is affected, and error correction can recover it.

### Why OFDM? — The Intuitive Justification

1. **Fights Frequency-Selective Fading:** A wideband channel has deep nulls at specific frequencies. With OFDM, each subcarrier occupies a *narrow* bandwidth where the channel is approximately **flat-fading**, eliminating the need for complex equalizers.
2. **Eliminates ISI via Guard Interval:** By inserting a **Cyclic Prefix (CP)** — a copy of the tail of the symbol at its beginning — multipath echoes from the previous symbol land inside the guard, never corrupting the next symbol.
3. **Spectral Efficiency:** Unlike classical FDM, OFDM subcarriers *overlap* in frequency, packing more data into the same bandwidth while remaining recoverable due to orthogonality.
4. **Computational Efficiency:** OFDM modulation/demodulation is implemented via the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT/IFFT)**, making it practically realizable in real-time systems.

### Key Standard Metrics

- **Subcarrier spacing:** $\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{T_u}$ (typically **15 kHz** in LTE, **312.5 kHz** in 5G NR numerology $\mu=1$).
- **Cyclic Prefix length:** $T_{CP} = \dfrac{N_{CP}}{N} \cdot T_u$ (where $N_{CP}$ is the CP sample count).
- **Total OFDM symbol duration:** $T_s = T_u + T_{CP}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept Anchor:** OFDM transforms a **frequency-selective wideband channel** (hard to equalize) into a collection of **flat-fading narrowband channels** (trivially equalized — one complex multiplication per subcarrier).

### Step 1 — Serial-to-Parallel Conversion
A high-speed serial bit stream $b[n]$ of rate $R_b$ bits/sec is broken into $N$ parallel substreams, each carrying a fraction $R_b / N$ of the data rate. This *expands* the symbol duration by a factor of $N$.

- **Why?** A longer symbol duration $T_s = N \cdot T_{original}$ means the multipath delay spread $\tau_{max}$ occupies a smaller *fractional* portion of the symbol. If $T_s \gg \tau_{max}$, intersymbol interference (ISI) becomes negligible.

### Step 2 — Constellation Mapping
Each parallel substream is mapped to a complex symbol $X_k$ from a chosen constellation (e.g., **BPSK, QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM**).

### Step 3 — IFFT-Based Modulation
The complex symbols $X_0, X_1, \dots, X_{N-1}$ are fed into an **N-point Inverse FFT**. The output of the IFFT is the discrete-time OFDM signal:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \cdot e^{j2\pi kn / N}, \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

- **Why?** The IFFT mathematically synthesizes $N$ orthogonal complex exponentials — one per subcarrier — and combines them. This replaces $N$ analog oscillators + mixers with a single DSP block.

### Step 4 — Cyclic Prefix Insertion
The last $N_{CP}$ samples of the IFFT output are copied and prepended to form a transmitted block of length $N + N_{CP}$. The transmitted sequence becomes:

$$x_{tx}[n] = x[(n \mod N)], \quad n = -N_{CP}, \dots, N-1$$

- **Why?** The cyclic prefix converts the **linear convolution** of signal and channel into a **circular convolution**. This is the critical mathematical property that makes single-tap frequency-domain equalization possible at the receiver.

### Step 5 — Channel Transmission
The signal passes through a multipath channel with impulse response $h(t)$ of length $L < N_{CP}$. The received signal is:

$$y(t) = x_{tx}(t) \ast h(t) + w(t)$$

where $w(t)$ is AWGN.

### Step 6 — Receiver Processing (Cyclic Prefix Removal)
The receiver discards the first $N_{CP}$ samples (which contain inter-symbol interference from the previous symbol due to multipath). What remains is the circularly-convolved useful portion.

### Step 7 — FFT-Based Demodulation
An N-point FFT is applied to recover the data on each subcarrier:

$$Y[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} y[n] \cdot e^{-j2\pi kn / N} = H[k] \cdot X[k] + W[k]$$

where $H[k]$ is the channel's frequency response at subcarrier $k$, and $W[k]$ is the noise in frequency domain.

### Step 8 — One-Tap Equalization
Each subcarrier is equalized by a single complex multiplication:

$$\hat{X}[k] = \dfrac{Y[k]}{H[k]}$$

This is the **simplest possible equalizer** — no adaptive filters, no matrix inversions.

## 2.2 The Orthogonality Condition — The Heart of OFDM

Two subcarriers $f_k$ and $f_m$ are **orthogonal** over the symbol interval $[0, T_u]$ if and only if:

$$\int_{0}^{T_u} e^{j2\pi f_k t} \cdot \left(e^{j2\pi f_m t}\right)^{*} \, dt = T_u \cdot \delta_{km}$$

This is satisfied when the subcarrier frequencies are spaced at integer multiples of the reciprocal of the symbol duration:

$$f_k = f_0 + k \cdot \Delta f, \quad \Delta f = \frac{1}{T_u}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** At the center frequency of one subcarrier, the spectrum of *every other* subcarrier has a **zero crossing**. Thus, although their spectral main lobes overlap, the receiver can sample each subcarrier exactly at the nulls of the others, extracting clean data.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Formula / Definition** | **Physical Meaning** | **Typical Value / Unit** |
|------------|--------------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| OFDM Signal | $s(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k e^{j2\pi f_k t}$ | Sum of $N$ orthogonal subcarriers | Continuous-time baseband |
| Subcarrier Spacing | $\Delta f = 1 / T_u$ | Frequency separation of subcarriers | 15 kHz (LTE), 312.5 kHz (5G NR) |
| Orthogonality Condition | $\int_0^{T_u} e^{j2\pi f_k t} e^{-j2\pi f_m t} dt = T_u \delta_{km}$ | Cross-product is zero for $k \neq m$ | Condition for ICI-free demod |
| OFDM Bandwidth (approx.) | $B \approx N \cdot \Delta f$ | Total occupied spectrum | Hz |
| Useful Symbol Duration | $T_u = N \cdot T_s$ | Duration of useful data part | $\mu$s |
| Cyclic Prefix Duration | $T_{CP} = N_{CP} \cdot T_s$ | Guard against multipath | $\mu$s |
| Total Symbol Duration | $T_{sym} = T_u + T_{CP}$ | Full OFDM symbol period | $\mu$s |
| Sampling Interval | $T_s = 1 / (N \cdot \Delta f)$ | Baseband sample spacing | seconds |
| FFT Output Relation | $Y[k] = H[k] \cdot X[k] + W[k]$ | Per-subcarrier received symbol | Complex number |
| Spectral Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\log_2 M}{1 + N_{CP}/N}$ | Bits/sec/Hz, $M$ = constellation size | bps/Hz |
| PAPR (ideal upper bound) | $\text{PAPR} \le 10 \log_{10}(N)$ | Peak-to-Average Power Ratio | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** When writing the orthogonality integral, **never forget the limits of integration** — the exam checker explicitly looks for $\int_0^{T_u}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **4G LTE / 5G NR:** Entire downlink/uplink physical layer built on OFDM (downlink) and DFT-spread OFDM (uplink).
- **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11a/g/n/ac/ax):** OFDM with 64 subcarriers (52 used) at 312.5 kHz spacing.
- **DVB-T (Digital TV):** OFDM with 2K/8K modes for robust HDTV broadcast.
- **ADSL / VDSL Broadband:** Discrete Multitone (DMT) — a practical variant of OFDM for copper loops.
- **Power Line Communication (PLC):** OFDM enables robust data over noisy electrical wiring.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Engineering Takeaway:** OFDM is the **dominant modulation paradigm** of the modern wireless age — every device you carry that streams video over cellular or Wi-Fi is using a descendant of this technique.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Proof of the Orthogonality Condition

**Goal:** Show that the inner product of two subcarriers over one symbol duration equals $T_u$ if $k = m$ and zero otherwise.

**Starting Point:**

$$I = \int_{0}^{T_u} e^{j2\pi f_k t} \cdot e^{-j2\pi f_m t} \, dt$$

**Step 1 — Combine the exponentials.** Using the exponent rule $e^a \cdot e^b = e^{a+b}$:

$$I = \int_{0}^{T_u} e^{j2\pi (f_k - f_m) t} \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Set $\Delta f_{km} = f_k - f_m$.**

$$I = \int_{0}^{T_u} e^{j2\pi \Delta f_{km} t} \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration.** The integral of $e^{at}$ is $\dfrac{e^{at}}{a}$:

$$I = \left[ \frac{e^{j2\pi \Delta f_{km} t}}{j2\pi \Delta f_{km}} \right]_{0}^{T_u}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the limits.**

$$I = \frac{e^{j2\pi \Delta f_{km} T_u} - 1}{j2\pi \Delta f_{km}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\Delta f_{km} = (k - m) \cdot \dfrac{1}{T_u}$ (from the subcarrier definition $f_k = f_0 + k / T_u$).**

$$I = \frac{e^{j2\pi (k-m)} - 1}{j2\pi \cdot \frac{(k-m)}{T_u}}$$

**Step 6 — Apply Euler's identity $e^{j2\pi (k-m)} = 1$ for integer $(k-m)$.**

The numerator becomes $1 - 1 = 0$, provided $k \neq m$.

**Case A — When $k = m$:** The denominator $j2\pi \Delta f_{km} = 0$. The integral is taken in the limit (L'Hôpital's rule), which yields $T_u$.

**Case B — When $k \neq m$:** The numerator is exactly $0$, so $I = 0$.

**Conclusion:**

$$I = T_u \cdot \delta_{km} = \begin{cases} T_u, & k = m \\ 0, & k \neq m \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** This proves ICI cannot occur in an ideal OFDM system — the matched filter output for subcarrier $k$ is mathematically blind to all other subcarriers' data.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: OFDM Signal Generation via IFFT

**Goal:** Show that the IFFT operation of $N$ complex symbols produces the OFDM time-domain signal.

**Definition — Discrete-time OFDM symbol (before CP):**

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \cdot e^{j2\pi kn / N}, \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

**Step 1 — Define the sampling rate.** Since subcarriers are spaced $\Delta f = 1/T_u$ apart and there are $N$ of them, the sampling frequency of the system is:

$$f_s = N \cdot \Delta f = \frac{N}{T_u}$$

**Step 2 — Define the sampling interval.** $T_{sample} = \dfrac{1}{f_s} = \dfrac{T_u}{N}$.

**Step 3 — Show equivalence with continuous form.** Substituting $t = n \cdot T_{sample} = n \cdot T_u / N$ into the continuous expression:

$$s(n T_u / N) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \cdot e^{j2\pi (f_0 + k/T_u) \cdot nT_u / N}$$

**Step 4 — The DC offset term $e^{j2\pi f_0 t}$ is absorbed** into a redefinition of $X_k$ or removed by setting $f_0 = 0$ for baseband. This yields:

$$s(n T_u / N) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \cdot e^{j2\pi kn / N}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the IFFT formula.** The standard IFFT definition is:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{j2\pi kn / N}$$

This is **identical** to the sampled OFDM signal. Thus, IFFT implements OFDM modulation. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Effect of Cyclic Prefix on Convolution

**Goal:** Show that a cyclic prefix converts linear convolution with the channel into circular convolution.

**Setup:** Channel impulse response has length $L$ samples, with $L \le N_{CP}$. The transmitted block (after CP insertion) is:

$$\mathbf{x}_{tx} = \big[ x[N-N_{CP}], \dots, x[N-1], \; x[0], x[1], \dots, x[N-1] \big]$$

Total length: $N + N_{CP}$. The receiver removes the first $N_{CP}$ samples, leaving $\mathbf{x} = [x[0], \dots, x[N-1]]$.

**Step 1 — Linear convolution at the receiver (post-CP-removal, over useful $N$ samples):**

$$y[n] = \sum_{l=0}^{L-1} h[l] \cdot x[(n-l) \mod N], \quad n = 0, \dots, N-1$$

**Step 2 — Why the modulo?** Because the cyclic prefix ensures that $x[(n-l) \mod N]$ is the *correctly wrapped-around* sample — the previous symbol's interference landed entirely in the removed CP, and the samples that "wrap around" are the CP copies of the *current* symbol.

**Step 3 — This is circular convolution.** In matrix form:

$$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H}_{circ} \cdot \mathbf{x}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the DFT (FFT).** The DFT of a circular convolution equals the pointwise product of the DFTs (this is the **Circular Convolution Theorem**):

$$Y[k] = H[k] \cdot X[k]$$

**Step 5 — Equalization** is a one-tap complex divider per subcarrier:

$$\hat{X}[k] = \frac{Y[k]}{H[k]} = X[k] + \frac{W[k]}{H[k]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CP is essential:** Without the cyclic prefix, the convolution would be *linear* (no modulo wrap-around), and the DFT relationship $Y[k] = H[k]X[k]$ would fail. ISI between adjacent OFDM symbols would also leak in. The CP solves both problems simultaneously.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example: 4-Subcarrier OFDM

**Problem:** Generate an OFDM symbol with $N = 4$ subcarriers and data symbols $X = [1, j, -1, -j]$ (where $j = \sqrt{-1}$). Compute the IFFT.

**Step 1 — Write the IFFT formula explicitly for $N = 4$:**

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{k=0}^{3} X_k \cdot e^{j2\pi kn / 4}$$

**Step 2 — Compute for $n = 0$:**

$$x[0] = \frac{1}{4} \left( 1 + j + (-1) + (-j) \right) = \frac{1}{4}(0) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute for $n = 1$:** Note $e^{j2\pi k/4}$ cycles as $1, j, -1, -j$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3$.

$$x[1] = \frac{1}{4} \left( 1 \cdot 1 + j \cdot j + (-1)(-1) + (-j)(-j) \right) = \frac{1}{4}(1 + j^2 + 1 + j^2)$$

$$= \frac{1}{4}(1 - 1 + 1 - 1) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Compute for $n = 2$:** $e^{j\pi k}$ alternates as $1, -1, 1, -1$.

$$x[2] = \frac{1}{4} \left( 1 - j - 1 + j \right) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Compute for $n = 3$:** $e^{j3\pi k/2}$ cycles as $1, -j, -1, j$.

$$x[3] = \frac{1}{4} \left( 1 + j(-j) + (-1)(-1) + (-j)(j) \right) = \frac{1}{4}(1 + 1 + 1 + 1) = 1$$

**Result:** $\mathbf{x} = [0, 0, 0, 1]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** This data sequence (a constant-magnitude vector $\{1, j, -1, -j\}$ known as a *Zadoff-Chu*–like single-frequency vector) maps to a pure single-frequency time-domain sinusoid — exactly the IFFT's deterministic behavior. It illustrates how subcarrier $k=3$ carried all the energy.
