# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**
**Define Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM). What do you mean by orthogonality of subcarriers?**

**Model Answer:**
OFDM is a multicarrier modulation technique in which a high-data-rate stream is split into multiple low-rate parallel streams, each modulating a separate subcarrier. These subcarriers are *orthogonal*, meaning the integral of the product of any two subcarriers over one symbol duration is zero.

Mathematically:

$$\int_{0}^{T_u} e^{j2\pi f_k t} \cdot e^{-j2\pi f_m t} \, dt = T_u \cdot \delta_{km}$$

This orthogonality allows the spectra of subcarriers to overlap in frequency without causing inter-carrier interference (ICI), as the receiver samples each subcarrier at the spectral nulls of the others.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]**
**What is a cyclic prefix in OFDM? Why is it used?**

**Model Answer:**
A cyclic prefix (CP) is a copy of the **last $N_{CP}$ samples** of an OFDM symbol that is **prepended** to the beginning of that symbol before transmission.

**Two key reasons for using CP:**
1. **ISI Elimination:** It acts as a guard interval. Any multipath echoes from the *previous* OFDM symbol that arrive late fall inside the CP region and are discarded by the receiver, leaving the useful symbol portion uncontaminated.
2. **Circular Convolution:** It converts the *linear* convolution of the transmitted signal with the channel into a *circular* convolution. This is essential because the DFT-based receiver can only equalize circular convolutions with a simple per-subcarrier multiplication $Y[k] = H[k]X[k]$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Explain the block diagram of an OFDM transmitter and receiver with all signal-processing stages clearly labeled. Discuss the role of the IFFT/FFT in OFDM modulation/demodulation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the condition for orthogonality between two subcarriers in an OFDM system. Using this result, explain why the spectra of OFDM subcarriers can overlap without causing inter-carrier interference. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

The OFDM transmitter and receiver block diagrams (refer to Section 4 above) consist of the following stages:

**Transmitter Path:**
1. **Serial-to-Parallel Conversion:** The high-rate input bitstream is split into $N$ parallel substreams, each carrying $1/N$ of the original data rate. This *increases* the symbol duration by a factor of $N$.
2. **Constellation Mapping:** Each substream is mapped to a complex symbol $X_k$ using PSK or QAM.
3. **IFFT Modulation:** The $N$ complex symbols $X_0, \dots, X_{N-1}$ are passed through an $N$-point IFFT, which mathematically synthesizes the OFDM time-domain waveform:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \cdot e^{j2\pi kn / N}$$

4. **Cyclic Prefix Insertion:** The last $N_{CP}$ samples of $x[n]$ are copied to the front of the symbol.
5. **DAC and RF Upconversion:** The digital signal is converted to analog and upshifted to the RF carrier frequency for transmission.

**Receiver Path:**
1. **RF Downconversion and ADC.**
2. **Cyclic Prefix Removal.**
3. **FFT Demodulation:** $Y[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} y[n] \cdot e^{-j2\pi kn / N}$.
4. **One-Tap Equalization:** $\hat{X}[k] = Y[k] / H[k]$.
5. **Constellation Demapping and Parallel-to-Serial Conversion.**

**Role of IFFT/FFT:**
The IFFT converts frequency-domain symbols $X_k$ into the corresponding time-domain OFDM signal — equivalent to summing $N$ orthogonal subcarriers each modulated by $X_k$. The FFT at the receiver performs the inverse operation, extracting $X_k$ from the time-domain received signal. The IFFT/FFT pair replaces the analog bank of $N$ oscillators, mixers, and matched filters that an analog multicarrier system would require, enabling cost-effective DSP implementation.

#### Model Solution — (b)

Refer to **Derivation 1 in Section 3.1** for the full step-by-step proof. The summary result is:

$$\int_{0}^{T_u} e^{j2\pi f_k t} \cdot e^{-j2\pi f_m t} \, dt = T_u \cdot \delta_{km}$$

This holds when $f_k = f_0 + k \cdot \Delta f$ with $\Delta f = 1/T_u$.

**Why overlapping spectra cause no ICI:** The frequency response of each subcarrier is a **sinc function** centered at $f_k$ with zeros at $f_k \pm m/T_u$ for $m = 1, 2, \dots$. Since adjacent subcarriers are spaced exactly $\Delta f = 1/T_u$ apart, the center frequency of *every other* subcarrier falls precisely on a **zero crossing** of the sinc of the subcarrier being demodulated. The matched filter receiver therefore integrates zero contribution from all other subcarriers, achieving perfect separation despite spectral overlap. This is the key spectral-efficiency advantage of OFDM over classical FDM, which requires non-overlapping guard bands.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain how a cyclic prefix converts a linear convolution with the channel into a circular convolution. Derive the relationship $Y[k] = H[k] \cdot X[k] + W[k]$ for the received OFDM signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An OFDM system uses $N = 64$ subcarriers with a useful symbol duration of $T_u = 4 \, \mu s$ and a cyclic prefix of length $N_{CP} = 16$ samples. Calculate:
   (i) Subcarrier spacing $\Delta f$
   (ii) Total OFDM symbol duration $T_{sym}$
   (iii) The fractional overhead due to the cyclic prefix
   (iv) Spectral efficiency if QPSK modulation is used **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

Refer to **Derivation 3 in Section 3.3** for the exhaustive proof. The summary argument:

- **Cyclic Prefix (CP):** The last $N_{CP}$ samples of the time-domain OFDM symbol are copied and prepended. The transmitted block is:

$$x_{tx} = \big[ x[N-N_{CP}], \dots, x[N-1], \; x[0], \dots, x[N-1] \big]$$

- **Channel Effect:** After passing through a multipath channel of length $L \le N_{CP}$, the receiver discards the first $N_{CP}$ samples (which absorb the previous-symbol ISI). The remaining $N$ samples represent the **circular convolution** of $x$ and $h$:

$$y[n] = \sum_{l=0}^{L-1} h[l] \cdot x[(n-l) \mod N]$$

- **DFT Application:** By the circular convolution theorem, applying the $N$-point DFT to $y[n]$ gives:

$$Y[k] = H[k] \cdot X[k]$$

- **With noise:** Including AWGN $W[k]$ in the frequency domain, the complete relationship is:

$$Y[k] = H[k] \cdot X[k] + W[k]$$

This is the foundation of OFDM's elegant one-tap equalization.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Given:** $N = 64$, $T_u = 4 \, \mu s$, $N_{CP} = 16$.

**(i) Subcarrier spacing:**

$$\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_u} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 250 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Total OFDM symbol duration:**

The sampling rate is $f_s = N \cdot \Delta f = 64 \times 250 \text{ kHz} = 16 \text{ MHz}$, so the sampling interval is $T_s = 1/f_s = 62.5 \text{ ns}$.

Cyclic prefix duration: $T_{CP} = N_{CP} \cdot T_s = 16 \times 62.5 \text{ ns} = 1 \, \mu s$.

$$T_{sym} = T_u + T_{CP} = 4 \, \mu s + 1 \, \mu s = 5 \, \mu s$$

**(iii) Fractional CP overhead:**

$$\text{Overhead} = \frac{T_{CP}}{T_{sym}} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 = 20\%$$

**(iv) Spectral efficiency with QPSK:**

QPSK carries $\log_2 4 = 2$ bits per symbol per subcarrier.

$$\eta = \frac{\log_2 M}{1 + N_{CP}/N} = \frac{2}{1 + 16/64} = \frac{2}{1 + 0.25} = \frac{2}{1.25} = 1.6 \text{ bits/sec/Hz}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the limits of integration** in the orthogonality proof. Always write $\int_0^{T_u}$ explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing $T_u$ with $T_{sym}$:** $T_u$ is the *useful* duration (FFT window), $T_{sym}$ includes the cyclic prefix. Subcarrier spacing uses $T_u$, not $T_{sym}$.
> 3. **Omitting the $1/N$ factor in IFFT:** Several students write $x[n] = \sum X_k e^{j2\pi kn/N}$ and lose a mark.
> 4. **Mixing up S/P and P/S converter positions** in block diagrams.
> 5. **Stating CP "removes ISI" without explaining the mechanism** (modular wrap-around / circular convolution). Examiners expect the *why*, not just the *what*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — OFDM Basics**

- **OFDM** = **O**rthogonal **F**requency **D**ivision **M**ultiplexing — a multicarrier modulation scheme.
- The **data stream is split** into $N$ parallel low-rate substreams, each modulating a separate subcarrier.
- **Subcarrier spacing** $\Delta f = 1/T_u$ ensures perfect orthogonality over the useful symbol duration $T_u$.
- **Orthogonality condition** (the heart of OFDM): $\int_0^{T_u} e^{j2\pi f_k t} \cdot e^{-j2\pi f_m t} \, dt = T_u \cdot \delta_{km}$.
- **IFFT/FFT** implement OFDM modulation/demodulation in the digital domain — replacing $N$ analog oscillators.
- **Cyclic Prefix (CP):** A copy of the symbol's tail prepended to absorb multipath ISI and convert linear → circular convolution.
- **CP length must exceed maximum delay spread:** $T_{CP} \ge \tau_{max}$.
- **Total OFDM symbol duration:** $T_{sym} = T_u + T_{CP}$.
- **Received frequency-domain model:** $Y[k] = H[k] X[k] + W[k]$.
- **One-tap equalization:** $\hat{X}[k] = Y[k] / H[k]$.
- **Trade-off:** Larger CP = better ISI protection but lower spectral efficiency (CP is overhead).
- **Advantages:** Robust against frequency-selective fading, simple equalization, high spectral efficiency.
- **Disadvantages:** High **PAPR** (Peak-to-Average Power Ratio), sensitive to frequency offsets and phase noise, requires linear amplifiers.
- **Real-world use:** 4G LTE, 5G NR, Wi-Fi (802.11a/g/n/ac/ax), DVB-T, ADSL, PLC.
- **Spectral efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{\log_2 M}{1 + N_{CP}/N}$ bits/sec/Hz.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Access for OFDM Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multiple Access for OFDM Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Multiple Access for OFDM Systems** refers to the set of techniques that allow multiple users to share the orthogonal subcarriers of an OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing) framework simultaneously, without mutual interference. The principal schemes adopted in modern broadband wireless standards (LTE, 5G NR, WiMAX) are:

- **OFDMA** (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access)
- **SC-FDMA** (Single Carrier Frequency Division Multiple Access)
- **MC-CDMA** (Multi-Carrier Code Division Multiple Access)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** OFDMA is the *multiple access* scheme adopted in the LTE downlink, while **SC-FDMA** is mandated for the LTE uplink because of its superior PAPR (Peak-to-Average Power Ratio) properties. **MC-CDMA** combines OFDM with spread-spectrum CDMA and is used in specific broadband wireless access scenarios.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Multi-Lane Highway" Picture

Imagine a wide, multi-lane expressway (the available system bandwidth, e.g., **20 MHz**). Each lane is a *subcarrier*, kept perfectly orthogonal to its neighbours.

- **OFDM** = dividing the highway into many parallel narrow lanes; one truck (data symbol) per lane.
- **OFDMA** = assigning *different groups of lanes to different users* in each time slot (a *resource block*).
- **SC-FDMA** = each user transmits on a *contiguous block of lanes*, but the data is first spread in the time domain using a **DFT precoder** (one big truck per user, taking multiple lanes together sequentially).
- **MC-CDMA** = each user puts their data on *all lanes at once*, but uses a unique *spreading code* so the receiver can separate them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Orthogonality Matters:** Subcarriers are spaced exactly at the symbol rate, $1/T_s$, so their spectra overlap but do **not** interfere. This is the cornerstone of every multiple-access OFDM scheme.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Recap

| Metric | Typical Value in LTE |
|---|---|
| Subcarrier spacing ($\Delta f$) | **15 kHz** |
| Useful symbol duration ($T_u$) | **66.67 $\mu$s** |
| Cyclic prefix (CP) length | **4.69 $\mu$s** (normal) |
| FFT size ($N_{FFT}$) | **2048** (for 20 MHz) |
| Subcarriers per RB | **12** |
| Resource Block (RB) bandwidth | **180 kHz** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The OFDM Foundation (Quick Recap)

A baseband OFDM signal with **$N$** orthogonal subcarriers carrying complex symbols $X_k$ is:

$$
s(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \, e^{\,j 2\pi k \Delta f \, t}, \quad 0 \le t \le T_s
$$

The **orthogonality condition** between subcarrier $k$ and $m$ is:

$$
\int_{0}^{T_s} e^{\,j2\pi k \Delta f t}\!\cdot e^{-j2\pi m \Delta f t}\,dt = T_s \,\delta_{km}
$$

where $\delta_{km}$ is the Kronecker delta. This is satisfied when $\Delta f = 1/T_s$.

## 2.2 OFDMA — Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access

OFDMA extends OFDM by **assigning subsets of subcarriers to different users** in each scheduling interval.

**Key Operating Principles:**
- The available $N$ subcarriers are partitioned into **resource blocks (RBs)**.
- A scheduler (e.g., Proportional Fair, Max C/I) dynamically allocates RBs based on **channel quality indicator (CQI)** reports.
- Each user gets a unique *time–frequency* tile in the 2-D resource grid.
- In LTE, a **subframe = 1 ms = 2 slots**, each slot carrying **6 or 7 OFDM symbols** depending on CP type.

**Advantages:**
- High spectral efficiency (subcarriers fully packed, no guard bands).
- Robust against frequency-selective fading (frequency-domain scheduling).
- Flexible bandwidth allocation — granularity of 180 kHz.

**Disadvantages:**
- **High PAPR** → requires linear power amplifiers (PA inefficiency).
- Sensitive to **carrier frequency offset (CFO)**.

## 2.3 SC-FDMA — Single Carrier FDMA

SC-FDMA uses a **DFT precoder** before the standard OFDM modulator. This produces a *single-carrier* signal in the time domain while retaining the *multi-carrier* flexibility in the frequency domain.

**Block Chain:**
> `User Bits → M-ary Mapper → Serial-to-Parallel → M-point DFT → Subcarrier Mapping (Localized or Distributed) → N-point IFFT → Add CP → DAC`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** The DFT size is **$M$** and IFFT size is **$N$** (with $M \le N$). The DFT-precoding spreads each QAM symbol across all $M$ subcarriers, lowering the instantaneous envelope fluctuation → **lower PAPR**.

**Subcarrier Mapping Modes:**
- **Localized (L-SC-FDMA):** Consecutive block of $M$ subcarriers.
- **Distributed (D-SC-FDMA):** Equi-spaced subcarriers (interleaved).

**PAPR Comparison (typical):**
- OFDM/OFDMA: **\approx 12 dB** for 64-QAM
- SC-FDMA: **\approx 5–7 dB** for the same constellation

This is why 3GPP chose SC-FDMA for LTE uplink.

## 2.4 MC-CDMA — Multi-Carrier CDMA

MC-CDMA transmits each data symbol on **all $N$** subcarriers simultaneously, but multiplied by a user-specific **spreading code** $C_m(k) \in \{+1, -1\}$ of length $N_c$ (usually $N_c = N$).

**Transmitted signal on subcarrier $k$ for user $m$:**

$$
X_m(k) = d_m \cdot C_m(k) \cdot a_m(k)
$$

where $d_m$ is the data symbol and $a_m(k)$ is the channel gain equalizer coefficient.

**Receiver uses code orthogonality** (Walsh–Hadamard codes) to separate users even after frequency-selective fading.

**Advantages over OFDMA:** Robust against deep fades (frequency diversity).  
**Disadvantages:** Higher complexity; multiple-access interference (MAI) if orthogonality is destroyed by the channel.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | OFDM subcarrier spacing | $\Delta f = 1/T_u$ | Hz |
| 2 | Useful symbol duration | $T_u = 1/\Delta f$ | seconds |
| 3 | Total symbol period | $T_s = T_u + T_{CP}$ | seconds |
| 4 | OFDM signal | $s(t)=\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k e^{j2\pi k \Delta f t}$ | baseband |
| 5 | PAPR of OFDM | $\mathrm{PAPR} = \dfrac{\max \vert s(t)\vert^2}{E[\vert s(t)\vert^2]}$ | linear or dB |
| 6 | PAPR (dB) | $\mathrm{PAPR}_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(\mathrm{PAPR})$ | dB |
| 7 | SC-FDMA PAPR (approx.) | $\mathrm{PAPR}_{SC\text{-}FDMA} \approx \mathrm{PAPR}_{SC} + 10\log_{10}(N/M)$ | dB |
| 8 | Resource Block bandwidth | $\mathrm{BW}_{RB} = 12 \cdot \Delta f$ | Hz (e.g., 180 kHz) |
| 9 | MC-CDMA spreading factor | $SF = N_c$ | unitless |
| 10 | OFDM signal-to-noise per subcarrier | $\mathrm{SNR}_k = \dfrac{\vert H_k\vert^2 P_k}{\sigma^2}$ | dB |
| 11 | Channel capacity (Shannon) per subcarrier | $C_k = \Delta f \log_2(1+\mathrm{SNR}_k)$ | bps |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The formulas above govern every design decision in 4G/5G — from subcarrier spacing choice in URLLC (60 kHz) to PAPR-limited uplink power amplifier sizing in smartphones.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — OFDM Signal from IDFT

**Goal:** Show that the discrete-time OFDM baseband signal is the Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform (IDFT) of the symbol block $X_k$.

**Step 1.** Sample the continuous-time OFDM signal at $t = nT_s/N$ for $n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$:

$$
s(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \, e^{\,j2\pi k n / N}
$$

**Step 2.** Recognise this sum as the **IDFT** definition (up to a $1/N$ scaling that we absorb into transmitter gain):

$$
s(n) = N \cdot \mathrm{IDFT}\{X_k\}
$$

**Step 3.** Hence, generating OFDM in practice requires only an **IFFT** — a fast algorithm (Cooley–Tukey) requiring $O(N \log_2 N)$ complex multiplications. This is the practical reason OFDM/OFDMA is implementable in real-time on a smartphone DSP.

> [!NOTE]
> The cyclic prefix converts linear convolution with the channel into circular convolution, allowing the receiver to perform **one-tap equalisation** per subcarrier: $\hat{X}_k = Y_k / H_k$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — SC-FDMA PAPR vs. OFDMA PAPR

**Starting point:** In OFDMA, $N$ independent QAM symbols modulate $N$ subcarriers. The discrete-time envelope has Gaussian-like amplitude distribution with PAPR approaching $N$ as $N \to \infty$.

For SC-FDMA, the **$M$-point DFT** of the data block decorrelates the IFFT input, giving a signal whose amplitude distribution resembles that of a **single-carrier QAM** system.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{PAPR}_{OFDMA} &\approx 10\log_{10}(N) \quad \text{(upper bound, large } N\text{)} \\
\mathrm{PAPR}_{SC\text{-}FDMA} &\approx 10\log_{10}(N) - 10\log_{10}(M) \\
&= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N}{M}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style):**  
Take $N = 1024$ (FFT) and $M = 256$ (DFT spread). Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{PAPR}_{OFDMA} &\approx 10\log_{10}(1024) = 30 \text{ dB (worst case)} \\
\mathrm{PAPR}_{SC\text{-}FDMA} &\approx 10\log_{10}(1024/256) = 6 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

This $\approx 4\times$ reduction is precisely why LTE chose SC-FDMA for uplink, preserving mobile-battery life.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — OFDMA Subcarrier Allocation & Spectral Efficiency

Let the system have bandwidth $B$ split into $N$ orthogonal subcarriers, and let $K$ active users share $N$ subcarriers with an allocation vector $\mathbf{A} = [A_0, A_1, \dots, A_{N-1}]$, where $A_k \in \{0,1,\dots,K\}$ indicates the user on subcarrier $k$.

The **sum capacity** under adaptive bit-loading is:

$$
C_{sum} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \Delta f \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{\vert H_k\vert^2 P_k}{\sigma^2}\right)
$$

Under **equal power allocation** $P_k = P/N$:

$$
C_{sum} = \Delta f \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{\vert H_k\vert^2 P}{N\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**OFDMA spectral efficiency** (bits/s/Hz) becomes:

$$
\eta = \frac{C_{sum}}{B} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{\vert H_k\vert^2 P}{N\sigma^2}\right)
$$

This is the *water-filling* upper bound that any practical scheduler (e.g., Round-Robin, Proportional-Fair) tries to approach.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OFDMA Resource Grid (Time–Frequency)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph TimeAxis["Time Axis (1 subframe = 1 ms)"]
        t0["Slot 0"] --> t1["Slot 1"]
    end
    subgraph FreqAxis["Frequency Axis (12 subcarriers per RB)"]
        f0["Subcarrier 0"]
        f1["Subcarrier 1"]
        f2["Subcarrier 2"]
        f3["Subcarrier 3"]
        f4["Subcarrier 4"]
        f5["Subcarrier 5"]
        f6["Subcarrier 6"]
        f7["Subcarrier 7"]
        f8["Subcarrier 8"]
        f9["Subcarrier 9"]
        f10["Subcarrier 10"]
        f11["Subcarrier 11"]
    end
    u1["User 1 RB"]:::u1style
    u2["User 2 RB"]:::u2style
    u3["User 3 RB"]:::u3style
    u1 -.- f0
    u1 -.- f1
    u1 -.- f2
    u1 -.- f3
    u2 -.- f4
    u2 -.- f5
    u2 -.- f6
    u2 -.- f7
    u3 -.- f8
    u3 -.- f9
    u3 -.- f10
    u3 -.- f11
    classDef u1style fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#d2691e,stroke-width:2px
    classDef u2style fill:#a5d8ff,stroke:#1e5fa8,stroke-width:2px
    classDef u3style fill:#b5e8b0,stroke:#3a8a35,stroke-width:2px
```

> The diagram above illustrates **OFDMA's defining feature**: distinct users occupy *different* time–frequency tiles in the same subframe.

## 4.2 SC-FDMA Transmitter Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["User Data Bits"] --> B["M-QAM Mapper"]
    B --> C["Serial to Parallel S/P"]
    C --> D["M-point DFT Precoder"]
    D --> E["Subcarrier Mapper Localized or Distributed"]
    E --> F["N-point IFFT N greater than M"]
    F --> G["Add Cyclic Prefix"]
    G --> H["DAC and RF Upconversion"]
    H --> I["PA Power Amplifier"]
    I --> J["Antenna Tx"]
    classDef dft fill:#ffe599,stroke:#b38600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef iff fill:#c9e7ff,stroke:#1e5fa8,stroke-width:2px
    class D dft
    class F iff
```

> The **yellow block** (DFT precoder) is the only architectural difference from a conventional OFDMA transmitter — yet it is what makes SC-FDMA PAPR-friendly.

## 4.3 OFDMA vs SC-FDMA vs MC-CDMA — Comparison Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    R["Multiple Access over OFDM"] --> R1["OFDMA"]
    R --> R2["SC-FDMA"]
    R --> R3["MC-CDMA"]
    R1 --> R1a["Subcarriers partitioned per user"]
    R1 --> R1b["High PAPR about 12 dB"]
    R1 --> R1c["Used in LTE 4G Downlink and 5G NR"]
    R2 --> R2a["DFT precoded single carrier signal"]
    R2 --> R2b["Low PAPR about 5 to 7 dB"]
    R2 --> R2c["Used in LTE 4G Uplink"]
    R3 --> R3a["Spread spectrum on all subcarriers"]
    R3 --> R3b["Frequency diversity gain"]
    R3 --> R3c["Used in 3G HIPERLAN 2 and BWA"]
    classDef ofdma fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#d2691e,stroke-width:2px
    classDef scfdma fill:#a5d8ff,stroke:#1e5fa8,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mccdma fill:#d8b6ff,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px
    class R1 ofdma
    class R2 scfdma
    class R3 mccdma
```

## 4.4 OFDMA Receiver with One-Tap Equalisation

```mermaid
graph LR
    Rx["Received Signal r n"] --> CP["Remove CP"]
    CP --> FFT["N-point FFT"]
    FFT --> EQ["One-Tap Equalizer Yk divided by Hk"]
    EQ --> DM["Demap per subcarrier to user"]
    DM --> P["Parallel to Serial P/S"]
    P --> OUT["Recovered Data"]
    classDef eq fill:#b5e8b0,stroke:#3a8a35,stroke-width:2px
    class EQ eq
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between OFDM and OFDMA. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

- **OFDM** is a *modulation* technique that splits a wideband signal into many orthogonal narrowband subcarriers; it supports only **one user** at a time over the full set of subcarriers.
- **OFDMA** is a *multiple-access* scheme built on OFDM where the subcarriers are partitioned into **resource blocks**, and *multiple users* are assigned different time–frequency tiles simultaneously.
- OFDMA additionally incorporates a **scheduling algorithm** (e.g., Proportional Fair) and a **resource allocation map** indicating which user occupies which subcarrier.
- In short: **OFDM is one-user modulation; OFDMA is multi-user scheduling on top of OFDM.**

### Q2. Why is SC-FDMA preferred over OFDMA for the LTE uplink? `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

- LTE uplink is powered by a **battery-constrained handset**; the Power Amplifier (PA) efficiency is critical.
- SC-FDMA uses a **DFT precoder** before the IFFT, producing a *single-carrier-like* time-domain signal with low envelope fluctuation.
- The resulting **PAPR is 2–5 dB lower** than OFDMA, allowing the PA to operate in its linear (efficient) region for longer.
- Throughput and latency are largely unaffected because of localised/distributed subcarrier mapping.
- Hence, 3GPP mandated **SC-FDMA for uplink** in LTE releases 8/9/10/11.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

### **Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the OFDMA transmitter and receiver. Discuss the role of the cyclic prefix. **(7 marks)**

### **Q. (b)** An LTE system uses $N = 1024$ subcarriers with subcarrier spacing $\Delta f = 15$ kHz. Compute (i) useful symbol duration, (ii) subframe duration, (iii) total bandwidth occupied. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**OFDMA Transmitter:**

1. **Channel coding & interleaving** of each user's bit stream.
2. **Bit-to-symbol mapping** (QPSK/16-QAM/64-QAM) producing $X_m(k)$ for user $m$ on subcarrier $k$.
3. **Subcarrier allocation map** from MAC scheduler assigns RBs.
4. **N-point IFFT** generates time-domain samples for *all* users simultaneously:

   $$
   s(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{\,j2\pi k n / N}
   $$

5. **Cyclic prefix (CP) insertion**: copy the last $N_g$ samples and prepend:

   $$
   s_{cp}(n) = s(N-N_g+n), \quad n=0,\dots,N_g-1
   $$

6. **DAC → RF upconversion → Antenna**.

**Role of Cyclic Prefix:**

- Converts **linear convolution** of signal with channel into **circular convolution**.
- The receiver FFT diagonalises this circular convolution: $Y_k = H_k X_k$.
- Enables **one-tap frequency-domain equalisation**: $\hat{X}_k = Y_k / H_k$.
- Absorbs **multipath delay spread** up to $T_{CP}$; CP length must exceed maximum delay spread.
- Trades efficiency: CP overhead $\eta_{CP} = T_{CP} / (T_u + T_{CP}) \approx 7\%$ for normal CP in LTE.

**OFDMA Receiver:**

1. **Remove CP** → **N-point FFT** → extract $Y_k$.
2. **Channel estimation** using pilot subcarriers (reference signals).
3. **Per-subcarrier equalisation** $\hat{X}_k = Y_k / H_k$.
4. **Demapping & decoding** of each user's allocated subcarriers.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Given:** $N = 1024$, $\Delta f = 15$ kHz.

**(i) Useful symbol duration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_u &= \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{15 \times 10^3} \\
&= 66.67 \; \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Subframe duration:**  
In LTE, **1 subframe = 2 slots = 14 OFDM symbols** (normal CP) or **12 symbols** (extended CP). With normal CP, $T_{CP} = 160 \cdot T_s = 4.69\ \mu s$ where $T_s = 1/30.72\,\text{MHz} = 32.55\,\text{ns}$. Therefore $T_{sym} = T_u + T_{CP} = 66.67 + 4.69 = 71.36\ \mu s$. The subframe is:

$$
T_{SF} = 14 \times 71.36\ \mu s = 1.0\ \text{ms}
$$

**(iii) Total bandwidth:**

$$
B = N \cdot \Delta f = 1024 \times 15\text{ kHz} = 15.36\ \text{ MHz}
$$

This matches the 3GPP 15 MHz LTE channel raster (3 resource blocks reserved as guard).

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`

### **Q. (a)** Compare OFDMA, SC-FDMA, and MC-CDMA in terms of PAPR, multiple-access mechanism, and typical application. **(7 marks)**

### **Q. (b)** Explain the working of SC-FDMA transmitter with block diagram. A 20 MHz LTE system uses $N = 2048$ IFFT and $M = 512$ DFT blocks. Calculate the PAPR improvement over OFDMA in dB. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

| Parameter | **OFDMA** | **SC-FDMA** | **MC-CDMA** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Multiple-access mechanism | Subcarrier partitioning (frequency-domain) | DFT-precoded, time-domain single-carrier | Spread spectrum on all subcarriers via unique codes |
| PAPR | **High** (≈ 12 dB) | **Low** (≈ 5–7 dB) | **Moderate** (≈ 8–10 dB) |
| Receiver complexity | Low (1-tap equaliser) | Moderate (DFT de-spreading + 1-tap) | High (code correlation + equaliser) |
| Frequency diversity | Per-RB only | Depends on mapping (localised/distributed) | **High** (full-band spreading) |
| MAI resistance | None (orthogonal in freq.) | None | Excellent with orthogonal codes |
| Typical application | LTE **downlink**, 5G NR, WiMAX | LTE **uplink** | HIPERLAN/2, BWA, future 6G research |

#### Model Solution — (b)

**SC-FDMA Transmitter Working:**

1. The user's serial bit stream is mapped to $M$ complex QAM symbols.
2. These are passed through a **serial-to-parallel converter** to form a block of $M$ symbols.
3. The block is fed to a **$M$-point DFT** (the "DFT precoder"). This spreads each symbol's energy across $M$ subcarriers.
4. The $M$ DFT outputs are **mapped to $M$ of the $N$ IFFT inputs**:
   - **Localized mapping:** consecutive $M$ inputs.
   - **Distributed mapping:** equally spaced inputs.
5. An **$N$-point IFFT** converts the mapped block to time-domain samples.
6. A **cyclic prefix** is added; the signal is D/A-converted and transmitted.

**PAPR Improvement Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{PAPR}_{OFDMA} &= 10 \log_{10}(N) = 10\log_{10}(2048) \approx 33.11 \text{ dB (worst case)} \\
\mathrm{PAPR}_{SC\text{-}FDMA} &= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N}{M}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2048}{512}\right) \\
&= 10\log_{10}(4) = 6.02 \text{ dB} \\
\Delta \mathrm{PAPR} &= 33.11 - 6.02 = 27.09 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice, with clipping and oversampling, the *effective* PAPR improvement for a given CCDF is **\approx 2–4 dB**, but the theoretical bound is dramatic — illustrating why DFT spreading is the cornerstone of LTE uplink.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Do **not** confuse the *DFT size* $M$ with the *IFFT size* $N$ — the IFFT must be larger or equal to the DFT ($N \ge M$) to fit the SC-FDMA bandwidth.
> 2. PAPR of 33 dB is an *upper bound* with oversampling. The textbook exam question expects the **approximate** formula above, not a CCDF-curve integration.
> 3. Many students forget to convert PAPR to dB using $10\log_{10}$ — that costs a full mark.
> 4. When asked to "compare" schemes, *always* present a table — KTU valuators award easy marks for structured comparison.
> 5. Cyclic prefix overhead $\eta_{CP} = T_{CP}/(T_u+T_{CP})$ must be shown, not just stated as "negligible".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **OFDM** is single-user modulation over $N$ orthogonal subcarriers with cyclic-prefix-based one-tap equalisation.
- **OFDMA** multiplies OFDM by *subcarrier partitioning* + *scheduling* — used in LTE downlink and 5G NR.
- **SC-FDMA** inserts a **DFT precoder** before IFFT, giving *single-carrier-like* low PAPR — used in LTE uplink.
- **MC-CDMA** spreads each symbol on **all** subcarriers using a user-specific code — high frequency diversity, higher complexity.
- Orthogonality condition: $\Delta f = 1/T_u$.
- Cyclic prefix length must exceed the channel's maximum delay spread.
- Standard LTE numerology: $\Delta f = 15$ kHz, $T_u = 66.67\ \mu s$, $T_{sym} \approx 71.36\ \mu s$, **1 subframe = 1 ms = 14 symbols**.
- **12 subcarriers = 1 Resource Block = 180 kHz**.
- **PAPR comparison rule of thumb:** OFDMA $\sim 10\log_{10}N$ dB; SC-FDMA $\sim 10\log_{10}(N/M)$ dB.
- Capacity per subcarrier: $C_k = \Delta f \log_2(1+\mathrm{SNR}_k)$; sum capacity drives the scheduler.
- MC-CDMA codes are typically **Walsh–Hadamard** of length equal to the spreading factor.
- Always remember to add **one blank line** before and after display equations in your answer sheet.
- Examiner mantra: **structured comparison = easy marks; block diagrams = easy marks; correct units = easy marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access (OFDMA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access (OFDMA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access (OFDMA)** is a multi-carrier, multi-user digital modulation and multiple access scheme derived from Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM). In OFDMA, the total available system bandwidth is divided into a large number of **orthogonal, overlapping subcarriers** (mathematically spaced at the reciprocal of the useful symbol period). These subcarriers are then grouped into **subchannels (or Resource Blocks, RBs)**, and different subchannel groups are dynamically assigned to different users in the same cell, simultaneously, in both the **frequency domain** and the **time domain**.

The formal KTU definition reads:

> OFDMA is a hybrid of OFDM and FDMA in which the orthogonality of subcarriers is preserved so that the spectra of individual subcarriers can overlap without mutual interference, and orthogonal subcarrier sets (subchannels) are allocated to distinct users to enable simultaneous multi-user communication over a shared wideband channel.

The scheme is the foundational multiple access technology behind **4G LTE, 4G LTE-Advanced, WiMAX (IEEE 802.16e/m)**, and the **5G New Radio (NR)** air interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> OFDMA is a multi-user version of OFDM in which subsets of orthogonal subcarriers are assigned to individual users as subchannels, allowing multiple users to be multiplexed in frequency and time within a single OFDM symbol.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway with Lanes" Picture

Imagine a wide multi-lane super-highway (the total system bandwidth, say **20 MHz**). Each lane is a *subcarrier*, and the lanes are so cleverly spaced that the cars in adjacent lanes do not touch even though the lanes are shared. This is the **orthogonality** property.

- In plain **OFDM**, the whole highway is reserved for *one big convoy* of a single user during one symbol interval. The user fills the whole bandwidth every symbol.
- In **OFDMA**, the same highway is split into lane-bundles (subchannels), and **different users (User A, User B, User C…) drive their own convoy simultaneously in their own bundles of lanes** during the same symbol interval.

A simpler analogy: think of a large office whiteboard split into a grid. Each **row** of the grid represents a subcarrier; each **column** represents an OFDM symbol (time). A user is given a rectangular patch (a few rows × a few columns) on the board. Different users get different patches. The grid cells (one row × one column) are called **Resource Elements (REs)**.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value (LTE 4G) | Typical Value (5G NR) |
|---|---|---|
| FFT size ($N_{FFT}$) | **2048** | 512 / 1024 / 2048 / 4096 |
| Subcarrier spacing ($\Delta f$) | **15 kHz** | 15 / 30 / 60 / 120 / 240 kHz |
| Useful symbol duration ($T_u$) | **66.67 µs** | Scalable |
| Cyclic prefix (normal) | **4.69 µs** | Variable |
| Subcarriers per RB | **12** | 12 |
| Subcarriers per Resource Element Group | varies | varies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter in KTU exams:** Examiners frequently test the relationship $\Delta f = 1/T_u$ and ask students to compute the useful symbol time for a given subcarrier spacing. For 15 kHz, $T_u = 1 / 15000 \approx 66.67\ \mu s$.

---

### 1.4 Position of OFDMA in Modern Cellular Standards

OFDMA replaced **CDMA (Code Division Multiple Access)** used in 3G (WCDMA/HSPA) because it offers:
1. **Robustness against multipath fading** through the cyclic prefix.
2. **High spectral efficiency** due to overlapping but orthogonal subcarriers.
3. **Granular, dynamic resource allocation** to users based on instantaneous channel quality (used in *channel-aware scheduling*).
4. **Ease of MIMO integration** because each subcarrier sees a flat-fading channel.

> [!TIP]
> The KTU board examiner expects you to know *why* OFDMA is preferred over CDMA in 4G/5G. The crisp one-line answer: **"OFDMA avoids intra-cell interference through orthogonality and provides flexible frequency-domain scheduling, whereas CDMA suffers from Multiple Access Interference (MAI) and requires tight power control."**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Conceptual Flow: From OFDM to OFDMA

**Step 1 — OFDM signal construction.**
A high-rate bit stream is split into $N$ low-rate parallel streams, each modulating one of $N$ orthogonal subcarriers. The continuous-time OFDM signal in one symbol period $T_u$ is

$$
x(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \, e^{\,j 2\pi f_k t}, \quad 0 \le t < T_u
$$

where $X_k$ is the complex modulation symbol on the $k^{th}$ subcarrier and $f_k$ is its frequency.

**Step 2 — Orthogonality condition.**
The subcarriers must satisfy

$$
\int_0^{T_u} e^{\,j 2\pi f_m t}\, e^{-j 2\pi f_n t}\, dt = 0 \quad \text{for } m \neq n
$$

This forces the subcarrier spacing to be

$$
\Delta f = f_{k+1} - f_k = \frac{1}{T_u}
$$

so the spectrum of each subcarrier has a **null exactly at the center of every other subcarrier** — this is the "overlap without interference" property.

**Step 3 — Extension to multiple access.**
In OFDMA, the set $\{0, 1, \ldots, N-1\}$ of subcarrier indices is partitioned into $U$ disjoint subsets $\mathcal{S}_1, \mathcal{S}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{S}_U$, one per user. User $u$ transmits on the subcarriers indexed in $\mathcal{S}_u$. Because the subcarriers are mutually orthogonal, the receiver for user $u$ can extract $X_k$ for $k \in \mathcal{S}_u$ without interference from other users.

**Step 4 — Resource grid abstraction.**
The 2-D grid (subcarriers × OFDM symbols) is the **time-frequency resource grid**. The basic unit is the **Resource Element (RE)** = 1 subcarrier × 1 OFDM symbol. In LTE, a **Resource Block (RB)** = 12 subcarriers × 7 OFDM symbols (normal cyclic prefix).

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum required formula set** for any Part A or Part B question on OFDMA. Memorise it.

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k e^{j 2\pi f_k t}$ | Continuous-time OFDM baseband signal |
| 2 | $\Delta f = 1 / T_u$ | Orthogonality condition: subcarrier spacing equals reciprocal of useful symbol time |
| 3 | $T_u = 1 / \Delta f$ | Useful OFDM symbol duration |
| 4 | $T_{sym} = T_u + T_{CP}$ | Total symbol duration with cyclic prefix |
| 5 | $X_n = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x_k e^{\,j 2\pi kn / N}$ | Inverse DFT mapping the time-domain samples to subcarriers |
| 6 | $x_k = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} X_n e^{-j 2\pi kn / N}$ | DFT used at receiver to recover symbols |
| 7 | $N = BW / \Delta f$ | Number of orthogonal subcarriers fitting in a bandwidth $BW$ |
| 8 | $\text{PAPR} = \dfrac{\max \vert x(t) \vert^{2}}{\mathbb{E}\left[ \vert x(t) \vert^{2} \right]}$ | Peak-to-Average Power Ratio; high in OFDM, must be ≤ **6–9 dB** in practice |
| 9 | $L_{CP} \ge \tau_{max}$ | Cyclic prefix length must be ≥ maximum channel delay spread to remove ISI |
| 10 | $N_{RB} = N_{FFT} / 12$ | Number of Resource Blocks (LTE) when $N_{FFT} = 2048$ → $N_{RB} = 100$ per antenna port (10 MHz) |
| 11 | $\eta_{spectral} = \dfrac{\log_2 M}{1 + T_{CP}/T_u}$ bits/s/Hz | Spectral efficiency for $M$-ary modulation with cyclic prefix overhead |
| 12 | $f_k = f_0 + k \Delta f$ | Center frequency of $k^{th}$ subcarrier |

> **Engineering Utility:** The orthogonality condition (formula 2) is *the* reason OFDMA achieves high spectral efficiency. Equations 5 and 6 show that OFDM is implemented with the **IFFT/FFT** in hardware, which is why OFDMA systems are so cost-effective: a single $N$-point IFFT generates the entire multi-carrier waveform digitally.

---

### 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why subcarrier spacing is $1/T_u$?** Because that is the *exact* spacing at which the sinc-shaped spectra of adjacent subcarriers cross zero at each subcarrier's center frequency. Closer → inter-carrier interference (ICI); farther → wasted bandwidth.
- **Why use cyclic prefix?** Multipath causes Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI). Appending a copy of the *tail* of each OFDM symbol as a guard interval converts the linear convolution of the channel into a circular convolution, so the FFT diagonalises the channel and each subcarrier experiences a single complex gain.
- **Why granular resource allocation?** Wireless channels are *frequency-selective*. A user close to the base station may have a strong sub-band at 2.4 GHz, while another user nearby has a null there. OFDMA's scheduler exploits this *multi-user diversity* by giving each user the subcarriers where it is strongest — boosting aggregate cell throughput by 30–70 % in practice.

> [!NOTE]
> This multi-user diversity gain is the single most important KTU point about OFDMA. Examiners expect: *"OFDMA enables frequency-selective scheduling across users, harvesting multi-user diversity gain."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Orthogonality Condition for Subcarriers

We want the cross-correlation between subcarriers $m$ and $n$ over one useful symbol period $T_u$ to be zero. Define the inner product

$$
I_{m,n} \;=\; \int_0^{T_u} e^{\,j 2\pi f_m t}\, e^{-j 2\pi f_n t}\, dt
$$

**Step 1** — Combine the exponentials:

$$
I_{m,n} \;=\; \int_0^{T_u} e^{\,j 2\pi (f_m - f_n) t}\, dt
$$

**Step 2** — Perform the integration:

$$
I_{m,n} \;=\; \left[ \frac{e^{\,j 2\pi (f_m - f_n) t}}{j 2\pi (f_m - f_n)} \right]_0^{T_u}
$$

**Step 3** — Evaluate the limits:

$$
I_{m,n} \;=\; \frac{e^{\,j 2\pi (f_m - f_n) T_u} - 1}{j 2\pi (f_m - f_n)}
$$

**Step 4** — Let $\Delta f_{mn} = f_m - f_n$ and use Euler's identity. For $I_{m,n} = 0$ we need

$$
\sin\!\left( \pi \Delta f_{mn} T_u \right) \;=\; 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta f_{mn} \neq 0
$$

**Step 5** — The smallest non-zero solution is

$$
\pi \Delta f_{mn} T_u \;=\; \pi \cdot q, \quad q \in \mathbb{Z}, \ q \neq 0
$$

Taking $q = 1$ (minimum spacing) gives the orthogonality condition:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta f \;=\; \frac{1}{T_u}\;}
$$

Therefore, integer multiples of $\Delta f$ also satisfy orthogonality, justifying the choice $f_k = f_0 + k \Delta f$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of OFDM Modulation via IDFT

A practical OFDM transmitter uses an **N-point IDFT** to map $N$ frequency-domain symbols $X_0, X_1, \ldots, X_{N-1}$ into $N$ time-domain samples $x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_{N-1}$.

**Step 1** — Sample the continuous-time OFDM signal at $t = n T_s$, where $T_s = T_u / N$:

$$
x(n T_s) \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \, e^{\,j 2\pi k \Delta f \cdot n T_s}
$$

**Step 2** — Substitute $\Delta f \cdot T_s = (1/T_u) \cdot (T_u/N) = 1/N$:

$$
x_n \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \, e^{\,j 2\pi k n / N}
$$

**Step 3** — Identify this as the **N-point IDFT** of $\{X_k\}$:

$$
\boxed{\;x_n \;=\; \text{IDFT}\{X_k\} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k e^{\,j 2\pi k n / N}\;}
$$

**Step 4** — At the receiver, an $N$-point DFT recovers the symbols:

$$
\boxed{\;X_k \;=\; \text{DFT}\{x_n\} \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_n e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The IDFT/DFT relationship proves that an OFDM modulator is *literally* a single IFFT chip. This is why modern 4G/5G baseband processors are dominated by a programmable FFT accelerator.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Computing LTE Downlink Parameters

**Given:** LTE system with $BW = 10\ \text{MHz}$, subcarrier spacing $\Delta f = 15\ \text{kHz}$, FFT size $N_{FFT} = 1024$, normal cyclic prefix of 4.69 µs.

**Step 1** — Number of usable subcarriers:

$$
N_{usable} \;=\; \left\lfloor \frac{BW}{\Delta f} \right\rfloor \;=\; \left\lfloor \frac{10 \times 10^6}{15 \times 10^3} \right\rfloor \;=\; 666
$$

(In practice 600 of these are used for data.)

**Step 2** — Useful symbol duration:

$$
T_u \;=\; \frac{1}{\Delta f} \;=\; \frac{1}{15\,000} \;=\; 66.67\ \mu s
$$

**Step 3** — Number of Resource Blocks:

$$
N_{RB} \;=\; \frac{N_{usable}}{12} \;=\; \frac{600}{12} \;=\; 50\ \text{RBs}
$$

**Step 4** — Total OFDM symbol duration with normal CP:

$$
T_{sym} \;=\; T_u + T_{CP} \;=\; 66.67 + 4.69 \;=\; 71.36\ \mu s
$$

**Step 5** — Number of OFDM symbols in a 1 ms subframe (LTE):

$$
N_{sym/sub} \;=\; \frac{1000\ \mu s}{71.36\ \mu s} \;\approx\; 14\ \text{symbols}
$$

(Matches the LTE standard: 14 OFDM symbols per subframe with normal CP.)

---

### 3.4 Derivation of PAPR for Multi-Carrier Signals

For a baseband OFDM signal $x(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k e^{j 2\pi f_k t}$ with $N$ independently modulated subcarriers using the same constellation power $\sigma^2$:

**Step 1** — The instantaneous power at time $t$ is

$$
P(t) \;=\; \vert x(t) \vert^2 \;=\; \left\vert \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k e^{j 2\pi f_k t} \right\vert^2
$$

**Step 2** — The expected (average) power, by independence of $\{X_k\}$, is

$$
\mathbb{E}[P] \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \mathbb{E}[\vert X_k \vert^2] \;=\; N \sigma^2
$$

**Step 3** — The peak power (worst-case, all symbols add coherently with the same phase) is

$$
P_{peak} \;=\; \left\vert \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X_k \vert \right\vert^2 \;\approx\; (N \sqrt{\sigma^2})^2 \;=\; N^2 \sigma^2
$$

**Step 4** — The PAPR is

$$
\boxed{\;\text{PAPR} \;=\; \frac{P_{peak}}{\mathbb{E}[P]} \;\approx\; N\;}
$$

In dB: $\text{PAPR}_{dB} \approx 10 \log_{10}(N)$. For $N = 1024$, PAPR ≈ **30 dB**, which is why practical OFDMA systems must use power amplifier back-off or PAPR-reduction techniques (clipping, selected mapping, partial transmit sequences).

> [!TIP]
> KTU short-answer favourite: *"Why is PAPR high in OFDM?"* — **Answer: Many subcarriers can add constructively in the worst case, producing peaks $\sqrt{N}$ times the RMS. Since the PA must amplify these peaks linearly, efficiency is reduced."**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 OFDMA Transmitter / Receiver Block Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **end-to-end signal-processing chain** of an OFDMA transceiver. Multiple users' data are mapped onto disjoint subcarriers, modulated by the IFFT, and sent over the channel. The receiver reverses the process for each user.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TX[TRANSMITTER SIDE]
        A1[User 1 Bit Stream] --> B1[QAM Mapper U1]
        A2[User 2 Bit Stream] --> B2[QAM Mapper U2]
        A3[User 3 Bit Stream] --> B3[QAM Mapper U3]
        B1 --> C1[Subcarrier Allocator]
        B2 --> C1
        B3 --> C1
        C1 --> D1[N point IFFT]
        D1 --> E1[Add Cyclic Prefix]
        E1 --> F1[DAC and Low Pass Filter]
        F1 --> G1[RF Upconversion and Power Amplifier]
        G1 --> H1[Transmit Antenna]
    end

    H1 -. Wireless Multipath Channel .-> I1[Receive Antenna]

    subgraph RX[RECEIVER SIDE]
        I1 --> J1[RF Downconversion and LNA]
        J1 --> K1[ADC and Matched Filter]
        K1 --> L1[Remove Cyclic Prefix]
        L1 --> M1[N point FFT]
        M1 --> N1[Subcarrier Deallocator]
        N1 --> O1[QAM Demapper U1]
        N1 --> O2[QAM Demapper U2]
        N1 --> O3[QAM Demapper U3]
        O1 --> P1[User 1 Bit Stream]
        O2 --> P2[User 2 Bit Stream]
        O3 --> P3[User 3 Bit Stream]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **Subcarrier Allocator** is the heart of OFDMA — it is the *scheduler* that decides which user gets which subcarriers based on Channel Quality Indicator (CQI) feedback. The IFFT/FFT pair is the OFDM engine. The **Cyclic Prefix** is appended *after* the IFFT and removed *before* the FFT.

---

### 4.2 Time-Frequency Resource Grid in OFDMA

The diagram below is a **2-D resource-allocation map**. The vertical axis represents the subcarriers (frequency), the horizontal axis represents successive OFDM symbols (time). Each cell is one Resource Element. Users 1, 2 and 3 are allocated non-overlapping subcarrier groups over successive symbols.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Time Frequency Resource Grid of One OFDMA Frame]
    R --> SC0[Subcarrier Group 0 to 11]
    R --> SC1[Subcarrier Group 12 to 23]
    R --> SC2[Subcarrier Group 24 to 35]
    R --> SC3[Subcarrier Group 36 to 47]

    SC0 --> AU1[Allocated to User 1 RB 0]
    SC1 --> AU2[Allocated to User 2 RB 1]
    SC2 --> AU3[Allocated to User 3 RB 2]
    SC3 --> AU4[Allocated to User 1 RB 3]

    AU1 --> N1[Each RB is 12 subcarriers by 7 OFDM symbols]
    AU2 --> N1
    AU3 --> N1
    AU4 --> N1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Concept:** Different users occupy **different subcarriers** in the **same OFDM symbol**. This is the defining property of OFDMA: simultaneous users, orthogonal subcarriers, no inter-user interference *as long as* the carrier-frequency-offset (CFO) is within the cyclic-prefix tolerance.

---

### 4.3 Frequency-Domain View: Orthogonality of Subcarriers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Total System Bandwidth BW]
    F --> A[Subcarrier 0 f0 Spectrum sinc shaped]
    F --> B[Subcarrier 1 f1 Spectrum sinc shaped]
    F --> C[Subcarrier 2 f2 Spectrum sinc shaped]
    F --> D[Subcarrier k fk Spectrum sinc shaped]

    A -. peak at f0 null at f1 f2 .-> E[Orthogonal Overlap]
    B -. peak at f1 null at f0 f2 .-> E
    C -. peak at f2 null at f0 f1 .-> E
    D -. peak at fk null at f0 f1 .-> E
```

> **Key visual takeaway:** Each subcarrier's spectrum is a **sinc function**. The spacing $\Delta f = 1/T_u$ places every subcarrier's spectrum zero exactly at the centre frequency of every other subcarrier. Hence, the spectra *overlap physically* but the subcarriers are *mathematically orthogonal*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part A carries **3 marks per question**, typically one or two definitions / short derivations. Answers should be **concise (3–5 sentences)** and **to-the-point**.

---

#### Q1. Define OFDMA. How is it different from OFDM?  [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**

OFDMA (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access) is a multi-user version of OFDM in which the available bandwidth is divided into a large number of orthogonal subcarriers and these subcarriers are grouped into subchannels assigned to different users for simultaneous transmission. In plain OFDM, all subcarriers are used by a *single* user per symbol. In OFDMA, different *users* simultaneously occupy *different* subsets of subcarriers. Thus, OFDMA = OFDM + multi-user subcarrier allocation. It enables frequency-selective scheduling and is the multiple access scheme used in 4G LTE, WiMAX and 5G NR.

---

#### Q2. State the orthogonality condition for OFDM subcarriers and explain its significance.  [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer:**

The orthogonality condition requires that the subcarrier spacing equals the reciprocal of the useful symbol duration, i.e. $\Delta f = 1 / T_u$. Subcarriers are placed at frequencies $f_k = f_0 + k \Delta f$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$. Under this condition, the spectra of subcarriers (sinc-shaped) overlap physically but have zero cross-correlation, so the receiver can recover each subcarrier's data without inter-carrier interference. This permits tight packing of subcarriers and yields high **spectral efficiency**, which is the central reason OFDMA is the 4G/5G multiple-access technology.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B carries **14 marks per question** with **internal choice** (i.e., you must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B). Each question typically splits into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the OFDMA transmitter. Discuss the role of the IFFT, cyclic prefix, and subcarrier allocator. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The OFDMA transmitter has the following functional blocks:

1. **Bit stream of each user:** $U$ user bit streams $\{b^{(u)}\}$, $u = 1, \ldots, U$ enter the transmitter.
2. **QAM mapper:** Each stream is mapped to complex symbols $X_k^{(u)}$ (BPSK, QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM etc.) at a low symbol rate $R_b / N$.
3. **Subcarrier allocator (scheduler):** A central scheduler assigns each user $u$ a set of subcarrier indices $\mathcal{S}_u$ based on CQI feedback. The allocator places $X_k^{(u)}$ at the $N$ input bins of the IFFT, with nulls for subcarriers not used by that user.
4. **N-point IFFT:** Computes $x_n = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$, transforming the frequency-domain data into a time-domain OFDM symbol.
5. **Cyclic prefix insertion:** The last $N_{CP}$ samples of the IFFT output are copied and prepended to form $x_n^{CP}$ of length $N + N_{CP}$. The CP length must be $\ge$ the maximum channel delay spread to absorb multipath echoes and remove ISI.
6. **DAC and RF chain:** The samples are converted to analogue, upconverted to RF, amplified and radiated.

**Roles:**
- **IFFT:** It is the modulator that creates all $N$ orthogonal subcarriers in one hardware block, eliminating the need for $N$ separate oscillators and mixers.
- **Cyclic prefix:** It converts the channel's linear convolution with the transmitted signal into a circular convolution, which makes the channel diagonal in the frequency domain. Each subcarrier thus sees a *flat* (single-tap) complex gain, simplifying equalisation to one complex multiplication per subcarrier.
- **Subcarrier allocator:** It is the *intelligence* of OFDMA — it exploits **multi-user diversity** by giving each user the subcarriers with the highest instantaneous SNR, yielding 30–70 % throughput gain over time-division scheduling.

**(b)** Derive the orthogonality condition for OFDM subcarriers. Show that the minimum subcarrier spacing is $\Delta f = 1/T_u$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Consider two subcarriers with frequencies $f_m$ and $f_n$. Their cross-correlation over the useful symbol interval $T_u$ is

$$
I_{m,n} \;=\; \int_0^{T_u} e^{j 2\pi f_m t} \, e^{-j 2\pi f_n t} \, dt
$$

Combining the exponentials,

$$
I_{m,n} \;=\; \int_0^{T_u} e^{j 2\pi (f_m - f_n) t} \, dt
$$

Let $\Delta f_{mn} = f_m - f_n$. Then

$$
I_{m,n} \;=\; \frac{e^{j 2\pi \Delta f_{mn} T_u} - 1}{j 2\pi \Delta f_{mn}}
$$

Using Euler's identity $e^{j\theta} - 1 = 2j \sin(\theta/2) e^{j\theta/2}$,

$$
I_{m,n} \;=\; \frac{\sin(\pi \Delta f_{mn} T_u)}{\pi \Delta f_{mn}} \, e^{j \pi \Delta f_{mn} T_u}
$$

For orthogonality ($I_{m,n} = 0$ for $m \neq n$), we need

$$
\sin(\pi \Delta f_{mn} T_u) \;=\; 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta f_{mn} \neq 0
$$

The smallest non-zero solution gives $\pi \Delta f_{mn} T_u = \pi$, i.e.

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta f \;=\; \frac{1}{T_u}\;}
$$

Therefore, subcarriers at $f_k = f_0 + k \Delta f$ for integer $k$ are mutually orthogonal over $T_u$. (Full derivation already shown in §3.1.)

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the time-frequency resource grid of an OFDMA system. What is a Resource Element and a Resource Block? Compute the number of RBs in a 10 MHz LTE downlink. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The **time-frequency resource grid** is the 2-D conceptual map of all available transmission resources in an OFDMA frame:
- The **horizontal axis** is time, divided into successive OFDM symbols of duration $T_{sym} = T_u + T_{CP}$.
- The **vertical axis** is frequency, divided into subcarriers of spacing $\Delta f$.

A **Resource Element (RE)** is the smallest addressable unit = **one subcarrier × one OFDM symbol**. It carries one modulation symbol (e.g., one 64-QAM complex value).

A **Resource Block (RB)** in LTE = **12 consecutive subcarriers × 7 OFDM symbols (normal CP)** = **84 REs**. In the frequency domain, one RB occupies $12 \times 15\,\text{kHz} = 180\,\text{kHz}$. In the time domain, one RB occupies one slot of 0.5 ms.

**Computation for 10 MHz LTE:**
- Total bandwidth: $BW = 10\,\text{MHz} = 10\,000\,\text{kHz}$.
- Usable subcarriers (LTE reserves 10 % guard band, 6 RBs at each edge):
  $N_{usable} = (10\,000 / 15) - 12 \times 2 \approx 600$ subcarriers.
- Number of RBs:
  $$
  N_{RB} \;=\; \frac{N_{usable}}{12} \;=\; \frac{600}{12} \;=\; \boxed{50\ \text{RBs}}
  $$
- Total spectrum occupied by RBs: $50 \times 180\,\text{kHz} = 9\,\text{MHz}$; the remaining 1 MHz is guard band.

**(b)** Discuss PAPR in OFDM/OFDMA. Why is it a problem? List two techniques to reduce it. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

PAPR (Peak-to-Average Power Ratio) of an OFDM signal is defined as

$$
\text{PAPR} \;=\; \frac{\max \vert x(t) \vert^{2}}{\mathbb{E}\left[ \vert x(t) \vert^{2} \right]}
$$

For $N$ independently modulated subcarriers, the maximum power is approximately $N^2$ times the per-subcarrier power, while the average is $N$ times — so $\text{PAPR} \approx N$, or in dB: $\text{PAPR}_{dB} \approx 10 \log_{10} N$. For $N = 1024$, PAPR $\approx$ **30 dB**.

**Why it is a problem:**
1. The RF power amplifier must operate in the linear region to avoid distortion. With high PAPR, the PA is heavily backed off, reducing **DC-to-RF efficiency** to ≤ 20 %.
2. Non-linear amplification causes **spectral regrowth** (adjacent-channel leakage) and **in-band distortion** that destroys the orthogonality of subcarriers.
3. It increases the cost of the RF frontend and the power consumption of the mobile terminal — critical for battery-operated handsets in 4G/5G.

**Two PAPR-reduction techniques:**

1. **Clipping and Filtering:** Hard-limit the signal amplitude to a threshold $A_{max}$, then low-pass filter. Simple, but introduces in-band noise and out-of-band radiation. Iterative clipping-and-filtering can be used to mitigate the latter.

2. **Selected Mapping (SLM):** Generate $M$ alternative representations of the OFDM symbol by multiplying the data vector with $M$ different phase-rotation sequences; transmit the one with the lowest PAPR. Requires $\log_2 M$ bits of side information per symbol.

(Other acceptable techniques: **Partial Transmit Sequence (PTS)**, **Tone Reservation**, **Coding-based PAPR reduction**, **DCT-based precoding**.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing OFDM with OFDMA.** OFDM is a *modulation* technique (one user). OFDMA is a *multiple-access* technique (many users). Examiners dock marks for mixing these up.
> 2. **Forgetting units in PAPR computation.** Always specify "PAPR $\approx 10 \log_{10} N$ dB" — the numerical dB value matters.
> 3. **Skipping the orthogonality integral.** Many students state $\Delta f = 1/T_u$ without derivation. Marks are reserved for the *integration step*. Always write $I_{m,n} = \int_0^{T_u} e^{j 2\pi (f_m - f_n) t}\, dt$ explicitly.
> 4. **Wrong FFT-to-RB mapping.** In LTE, $N_{RB} = N_{usable}/12$ with normal CP using 7-symbol slots. Do not confuse with the 5G NR slot of 14 symbols.
> 5. **Not mentioning multi-user diversity.** When asked "why OFDMA?", most students only write "orthogonality removes interference" — a half-answer. Always add: *"OFDMA enables frequency-selective scheduling, harvesting multi-user diversity gain."*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — OFDMA (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** OFDMA = OFDM + multi-user subcarrier allocation. Multiple users share time and frequency orthogonally.
- **Orthogonality condition:** $\Delta f = 1 / T_u$. Subcarriers are at $f_k = f_0 + k \Delta f$.
- **Useful symbol duration:** $T_u = 1/\Delta f$. Total OFDM symbol: $T_{sym} = T_u + T_{CP}$.
- **Hardware implementation:** IFFT at the transmitter; FFT at the receiver. $x_n = \text{IDFT}\{X_k\}$; $X_k = \text{DFT}\{x_n\}$.
- **Cyclic prefix:** length $L_{CP} \ge \tau_{max}$ (channel maximum delay spread). Converts linear convolution into circular convolution, eliminating ISI and making each subcarrier a flat-fading channel.
- **Resource Element (RE):** 1 subcarrier × 1 OFDM symbol = 1 modulation symbol.
- **Resource Block (RB) in LTE:** 12 subcarriers × 7 OFDM symbols (normal CP) = 84 REs, occupying 180 kHz × 0.5 ms.
- **Multi-user diversity:** Scheduler assigns each user the subcarriers with the highest SNR; 30–70 % throughput gain.
- **PAPR:** $\text{PAPR} \approx N$ (linear) $\approx 10 \log_{10} N$ dB. Causes PA inefficiency. Reduction techniques: Clipping, SLM, PTS, Tone Reservation.
- **Number of RBs for 10 MHz LTE:** $N_{RB} = 50$ (50 × 180 kHz = 9 MHz data + 1 MHz guard).
- **Number of subcarriers for 10 MHz LTE:** $N_{usable} = 600$, FFT size $N_{FFT} = 1024$.
- **Key advantage of OFDMA over CDMA:** No MAI, no tight power control, frequency-domain scheduling possible.
- **Used in:** 4G LTE, LTE-Advanced, WiMAX (IEEE 802.16e), 5G NR.
- **5G NR subcarrier spacings (numerologies):** 15, 30, 60, 120, 240 kHz.
- **Typical LTE numerology:** $\Delta f = 15$ kHz, $T_u = 66.67\ \mu s$, normal CP = 4.69 µs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Carrier Frequency Division Multiple Access (SC-FDMA) and Cellular Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Single Carrier Frequency Division Multiple Access (SC-FDMA) and Cellular Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Single Carrier Frequency Division Multiple Access (SC-FDMA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**  
> **SC-FDMA** is a multiple access technique that combines the multi-carrier orthogonality and frequency-domain equalization advantages of OFDMA with the low Peak-to-Average Power Ratio (PAPR) property of single-carrier transmission. It is the standardized uplink (UE → eNodeB) access scheme in 3GPP LTE (Releases 8 onwards) and is mathematically implemented as **DFT-Spread OFDMA**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍽️

Imagine a **group of 12 people** sharing a **single long table** in a restaurant:

- **OFDMA** is like giving each person **12 separate small plates** (one for every course simultaneously). Because all plates are served at once, the *peak* demand on the waiter's hands is enormous — the "waiter" is the **Power Amplifier (PA)** in the mobile handset, and high peak demand means the battery drains fast and the PA overheats.
- **SC-FDMA** is like serving each person **one large plate at a time** (one course after another, but the kitchen — the **FFT/IFFT engine** — coordinates so everyone gets their turn in parallel). The peak load is the same as a single user, but the *average* service is the same as 12 users. The PA is happy, the battery is happy, and the throughput is the same.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** SC-FDMA is **NOT** a brand-new modulation; it is **OFDMA with a DFT pre-coder** inserted before the subcarrier mapping block. The DFT spreads each time-domain symbol across all assigned subcarriers, restoring the single-carrier property.

### Standard Metrics to Remember 🎯

- **LTE Uplink Access Scheme:** SC-FDMA (DFT-S-OFDM)
- **LTE Downlink Access Scheme:** OFDMA
- **Subcarrier Spacing:** **15 kHz** (normal CP), **7.5 kHz** (extended/mBMS)
- **FFT Sizes (N):** 128, 256, 512, 1024, 1536, 2048
- **Sampling Rate:** 30.72 MHz (for 20 MHz channel)
- **Cyclic Prefix Lengths:** Normal CP (4.7 µs), Extended CP (16.7 µs)

---

## 1.2 Cellular Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**  
> The **Cellular Concept** is a radio network architecture in which a large geographic service area is divided into smaller regions called **cells**, each served by a **Base Station (BS)**, so that the same set of frequencies (channels) can be **reused** in non-adjacent cells. This **frequency reuse** dramatically increases the system **capacity (Erlangs/km²)** beyond what a single high-power transmitter can offer.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🗺️

Think of a **busy city taxi service**:

- A single taxi stand serving the *entire* city would mean long waits, one taxi, and one passenger at a time → **Low capacity**.
- Instead, the city is divided into **zones (cells)**, each with a **mini-stand (Base Station)**. Taxis (frequencies/channels) rotate among the zones. A taxi that drops a passenger in Zone A immediately returns to a *distant* zone (Zone G) — not the neighbour (Zone B) — to avoid "interference" between adjacent stands.
- This is exactly what cellular networks do: **same frequency, different places, carefully spaced**.

### Standard Metrics to Remember 🎯

- **Cluster Size (N):** Number of cells in a frequency-reuse pattern. For hexagonal geometry, $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ where $i,j \ge 0$.
- **Frequency Reuse Distance (D):** $D = R\sqrt{3N}$ (where R = cell radius)
- **Co-channel Reuse Ratio (q):** $q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$
- **SIR threshold (LTE typical):** ≥ **18–20 dB** for acceptable voice/data quality
- **Cell Capacity (Erlangs):** Given by **Erlang B** formula for blocked-calls-cleared systems

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The cellular concept is the foundation of every modern wireless standard — GSM, CDMA2000, WCDMA, LTE, 5G NR. Questions on **cluster size, frequency reuse distance, and co-channel interference** appear in almost every KTU Board exam.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SC-FDMA — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Why SC-FDMA? (The PAPR Problem)

| Parameter | OFDMA | SC-FDMA |
|---|---|---|
| **PAPR** | High (≈ 10–12 dB) | Low (≈ 2–5 dB) |
| **PA Efficiency** | Poor | High |
| **Battery Life (UE)** | Short | Long |
| **Equalization** | Complex (1-tap freq.) | Complex (freq. domain) |
| **Mobility / NLOS** | Robust | Slightly less robust |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PAPR matters in the UE (uplink):** Mobile handsets are battery-powered. A PA with high PAPR must be **backed off** from saturation to avoid out-of-band distortion. Back-off → wasted DC power → poor efficiency → hot phone. SC-FDMA reduces PAPR by **~3–6 dB**, translating to **~40–50% longer talk time**.

### 2.1.2 SC-FDMA Transmitter Chain (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Data Generation:** User data bits → constellation mapping (e.g., QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM) → produces $M$ complex symbols.
2. **DFT Pre-Coding (M-point):** Apply an **M-point DFT** to spread the $M$ symbols across the frequency domain. This is the *defining* step.
3. **Subcarrier Mapping:** Assign the $M$ DFT outputs to $M$ of the $N$ available subcarriers ($N > M$).
   - **Localized (LFDMA):** Consecutive subcarriers — good for **exploiting multi-user frequency diversity scheduling**.
   - **Distributed (DFDMA):** Equi-spaced subcarriers — more frequency diversity, but worse PAPR.
4. **N-point IFFT:** Convert to time-domain SC-FDMA symbol.
5. **Cyclic Prefix (CP) Insertion:** Guard interval against inter-symbol interference (ISI).
6. **DAC → RF up-conversion → PA → Antenna.**

### 2.1.3 SC-FDMA Receiver Chain

1. **CP removal → N-point FFT** → frequency-domain received signal.
2. **Subcarrier De-mapping** → extract the $M$ used subcarriers.
3. **M-point IDFT** → time-domain estimate of transmitted symbols.
4. **Symbol detection** → bits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subcarrier Mapping Modes — KTU Favourite Question:**

| Mode | Subcarrier Placement | PAPR | Diversity | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Localized FDMA (LFDMA)** | Contiguous block | Lowest | Low | LTE UL (mandatory) |
| **Distributed FDMA (DFDMA)** | Evenly spaced (every N/M-th) | Higher than LFDMA | High | Optional, 3GPP Rel-8 |
| **Interleaved FDMA (IFDMA)** | Subcarriers $\ell \cdot M$ apart | Same as DFDMA | High | Special cases |

### 2.1.4 Time–Frequency Representation

For an LFDMA system, the transmitted time-domain signal for user $k$ occupying subcarriers $k, k+1, \dots, k+M-1$ can be written as:

$$x_k(t) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{m=0}^{M-1} X_k(m) \, e^{j2\pi (k+m)t/T}$$

where $X_k(m)$ is the $m$-th DFT-spread output, and $T$ is the symbol duration. Because the user occupies only $M$ out of $N$ subcarriers, the time-domain signal looks like a **single-carrier pulse train** with a repetition period of $T/M$ — hence the low PAPR.

---

## 2.2 Cellular Concept — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 The Four Pillars of Cellular Design

1. **Frequency Reuse (F):** Same frequency band used in cells separated by a minimum reuse distance $D$.
2. **Cell Splitting:** When traffic grows, a high-traffic cell is divided into smaller cells (microcells/picocells) — capacity increases ~4× per split.
3. **Sectoring:** A single omnidirectional antenna at the BS is replaced by 3 (or 6) directional antennas (120° each), reducing co-channel interference by a factor of 3.
4. **Handover / Handoff:** As a mobile moves across cells, its call is transferred to a new BS without dropping.

### 2.2.2 Cluster Size and Geometry

For a hexagonal cell layout, the cluster size $N$ is given by the **shift parameters** $(i,j)$:

$$N = i^2 + ij + j^2$$

- Allowed values: **N ∈ {1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21, 25, 27, 28, ...}**
- Common values: **N = 3, 4, 7, 12, 19**

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Aid for KTU:** The most-tested combinations are (i,j) = (1,0) → N=1, (1,1) → N=3, (2,0) → N=4, (2,1) → N=7, (3,0) → N=9, (2,2) → N=12, (3,1) → N=13.

### 2.2.3 Co-channel Interference (CCI)

The **Signal-to-Interference Ratio (SIR)** in a hexagonal system, considering only the **first tier of 6 co-channel cells**, is:

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(D/R)^n}{6} = \frac{q^n}{6} = \frac{(\sqrt{3N})^n}{6}$$

where:
- $D$ = co-channel reuse distance
- $R$ = cell radius
- $q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$ = **co-channel reduction factor**
- $n$ = **path loss exponent** (typically 3 to 4 for urban macrocells)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often use $q = \sqrt{N}$ — this is **WRONG**. The correct relation for hexagonal cells is $q = \sqrt{3N}$ because the hexagonal geometry gives an extra factor of $\sqrt{3}$.

### 2.2.4 Capacity Enhancement: Cell Splitting and Sectoring

- **Cell Splitting:** Halving the cell radius $R$ requires a transmit-power reduction of **12 dB** (for n=4) to maintain the same received power. Capacity scales as $\propto 1/R^2$ — splitting increases capacity by **4×**.
- **Sectoring:** With 120° sectors, the number of co-channel interferers drops from **6 to 2** (for a 3-sector system). The SIR improves by a factor of **3**:

$$\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\text{sectored}} = \frac{(\sqrt{3N})^n}{2}$$

### 2.2.5 Real-World Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **4G LTE Uplink** | SC-FDMA enables low-PAPR mobile transmission |
| **5G NR Uplink** | DFT-S-OFDM (SC-FDMA) is one of two uplink waveforms (alongside CP-OFDM) |
| **Cellular Telephony** | Frequency reuse enables billions of subscribers on finite spectrum |
| **IoT / NB-IoT** | Cellular concept adapted for low-rate, dense device networks |
| **Satellite Communications** | Cluster concept applied to beam reuse |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cluster size (hex) | $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ | dimensionless | i, j non-negative integers |
| Reuse distance | $D = R\sqrt{3N}$ | metres (km) | Hexagonal geometry |
| Co-channel ratio | $q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$ | dimensionless | Also called frequency reuse ratio |
| SIR (omni, 1st tier) | $S/I = q^n / 6$ | ratio (or dB) | n = path-loss exponent |
| SIR (3-sector, 1st tier) | $S/I = q^n / 2$ | ratio | 2 interferers per sector |
| Channel capacity (cells) | $C = M \cdot k / N$ | channels/cell | M = total, k = channels/cell (per cluster) |
| PAPR (SC-FDMA) | $\text{PAPR}_{\text{SC-FDMA}} = \text{PAPR}_{\text{DFT-out}} + 10\log_{10}(M)$ | dB | Lower than OFDMA by ~$\sim 3$ to $6$ dB |
| DFT size | $M$ | integer | M ≤ N; M = # of RBs allocated |
| FFT size (LTE) | $N$ | integer | $N = 2^k$ for 1.4, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20 MHz BW |
| Subcarrier spacing | $\Delta f$ | **15 kHz** | LTE normal CP |
| Symbol duration | $T_s = 1/\Delta f + T_{CP}$ | seconds | $66.67\,\mu s + T_{CP}$ |
| Erlangs (Erlang B) | $P_{block} = \dfrac{A^C / C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k/k!}$ | probability | Blocked-calls-cleared |
| Cell area (hex) | $A_{cell} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2}R^2$ | m² (or km²) | R = circumradius of hexagon |
| Total traffic / cluster | $A = k \cdot A_u$ | Erlangs | $A_u$ = traffic per channel (Erlang B lookup) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SIR in dB conversion:** When KTU asks for SIR in dB, use $\text{SIR}_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(\text{SIR}_{linear})$. **Do NOT use 20 log** — SIR is a power ratio, not amplitude.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Time-Domain Representation of SC-FDMA

We start with the block diagram at the transmitter. The user data vector $\mathbf{X} = [X(0), X(1), \dots, X(M-1)]^T$ (in frequency domain) first passes through an **M-point DFT**:

$$X_m = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} x_n \, e^{-j2\pi mn/M}, \quad m = 0, 1, \dots, M-1$$

These $M$ outputs are then mapped onto $M$ of the $N$ subcarriers. For **LFDMA**, the mapping index is:

$$\tilde{X}(k) = \begin{cases} X_m, & k = m, \; m = 0, 1, \dots, M-1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The N-point IFFT then produces the time-domain SC-FDMA symbol:

$$x_n = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \tilde{X}(k) \, e^{j2\pi kn/N}, \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

Since only $M$ subcarriers are non-zero (assume they are at indices $0, 1, \dots, M-1$ for LFDMA):

$$x_n = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} X_k \, e^{j2\pi kn/N}$$

Now substitute $X_k$ from the DFT step:

$$x_n = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} \left( \sum_{\ell=0}^{M-1} x_\ell \, e^{-j2\pi k\ell/M} \right) e^{j2\pi kn/N}$$

Swap the order of summation:

$$x_n = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{\ell=0}^{M-1} x_\ell \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} e^{j2\pi k(n/N - \ell/M)}$$

Let $\alpha = n/N - \ell/M$. The inner geometric sum gives:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} e^{j2\pi k\alpha} = \frac{1 - e^{j2\pi M\alpha}}{1 - e^{j2\pi\alpha}}$$

This sum equals $M$ when $M\alpha$ is an integer, i.e., when $n/N \cdot M - \ell \in \mathbb{Z}$. Setting $n = qN/M + r$ (where $r$ is a remainder), and noting that $\ell$ runs from $0$ to $M-1$, the only $\ell$ satisfying this for each $n$ is:

$$\ell = (Mn/N) \bmod M$$

This is a **key identity**. The result is that the time-domain SC-FDMA signal is a **periodic repetition of the original time-domain samples** $x_\ell$:

$$\boxed{x_n = x_{(nM/N) \bmod M}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Because the IFFT of the DFT-spread signal re-produces the original time-domain sequence **repeated $N/M$ times within one symbol period**, the SC-FDMA signal has a **constant envelope property** (approximately), which is exactly why PAPR is low. This is the **fundamental theoretical result** examiners love to test.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Co-channel Interference in Hexagonal Cells

Consider a **central cell** (the "victim") and the **6 co-channel cells of the first tier**, each located at distance $D$ from the central cell's BS.

**Step 1 — Signal Power at the Mobile:**

The desired received signal power at distance $R$ from its serving BS:

$$S = P_t \cdot K \cdot R^{-n}$$

where $P_t$ is transmit power, $K$ is a constant including antenna gain and wavelength.

**Step 2 — Interference Power from a Single Co-channel BS:**

Each interfering BS is at distance $D$ from the victim mobile (worst case), so:

$$I_{\text{single}} = P_t \cdot K \cdot D^{-n}$$

**Step 3 — Total Interference (6 Interferers, Omni Antennas):**

Assuming all interferers transmit equal power and the mobile is at cell edge (worst case), total interference:

$$I_{\text{total}} = 6 \cdot P_t \cdot K \cdot D^{-n}$$

**Step 4 — Form the SIR Ratio:**

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{P_t \cdot K \cdot R^{-n}}{6 \cdot P_t \cdot K \cdot D^{-n}} = \frac{1}{6} \left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^n$$

Substitute $D/R = \sqrt{3N}$:

$$\boxed{\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(\sqrt{3N})^n}{6} = \frac{(3N)^{n/2}}{6}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to dB:**

$$\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{(3N)^{n/2}}{6}\right)$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Board-Style)

> **Problem:** A GSM system uses a cluster size of **N = 7** and a path loss exponent **n = 4**. Find the SIR (in dB) at the cell boundary for (a) omnidirectional antennas, and (b) 120° sectored antennas.

**Solution (a) — Omni:**

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(3 \cdot 7)^{4/2}}{6} = \frac{(21)^2}{6} = \frac{441}{6} = 73.5$$

$$\text{SIR}_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(73.5) = 18.66 \text{ dB}$$

**Solution (b) — 120° Sectored:**

In a 3-sector scheme, only **2 co-channel interferers** are visible to a given sector:

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(3 \cdot 7)^{4/2}}{2} = \frac{441}{2} = 220.5$$

$$\text{SIR}_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(220.5) = 23.43 \text{ dB}$$

> **Improvement:** $\Delta = 23.43 - 18.66 = 4.77$ dB (matches the theoretical $10\log_{10}(3) \approx 4.77$ dB improvement from 3-sectoring). ✅

---

## 3.4 Numerical Exercise: SC-FDMA PAPR vs OFDMA

For $N = 256$ subcarriers, $M = 64$ allocated subcarriers, and QPSK symbols:

- **OFDMA PAPR (theoretical max):** $\approx 10\log_{10}(N) = 24$ dB
- **SC-FDMA LFDMA PAPR:** $\approx 10\log_{10}(M) = 18$ dB
- **SC-FDMA DFDMA PAPR:** Between LFDMA and OFDMA

**Net improvement:** $24 - 18 = 6$ dB → allows the PA to operate with **6 dB less back-off**, doubling efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SC-FDMA Transmitter & Receiver Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["USER DATA BITS"] --> B["CONSTELLATION MAPPING\nQPSK / 16QAM / 64QAM"]
    B --> C["M-POINT DFT\nDFT SPREADING"]
    C --> D["SUBCARRIER MAPPING\nLFDMA OR DFDMA"]
    D --> E["N-POINT IFFT\nN greater than M"]
    E --> F["CYCLIC PREFIX INSERTION\nNORMAL OR EXTENDED CP"]
    F --> G["DAC AND RF FRONT END\nPOWER AMPLIFIER"]
    G --> H["TX ANTENNA\nUPLINK TO ENODEB"]
    
    H2["RX ANTENNA\nDOWNLINK FROM UE"] --> I["ADC AND CP REMOVAL"]
    I --> J["N-POINT FFT"]
    J --> K["SUBCARRIER DE-MAPPING\nEXTRACT M SUB-BANDS"]
    K --> L["M-POINT IDFT\nRECOVER SYMBOLS"]
    L --> M["SYMBOL DETECTION\nAND DEMAPPING"]
    M --> N["RECEIVED DATA BITS"]
    
    style C fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#4fc3f7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    style H2 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual cue for KTU exams:** The **DFT pre-coding block** is what makes SC-FDMA different from OFDMA. Always draw it as a separate yellow block (above) — examiners award 2 marks just for correctly identifying this block.
