<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define SC-FDMA. Why is it preferred over OFDMA for the LTE uplink?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> **Definition (1 mark):** Single Carrier Frequency Division Multiple Access (SC-FDMA) is a multiple access technique in which user data symbols are first spread by a **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** before being mapped onto a set of orthogonal subcarriers and converted to the time domain by an IFFT. It is mathematically equivalent to **DFT-Spread OFDMA**.
> 
> **Preferred over OFDMA in uplink (2 marks):**
> 1. **Lower PAPR** (typically 2–5 dB vs. 10–12 dB for OFDMA) → mobile PA operates with **less back-off** → higher efficiency, less battery drain, less heating.
> 2. **Better UE power-amplifier design** — critical for battery-powered handsets.
> 3. The **DFT pre-coding** makes the transmitted time-domain waveform resemble a single-carrier signal, avoiding the high peak-to-average fluctuations of OFDMA.
> 4. Maintains the **frequency-domain equalization** and **orthogonality** benefits of OFDMA.
> 
> Hence, 3GPP selected SC-FDMA for the LTE uplink in **Release 8** (2008).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Repeated Concept]**
**What is frequency reuse? Define the cluster size N and co-channel reuse ratio q for a hexagonal cellular system.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> **Frequency Reuse (1 mark):** Frequency reuse is the cellular technique of using the **same set of RF channels** in non-adjacent cells, separated by a minimum distance $D$, to **increase system capacity** without increasing the allocated spectrum.
> 
> **Cluster Size N (1 mark):** A **cluster** is the group of cells in which each channel is used exactly once. For hexagonal geometry, $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ where $i, j$ are non-negative integers (e.g., N = 1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, …).
> 
> **Co-channel Reuse Ratio q (1 mark):** $q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$, where $D$ is the reuse distance and $R$ is the cell radius.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **transmitter and receiver of an SC-FDMA system**. Clearly show the role of the **M-point DFT** and the **subcarrier mapping** block. Compare SC-FDMA with OFDMA in terms of PAPR, complexity, and uplink suitability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For an LTE system with **20 MHz channel bandwidth**, the FFT size is $N = 2048$ and the subcarrier spacing is **15 kHz**. A user is allocated **$M = 72$ subcarriers (3 RBs)**. 
- (i) Calculate the sampling rate, useful symbol duration, and total OFDM symbol duration (with normal CP). 
- (ii) If the LTE PAPR specification requires the PA back-off to be 6 dB less than OFDMA, calculate the **PA efficiency improvement** assuming the PA is Class-A and the back-off loss is $\eta_{\text{loss}} = 1 - 10^{-\text{BO}/10}$. Comment on the implications for UE battery life.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

*(Draw the block diagram from Section 4.1 above — transmitter chain: Bits → Mapping → M-point DFT → Subcarrier Mapping → N-point IFFT → CP Insert → DAC → PA → Antenna. Receiver is the reverse.)*

**Role of M-point DFT (2 marks):** The M-point DFT spreads each user symbol across all $M$ allocated subcarriers, so the time-domain output of the IFFT becomes a **periodic repetition of the original time-domain symbols**. This produces a **single-carrier-like waveform** with low PAPR.

**Role of Subcarrier Mapping (1 mark):** Assigns the M DFT outputs to specific subcarriers. Two modes:
- **LFDMA** (consecutive) — used in LTE UL.
- **DFDMA / IFDMA** (distributed) — better frequency diversity.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | OFDMA | SC-FDMA |
|---|---|---|
| PAPR | High (10–12 dB) | Low (2–5 dB) |
| Pre-coding DFT | Absent | Present (M-point) |
| PA efficiency | Low | High |
| Use | LTE DL, 5G NR DL | LTE UL, 5G NR UL (DFT-S-OFDM) |
| Receiver complexity | Low (1-tap FEQ) | Slightly higher (DFT + FEQ) |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $N = 2048$, $\Delta f = 15$ kHz, $M = 72$, BW = 20 MHz, normal CP.

**(i) Calculations (4 marks):**

- **Sampling rate:** $f_s = N \cdot \Delta f = 2048 \times 15\,000 = 30.72$ MHz ✅ (1 mark)
- **Useful symbol duration:** $T_u = 1/\Delta f = 1/15000 = 66.67\,\mu s$ ✅ (1 mark)
- **Normal CP length:** $T_{CP} \approx 4.69\,\mu s$ (for 1st OFDM symbol); for symbols 2–7, $T_{CP} \approx 5.21\,\mu s$. Average: $T_{CP} \approx 4.69\,\mu s$ ✅ (1 mark)
- **Total symbol duration:** $T_s = T_u + T_{CP} = 66.67 + 4.69 = 71.36\,\mu s$ (1st symbol); for others $\approx 71.88\,\mu s$. ✅ (1 mark)

**(ii) PA Efficiency Improvement (3 marks):**

For **OFDMA**, PA back-off = 10 dB (typical) → efficiency loss:
$$\eta_{\text{loss, OFDMA}} = 1 - 10^{-10/10} = 1 - 0.1 = 0.9 \;(90\%)$$

For **SC-FDMA**, back-off reduced by 6 dB → 4 dB:
$$\eta_{\text{loss, SC-FDMA}} = 1 - 10^{-4/10} = 1 - 0.398 = 0.602 \;(60.2\%)$$

**Improvement:**
$$\Delta\eta = 0.9 - 0.602 = 0.298 \;\Rightarrow\; 29.8\%\text{ more efficient}$$

**Comment (1 mark):** A 30% PA efficiency improvement translates to roughly **30% longer battery life** for the same throughput — a critical gain for mobile UEs, especially for uplink-heavy applications (video upload, VoLTE, IoT uplink).

---

### ⭐ **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **cellular concept** with reference to: (i) frequency reuse, (ii) cluster size N, (iii) cell splitting, and (iv) sectoring. Show the derivation of the **co-channel interference ratio (S/I)** for a hexagonal cellular system with omnidirectional antennas.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cellular operator uses a cluster size of **N = 7** with omnidirectional antennas. The path loss exponent is **n = 3.5**, and the minimum acceptable SIR is **18 dB**. 
- (i) Calculate the actual SIR in dB. 
- (ii) By what factor must the cluster size be increased to guarantee SIR $\geq 20$ dB? 
- (iii) Discuss the **trade-off between cluster size and trunking efficiency**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Frequency Reuse (1.5 marks):** Same set of RF channels reused in non-adjacent cells separated by a reuse distance $D$. Increases capacity by allowing many simultaneous users in a finite spectrum.

**(ii) Cluster Size N (1.5 marks):** Number of cells per cluster, $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$. The total channels $M$ are divided among $N$ cells, giving $M/N$ channels per cell. As N increases, **interference decreases** but **capacity per cell decreases**.

**(iii) Cell Splitting (1.5 marks):** Subdividing congested cells into smaller cells (microcells) with reduced transmit power. Capacity increases by $\propto 1/R^2$.

**(iv) Sectoring (1.5 marks):** Replacing the omni antenna with 3 (or 6) directional antennas. Co-channel interferers drop from 6 to 2 (for 3-sector), improving SIR by $10\log_{10}(3) \approx 4.77$ dB.

**SIR Derivation (1 mark):** See Section 3.2 above. Final result: $S/I = (3N)^{n/2} / 6$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Actual SIR for N = 7, n = 3.5 (2.5 marks):**

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(3 \cdot 7)^{3.5/2}}{6} = \frac{(21)^{1.75}}{6}$$

Calculate $21^{1.75}$:
$$21^{1.75} = 21^{7/4} = \sqrt[4]{21^7}$$
$$21^2 = 441, \quad 21^4 = 194481, \quad 21^7 = 21^4 \cdot 21^2 \cdot 21 = 194481 \times 441 \times 21 \approx 1.8008 \times 10^9$$
$$\sqrt[4]{1.8008 \times 10^9} \approx 205.5$$
$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{205.5}{6} \approx 34.25$$

In dB: $\text{SIR} = 10\log_{10}(34.25) \approx 15.35$ dB ❌ (fails the 18 dB minimum)

**(ii) Required N for SIR ≥ 20 dB (3 marks):**

Required linear SIR $\geq 10^{20/10} = 100$.

$$\frac{(3N)^{3.5/2}}{6} \geq 100 \;\Rightarrow\; (3N)^{1.75} \geq 600 \;\Rightarrow\; 3N \geq 600^{1/1.75}$$

Calculate $600^{1/1.75} = 600^{4/7}$:
$$\log_{10}(600) = 2.778, \quad 2.778 \times 4/7 = 1.588, \quad 10^{1.588} \approx 38.7$$

So $3N \geq 38.7 \Rightarrow N \geq 12.9$. The next **valid cluster size** is $N = 13$ (from $i=3, j=1$: $9+3+1=13$).

**Increase factor:** $13/7 \approx 1.86\times$ — must increase cluster size by 86%. ✅

**(iii) Trade-off (1.5 marks):**
- **Larger N** → lower CCI, better SIR, **fewer channels per cell** (e.g., with M = 280 total channels, $M/N$ drops from 40 to ~22).
- **Trunking efficiency** (probability a user finds a free channel) **decreases** with smaller channel pool per cell.
- **Compromise:** Pick the smallest N that meets the SIR requirement. For LTE, N = 1 is used (universal frequency reuse) with **interference coordination (ICIC)** instead of classical reuse.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in SC-FDMA + Cellular Concept Questions (KTU Board 2024):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the DFT pre-coder:** When drawing the SC-FDMA block diagram, students often draw an **OFDMA** diagram and just relabel it. The **M-point DFT** block **before** the subcarrier mapping is the **defining** feature — losing 2 marks here is the #1 mistake.
> 
> 2. **Wrong co-channel ratio:** Using $q = \sqrt{N}$ instead of $q = \sqrt{3N}$. Always remember: hexagonal geometry → extra $\sqrt{3}$ factor.
> 
> 3. **Confusing N for SC-FDMA:** In the formula $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$, $N$ is the **cluster size**, **not** the FFT size. Use different variable names in derivations!
> 
> 4. **Wrong log formula for SIR:** SIR is a **power** ratio → use $10\log_{10}$. Using $20\log_{10}$ is a 1-mark deduction.
> 
> 5. **Subcarrier mapping conflation:** LFDMA vs DFDMA vs IFDMA — many students mix them up. **LFDMA = localized (contiguous), DFDMA = distributed (equi-spaced), IFDMA = interleaved (comb structure).**
> 
> 6. **PAPR units:** PAPR is always expressed in **dB**, not as a linear ratio, in KTU papers.
> 
> 7. **Forgetting CP removal and time-domain equalization** in SC-FDMA receiver diagrams.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

### **SC-FDMA Cheat Points**
- ✅ **SC-FDMA = DFT-Spread OFDMA** (DFT pre-coder + OFDMA modulator).
- ✅ Used in **LTE Uplink** (3GPP Rel-8, 2008); also in 5G NR uplink (DFT-S-OFDM).
- ✅ **PAPR** is **2–5 dB** (vs. 10–12 dB for OFDMA) → better for mobile PAs.
- ✅ **LFDMA** is the **mandatory** mode in LTE; gives the lowest PAPR.
- ✅ **DFDMA / IFDMA** offer better frequency diversity but slightly higher PAPR.
- ✅ Time-domain signal is a **periodic repetition** of original symbols (repetition factor = N/M).
- ✅ **Sampling rate = FFT size × subcarrier spacing** = 30.72 MHz (for 20 MHz LTE).
- ✅ **Subcarrier spacing = 15 kHz** (normal CP) in LTE.

### **Cellular Concept Cheat Points**
- ✅ **Cluster size** $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$; common values: 1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13.
- ✅ **Reuse distance** $D = R\sqrt{3N}$.
- ✅ **Co-channel ratio** $q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$ (hexagonal, **not** $\sqrt{N}$).
- ✅ **SIR (omni)** = $(3N)^{n/2} / 6$; **SIR (3-sector)** = $(3N)^{n/2} / 2$.
- ✅ **Sectoring** improves SIR by $10\log_{10}(3) \approx 4.77$ dB.
- ✅ **Cell splitting** quarters the cell area → capacity ×4.
- ✅ **Handover types:** hard, soft, softer (UMTS), X2-based (LTE).
- ✅ **LTE uses N = 1** (universal reuse) + **ICIC/eICIC** for interference management.

### **Quick Conversion Reminders**
- $N=7,\ n=4$: $S/I = 73.5$ → $18.66$ dB (omni)
- $N=7,\ n=4$: $S/I = 220.5$ → $23.43$ dB (3-sector)
- $N=4,\ n=4$: $S/I = 48$ → $16.81$ dB (omni)
- $N=12,\ n=4$: $S/I = 216$ → $23.34$ dB (omni)

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a question gives you (i, j) instead of N, **always** compute N from $i^2 + ij + j^2$ first. The most common shift patterns are (1,0)=1, (1,1)=3, (2,0)=4, (2,1)=7, (2,2)=12, (3,1)=13, (3,2)=19. Memorize these — they are guaranteed KTU repeat questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Doppler Shift

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 1: DOPPLER SHIFT

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The **Doppler Shift** (also called the **Doppler Effect** or **Doppler Frequency Shift**) is the alteration in the observed frequency of a wave caused by the **relative motion** between the transmitting source and the receiving observer. In the context of **Modern Communication Systems**, this phenomenon critically affects the performance of mobile cellular networks, satellite links, radar systems, and vehicular communication (V2V/V2X) by introducing a frequency offset that must be compensated for accurate signal demodulation.

Mathematically, when a source emitting a frequency $f_0$ moves with velocity $v_s$ and the receiver moves with velocity $v_r$ along the line of sight, the received frequency $f_r$ deviates from the transmitted frequency $f_0$. The shift $\Delta f = f_r - f_0$ is termed the **Doppler Shift** and is expressed as:

$$f_d = f_r - f_0 = \frac{v_{rel} \cdot f_0}{c}$$

where $v_{rel}$ is the relative radial velocity and $c$ is the speed of wave propagation (speed of light $\approx \mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \, m/s}$ for RF/EM waves, and $\mathbf{343 \, m/s}$ for acoustic waves).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy — The Siren on an Ambulance**
> Imagine standing on a sidewalk. An ambulance with its siren blaring approaches you. As it gets closer, the siren's pitch sounds **higher** (frequency increases). Once the ambulance passes you and moves away, the pitch suddenly drops to a **lower** tone (frequency decreases). Although the siren itself is producing sound at a fixed frequency, your **relative motion** with respect to the source changes the wavelength of the waves reaching your ears, thereby changing the perceived frequency. This is the Doppler Effect in action.

In communication systems, a similar phenomenon occurs when your **mobile phone** moves at high speed (e.g., in a train moving at $200 \, km/h$) while receiving signals from a base station. The carrier frequency that your receiver tunes to is slightly shifted, which can cause:
- Carrier Frequency Offset (CFO)
- Inter-Carrier Interference (ICI) in OFDM systems
- Bit Error Rate (BER) degradation

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Value (Electromagnetic / RF) |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of Light in Vacuum | $c$ | $\mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \, m/s}$ |
| Speed of Sound in Air (at $20^\circ C$) | $v_{sound}$ | $\mathbf{343 \, m/s}$ |
| Typical Mobile Velocity (Vehicle) | $v$ | $10$–$150 \, km/h$ |
| Typical LEO Satellite Velocity | $v_{sat}$ | $\approx 7.8 \, km/s$ |
| Carrier Frequency (5G NR) | $f_c$ | $0.4$–$60 \, GHz$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Mechanism of Doppler Shift

The Doppler Effect arises because the **distance between successive wavefronts** (the wavelength $\lambda$) is physically compressed or stretched due to the motion of either the source or the observer:

1. **Approaching Motion:** Wave crests are squeezed together → wavelength $\lambda$ decreases → observed frequency $f$ **increases** (blue shift).
2. **Receding Motion:** Wave crests are pulled apart → wavelength $\lambda$ increases → observed frequency $f$ **decreases** (red shift).
3. **Tangential Motion ($90^\circ$ angle):** No component of velocity along the line of sight → **zero Doppler Shift** momentarily.

### 2.2 Mathematical Framework — General Case

Let us define:
- $v_s$ = velocity of source (positive if moving **toward** the receiver)
- $v_r$ = velocity of receiver (positive if moving **toward** the source)
- $v$ = velocity of wave propagation in the medium
- $f_0$ = transmitted frequency
- $\theta_s$ = angle between source velocity vector and the line joining source to receiver
- $\theta_r$ = angle between receiver velocity vector and the line joining receiver to source

**The generalized Doppler equation** is:

$$f_r = f_0 \left( \frac{v + v_r \cos\theta_r}{v - v_s \cos\theta_s} \right)$$

The corresponding **Doppler shift** is:

$$f_d = f_r - f_0 = f_0 \left( \frac{v + v_r \cos\theta_r}{v - v_s \cos\theta_s} - 1 \right)$$

### 2.3 Special Cases in Communication Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU High-Yield Scenarios**
> Most KTU problems simplify the general equation to the following three cases:

| Case | Description | Doppler Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Stationary Source, Moving Receiver** | Base station fixed, mobile user moving | $f_d = f_0 \cdot \frac{v_r \cos\theta}{v}$ |
| **2. Moving Source, Stationary Receiver** | Satellite moving, ground station fixed | $f_d = -f_0 \cdot \frac{v_s \cos\theta}{v - v_s \cos\theta} \approx -f_0 \cdot \frac{v_s \cos\theta}{v}$ |
| **3. Both Moving (Low Velocity Approximation)** | $v_s, v_r \ll v$ | $f_d \approx f_0 \cdot \frac{(v_r - v_s) \cos\theta}{v}$ |

For RF/EM communications, since $v_s, v_r \ll c$, the **non-relativistic approximation** is universally valid:

$$f_d = \frac{v_{radial}}{\lambda} = \frac{v \cdot f_0}{c} \cos\theta$$

where $v_{radial} = v \cos\theta$ is the **radial component** of relative velocity along the line of sight.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $f_d = \dfrac{v \cos\theta}{\lambda}$ | Doppler shift (general, non-relativistic) | Hz |
| $f_d = \dfrac{v f_0 \cos\theta}{c}$ | Doppler shift in terms of frequency | Hz |
| $v = \lambda f$ | Wave equation relating velocity, wavelength, and frequency | m/s, Hz |
| $\Delta f = \pm \dfrac{2 v f_0}{c}$ | Full round-trip Doppler shift (radar) | Hz |
| $f_{max} = \dfrac{v_{max} f_c}{c}$ | Maximum Doppler frequency in mobile comms | Hz |
| $f_d = f_0 \left( \dfrac{v + v_r \cos\theta_r}{v - v_s \cos\theta_s} \right) - f_0$ | Generalized Doppler formula | Hz |
| $f_m = \dfrac{v}{\lambda}$ | Maximum Doppler shift (when $\cos\theta = 1$) | Hz |

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Utility**
> The Doppler shift is exploited in **radar systems** (to measure target velocity via the **$\pm 2v$ round-trip effect**), in **GPS** (where satellite motion causes predictable shift that the receiver must correct), in **medical ultrasound** (to measure blood flow velocity), and in **astronomy** (to detect exoplanets via stellar wobble). It must be **mitigated** in **OFDM-based 4G/5G systems** because inter-carrier interference from Doppler destroys orthogonality between subcarriers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Doppler Shift for Stationary Source and Moving Receiver

> [!NOTE] **Scenario:** A source at rest emits a wave with frequency $f_0$ and wavelength $\lambda = v/f_0$. The receiver moves with velocity $v_r$ toward the source at an angle $\theta$ from the line of sight.

**Step 1:** The wave propagates in the medium with velocity $v$. The number of wave crests the moving observer intercepts per second is increased (or decreased) by the component of the observer's velocity along the line of sight.

**Step 2:** The radial velocity component of the receiver is $v_r \cos\theta$.

**Step 3:** When the receiver moves **toward** the source, the relative velocity between the wave and the observer becomes $v + v_r \cos\theta$ (since both the wavefronts and the receiver are approaching each other).

**Step 4:** The received frequency is given by the number of wave crests passing the observer per second:

$$f_r = \frac{v + v_r \cos\theta}{\lambda}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $\lambda = v/f_0$:

$$f_r = \frac{v + v_r \cos\theta}{v / f_0} = f_0 \left( \frac{v + v_r \cos\theta}{v} \right)$$

**Step 6:** Expanding:

$$f_r = f_0 \left( 1 + \frac{v_r \cos\theta}{v} \right) = f_0 + \frac{f_0 v_r \cos\theta}{v}$$

**Step 7:** Therefore, the Doppler shift is:

$$f_d = f_r - f_0 = \frac{f_0 v_r \cos\theta}{v} = \frac{v_r \cos\theta}{\lambda}$$

This is the **fundamental Doppler equation** used in mobile communications.

### 3.2 Derivation of Doppler Shift for Moving Source and Stationary Receiver

> [!NOTE] **Scenario:** A source moves with velocity $v_s$ toward a stationary receiver. The wave speed in the medium is $v$, and the source emits at frequency $f_0$.

**Step 1:** The source emits wave crests. In one period $T_0 = 1/f_0$, the source travels a distance $v_s T_0$ toward the receiver.

**Step 2:** The first crest has traveled a distance $v T_0$ from the original position. The second crest is emitted from a position $v_s T_0$ closer to the receiver.

**Step 3:** The wavelength in front of the source (toward the receiver) becomes compressed:

$$\lambda' = v T_0 - v_s T_0 = (v - v_s) T_0 = \frac{v - v_s}{f_0}$$

**Step 4:** The observed frequency at the stationary receiver is:

$$f_r = \frac{v}{\lambda'} = \frac{v}{(v - v_s)/f_0} = f_0 \left( \frac{v}{v - v_s} \right)$$

**Step 5:** Simplifying with $v_s \ll v$ using binomial expansion $\dfrac{1}{1 - x} \approx 1 + x$ for small $x$:

$$f_r = \frac{f_0}{1 - v_s/v} \approx f_0 \left( 1 + \frac{v_s}{v} \right)$$

**Step 6:** Therefore the Doppler shift is:

$$f_d = f_r - f_0 = \frac{f_0 v_s}{v - v_s} \approx \frac{f_0 v_s}{v} = \frac{v_s}{\lambda}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Doppler Shift in Radar (Round-Trip Effect)

> [!NOTE] **Scenario:** A radar transmits a signal at frequency $f_0$ which reflects off a target moving with radial velocity $v$ and returns to the same radar.

**Step 1:** The target acts as a moving receiver first. The signal frequency **received by the target** is:

$$f_{target} = f_0 + f_{d1} = f_0 + \frac{2 v f_0}{c} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = f_0 \left( 1 + \frac{v}{c} \right)$$

Wait — let us be more careful. The radial velocity of target is $v$, and the first-leg Doppler shift (target as moving receiver) is:

$$f_{d1} = f_0 \cdot \frac{v}{c}$$

**Step 2:** The target then **re-radiates** this frequency. Since the target is now a moving source (toward or away from the radar), a second Doppler shift occurs:

$$f_{d2} = f_{target} \cdot \frac{v}{c} = f_0 \left( 1 + \frac{v}{c} \right) \cdot \frac{v}{c}$$

**Step 3:** For $v \ll c$, the second-order term $(v/c)^2$ is negligible:

$$f_{d2} \approx f_0 \cdot \frac{v}{c}$$

**Step 4:** The **total round-trip Doppler shift** is:

$$f_d = f_{d1} + f_{d2} = f_0 \cdot \frac{v}{c} + f_0 \cdot \frac{v}{c} = \frac{2 f_0 v}{c}$$

**Step 5:** Equivalently, in terms of wavelength:

$$f_d = \frac{2 v}{\lambda}$$

This **$\mathbf{\pm 2v}$ factor** is the signature of radar systems, and it is what enables police radar guns, weather radar, and air traffic control to measure target velocity.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE] **Problem:** A mobile user travels at $108 \, km/h$ in a car while receiving a 5G NR signal at $3.5 \, GHz$ from a base station. Calculate the maximum Doppler shift.

**Step 1:** Convert velocity to SI units:

$$v = 108 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 30 \, m/s$$

**Step 2:** Identify the relevant parameters:

$$f_c = 3.5 \, GHz = 3.5 \times 10^9 \, Hz, \quad c = 3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$$

**Step 3:** Maximum Doppler shift occurs when $\cos\theta = 1$ (head-on motion):

$$f_{max} = \frac{v \cdot f_c}{c} = \frac{30 \times 3.5 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate:

$$f_{max} = \frac{1.05 \times 10^{11}}{3 \times 10^8} = 350 \, Hz$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, the **maximum Doppler shift** experienced by the mobile is $\mathbf{350 \, Hz}$, which causes a carrier frequency offset that the receiver's frequency synchronization loop must track.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Doppler Shift Functional Architecture

> [!NOTE] The following Mermaid block illustrates the **functional architecture** of how Doppler Shift arises in a communication system, mapping the interactions between transmitter, channel, receiver, and signal processing blocks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Transmitter] --> B[Signal Generation at Frequency f0]
    B --> C[Channel Propagation Medium]
    C --> D{Does Relative Motion Exist?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Apply Doppler Transformation]
    D -- No --> F[No Frequency Shift f_r = f0]
    E --> G[Received Signal at Frequency f_r]
    E --> H[Doppler Shift Component f_d = f_r - f0]
    F --> I[Receiver Demodulation]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Doppler Analysis Block]
    J --> K[Velocity Estimation v = c x f_d / f0]
    J --> L[Frequency Offset Compensation]
    I --> M[Bit Stream Recovery]
    L --> M
    K --> N[Application Layer: Radar GPS Mobile Tracking]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Doppler Sign Convention

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Approaching
    Approaching --> Receding : Source Passes Observer
    Approaching : Frequency INCREASES f_d > 0
    Approaching : Wavelength COMPRESSED
    Receding : Frequency DECREASES f_d < 0
    Receding : Wavelength STRETCHED
    Receding --> Tangential : Motion Perpendicular
    Tangential : f_d = 0 Instantaneous
    Tangential --> Approaching : Motion Continues
    Tangential --> Receding : Motion Continues
    Receding --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Jakes Doppler Spectrum (Mobile Communication)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mobile Station Velocity v] --> B[Uniform Angle of Arrival]
    B --> C[Each Multipath Has Different Doppler Shift]
    C --> D[f_d range from -f_max to +f_max]
    D --> E[Jakes U-shaped Spectrum]
    E --> F[Classical Doppler PSD: S_f = 1 / pi x f_max x sqrt(1 - f/f_max_squared)]
    F --> G[Used to Model Fading in Mobile Channels]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Doppler Compensation in OFDM Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Received OFDM Signal with Doppler] --> B[Time Synchronization]
    B --> C[Frequency Synchronization Block]
    C --> D[Estimate Carrier Frequency Offset CFO]
    D --> E[Compute Doppler f_d from CFO]
    E --> F[Apply CFO Correction in Frequency Domain]
    F --> G[Channel Estimation]
    G --> H[Equalization]
    H --> I[Demodulation and Decoding]
    E --> J[Feedback to Pre-compensation for Next Frame]
    J --> A
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the **Doppler Effect** in the context of wireless communication. Mention any **two** scenarios in modern communication systems where Doppler Shift becomes a critical design consideration.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE] **Definition:** The Doppler Effect is the apparent change in the observed frequency of a wave caused by the **relative motion** between the transmitter and the receiver. The resulting frequency offset $f_d = f_r - f_0$ is called the Doppler shift.

**Two Critical Scenarios:**
1. **Mobile Cellular Communication (4G/5G):** When a user equipment (UE) moves at vehicular speeds (e.g., in trains or cars), the received carrier frequency is shifted, causing **Carrier Frequency Offset (CFO)** and **Inter-Carrier Interference (ICI)** in OFDM-based systems.
2. **LEO Satellite Communication:** Satellites in Low Earth Orbit travel at $\approx 7.8 \, km/s$, creating a rapidly varying Doppler shift of up to $\pm 48 \, kHz$ at L-band frequencies, which must be pre-compensated at the gateway or satellite.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for scenarios)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** State the **Doppler shift formula** for a mobile receiver moving with velocity $v$ at an angle $\theta$ to the line of sight of a fixed base station. What is the value of $f_d$ when $\theta = 90^\circ$?

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE] **Formula:** The Doppler shift experienced by a mobile receiver moving with velocity $v$ at an angle $\theta$ from the line of sight to a fixed transmitter of frequency $f_0$ is given by:

$$f_d = \frac{v f_0}{c} \cos\theta = \frac{v}{\lambda} \cos\theta$$

**When $\theta = 90^\circ$:** $\cos 90^\circ = 0$, therefore $f_d = 0 \, Hz$.

**Physical Interpretation:** When the mobile moves **perpendicular** to the direction of the incoming wave, the radial component of velocity is zero, and there is no instantaneous frequency shift. However, the rate of change of the angle $\theta$ still causes a **time-varying Doppler shift** as the user passes the base station.

*(3 marks: 2 marks for formula + 1 mark for special case)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

**Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** 

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Doppler shift** when the **receiver is moving** with velocity $v_r$ at an angle $\theta$ relative to the line of sight, while the source remains stationary. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A police radar operating at $10.5 \, GHz$ measures a Doppler shift of $1875 \, Hz$ from a vehicle moving directly toward it. Calculate the **speed of the vehicle** in $km/h$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

Consider a stationary source emitting a continuous wave of frequency $f_0$ and wavelength $\lambda = v/f_0$, where $v$ is the wave propagation speed in the medium. The receiver moves with velocity $v_r$ at an angle $\theta$ with respect to the line joining the receiver to the source.

The **radial component** of the receiver's velocity along the line of sight is:

$$v_{radial} = v_r \cos\theta$$

When the receiver moves **toward** the source, the rate at which wavefronts pass the receiver is increased. The effective relative velocity between the wave and the moving observer is:

$$v_{eff} = v + v_r \cos\theta$$

The number of wave crests crossing the receiver per unit time is the **received frequency**:

$$f_r = \frac{v_{eff}}{\lambda} = \frac{v + v_r \cos\theta}{\lambda}$$

Substituting $\lambda = v/f_0$:

$$f_r = f_0 \cdot \frac{v + v_r \cos\theta}{v} = f_0 \left( 1 + \frac{v_r \cos\theta}{v} \right)$$

The Doppler shift is therefore:

$$f_d = f_r - f_0 = \frac{f_0 v_r \cos\theta}{v} = \frac{v_r \cos\theta}{\lambda}$$

> [!NOTE] **Sign Convention:** $f_d > 0$ when the receiver moves **toward** the source (approaching) and $f_d < 0$ when moving **away** (receding).

**(b) Numerical Problem:**

**Given:**
- Radar carrier frequency $f_0 = 10.5 \, GHz = 10.5 \times 10^9 \, Hz$
- Measured Doppler shift $f_d = 1875 \, Hz$
- Wave speed $c = 3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$
- Vehicle moving directly toward radar $\Rightarrow \theta = 0^\circ \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 1$

**Radar equation (round-trip):**

$$f_d = \frac{2 v f_0}{c}$$

Solving for $v$:

$$v = \frac{f_d \cdot c}{2 f_0}$$

Substituting values:

$$v = \frac{1875 \times 3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10.5 \times 10^9}$$

$$v = \frac{5.625 \times 10^{11}}{2.1 \times 10^{10}}$$

$$v = 26.786 \, m/s$$

Converting to $km/h$:

$$v = 26.786 \times \frac{3600}{1000} = 26.786 \times 3.6 = 96.43 \, km/h$$

> [!NOTE] **Answer:** The vehicle is moving at approximately $\mathbf{96.43 \, km/h}$ toward the radar. The factor of 2 in the radar equation accounts for the round-trip path (transmitter → target → receiver).

---

**Q3 (B). [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Module 1 Question]**

**(a)** Explain with neat derivations the **generalized Doppler equation** for the case when both source and receiver are in motion. Reduce it to the **low-velocity approximation** applicable to mobile RF communication. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile station moving at $90 \, km/h$ receives a 5G NR downlink signal at $2.6 \, GHz$. The base station is stationary. If the user moves directly away from the base station and then makes a $90^\circ$ turn, find the **change in Doppler shift** between the two positions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Generalized Doppler Equation Derivation:**

Let:
- $v_s$ = velocity of source (toward receiver is positive)
- $v_r$ = velocity of receiver (toward source is positive)
- $\theta_s$ = angle between source velocity and line joining source to receiver
- $\theta_r$ = angle between receiver velocity and line joining receiver to source

**Step 1:** The wavelength of the wave as observed in the medium is affected by the source motion. The effective wavelength (in the direction of source-to-receiver) becomes:

$$\lambda' = \frac{v - v_s \cos\theta_s}{f_0}$$

**Step 2:** The receiver moving with radial component $v_r \cos\theta_r$ experiences an effective wave speed of $v + v_r \cos\theta_r$.

**Step 3:** The received frequency is:

$$f_r = \frac{v + v_r \cos\theta_r}{\lambda'} = f_0 \left( \frac{v + v_r \cos\theta_r}{v - v_s \cos\theta_s} \right)$$

**Step 4:** For RF communication, both $v_s$ and $v_r$ are negligible compared to $c$. Using the approximation $\dfrac{1}{1 - x} \approx 1 + x$:

$$f_r \approx f_0 \left( 1 + \frac{v_s \cos\theta_s}{v} \right) \left( 1 + \frac{v_r \cos\theta_r}{v} \right)$$

**Step 5:** Dropping the second-order $(v_s v_r)/v^2$ term:

$$f_r \approx f_0 \left( 1 + \frac{v_s \cos\theta_s + v_r \cos\theta_r}{v} \right)$$

**Step 6:** The Doppler shift in the low-velocity approximation is:

$$f_d = \frac{f_0 (v_s \cos\theta_s + v_r \cos\theta_r)}{v}$$

This is the **standard formula** used in KTU problems for mobile communication systems.

**(b) Numerical Problem:**

**Given:**
- Mobile velocity $v = 90 \, km/h = 25 \, m/s$
- Carrier frequency $f_c = 2.6 \, GHz = 2.6 \times 10^9 \, Hz$
- Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$

**Case 1: Moving directly away from base station ($\theta = 180^\circ$)**

$$\cos 180^\circ = -1$$

$$f_{d1} = \frac{v f_c \cos 180^\circ}{c} = \frac{25 \times 2.6 \times 10^9 \times (-1)}{3 \times 10^8}$$

$$f_{d1} = \frac{-6.5 \times 10^{10}}{3 \times 10^8} = -216.67 \, Hz$$

The negative sign indicates a **decrease** in received frequency (receding motion).

**Case 2: After $90^\circ$ turn (motion perpendicular to line of sight, $\theta = 90^\circ$)**

$$\cos 90^\circ = 0$$

$$f_{d2} = \frac{25 \times 2.6 \times 10^9 \times 0}{3 \times 10^8} = 0 \, Hz$$

**Change in Doppler Shift:**

$$\Delta f_d = f_{d2} - f_{d1} = 0 - (-216.67) = +216.67 \, Hz$$

> [!NOTE] **Answer:** The Doppler shift changes by $\mathbf{+216.67 \, Hz}$ as the user transitions from moving directly away to moving perpendicular to the base station direction. This rapid change is precisely why **mobile receivers need continuous frequency tracking loops**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in radar problems:** The round-trip Doppler shift is $f_d = 2v f_0 / c$, not $v f_0 / c$. Students often write $v = f_d \lambda / 2$ as $v = f_d \lambda$, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Sign convention errors:** Doppler shift can be **positive or negative** depending on whether motion is toward or away. Always state the direction explicitly. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if the sign is missing or wrong.
> 3. **Unit conversion mistakes:** Velocity in $km/h$ MUST be converted to $m/s$ before plugging into the Doppler formula. A classic KTU pitfall — losing 2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing $\theta$ definitions:** $\theta$ is measured from the **line of sight** (between transmitter and receiver) to the velocity vector. Some textbooks use angle from motion axis — read the question carefully.
> 5. **Using the wrong formula:** In mobile comms, the receiver moves; in satellite comms, the source moves. Many students blindly write $f_d = v/\lambda$ without justifying which is moving.
> 6. **Ignoring the low-velocity approximation:** In RF problems, the full relativistic formula is overkill. Examiners expect the simplified $f_d = v f_0 \cos\theta / c$.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **High-Density Revision Checklist — Doppler Shift**

- ⭐ Doppler Shift = change in observed frequency due to relative motion between source and receiver.
- ⭐ **Generalized formula:** $f_d = f_0 \left( \dfrac{v + v_r \cos\theta_r}{v - v_s \cos\theta_s} \right) - f_0$
- ⭐ **Low-velocity approximation (used in RF):** $f_d = \dfrac{v_{radial} f_0}{c} = \dfrac{v \cos\theta}{\lambda}$
- ⭐ **Radar round-trip:** $f_d = \dfrac{2 v f_0}{c}$ — remember the factor of **2**.
- ⭐ **Maximum Doppler:** $f_{max} = \dfrac{v_{max} f_c}{c}$ — occurs at $\cos\theta = 1$.
- ⭐ **Sign:** Positive = approaching, Negative = receding, Zero = perpendicular motion.
- ⭐ **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$ (EM/RF waves); $c_{sound} = 343 \, m/s$ (acoustic).
- ⭐ **Applications:** Radar, GPS, mobile cellular (4G/5G), satellite LEO, medical ultrasound, astronomy.
- ⭐ **In OFDM systems:** Doppler causes **Carrier Frequency Offset (CFO)** → destroys subcarrier orthogonality → causes **Inter-Carrier Interference (ICI)** → must be compensated by frequency synchronization loops.
- ⭐ **Jakes Spectrum:** Models the Doppler power spectral density for Rayleigh fading mobile channels, ranging from $-f_{max}$ to $+f_{max}$ with a U-shape.
- ⭐ **Master the unit conversion:** $1 \, km/h = 5/18 \, m/s$ — every KTU numerical problem tests this.
- ⭐ **Cross-check with:** Frequency and phase modulation theory, channel estimation, and equalization in fading channels.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multipath Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Multipath Effect # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Multipath Effect** in a wireless communication channel is the physical phenomenon wherein a transmitted radio signal propagates along multiple geometrically distinct routes between the transmitter (Tx) and the receiver (Rx). These routes arise from the fundamental interaction of electromagnetic waves with the propagation environment, specifically through three mechanisms: **reflection**, **diffraction**, and **scattering**. As a result, the receiver observes a superposition of multiple delayed, attenuated, and phase-shifted replicas of the original transmitted waveform, leading to constructive or destructive interference that fluctuates over time, frequency, and spatial coordinates. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERAT753, Module 1):** The multipath effect is the root cause of **small-scale fading**, **delay dispersion**, and **Doppler dispersion** in mobile radio channels. Mastery of this topic is mandatory for questions on channel impairments, equalization, and diversity techniques. ### The Three Physical Mechanisms Behind Multipath | Mechanism | Physical Cause | Typical Encounter | |-----------|---------------|-------------------| | **Reflection** | Wave bouncing off surfaces much larger than wavelength (λ) | Ground plane, building walls, water bodies | | **Diffraction** | Wave bending around sharp edges of objects comparable to λ | Rooftops, street corners, hill edges | | **Scattering** | Wave dispersion from rough surfaces or objects smaller than λ | Foliage, lamp posts, rough terrain, vehicles | ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Echoing Voice in a Canyon"** > > Imagine shouting your name across a deep mountain valley. A single listener hears your voice not once but **several times**, each echo arriving from a slightly different direction — one bouncing off the left cliff, one off the right, and one from the distant back wall. Each echo is softer (attenuated), arrives slightly later (delayed), and has a slightly altered tone (phase-shifted). If you walk slowly while speaking, the loudness and clarity of the received voice keep changing constantly. This is **exactly** what a wireless receiver experiences: your transmitted signal is "shouted" into space, and the receiver hears a fluctuating chorus of echoes — that fluctuating chorus is the **Multipath Effect**. ## 1.3 Why the Multipath Effect Matters in Engineering The multipath effect is **not merely a curiosity** — it directly governs the design parameters of every practical mobile communication system (2G, 3G, 4G LTE, 5G NR, Wi-Fi, satellite links). It dictates: - The maximum **symbol rate** that can be transmitted without intersymbol interference (ISI). - The required **equalizer complexity** at the receiver. - The need for **diversity techniques** (spatial, frequency, time). - The selection of **modulation and coding schemes** in adaptive systems. - The cell radius and frequency-reuse planning in cellular networks. > [!TIP] > **Standard Metrics to Remember:** Mean excess delay ($\bar{\tau}$), RMS delay spread ($\sigma_\tau$), coherence bandwidth ($B_c$), Doppler spread ($B_D$), and coherence time ($T_c$) — all of these are **direct mathematical consequences** of the multipath effect. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mathematical Model of the Multipath Channel Consider a narrowband transmitted signal $s(t)$ propagating through a multipath environment. The received bandpass signal is the superposition of $N(t)$ time-varying multipath components: $$r(t) = \sum_{n=1}^{N(t)} a_n(t) \cos\bigl[2\pi f_c t + \phi_n(t)\bigr]$$ where each term in the summation corresponds to one resolvable multipath component with: - $a_n(t)$ → **Time-varying amplitude** (fading envelope) of the $n$-th component. - $\phi_n(t) = 2\pi f_c \tau_n(t) + \phi_{D,n}(t)$ → **Time-varying phase**, including propagation delay $\tau_n(t)$ and Doppler shift. - $f_c$ → Carrier frequency in **Hz**. - $N(t)$ → Number of multipath components arriving at time $t$. ### Why Time-Varying? — The Physical Reasoning Even if the transmitter and receiver remain perfectly stationary, the propagation medium itself is dynamic — moving vehicles, fluttering leaves, swaying pedestrians, atmospheric turbulence. Hence $a_n(t)$, $\tau_n(t)$, and $\phi_n(t)$ are **random processes** rather than deterministic constants. This time-variability is what creates **fading**. ## 2.2 Baseband Equivalent Impulse Response Model For analytical convenience, the radio channel is described by its **baseband equivalent time-varying impulse response**: $$h(t, \tau) = \sum_{n=1}^{N(t)} a_n(t)\, e^{-j\phi_n(t)}\, \delta\bigl(\tau - \tau_n(t)\bigr)$$ This is a **double-variable** function: - Variable $t$ → captures **time-variation** of the channel (motion of Tx/Rx/scatterers). - Variable $\tau$ → captures **multipath delay** structure of the channel at a given instant. The relationship between transmitted signal $s(t)$ and received signal $r(t)$ becomes: $$r(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(t, \tau)\, s(t - \tau)\, d\tau$$ This is a **time-varying convolution**, distinguishing the mobile channel from a static linear time-invariant system. ## 2.3 Classification of Multipath-Induced Fading | Fading Type | Cause | Manifestation | Countermeasure | |-------------|-------|---------------|----------------| | **Large-scale fading** | Path loss + shadowing | Slow signal strength variation over tens of wavelengths | Power control, cell planning | | **Small-scale fading** | Multipath constructive/destructive interference | Rapid fluctuation over half-wavelength displacement | Diversity, equalization | | **Flat fading** | Signal BW $\ll$ coherence BW | Entire bandwidth fades uniformly | Not a major issue; diversity helps | | **Frequency-selective fading** | Signal BW $>$ coherence BW | Different frequency components fade differently | Adaptive equalization, OFDM | | **Fast fading** | Symbol period $\gg$ coherence time | Channel changes within a symbol | Robust modulation, channel coding | | **Slow fading** | Symbol period $\ll$ coherence time | Channel roughly constant over many symbols | Standard receivers suffice | ## 2.4 Two Statistical Fading Distributions ### Rayleigh Fading (No Line-of-Sight, NLOS) When the **dominant direct path is obstructed** and only many small scattered components reach the receiver, the in-phase $I(t)$ and quadrature $Q(t)$ components of the received envelope are statistically independent zero-mean Gaussian random variables. The envelope $r = \sqrt{I^2 + Q^2}$ follows a **Rayleigh distribution**. The Probability Density Function (PDF) is: $$f_r(r) = \frac{r}{\sigma^2}\, e^{-r^2 / (2\sigma^2)}, \quad r \geq 0$$ where $\sigma^2$ is the average power of the multipath components. ### Rician Fading (Line-of-Sight, LOS) When a **strong dominant (specular) LOS component** of amplitude $A$ exists alongside scattered multipath, the envelope follows a **Rician distribution**: $$f_r(r) = \frac{r}{\sigma^2}\, e^{-(r^2 + A^2) / (2\sigma^2)}\, I_0\!\left(\frac{A\, r}{\sigma^2}\right), \quad r \geq 0$$ where $I_0(\cdot)$ is the modified Bessel function of the first kind (zeroth order), and the **Rician K-factor** is defined as: $$K_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > As $A \to 0$, the Rician distribution **reduces to the Rayleigh distribution**, confirming the latter as the LOS-free limiting case. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table contains every critical formula you must memorize for KTU ESE questions on Multipath Effect.** | # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|-----------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Received multipath signal | $r(t) = \sum_n a_n(t) \cos\bigl[2\pi f_c t + \phi_n(t)\bigr]$ | Bandpass form | | 2 | Baseband impulse response | $h(t,\tau) = \sum_n a_n(t)\, e^{-j\phi_n(t)}\, \delta(\tau - \tau_n)$ | Double-variable model | | 3 | Mean excess delay | $\bar{\tau} = \dfrac{\sum_n P(\tau_n)\,\tau_n}{\sum_n P(\tau_n)}$ | seconds | | 4 | RMS delay spread | $\sigma_\tau = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_n P(\tau_n)(\tau_n - \bar{\tau})^2}{\sum_n P(\tau_n)}}$ | seconds; coherence indicator | | 5 | Maximum excess delay | $\tau_{\max}$ such that $P(\tau_n) \approx 0$ for $\tau_n > \tau_{\max}$ | seconds | | 6 | Coherence bandwidth (50% corr.) | $B_c \approx \dfrac{1}{5\sigma_\tau}$ | Hz | | 7 | Coherence bandwidth (90% corr.) | $B_c \approx \dfrac{1}{50\sigma_\tau}$ | Hz | | 8 | Doppler shift | $f_d = \dfrac{v\, f_c\, \cos\theta}{c} = \dfrac{v\, \cos\theta}{\lambda}$ | Hz | | 9 | Maximum Doppler spread | $f_m = \dfrac{v}{\lambda}$ | Hz | | 10 | Coherence time (Jakes) | $T_c \approx \dfrac{0.423}{f_m}$ | seconds | | 11 | Rayleigh PDF | $f_r(r) = \dfrac{r}{\sigma^2}\, e^{-r^2 / (2\sigma^2)}$ | Envelope distribution | | 12 | Rician PDF | $f_r(r) = \dfrac{r}{\sigma^2}\, e^{-(r^2+A^2)/(2\sigma^2)}\, I_0(A r / \sigma^2)$ | With LOS component | | 13 | Rician K-factor | $K = \dfrac{A^2}{2\sigma^2}$ | Dimensionless (linear) | | 14 | Flat-fading condition | $B_s \ll B_c$ and $T_s \gg \sigma_\tau$ | $B_s$ = signal BW, $T_s$ = symbol period | | 15 | Frequency-selective condition | $B_s > B_c$ | ISI present | | 16 | Fast-fading condition | $T_s > T_c$ | Channel changes within symbol | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The formulas and models above are not academic abstractions — they are the **design language** of every radio engineer. Specific applications: - **LTE/5G NR Planning**: The Cyclic Prefix (CP) length in OFDM is chosen to be $\geq 4\sigma_\tau$ to combat ISI from multipath. - **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11n/ac/ax)**: Channel estimation and MIMO precoding matrices depend directly on $h(t,\tau)$. - **Satellite Communication (DVB-S2)**: Long Rician-dominated LOS channels permit high-order modulations like 32-APSK. - **Radar Systems**: Multipath returns from sea/ground surfaces are exploited for target detection above the horizon. - **GNSS (GPS)**: Multipath rejection is the limiting factor in urban canyon positioning accuracy. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Mean Excess Delay and RMS Delay Spread **Given:** A power delay profile (PDP) consisting of discrete multipath components with powers $P(\tau_n)$ arriving at delays $\tau_n$. **Goal:** Derive the mean excess delay $\bar{\tau}$ and RMS delay spread $\sigma_\tau$. ### Step 1 — Define the Total Received Power The total received power, summing contributions from all multipath components, is: $$P_m = \sum_{n=1}^{N} P(\tau_n)$$ This quantity serves as the normalization constant in subsequent definitions. ### Step 2 — Define the Mean Excess Delay By definition, the mean excess delay is the **first moment** of the power delay profile, normalized by the total power: $$\bar{\tau} = \frac{\sum_{n=1}^{N} P(\tau_n)\, \tau_n}{P_m}$$ > **Physical meaning:** It is the **weighted average delay** of all multipath components, where each delay is weighted by the power carried by that component. ### Step 3 — Define the RMS Delay Spread The RMS delay spread is the **square root of the second central moment** of the PDP: $$\overline{\tau^2} = \frac{\sum_{n=1}^{N} P(\tau_n)\, \tau_n^{\,2}}{P_m}$$ $$\sigma_\tau = \sqrt{\overline{\tau^2} - (\bar{\tau})^2} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{n=1}^{N} P(\tau_n)\,(\tau_n - \bar{\tau})^2}{P_m}}$$ > **Physical meaning:** It is the **standard deviation** of the delays about the mean — a quantitative measure of how "spread out" the multipath energy is in time. ### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A channel measurement yields the following power delay profile: | Delay $\tau_n$ ($\mu$s) | Power $P(\tau_n)$ (relative) | |--------------------------|-------------------------------| | 0 | 1.0 | | 0.1 | 0.5 | | 0.3 | 0.2 | | 0.5 | 0.1 | | 1.0 | 0.05 | **Compute $\bar{\tau}$ and $\sigma_\tau$.** **Step 4a — Total power:** $$P_m = 1.0 + 0.5 + 0.2 + 0.1 + 0.05 = 1.85$$ **Step 4b — Numerator for $\bar{\tau}$:** $$\sum_n P(\tau_n)\tau_n = (1.0)(0) + (0.5)(0.1) + (0.2)(0.3) + (0.1)(0.5) + (0.05)(1.0)$$ $$\sum_n P(\tau_n)\tau_n = 0 + 0.05 + 0.06 + 0.05 + 0.05 = 0.21\ \mu s$$ **Step 4c — Mean excess delay:** $$\bar{\tau} = \frac{0.21}{1.85} \approx 0.1135\ \mu s$$ **Step 4d — Numerator for $\overline{\tau^2}$:** $$\sum_n P(\tau_n)\tau_n^{\,2} = (1.0)(0)^2 + (0.5)(0.1)^2 + (0.2)(0.3)^2 + (0.1)(0.5)^2 + (0.05)(1.0)^2$$ $$\sum_n P(\tau_n)\tau_n^{\,2} = 0 + 0.005 + 0.018 + 0.025 + 0.05 = 0.098\ \mu s^2$$ **Step 4e — Second moment:** $$\overline{\tau^2} = \frac{0.098}{1.85} \approx 0.0530\ \mu s^2$$ **Step 4f — RMS delay spread:** $$\sigma_\tau = \sqrt{0.0530 - (0.1135)^2} = \sqrt{0.0530 - 0.0129} = \sqrt{0.0401}$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_\tau \approx 0.2002\ \mu s}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Coherence Bandwidth from RMS Delay Spread **Starting Point:** The frequency autocorrelation function of the channel is the Fourier transform of the power delay profile (Wiener–Khinchin theorem): $$R_H(\Delta f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} P(\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi \Delta f \tau}\, d\tau$$ For an exponentially decaying PDP (a common practical approximation): $$P(\tau) = \frac{1}{\sigma_\tau}\, e^{-\tau / \sigma_\tau}, \quad \tau \geq 0$$ **Step 1 — Compute the Fourier transform:** $$R_H(\Delta f) = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma_\tau}\, e^{-\tau/\sigma_\tau}\, e^{-j2\pi \Delta f \tau}\, d\tau$$ $$R_H(\Delta f) = \frac{1}{\sigma_\tau}\, \int_0^{\infty} e^{-(1/\sigma_\tau + j2\pi\Delta f)\tau}\, d\tau$$ **Step 2 — Evaluate the integral:** $$R_H(\Delta f) = \frac{1}{\sigma_\tau} \cdot \frac{1}{(1/\sigma_\tau) + j2\pi\Delta f} = \frac{1}{1 + j2\pi\sigma_\tau \Delta f}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the magnitude (the correlation coefficient):** $$\vert R_H(\Delta f) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi\sigma_\tau \Delta f)^2}}$$ **Step 4 — Define the coherence bandwidth** as the frequency separation $\Delta f = B_c$ at which the correlation drops to a specified level. **Case A — 50% correlation level ($\vert R_H \vert = 0.5$):** $$0.5 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi\sigma_\tau B_c)^2}}$$ $$1 + (2\pi\sigma_\tau B_c)^2 = 4$$ $$(2\pi\sigma_\tau B_c)^2 = 3$$ $$2\pi\sigma_\tau B_c = \sqrt{3}$$ $$\boxed{B_c = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\pi\sigma_\tau} \approx \frac{1}{5\sigma_\tau}}$$ **Case B — 90% correlation level ($\vert R_H \vert = 0.9$):** $$0.9 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi\sigma_\tau B_c)^2}}$$ $$1 + (2\pi\sigma_\tau B_c)^2 = \frac{1}{0.81} \approx 1.2346$$ $$(2\pi\sigma_\tau B_c)^2 \approx 0.2346$$ $$2\pi\sigma_\tau B_c \approx 0.4844$$ $$\boxed{B_c = \frac{0.4844}{2\pi\sigma_\tau} \approx \frac{1}{50\sigma_\tau}}$$ > [!TIP] > **Exam Memory Trick:** Remember the two constants **5** and **50** as "5 for 50%" and "50 for 90%". The smaller constant corresponds to the more relaxed correlation requirement (only 50% match), and hence a larger $B_c$. ## 3.3 Derivation: Doppler Shift and Coherence Time **Setup:** A mobile receiver moving with velocity $v$ at an angle $\theta$ relative to the direction of arrival of a planar wavefront. **Step 1 — Geometry of wavefront intersection:** If the receiver moves a distance $v\,dt$ in time $dt$, the number of wavelengths crossed is: $$dN = \frac{v\, dt\, \cos\theta}{\lambda}$$ **Step 2 — Phase increment due to motion:** $$d\phi = 2\pi\, dN = 2\pi\, \frac{v\, dt\, \cos\theta}{\lambda}$$ **Step 3 — Instantaneous frequency deviation:** $$f_d = \frac{1}{2\pi}\, \frac{d\phi}{dt} = \frac{v\, \cos\theta}{\lambda}$$ Equivalently, since $c = f_c \lambda$: $$\boxed{f_d = \frac{v\, f_c\, \cos\theta}{c}}$$ **Step 4 — Maximum Doppler shift** occurs at $\theta = 0$ (motion directly toward the source): $$f_m = \frac{v}{\lambda} = \frac{v\, f_c}{c}$$ **Step 5 — Coherence time** is defined as the time duration over which the channel's autocorrelation remains above 0.5. Using Jakes' classical derivation for an isotropic scattering environment: $$R_H(\Delta t) = J_0(2\pi f_m \Delta t)$$ where $J_0(\cdot)$ is the Bessel function of the first kind (zeroth order). Setting $R_H(\Delta t) = 0.5$: $$J_0(2\pi f_m T_c) = 0.5$$ The first zero of $J_0 - 0.5$ occurs at $2\pi f_m T_c \approx 2.658$, giving: $$\boxed{T_c \approx \frac{0.423}{f_m}}$$ An alternative simpler approximation is: $$T_c \approx \frac{9}{16\pi f_m}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation: Classification of Fading (Two-Channel Classification Theorem) **Step 1 — Define signal parameters:** Let $B_s$ denote the signal bandwidth and $T_s$ the symbol period. **Step 2 — Compute time dispersion parameters** from the channel: $\sigma_\tau$ and $B_c$. **Step 3 — Compute frequency dispersion parameters:** $f_m$ and $T_c$. **Step 4 — Compare signal to channel parameters:** | Comparison | Regime | Fading Type | |------------|--------|-------------| | $B_s \ll B_c$ and $T_s \gg \sigma_\tau$ | No dispersion in either domain | **Flat / Slow fading** | | $B_s \ll B_c$ and $T_s \ll T_c$ | Channel nearly static, narrowband | **Flat / Slow fading** | | $B_s > B_c$ | Time dispersion present | **Frequency-selective fading** | | $T_s > T_c$ | Channel changes within symbol | **Fast fading** | **Step 5 — Output classification (all four possible combinations):** | | **Slow fading** ($T_s \ll T_c$) | **Fast fading** ($T_s > T_c$) | |---|---|---| | **Flat** ($B_s \ll B_c$) | Flat-slow (e.g., pedestrian) | Flat-fast (e.g., indoor Wi-Fi with mobility) | | **Frequency-selective** ($B_s > B_c$) | Selective-slow (e.g., urban macrocell) | Selective-fast (rare, high-mobility broadband) | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Multipath Propagation Block Diagram (Mermaid) The diagram below depicts the functional architecture of how a transmitted signal traverses the multipath channel and reaches the receiver with impairments caused by reflection, diffraction, and scattering. ```mermaid flowchart LR tx[Transmitter
Source Signal s of t] antTx[Tx Antenna
Radiates EM Wave] chan[Multipath Channel
Mobile Radio Environment] refl[Reflection
Ground Walls Buildings] dif[Diffr

topics.title: Significance of Diversity in Wireless Communication Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Significance of Diversity in Wireless Communication Systems # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diversity** in wireless communication is a powerful transmission and reception technique that exploits the **independent fading behavior** of multiple signal paths (or multiple versions of the same signal) to **combat the detrimental effects of multipath fading** and improve the overall **link reliability (error performance and outage probability)** without requiring additional transmit power or bandwidth. In a multipath fading environment, the instantaneous received SNR $\gamma$ becomes a **random variable** rather than a deterministic value. Diversity introduces **statistical redundancy** so that the probability that *all* received copies of the signal are simultaneously in a deep fade becomes vanishingly small. > [!NOTE] > **Key Metric — Diversity Gain (dB):** The reduction in the required average SNR (or transmitted power) to achieve a target Bit Error Rate (BER) or outage probability, when a diversity scheme of order $M$ is employed instead of a single antenna/link. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are standing in a crowded classroom trying to hear your friend whisper an important exam tip. Suddenly, a noisy air-conditioner kicks in right next to your ear — you lose the message completely. Now imagine you asked **5 different classmates** standing at **5 different spots in the room** to listen and repeat the same message. Even though the noise drowns out 1 or 2 of them, it is extremely unlikely that the **same loud noise** will drown out all 5 simultaneously. You simply pick the loudest (or combine all 5 clean messages), and the message gets through. **That is exactly what diversity does:** - **Multiple classmates** = Multiple independent fading channels (different antennas, times, or frequencies). - **The random air-conditioner noise** = Multipath fading. - **Picking the best / combining** = Combining technique (SC / MRC / EGC). This redundancy converts a *fragile single link* into a *statistically robust communication link*. --- ## 1.3 Why Diversity is Significant — The Three Pillars | Pillar | Engineering Impact | |---|---| | **Fading Mitigation** | Eliminates deep fade-induced outage events | | **Power Efficiency** | Reduces transmit power by 10–30 dB for same BER | | **Spectral Efficiency** | Achieves reliability **without expanding bandwidth** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** Diversity is classified by KTU under the broader domain of **fading countermeasures**, alongside equalization, coding, and spread spectrum. It is one of the **most cost-effective** techniques in modern 4G/5G cellular and Wi-Fi systems. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Fading Problem A wireless signal arrives at the receiver via **N multiple paths**, each with its own random amplitude $a_k$ and phase $\phi_k$. The received complex baseband envelope is: $$ r(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k e^{j\phi_k} s(t - \tau_k) $$ When these phasors add **constructively**, the signal is strong; when they add **destructively**, the signal undergoes a **deep fade** of 20–40 dB below the mean level. Such fades occur every **half-wavelength** of mobile motion (≈10 cm at 2 GHz) — so a vehicle moving at 60 km/h experiences fades every few milliseconds. --- ## 2.2 The Diversity Principle — Operational Logic The fundamental insight is: > If the probability of a single link failing is $p$, and we have $M$ *independent* links, then the probability that **all** $M$ links fail is $p^M$ (which is much smaller). Diversity exploits this by ensuring that the $M$ replicas undergo **statistically independent fading**. Three requirements must be met: 1. **Independence:** Each branch must fade independently (sufficient antenna spacing / time / frequency separation). 2. **Same information:** All branches must carry the **same information-bearing signal**. 3. **Combination:** The receiver must use a **coherent or non-coherent combining rule**. --- ## 2.3 Classification of Diversity Techniques ### (a) Time Diversity - Transmit the same symbol in **multiple time slots**, separated by more than the **channel coherence time** $T_c$. - Achieved via **interleaving + channel coding**. - Simple but introduces **latency**. ### (b) Frequency Diversity - Transmit the same signal over **multiple carrier frequencies**, separated by more than the **coherence bandwidth** $B_c$. - Achieved via **OFDM subcarriers**, **spread spectrum (CDMA)**, or **frequency hopping**. ### (c) Space (Antenna) Diversity — *Most widely used* - Use **multiple antennas** at the receiver (SIMO), transmitter (MISO), or both (MIMO). - Antennas must be separated by $\geq \lambda/2$ to ensure independent fading. - Three sub-categories: - **Receive Diversity (SIMO)** — combining at the Rx. - **Transmit Diversity (MISO)** — uses space–time codes (Alamouti). - **Spatial Multiplexing (MIMO)** — increases data rate, not just reliability. --- ## 2.4 Combining Techniques — Mathematical Core ### (i) Selection Combining (SC) - Picks the branch with the **highest instantaneous SNR**. - Simpler hardware — only one RF chain active at a time. ### (ii) Maximal Ratio Combining (MRC) - **Co-phases** each branch and **weights** by its instantaneous SNR, then sums. - **Optimal linear combiner** in AWGN — maximizes output SNR. - Output SNR = **sum of all branch SNRs**. ### (iii) Equal Gain Combining (EGC) - Co-phases each branch and sums with **equal weights**. - Performance between SC and MRC; simpler than MRC (no amplitude weighting). --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations > [!NOTE] > **All formulas below are exam-critical for PERAT753 Module 1.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Rayleigh PDF of SNR | $f_{\gamma}(\gamma) = \dfrac{1}{\bar{\gamma}} e^{-\gamma/\bar{\gamma}}, \quad \gamma \geq 0$ | $\bar{\gamma}$ = avg SNR per branch | | 2 | Rayleigh CDF of SNR | $F_{\gamma}(\gamma) = 1 - e^{-\gamma/\bar{\gamma}}$ | Used in outage calculation | | 3 | **SC Outage Probability** (order $M$) | $P_{out}^{SC} = \left(1 - e^{-\gamma_{th}/\bar{\gamma}}\right)^{M}$ | $\gamma_{th}$ = threshold SNR | | 4 | **Average SNR with MRC** | $\bar{\gamma}_{MRC} = M \cdot \bar{\gamma}$ | **Linear SNR gain = $M$** | | 5 | **Average SNR with EGC** | $\bar{\gamma}_{EGC} = \bar{\gamma} \left[1 + (M-1)\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right]$ | Approx. for Rayleigh | | 6 | **Diversity Order** | Slope of $\log P_e$ vs. $\log \bar{\gamma}$ at high SNR | Equals number of independent branches | | 7 | BER(BPSK, Rayleigh, no diversity) | $P_b = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(1 - \sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{\gamma}}{1+\bar{\gamma}}}\right)$ | Single-branch baseline | | 8 | BER(BPSK, Rayleigh, **MRC order $M$**) | $P_b = \left[\dfrac{1}{2}\left(1 - \sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{\gamma}}{1+\bar{\gamma}}}\right)\right]^{M}$ | MGF-based exact form | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall Reminder:** In the table above, the symbol $\vert x \vert$ is rendered using `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. In your exam scripts, write it as $|x|$ in LaTeX. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Diversity Type Used | |---|---| | 4G LTE / 5G NR Base Stations | Receive antenna diversity (4–8 antennas) + MIMO | | Mobile handsets | Receive diversity with 2 antennas (MRC) | | Wi-Fi (802.11n/ac/ax) | MIMO spatial multiplexing | | Satellite communication | Time + frequency diversity via coding | | Underwater acoustic links | Time + frequency (channel varies slowly) | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Outage Probability for Selection Combining (Order $M$) > **Goal:** Derive $P_{out}^{SC} = \left(1 - e^{-\gamma_{th}/\bar{\gamma}}\right)^{M}$ for i.i.d. Rayleigh fading branches. **Step 1 — Define outage of a single branch.** Outage on a single branch occurs when its instantaneous SNR $\gamma_i$ falls below the threshold $\gamma_{th}$: $$ P(\gamma_i < \gamma_{th}) = \int_{0}^{\gamma_{th}} f_{\gamma_i}(\gamma)\, d\gamma $$ **Step 2 — Substitute Rayleigh CDF.** For Rayleigh fading, the CDF of SNR on branch $i$ is: $$ F_{\gamma_i}(\gamma) = 1 - e^{-\gamma/\bar{\gamma}} $$ Therefore, the **single-branch outage probability** is: $$ P_{out,\,1} = F_{\gamma_i}(\gamma_{th}) = 1 - e^{-\gamma_{th}/\bar{\gamma}} $$ **Step 3 — Apply independence.** With $M$ i.i.d. branches, the event that the **best branch's SNR** falls below $\gamma_{th}$ requires **all** branches to be below threshold simultaneously. Since the branches are independent: $$ P_{out}^{SC} = \prod_{i=1}^{M} P(\gamma_i < \gamma_{th}) = \prod_{i=1}^{M} F_{\gamma_i}(\gamma_{th}) $$ **Step 4 — Substitute identical distribution.** Because all $M$ branches have the same Rayleigh distribution: $$ P_{out}^{SC} = \left[F_{\gamma}(\gamma_{th})\right]^{M} = \left(1 - e^{-\gamma_{th}/\bar{\gamma}}\right)^{M} $$ $$ \boxed{\,P_{out}^{SC} = \left(1 - e^{-\gamma_{th}/\bar{\gamma}}\right)^{M}\,} $$ > **Interpretation:** Doubling the number of branches from $M$ to $2M$ squares the outage probability reduction factor — a **massive reliability improvement**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average SNR for Maximal Ratio Combining > **Goal:** Show that MRC combiner output SNR equals the **sum** of branch SNRs. **Step 1 — Received signal on branch $i$.** Let $r_i = h_i s + n_i$, where $h_i$ is the complex channel gain and $n_i \sim \mathcal{CN}(0, N_0)$ is AWGN. **Step 2 — MRC combining rule.** The combined signal is the weighted sum: $$ r_{MRC} = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_i^{\,*} r_i, \qquad w_i = h_i $$ (Each branch is weighted by the **conjugate** of its channel gain to perform coherent combining.) **Step 3 — Compute output SNR.** For Gaussian noise with variance $N_0$ per branch, the SNR after combining is: $$ \gamma_{MRC} = \frac{\left|\sum_{i=1}^{M} w_i^{\,*} h_i\right|^{2}}{N_0 \sum_{i=1}^{M} \vert w_i \vert^{2}} = \frac{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{M} \vert h_i \vert^{2}\right)^{2}}{N_0 \sum_{i=1}^{M} \vert h_i \vert^{2}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{M} \vert h_i \vert^{2}}{N_0} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute $\gamma_i = \vert h_i \vert^{2}/N_0$:** $$ \boxed{\,\gamma_{MRC} = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \gamma_i\,} $$ **Step 5 — Take expectation (average SNR):** $$ \bar{\gamma}_{MRC} = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{M} \gamma_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \bar{\gamma}_i = M \bar{\gamma} $$ $$ \boxed{\,\bar{\gamma}_{MRC} = M \bar{\gamma}\,} $$ > **Engineering Insight:** MRC achieves an **array gain of $M$ (linear)** and a **diversity order of $M$** simultaneously — the best of all linear combining schemes. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SC vs. EGC vs. MRC | Metric | Selection Combining (SC) | Equal Gain Combining (EGC) | Maximal Ratio Combining (MRC) | |---|---|---|---| | **Combining Rule** | Pick max SNR branch | Co-phase + equal-weight sum | Co-phase + SNR-weighted sum | | **Output SNR (Avg)** | $\bar{\gamma}\sum_{k=1}^{M}\frac{1}{k}$ | $\bar{\gamma}[1 + (M-1)\pi/4]$ | $M\bar{\gamma}$ | | **Diversity Order** | $M$ | $M$ | $M$ | | **Implementation Complexity** | **Low** (single RF chain) | Medium (need $M$ coherent detectors) | High (need full CSI amplitude + phase) | | **Performance Rank** | Worst of the three | Middle | **Best** (optimal in AWGN) | | **Hardware Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest | | **Outage Probability** | $\left(1-e^{-\gamma_{th}/\bar{\gamma}}\right)^{M}$ | Slightly better than SC | Best (lowest) | | **Best Use Case** | Low-cost IoT, simple receivers | Mobile handsets | Base stations, high-tier LTE/5G NR | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Memory Hook:** At **high SNR**, all three schemes achieve the **same diversity order $M$**, but MRC maintains a **~1–2 dB array gain advantage** over SC and EGC. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Diversity Receiver Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR TX[Transmitter
s t of t] CH1[Channel h1
Branch 1] CH2[Channel h2
Branch 2] CHM[Channel hM
Branch M] R1[Antenna 1
Receiver RF] R2[Antenna 2
Receiver RF] RM[Antenna M
Receiver RF] COMB[Combining Block
SC or EGC or MRC] DEC[Detector and
Decoder] OUT[Recovered Bits
r hat t] TX --> CH1 TX --> CH2 TX --> CHM CH1 --> R1 CH2 --> R2 CHM --> RM R1 --> COMB R2 --> COMB RM --> COMB COMB --> DEC DEC --> OUT style TX fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style COMB fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style DEC fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style OUT fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** A single transmitted symbol $s(t)$ propagates through $M$ independently fading channels $(h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_M)$, is received by $M$ antennas, combined via the chosen algorithm, and then detected. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Choosing a Diversity Scheme ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Wireless Link Design] --> B{Need high
data rate?} B -- Yes --> C[MIMO Spatial
Multiplexing] B -- No --> D{Number of
antennas available?} D -- Only 1 RF chain --> E[Selection Combining SC] D -- Multiple RF chains but limited CSI --> F[Equal Gain Combining EGC] D -- Full CSI available --> G[Maximal Ratio Combining MRC] E --> H{Hardware cost
constraint?} H -- Low --> E H -- Medium --> F F --> I[Channel coding
and interleaving] G --> I C --> I I --> J[End: Reliable
Wireless Link] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style J fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** This is the **KTU exam-ready decision tree** — students should be able to justify the choice of diversity scheme based on (i) data rate requirement, (ii) hardware cost, and (iii) availability of channel state information (CSI). --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Conceptual Map — Diversity Taxonomy ```mermaid mindmap root((Diversity
Techniques)) Time Repetition coding Interleaving ARQ retransmission Frequency OFDM subcarriers Spread spectrum CDMA Frequency hopping Space Receive SIMO Transmit MISO Full MIMO Combining Selection SC Equal Gain EGC Maximal Ratio MRC ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** A unified classification — diversity can be applied in **time, frequency, space**, or combined (hybrid). The **combining block** is what extracts the reliability benefit at the receiver. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define diversity in wireless communication. Why is it essential for fading channels?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Diversity** is a technique that transmits the same information-bearing signal over **multiple independently fading channels** (time, frequency, or space) and combines the received copies at the receiver to mitigate the effects of multipath fading. **Why essential (2 marks):** 1. **Multipath fading** causes deep signal fades of 20–40 dB, leading to high BER and outage. 2. By providing **statistically independent replicas**, the probability that *all* copies simultaneously fade becomes vanishingly small → **$P(\text{outage}) = p^M$ instead of $p$**. 3. It improves **link reliability without increasing transmit power or bandwidth** — a spectral- and power-efficient solution. > **[1 mark]** — correct definition; **[2 marks]** — clear justification with fading impact. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Compare Selection Combining (SC) and Maximal Ratio Combining (MRC) in terms of performance and complexity.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | SC | MRC | |---|---|---| | Combining rule | Pick highest SNR branch | Weighted coherent sum | | Diversity order | $M$ | $M$ | | Avg output SNR | $\bar{\gamma}\sum_{k=1}^{M}\frac{1}{k}$ | $M\bar{\gamma}$ (highest) | | Complexity | **Low** | **High** | | CSI requirement | Only SNR comparison | Full amplitude + phase CSI | **Conclusion (1 mark):** MRC is the **optimal linear combiner** achieving maximum SNR, but SC is preferred in low-cost IoT devices due to its simplicity. --- ## PART B — Full ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 1]` #### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the three major types of diversity — Time, Frequency, and Space — with their engineering applications and the conditions under which they are independent. **Model Solution:** **Time Diversity (2 marks):** - The same symbol is transmitted in **different time slots** separated by more than the channel **coherence time** $T_c = \frac{1}{2 f_D}$, where $f_D$ is the Doppler spread. - Independence is achieved when slot separation $\Delta t \gg T_c$. - Implemented via **interleaving + convolutional/turbo codes**. - **Drawback:** Introduces latency; unsuitable for delay-sensitive voice. - **Application:** GSM control channels, ARQ retransmissions. **Frequency Diversity (2 marks):** - The signal is transmitted over **multiple carrier frequencies** spaced by more than the **coherence bandwidth** $B_c \approx 1/(5\sigma_\tau)$, where $\sigma_\tau$ is RMS delay spread. - Implemented via **OFDM subcarriers**, **spread spectrum (DS-CDMA)**, or **frequency hopping**. - **Application:** Wi-Fi (OFDM), 3G CDMA, military HF links. **Space (Antenna) Diversity (3 marks):** - Multiple antennas at Rx (or Tx) with **physical separation $\geq \lambda/2$** to decorrelate fading. - Three sub-categories: - **SIMO (receive diversity):** Common at base stations; uses SC/MRC. - **MISO (transmit diversity):** Uses space–time codes (e.g., Alamouti). - **MIMO:** Combines diversity + spatial multiplexing for higher data rate. - **Application:** 4G LTE (2×2, 4×4 MIMO), 5G NR (massive MIMO with 64–256 antennas). **Independence conditions summary:** $$ \text{Time: } \Delta t \gg T_c \quad ; \quad \text{Frequency: } \Delta f \gg B_c \quad ; \quad \text{Space: } d \geq \lambda/2 $$ #### (b) [7 Marks] Derive the outage probability expression for Selection Combining of order $M$ over i.i.d. Rayleigh fading channels. Plot qualitatively the outage probability vs. average SNR for $M = 1, 2, 4$. **Model Solution:** **Derivation (5 marks):** 1. Rayleigh CDF of a single branch: $$F_{\gamma_i}(\gamma) = 1 - e^{-\gamma/\bar{\gamma}}$$ 2. Single-branch outage at threshold $\gamma_{th}$: $$P_{out,1} = F_{\gamma_i}(\gamma_{th}) = 1 - e^{-\gamma_{th}/\bar{\gamma}}$$ 3. For SC, the system is in outage only if **all** $M$ branches are below $\gamma_{th}$. Since branches are i.i.d. and independent: $$P_{out}^{SC} = \prod_{i=1}^{M} F_{\gamma_i}(\gamma_{th}) = \left(1 - e^{-\gamma_{th}/\bar{\gamma}}\right)^{M}$$ 4. **Final boxed result:** $$\boxed{\,P_{out}^{SC} = \left(1 - e^{-\gamma_{th}/\bar{\gamma}}\right)^{M}\,}$$ **Qualitative Plot (2 marks):** ``` P_out (log scale) | | \ M=1 | \ | \ M=2 | \ / | \/ | /\ M=4 | / \ / | / \/ | / /\ | / / \ |/____/_____\______ SNR (dB) ``` - All curves decay exponentially, but **steeper slope for higher $M$**. - At high SNR, $P_{out}^{SC} \approx \left(\gamma_{th}/\bar{\gamma}\right)^{M}$ — slope $M$ on log–log scale confirms **diversity order = $M$**. --- ### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 1]` #### (a) [7 Marks] With the help of a block diagram, explain the Maximal Ratio Combining (MRC) technique. Prove that the average output SNR is $M$ times the average branch SNR. **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram Description (2 marks):** The MRC receiver has $M$ branches; each branch consists of: - Antenna + matched filter → produces $r_i$. - Channel estimator → provides $h_i$ estimate. - **Co-phasing block** multiplies $r_i$ by $h_i^{*}/\vert h_i \vert$ to align phases. - **Weighting block** multiplies by $w_i = h_i$ (proportional to branch SNR). - **Summer** adds all weighted, co-phased branches → output $r_{MRC}$. **Mathematical Proof (5 marks):** 1. Received signal on branch $i$: $$r_i = h_i s + n_i, \quad n_i \sim \mathcal{CN}(0,N_0)$$ 2. MRC combiner output: $$r_{MRC} = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_i^{*} r_i = \sum_{i=1}^{M} h_i^{*} (h_i s + n_i)$$ 3. Output SNR (after simplifying, using $|h_i|^2 = \gamma_i N_0$): $$\gamma_{MRC} = \frac{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{M} \vert h_i \vert^{2}\right)^{2}}{N_0 \sum_{i=1}^{M} \vert h_i \vert^{2}} = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \frac{\vert h_i \vert^{2}}{N_0} = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \gamma_i$$ 4. Taking expectation: $$\bar{\gamma}_{MRC} = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \bar{\gamma}_i = M \bar{\gamma}$$ 5. **Conclusion:** $$\boxed{\,\bar{\gamma}_{MRC} = M \bar{\gamma}\,}$$ #### (b) [7 Marks] Compare SC, EGC, and MRC. Under what conditions does EGC become equivalent to MRC? Calculate the diversity gain (in dB) achieved by MRC with $M = 4$ branches at a target BER of $10^{-3}$ for BPSK in Rayleigh fading. **Model Solution:** **Comparison (3 marks):** | Criterion | SC | EGC | MRC | |---|---|---|---| | Complexity | Low | Medium | High | | Average output SNR | Lowest | Middle | Highest | | Diversity order | $M$ | $M$ | $M$ | | CSI needed | SNR only | Phase only | Full (amp + phase) | **When EGC = MRC (1 mark):** EGC becomes equivalent to MRC only when **all branch SNRs are equal**, i.e., $\gamma_1 = \gamma_2 = \cdots = \gamma_M$, because the equal weights become identical to the SNR-proportional weights. **Numerical Calculation (3 marks):** BPSK BER over Rayleigh with MRC order $M$: $$ P_b = \left[\frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \sqrt{\frac{\bar{\gamma}}{1+\bar{\gamma}}}\right)\right]^{M} $$ Solving numerically (or via standard curves) for $P_b = 10^{-3}$, $M=4$: - Single branch ($M=1$): requires $\bar{\gamma}_1 \approx 23.5$ dB (well-known result for Rayleigh BPSK at $10^{-3}$). - With $M=4$ MRC: requires $\bar{\gamma}_4 \approx 13.8$ dB (approximate). $$ \text{Diversity Gain} = \bar{\gamma}_1 - \bar{\gamma}_4 \approx 23.5 - 13.8 = 9.7 \text{ dB} $$ **Result:** $$\boxed{\,\text{Diversity Gain} \approx 9.7 \text{ dB for } M = 4 \text{ at BER} = 10^{-3}\,}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!** > 1. **Do NOT confuse Diversity Order with Array Gain.** Diversity order = **slope of BER curve on log–log plot**; Array gain = horizontal shift in SNR. MRC provides **both**. > 2. **Do NOT write "SC is always worse than MRC" without qualification.** At *high SNR*, both have the **same diversity order $M$**; MRC only has a small (1–2 dB) constant array gain advantage. > 3. **For outage probability derivation:** You must show the **independence assumption explicitly** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the product rule is applied without justifying i.i.d. > 4. **Numerical diversity gain calculation:** If you don't have a curve/calculator, quote the **standard reference value** of $\sim 23.5$ dB for Rayleigh BPSK at $10^{-3}$ and note that MRC reduces it by ≈ 9–10 dB for $M=4$. > 5. **Block diagrams must have labels** — unlabelled blocks fetch zero credit in KTU valuation. --- ## 📌 TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ **Diversity** = transmitting multiple independently fading copies of the same information to combat multipath fading. - ✅ Three main types: **Time** ($\Delta t \gg T_c$), **Frequency** ($\Delta f \gg B_c$), **Space** ($d \geq \lambda/2$). - ✅ Three combining methods: **SC** (low complexity), **EGC** (medium), **MRC** (optimal linear combiner). - ✅ **MRC output SNR = sum of branch SNRs** → average SNR = $M\bar{\gamma}$. - ✅ **SC outage probability** for $M$ i.i.d.
