Rayleigh branches: $P_{out} = (1 - e^{-\gamma_{th}/\bar{\gamma}})^M$. - ✅ **Diversity order** = slope of $\log P_e$ vs $\log \bar{\gamma}$ curve = number of independent branches $M$. - ✅ **BPSK BER over Rayleigh with $M$-branch MRC**: $P_b = \left[\frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \sqrt{\bar{\gamma}/(1+\bar{\gamma})}\right)\right]^{M}$. - ✅ Diversity gain for $M=4$ at BER $=10^{-3}$ is ≈ **9–10 dB**. - ✅ **SC and MRC achieve the same diversity order**; only the array gain differs. - ✅ Applications: 4G/5G base stations (MIMO + MRC), mobile handsets (SC/EGC), Wi-Fi (MIMO), satellite links (time-frequency coding). - ✅ **Independence condition is critical** — diversity gain is lost if branches are correlated.

topics.title: Short Range Communication System: Introduction to Current Wireless Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Short Range Communication System: Introduction to Current Wireless Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

A **Short Range Communication System (SRCS)** is a class of wireless communication systems engineered to operate over limited physical distances, typically spanning from a few centimeters up to approximately **100 meters**, characterized by low transmit power (usually under **100 mW**), unlicensed spectrum usage, and consumer-grade hardware. These systems form the foundational physical and data-link layers of modern **Personal Area Networks (PAN)**, **Body Area Networks (BAN)**, and **Wireless Local Area Networks (WLAN)**, enabling ubiquitous connectivity for the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, smart environments, and embedded cyber-physical systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Snapshot:** Short-range wireless systems are characterized by **constrained coverage (≤ 100 m)**, **low power consumption (mW range)**, **license-exempt ISM bands** (Industrial, Scientific, Medical), and **high device density** in localized clusters.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Room Whisperer"

Imagine you are standing in a packed auditorium:

- **Long-range communication (Cellular 4G/5G)** is like **shouting through a microphone connected to loudspeakers** — your voice travels kilometers, but it requires huge power, infrastructure (towers), and licensing (broadcast permits).
- **Short-range communication** is like **whispering to the person sitting next to you** — only a few meters of reach, minimal energy, no license required, and the conversation is private and immediate.
- **Wi-Fi** is the **middle ground** — like talking to a small group in a room using a moderate voice.

This "whisper" analogy explains why short-range systems use **tiny batteries (coin cells)**, operate in **unlicensed bands (2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, 13.56 MHz)**, and yet form the **dominant 70%** of all wireless devices shipped globally — because they are cheap, energy-efficient, and easy to deploy.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

The following metrics are universally used to classify and benchmark SRCS:

- **Operating Range**: $\leq$ **100 m** (NFC: 10 cm, BLE: 10–100 m, Wi-Fi: 50–100 m)
- **Transmit Power**: **0 dBm to 20 dBm** (1 mW to 100 mW)
- **Data Rate**: **kbps to Gbps** (NB-IoT: 250 kbps, UWB: 1 Gbps, Wi-Fi 6E: 9.6 Gbps)
- **Latency**: **1 ms to 100 ms** (BLE: 7.5 ms, UWB: <1 ms)
- **Frequency Bands**: **Sub-1 GHz, 2.4 GHz ISM, 5 GHz, 60 GHz mmWave, 13.56 MHz (NFC)**
- **Battery Life**: **Months to Years** (ZigBee nodes: 2+ years on AA battery)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Current Wireless Technologies:** KTU PERAT753 Module 2 emphasizes **NFC, RFID, Bluetooth (Classic + BLE), Wi-Fi (802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax), ZigBee (802.15.4), UWB (802.15.4z), IrDA, and Li-Fi** as the canonical SRCS portfolio.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Classification — The Three-Tier SRCS Model

Short-range wireless systems are architecturally classified into three operational tiers based on coverage, power, and use-case:

#### Tier 1: Near-Field / Body-Centric (0 – 10 cm)
- Operates via **inductive or capacitive coupling** (no radiating wave).
- **NFC** (13.56 MHz, 424 kbps), **RFID** (LF/HF/UHF bands).
- Used for contactless payments, access badges, medical implants.

#### Tier 2: Personal Area Network / BAN (0 – 10 m)
- **Bluetooth Classic + BLE** (2.4 GHz), **ZigBee** (2.4 GHz), **ANT+**, **IrDA** (optical 850 nm).
- Used for wearables, hearing aids, fitness bands, smart home sensors.

#### Tier 3: Local Area Network / WMAN (10 – 100 m)
- **Wi-Fi family** (802.11), **UWB** (3.1–10.6 GHz), **Li-Fi** (visible light 400–800 THz).
- Used for indoor broadband, AR/VR headsets, vehicle key fobs, indoor positioning.

### 2.2 Operational Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Spectrum Access:** SRCS operates in **license-exempt ISM bands** (902–928 MHz, 2.4–2.4835 GHz, 5.15–5.825 GHz) regulated by FCC/ETSI to avoid licensing cost.

**Step 2 — Modulation & Coding:** Each technology selects modulation matched to its data-rate vs. robustness trade-off — e.g., BLE uses **Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying (GFSK)**, Wi-Fi uses **OFDM**, UWB uses **Pulse Position Modulation (PPM)**.

**Step 3 — Multiple Access:** CSMA/CA (Wi-Fi), Frequency Hopping (Bluetooth), TDMA + CSMA (ZigBee).

**Step 4 — Power Management:** Sleep cycles, duty cycling, and adaptive transmit power scaling.

**Step 5 — Security:** AES-128 (ZigBee), LE Secure Connections (BLE), WPA3 (Wi-Fi).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations govern SRCS link-budget design and appear frequently in 14-mark derivations:

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Free Space Path Loss (FSPL) | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | dB; $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz |
| 2 | Friis Transmission Equation | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | Watts; $\lambda$ = wavelength |
| 3 | Received Power (dBm form) | $P_r[\text{dBm}] = P_t[\text{dBm}] + G_t + G_r - PL$ | dBm |
| 4 | Log-Normal Shadowing | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$ | dB; $n$ = path loss exponent |
| 5 | Path Loss Exponent (typical) | $n = 2$ free space; $n \in [2.7, 4]$ indoor; $n \in [3, 5]$ obstructed | dimensionless |
| 6 | Shannon Capacity | $C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | bits/sec |
| 7 | Thermal Noise Floor | $N = kTB$ | Watts; $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| 8 | SNR at Receiver | $SNR_{dB} = P_r - N_{dBm}$ | dB |
| 9 | Link Budget Equation | $P_t = P_{r,\min} + PL + M_f - G_t - G_r$ | dBm |
| 10 | Energy per Bit | $E_b = \dfrac{P_t}{R_b}$ | Joules/bit |
| 11 | Maximum Range (Free Space) | $d_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{P_t G_t G_r}{P_{r,\min}}}$ | meters |
| 12 | Bluetooth Hop Rate | $1600$ hops/sec across $79$ channels | fixed spec |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** In 14-mark problems, examiners expect students to identify the correct formula, state **all assumed values** ($P_t$, $G_t$, $G_r$, $f$), and convert units carefully (dBm ↔ Watts using $P[W] = 10^{P[dBm]/10} \times 10^{-3}$).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

SRCS underpin four industry verticals that define modern electronics:

1. **Consumer IoT** — Smart speakers (Wi-Fi), smart locks (BLE + ZigBee), wearables (BLE).
2. **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — Factory sensor networks (ZigBee/Thread), asset tracking (UWB).
3. **Automotive** — Keyless entry (UWB), infotainment (Wi-Fi), tire pressure monitoring (RFID).
4. **Healthcare** — Patient monitoring (BAN with BLE), surgical instrument tracking (RFID).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Free Space Path Loss (FSPL) from First Principles

The **Friis transmission equation** is the cornerstone of all short-range link-budget calculations. We derive it rigorously:

**Step 1 — Isotropic Radiated Power Density.** For an isotropic radiator with transmit power $P_t$, the power density at distance $d$ is distributed uniformly over the surface of a sphere of radius $d$:

$$S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \quad \left[\frac{W}{m^2}\right]$$

This is because the surface area of a sphere is $4\pi d^2$.

**Step 2 — Directional Gain Effect.** A practical antenna with gain $G_t$ focuses energy, increasing the density in the direction of the receiver:

$$S = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

**Step 3 — Effective Aperture of Receiving Antenna.** The receive antenna captures power proportional to its **effective aperture** $A_e$, related to gain $G_r$ and wavelength $\lambda$ by:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

**Step 4 — Power Captured at Receiver.** Combining:

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi} = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

This is the **Friis Transmission Equation** in linear units.

**Step 5 — Convert to dB Form for Engineering Use.** Divide both sides by $P_t$ and take $10\log_{10}$:

$$\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

$$10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_r}{P_t}\right) = 10\log_{10}(G_t G_r) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)$$

$$PL_{dB} = -10\log_{10}(G_t G_r) - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Eliminate Wavelength using $c = f\lambda$.** Substitute $\lambda = c/f$:

$$PL_{dB} = -10\log_{10}(G_t G_r) - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{c}{4\pi d f}\right)$$

$$PL_{dB} = -10\log_{10}(G_t G_r) + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$

For unity-gain isotropic antennas ($G_t = G_r = 1$), this collapses to the famous **FSPL equation**:

$$FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$$

where $d$ is in **kilometers** and $f$ in **MHz** (the constant $32.44 = 20\log_{10}(4\pi/c)$ converts unit systems).

**Step 7 — Sanity Check at 2.4 GHz.** For $f = 2400$ MHz, $d = 10$ m = 0.01 km:

$$FSPL = 20\log_{10}(0.01) + 20\log_{10}(2400) + 32.44 = -40 + 67.6 + 32.44 = 60.04 \text{ dB}$$

This matches the canonical "**60 dB at 10 m for 2.4 GHz**" benchmark used by Bluetooth designers.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Range of an SRCS Link

Given a minimum receiver sensitivity $P_{r,\min}$, derive the maximum communication distance:

**Step 1 — Rearrange Friis Equation:**

$$P_{r,\min} = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d_{max}}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $d_{max}$:**

$$d_{max}^2 = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 P_{r,\min}}$$

$$d_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{P_t G_t G_r}{P_{r,\min}}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example — BLE Class 2 Link.** Given $P_t = 0$ dBm (1 mW), $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi (linear = 1.585), $f = 2.45$ GHz, $P_{r,\min} = -70$ dBm (100 pW):

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.45 \times 10^9} = 0.1224 \text{ m}$$

$$d_{max} = \frac{0.1224}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{(10^{-3})(1.585)^2}{10^{-10}}}$$

$$d_{max} = 0.00974 \times \sqrt{\frac{2.512 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-10}}} = 0.00974 \times \sqrt{2.512 \times 10^{7}}$$

$$d_{max} = 0.00974 \times 5012 = 48.8 \text{ m}$$

This matches the BLE Class 2 specification of **~50 m** line-of-sight range.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SRCS Technologies

| Technology | Standard | Frequency | Range | Data Rate | Tx Power | Modulation | Topology | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NFC** | ISO 14443 | 13.56 MHz | 10 cm | 424 kbps | < 500 mW | ASK | P2P | Payments |
| **RFID HF** | ISO 15693 | 13.56 MHz | 1 m | 26 kbps | < 100 mW | Backscatter | Reader-Tag | Inventory |
| **RFID UHF** | EPC Gen2 | 860–960 MHz | 10 m | 640 kbps | < 2 W EPC | Backscatter | Reader-Tag | Supply chain |
| **BLE** | 802.15.1 | 2.4 GHz | 100 m | 2 Mbps | 1–100 mW | GFSK | Star/P2P | Wearables |
| **Bluetooth Classic** | 802.15.1 | 2.4 GHz | 100 m | 3 Mbps | 1–100 mW | FHSS/GFSK | Piconet | Audio |
| **ZigBee** | 802.15.4 | 2.4 GHz | 100 m | 250 kbps | 1–100 mW | O-QPSK | Mesh | Smart home |
| **Wi-Fi 6** | 802.11ax | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz | 100 m | 9.6 Gbps | 30–200 mW | OFDMA | Star | Broadband |
| **UWB** | 802.15.4z | 3.1–10.6 GHz | 10 m | 1 Gbps | < 10 mW | PPM/BPM | Star | Ranging |
| **IrDA** | IrPHY | 850 nm optical | 1 m | 4 Mbps | < 100 mW | OOK | P2P | Legacy remote |
| **Li-Fi** | IEEE 802.11bb | 400–800 THz | 10 m | 10 Gbps | LED 1–10 W | OFDM | Star | Secure indoor |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Generic Short-Range Transceiver

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the canonical block-level functional architecture of an SRCS transceiver, mapped from antenna to baseband processing:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["RF ANTENNA 2P4 GHZ"] --> B["LNA LOW NOISE AMP"]
    B --> C["MIXER DOWN CONVERSION"]
    D["LOCAL OSCILLATOR"] --> C
    C --> E["CHANNEL FILTER BW 1 MHZ"]
    E --> F["ADC 12 BIT SAMPLING"]
    F --> G["BASEBAND MODEM GFSK OFDM"]
    G --> H["MAC LAYER CSMA CA"]
    H --> I["APPLICATION LAYER USER DATA"]

    J["USER DATA INPUT"] --> K["MAC LAYER ENCAPSULATION"]
    K --> L["BASEBAND MODULATION"]
    L --> M["DAC RECONSTRUCTION"]
    M --> N["MIXER UP CONVERSION"]
    D --> N
    N --> O["POWER AMPLIFIER 0 TO 20 DBM"]
    O --> P["RF ANTENNA TX PATH"]

    classDef rf fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
    classDef bb fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#7F6000,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#E2EFDA,stroke:#385723,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E,N,O,P rf
    class F,G,M bb
    class H,I,J,K,L app
```

### 4.2 Classification Flowchart — SRCS by Range and Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SHORT RANGE WIRELESS SYSTEMS"] --> B["NEAR FIELD 0 TO 10 CM"]
    A --> C["PAN BODY AREA 0 TO 10 M"]
    A --> D["LOCAL AREA 10 TO 100 M"]

    B --> B1["NFC 13P56 MHZ"]
    B --> B2["INDUCTIVE RFID LF HF"]
    B --> B3["IMPLANTABLE MEDICAL DEVICES"]

    C --> C1["BLUETOOTH CLASSIC BLE 2P4 GHZ"]
    C --> C2["ZIGBEE 2P4 GHZ MESH"]
    C --> C3["ANT PLUS BODY SENSORS"]
    C --> C4["IRDA OPTICAL 850 NM"]

    D --> D1["WI FI 802P11 A B G N AC AX"]
    D --> D2["UWB 3P1 TO 10P6 GHZ RANGING"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
> *Define a Short Range Communication System. List any four characteristics that distinguish SRCS from long-range cellular systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Short Range Communication System (SRCS) is a wireless communication system designed to operate over distances typically from a few centimeters up to **100 meters**, using low transmit power in unlicensed frequency bands.

**Four distinguishing characteristics:**

1. **Limited Range**: Operates within $\leq 100$ m, vs. cellular's multi-kilometer coverage.
2. **Low Power Consumption**: Transmit power typically between 0 dBm and 20 dBm (1 mW to 100 mW), enabling battery lifetimes of months to years.
3. **Unlicensed Spectrum**: Uses ISM bands (2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, 13.56 MHz) without spectrum licensing cost.
4. **High Device Density**: Supports dense deployment (e.g., ZigBee mesh of 65,000 nodes), unlike cellular's macro-cell architecture.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each × 4 = 2 marks; 3 marks total)*

---

#### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
> *Differentiate between Bluetooth Classic (BR/EDR) and Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) in terms of frequency hopping, data rate, and power consumption.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Bluetooth Classic (BR/EDR) | Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) |
|---|---|---|
| **Hop Rate** | 1600 hops/sec | Adaptive (used during connection) |
| **Data Rate** | 1 Mbps (Basic Rate), 2–3 Mbps (EDR) | 1 Mbps (LE 1M), 2 Mbps (LE 2M), 125–500 kbps (LE Coded) |
| **Power Consumption** | ~30 mA continuous during connection | ~5–15 mA peak, supports months/years on coin cell |
| **Modulation** | GFSK, $\pi/4$-DQPSK, 8-DPSK | GFSK only |
| **Use Case** | Audio streaming, file transfer | IoT sensors, wearables, beacons |

*(1 mark per correct comparison parameter — 3 marks total)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question B — Choice A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a generic short-range wireless transceiver. Identify the function of each block. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A BLE transmitter operates at 2.45 GHz with $P_t = 0$ dBm, antenna gains $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi, and minimum receiver sensitivity $P_{r,\min} = -70$ dBm. Calculate:
> - (i) The Free Space Path Loss at a distance of 10 m. **(3 marks)**
> - (ii) The received power at 10 m. **(2 marks)**
> - (iii) The maximum communication range in free space. **(2 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)** [7 marks]

A short-range wireless transceiver consists of two parallel paths — **Transmit (TX)** and **Receive (RX)** — sharing a common antenna through a duplexer or RF switch. The functional blocks are:

1. **Antenna**: Converts electrical signals to EM waves (TX) or vice versa (RX). For 2.4 GHz SRCS, common antennas include chip antennas, PCB trace antennas, and ceramic patch antennas.

2. **Low Noise Amplifier (LNA)**: First active stage on RX path; amplifies the weak incoming signal (often below -90 dBm) while adding minimum noise (NF ≈ 2–3 dB).

3. **Mixer**: Multiplies the incoming RF signal with the Local Oscillator (LO) to produce an Intermediate Frequency (IF) or directly to baseband (zero-IF architecture common in BLE).

4. **Channel Select Filter**: Typically a SAW or ceramic bandpass filter centered at the IF with bandwidth matched to the channel (e.g., 1 MHz for BLE).

5. **ADC/DAC**: Converts between analog and digital domains; resolution typically 10–12 bits for SRCS.

6. **Baseband Modem**: Performs modulation/demodulation (GFSK for BLE, OFDM for Wi-Fi), channel coding (FEC), and equalization.

7. **MAC Layer Processor**: Implements CSMA/CA, framing, ARQ retransmissions, and power management.

8. **Power Amplifier (PA)**: Boosts the modulated signal to the required TX power (0 to 20 dBm); efficiency 30–50% for SRCS PAs.

9. **Local Oscillator**: Synthesizes the carrier frequency, typically using a fractional-N PLL locked to a crystal reference.

*(Refer to SECTION 4.1 Mermaid diagram for visual mapping. 7 marks: 1 mark per major block function with brief justification.)*

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)** [7 marks]

**Given:** $f = 2.45$ GHz = 2450 MHz, $P_t = 0$ dBm, $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi, $P_{r,\min} = -70$ dBm, $d = 10$ m.

**(i) FSPL at $d = 10$ m = 0.01 km:**

$$FSPL = 20\log_{10}(0.01) + 20\log_{10}(2450) + 32.44$$

$$FSPL = 20(-2) + 20(3.389) + 32.44$$

$$FSPL = -40 + 67.78 + 32.44 = 60.22 \text{ dB}$$

**(3 marks)**

**(ii) Received Power at 10 m:**

$$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL$$

$$P_r = 0 + 2 + 2 - 60.22 = -56.22 \text{ dBm}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Maximum Range in Free Space:**

For free space, FSPL = $P_t + G_t + G_r - P_{r,\min}$:

$$FSPL_{max} = 0 + 2 + 2 - (-70) = 74 \text{ dB}$$

$$74 = 20\log_{10}(d_{max}) + 20\log_{10}(2450) + 32.44$$

$$20\log_{10}(d_{max}) = 74 - 67.78 - 32.44 = -26.22$$

$$\log_{10}(d_{max}) = -1.311 \quad \text{where } d \text{ in km}$$

$$d_{max} = 10^{-1.311} = 0.0489 \text{ km} = 48.9 \text{ m}$$

$$d_{max} \approx 49 \text{ m}$$

**(2 marks)**

---

#### **Question B — Choice B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **(a)** Compare and contrast the following short-range wireless technologies in a tabular form: NFC, BLE, ZigBee, and Wi-Fi 6. Cover frequency band, range, data rate, modulation, typical applications, and power consumption. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the Friis transmission equation starting from the definition of isotropic power density. State all assumptions clearly and convert the final result into the Free Space Path Loss (FSPL) form in dB. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)** [7 marks]

| Parameter | **NFC** | **BLE** | **ZigBee** | **Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency** | 13.56 MHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz (also 868/915 MHz) | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz |
| **Range** | $\leq$ 10 cm | $\leq$ 100 m | $\leq$ 100 m | $\leq$ 100 m |
| **Data Rate** | 106–424 kbps | 1–2 Mbps | 250 kbps | Up to 9.6 Gbps |
| **Modulation** | ASK | GFSK | O-QPSK | OFDMA (1024-QAM) |
| **Power** | < 500 mW | < 100 mW | < 100 mW | < 200 mW |
| **Topology** | P2P | Star / P2P | Mesh / Star | Star (AP-centric) |
| **Applications** | Payments, ID | Wearables, beacons | Smart home, sensors | Broadband, video |

*(1 mark per row × 7 rows capped = 7 marks)*

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)** [7 marks]

**Assumptions:**
1. Free-space propagation (no obstructions, reflections, or multipath).
2. Far-field operation ($d \gg \lambda$, typically $d \geq 2\lambda$).
3. Antennas are aligned and polarization-matched.
4. Lossless, isotropic reference radiators for the derivation baseline.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1** — Isotropic power density at distance $d$:

$$S_{iso} = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

**Step 2** — Include transmit antenna gain $G_t$:

$$S = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

**Step 3** — Receive antenna effective aperture $A_e = \dfrac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$:

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

**Step 4** — Path Loss in linear form:

$$PL = \frac{P_t}{P_r} = \frac{(4\pi d)^2}{G_t G_r \lambda^2}$$

**Step 5** — Convert to dB:

$$PL_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{(4\pi d)^2}{G_t G_r \lambda^2}\right) = 20\log_{10}(4\pi d) - 10\log_{10}(G_t G_r) - 20\log_{10}(\lambda)$$

**Step 6** — Substitute $\lambda = c/f$ and apply unit conversion ($d$ in km, $f$ in MHz):

$$PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44 - G_t - G_r$$

For unity-gain isotropic antennas ($G_t = G_r = 0$ dBi):

$$\boxed{FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44}$$

where $d$ is in km and $f$ in MHz. **(7 marks: 2 for assumptions + 3 for derivation + 2 for final FSPL form)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in SRCS Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Students frequently plug $d$ in meters into the FSPL equation expecting $f$ in MHz. Always convert: $d$ in **km** and $f$ in **MHz**, OR $d$ in meters and $f$ in GHz with the constant becoming $20\log_{10}(4\pi/c) = -147.55$.
> 2. **Forgetting Antenna Gains (–1 mark):** Always state whether $G_t, G_r$ are unity or specified. If 2 dBi each, **add them**, do not omit.
> 3. **dBm vs. Watts Confusion (–2 marks):** Use $P[W] = 10^{P[dBm]/10} \times 10^{-3}$ to convert. Mixing units in Friis equation is the **#1 mark killer**.
> 4. **Missing "free-space" Assumption (–1 mark):** Friis only applies in line-of-sight. In multipath indoor environments, use the **log-distance path loss model** with $n = 3$ to 4.
> 5. **Skipping the Diagram (–2 marks):** A labelled block diagram is **mandatory** for 7-mark architecture questions. KTU examiners explicitly state "with neat block diagram" — missing it forfeits 2 marks automatically.
> 6. **No Final Units (–0.5 to –1 mark):** Always write the **final answer with units** (dBm, m, Mbps). A bare number is incomplete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — SRCS Fundamentals**

- **SRCS Definition**: Wireless systems operating $\leq 100$ m with low power ($\leq 100$ mW) in license-exempt ISM bands.
- **Tier 1 (Near-Field, 0–10 cm)**: NFC (13.56 MHz), Inductive RFID — operate via magnetic coupling, not radiating waves.
- **Tier 2 (PAN/BAN, 0–10 m)**: BLE, Bluetooth Classic, ZigBee, ANT+, IrDA — predominantly 2.4 GHz ISM.
- **Tier 3 (LAN, 10–100 m)**: Wi-Fi (802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax/be), UWB (802.15.4z), Li-Fi (802.11bb).
- **BLE** = Bluetooth Low Energy, modulation = GFSK, $f = 2.4$ GHz, hop rate = 1600 hops/s, range $\sim 50$ m.
- **ZigBee** = Mesh-topology, 802.15.4 PHY/MAC, O-QPSK modulation, 250 kbps, 65,000 node networks.
- **NFC** = 13.56 MHz, ASK modulation, 10 cm range, three modes (reader/writer, peer-to-peer, card emulation).
- **Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax)** = OFDMA + 1024-QAM, 9.6 Gbps peak, operates in 2.4/5/6 GHz bands.
- **UWB** = Impulse radio, 3.1–10.6 GHz, sub-nanosecond pulses, enables cm-accurate ranging (Apple AirTag, car key fobs).
- **Li-Fi** = Visible light communication (400–800 THz), uses LED modulation, secure because light doesn't penetrate walls.
- **FSPL Formula (dB)**: $20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44$ for unity gain.
- **Friis Equation**: $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ — valid only in **far-field** and **free-space**.
- **Path Loss Exponent**: $n = 2$ (free space), 2.7–3.5 (indoor LOS), 3–5 (obstructed indoor).
- **Maximum Range Derivation**: $d_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{P_t G_t G_r}{P_{r,\min}}}$.
- **Typical Bluetooth Class 2 Range**: $\sim 50$ m at 0 dBm TX power with -70 dBm RX sensitivity at 2.45 GHz.
- **ISM Bands (Unlicensed)**: 13.56 MHz, 433 MHz, 902–928 MHz, 2.4–2.4835 GHz, 5.15–5.825 GHz, 60 GHz.
- **Application Mapping**: Payments → NFC; Wearables → BLE; Smart Home → ZigBee/Thread; Broadband → Wi-Fi; Ranging → UWB; Secure Indoor → Li-Fi.
- **Security Standards**: AES-128 (ZigBee), LE Secure Connections (BLE), WPA3 (Wi-Fi), ISO 14443-4 (NFC).
- **Common 14-Mark Structure**: Part (a) = Block diagram + explanation (7 marks); Part (b) = Numerical problem with Friis/FSPL (7 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Background and Current Scenario
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Background and Current Scenario # MODULE 2 — BACKGROUND AND CURRENT SCENARIO ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > The *Background and Current Scenario* of modern communication systems refers to the **chronological evolution of telecommunication technologies** from first-generation (1G) analog systems to the present-day fifth-generation (5G) and emerging sixth-generation (6G) networks, along with the **driving forces** (spectrum scarcity, data explosion, latency demands, IoT proliferation) that shaped each generational leap. It establishes the *engineering rationale* for adopting techniques such as **OFDM, MIMO, cognitive radio, and mmWave communications** in contemporary transceiver design. ### Conceptual Analogy — "From Smoke Signals to Smartphones" Imagine communication as humanity's attempt to shout across an ever-widening canyon. The early Greeks used fire-lit torches to send crude signals; each technological breakthrough is essentially a new way to make our voice **louder, clearer, faster, and able to reach more listeners simultaneously**. Modern communication systems are the *cumulative result* of a 150-year engineering effort to send **more bits per second (bps)** across **less radio spectrum (Hz)**, with **lower error probability** and **minimum power consumption**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Metrics Driving Each Generation:** > - **Spectral Efficiency** $(\eta)$ — bits transmitted per second per Hz of bandwidth (bps/Hz). > - **Peak Data Rate** $(R)$ — measured in **Mbps** (4G) or **Gbps** (5G). > - **End-to-End Latency** — round-trip delay in **milliseconds (ms)**. > - **Connection Density** — devices supported per **km²**. > - **Energy Efficiency** — joules consumed per bit transmitted (J/bit). ### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded) - Speed of light in free space: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** - Carrier frequencies for 5G FR2 (mmWave): **24 GHz – 52 GHz** - Sub-6 GHz 5G band (FR1): **450 MHz – 6 GHz** - Shannon's channel capacity bound: $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ where $C$ is in **bps** and $B$ in **Hz** - ITU-R minimum 5G peak data rate target: **20 Gbps** (downlink) / **10 Gbps** (uplink) - 1G voice bandwidth requirement: **30 kHz** per channel --- ## 1.2 Why Study the "Background and Current Scenario"? > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 scheme places this topic at the **gateway of Module 2** because every subsequent module (cellular architecture, OFDM, MIMO, channel coding) is *historically motivated*. Without understanding **why** analog gave way to digital, or **why** 4G LTE could not satisfy IoT traffic, a student cannot justify the engineering choices made in 5G NR (New Radio) or proposed 6G architectures. The **three pillars** of the current scenario in modern communications are: 1. **Explosive mobile data growth** — Cisco's VNI projects global mobile data traffic will reach **\textasciitilde 77 exabytes/month by 2025** (≈ 5× of 2020 levels). 2. **Spectrum scarcity** — usable sub-6 GHz spectrum is finite; this drives the move to **mmWave, terahertz (THz)**, and **dynamic spectrum sharing (cognitive radio)**. 3. **Vertical-specific Quality of Service (QoS)** — autonomous vehicles demand **<1 ms** latency, while smart-meter IoT demands **15+ year battery life**, forcing a *one-size-fits-one* network design philosophy. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Generations — Structured Analytical Breakdown ### 2.1.1 First Generation (1G) — Analog Voice (1980s) - **Technology:** Advanced Mobile Phone System (AMPS), Nordic NMT, TACS. - **Modulation:** Frequency Modulation (FM) with Frequency Division Multiple Access (FDMA). - **Channel Bandwidth:** **30 kHz** per user. - **Why it became obsolete:** No encryption, poor spectral efficiency (\textasciitilde 0.1 bps/Hz), susceptibility to eavesdropping, no data services. ### 2.1.2 Second Generation (2G) — Digital Voice & SMS (1990s) - **Technology:** GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications), IS-95 (CDMA-One). - **Modulation:** GMSK (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying), QPSK. - **Multiple Access:** TDMA (GSM) and CDMA (IS-95). - **Data Service:** GPRS (2.5G) at **\textasciitilde 114 kbps**, EDGE (2.75G) at **\textasciitilde 384 kbps**. - **Innovation:** Digital encryption (A5/1), SIM card introduction. ### 2.1.3 Third Generation (3G) — Mobile Internet (2000s) - **Technology:** UMTS (WCDMA), CDMA2000, TD-SCDMA. - **Modulation:** QPSK, 16-QAM. - **Key Innovation:** Spread-spectrum CDMA, soft handover, packet-switched core. - **Peak Data Rate:** **2 Mbps** (initial) → **14.4 Mbps** (HSPA+). - **Why important:** First *true mobile internet* generation enabling video calling, mobile TV. ### 2.1.4 Fourth Generation (4G) — All-IP Broadband (2010s) - **Technology:** LTE, LTE-Advanced, WiMAX. - **Modulation:** OFDMA downlink, SC-FDMA uplink, up to **64-QAM**. - **Multiple Access:** OFDMA + MIMO (up to 4×4). - **Peak Data Rate:** **1 Gbps** (theoretical) / **100 Mbps** (practical). - **Architectural Shift:** Flat all-IP SAE (System Architecture Evolution) — circuit-switched core abandoned. - **Latency:** **\textasciitilde 10 ms** (radio access network). ### 2.1.5 Fifth Generation (5G) — Connected Everything (2020s) - **Technology:** 5G NR (New Radio), NSA + SA deployment. - **Modulation:** OFDMA with CP, up to **256-QAM** in FR1 and FR2. - **Waveform Flexibility:** Numerology-based subcarrier spacing (15/30/60/120/240 kHz) — supports eMBB, URLLC, mMTC. - **Peak Data Rate:** **20 Gbps** (downlink) / **10 Gbps** (uplink). - **Latency:** **< 1 ms** (URLLC). - **Connection Density:** **10⁶ devices / km²** (mMTC). - **Innovations:** Massive MIMO (64–256 antennas), beamforming, mmWave (24–52 GHz), network slicing, edge computing. ### 2.1.6 Sixth Generation (6G) — Research Horizon (2030+) - **Projected Spectrum:** Sub-THz (**100 GHz – 1 THz**), visible light communication (VLC), satellite mega-constellations (LEO). - **Targets:** **1 Tbps** peak rate, **sub-0.1 ms** latency, **AI-native air interface**, **3D coverage** (airborne/space-ground integrated network). - **Research Challenges:** THz channel modeling, ultra-massive MIMO (UM-MIMO), reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RIS), quantum-secured links. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates every equation a student will need for derivation and numerical problems on this topic. **Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.** | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Range | Physical Significance | |---|-------------------|------------|---------------|------------------------| | 1 | Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bps, Hz, dimensionless SNR | Upper bound on reliable data rate | | 2 | Spectral Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{R}{B}$ | bps/Hz | Bits squeezed per Hz of spectrum | | 3 | Free-Space Path Loss | $\text{FSPL} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | dB (d in km, f in MHz) | mmWave link budget reference | | 4 | Link Budget | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{PL} - L_{misc}$ | dBm | Receiver power calculation | | 5 | Latency Constraint (URLLC) | $T_{total} = T_{proc} + T_{queue} + T_{tx} \le 1 \text{ ms}$ | ms | 5G URLLC budget | | 6 | OFDM Subcarrier Spacing | $\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{T_{sym}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{N_{CP}/N_{FFT}}$ | Hz | 5G NR flexible numerology | | 7 | Cell Capacity (MIMO) | $C_{cell} = N_{stream} \cdot B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}_{eff})$ | bps | Massive MIMO gain | | 8 | Connection Density | $\rho_{conn} = \dfrac{N_{dev}}{A_{cell}}$ | devices/km² | mMTC metric | | 9 | Energy per Bit | $E_b/N_0 = \dfrac{S}{(R/N)}$ | dimensionless | Link performance figure of merit | | 10 | Noise Floor | $N = kTB$ | W | k = $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K | --- ## 2.3 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering - **Shannon's equation** establishes a *hard physical limit*. Engineers can never exceed $C$, so all of 5G/6G research aims to *approach* this limit by improving SNR (via beamforming) or $B$ (via mmWave). - **FSPL** in dB is the *first calculation* a student makes when designing a 5G mmWave backhaul link; the **+32.44** constant applies only when d is in **km** and f in **MHz**. - **MIMO cell capacity** is *linear* in the number of spatial streams — this is precisely why 4×4 MIMO quadruples 4G throughput without additional spectrum. - **Connection density** is the metric that drove the design of NB-IoT and LTE-M in 4G, and mMTC in 5G. > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "Compare 4G and 5G," always quote the **three IMT-2020 KPIs**: (i) peak data rate, (ii) user-plane latency, (iii) connection density. These are the official ITU-R M.2410 metrics. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shannon Capacity from First Principles **Goal:** Derive the *maximum achievable data rate* $C$ of an ideal band-limited AWGN channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz and signal-to-noise ratio $\text{SNR}$. ### Step 1 — Statement of the Channel Model Consider a discrete-time AWGN channel with input $X$, output $Y$, and additive white Gaussian noise $N \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$: $$ Y = X + N $$ ### Step 2 — Bandwidth and Sampling By the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem**, a signal of bandwidth $B$ Hz can be represented by $2B$ independent samples per second. Each sample can convey a *distinguishable level*. ### Step 3 — Number of Distinguishable Levels The signal power is $S$, and noise power in bandwidth $B$ is $N = \sigma^2 B$. The peak-to-peak signal range is divided by the RMS noise $\sigma$, giving a *M*-level discrete alphabet: $$ M = \sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}} = \sqrt{1 + \text{SNR}} $$ *Reasoning:* The number of distinguishable amplitude steps grows with the square root of $(1 + \text{SNR})$ because the signal voltage and noise voltage add in quadrature. ### Step 4 — Bits per Sample A symbol alphabet of size $M$ conveys $\log_2(M)$ bits per symbol. Therefore: $$ \text{bits per sample} = \log_2\!\left(\sqrt{1 + \text{SNR}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) $$ ### Step 5 — Samples per Second The Nyquist rate provides $2B$ samples per second, so the total data rate $C$ becomes: $$ C = 2B \cdot \frac{1}{2}\log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) $$ ### Step 6 — Final Form (Shannon's Law) $$ \boxed{\,C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})\quad [\text{bps}]\,} $$ This is the *unattainable upper bound*; practical codes (LDPC, Polar) approach within **0.1 dB** of it in modern 5G NR. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spectral Efficiency Evolution Across Generations **Goal:** Show quantitatively how each generation improved $\eta$ (bps/Hz). ### Step 1 — Define Spectral Efficiency For a peak rate $R$ over channel bandwidth $B$: $$ \eta = \frac{R}{B} \quad \text{[bps/Hz]} $$ ### Step 2 — Plug in Generational Values - 1G (AMPS): $R = 9.6$ kbps analog voice digitized at best, $B = 30$ kHz → $\eta_{1G} \approx 0.32$ bps/Hz - 2G (GSM with GPRS): $R = 114$ kbps, $B = 200$ kHz → $\eta_{2G} \approx 0.57$ bps/Hz - 3G (HSPA+): $R = 14.4$ Mbps, $B = 5$ MHz → $\eta_{3G} \approx 2.88$ bps/Hz - 4G (LTE-A, 4×4 MIMO): $R = 600$ Mbps, $B = 20$ MHz → $\eta_{4G} \approx 30$ bps/Hz - 5G (mmWave, 64×64 MIMO, 256-QAM): $R = 20$ Gbps, $B = 800$ MHz (FR2 carrier aggregation) → $\eta_{5G} \approx 25$ bps/Hz peak, but with much **wider bandwidth** in absolute terms. ### Step 3 — Efficiency Multiplier The growth factor from 1G to 4G is: $$ G_{\eta} = \frac{\eta_{4G}}{\eta_{1G}} = \frac{30}{0.32} \approx 93.75 $$ This **\textasciitilde 94× improvement** came from three engineering levers: 1. **Digital modulation** (2G): GMSK, 8-PSK. 2. **Spread spectrum / CDMA** (3G): Code-division orthogonality. 3. **OFDM + MIMO** (4G): Frequency and spatial multiplexing. 4. **Massive MIMO + mmWave + 256-QAM** (5G). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Free-Space Path Loss (Friss Transmission Equation) **Goal:** Derive the dB form of FSPL for line-of-sight (LoS) link budget. ### Step 1 — Isotropic Radiator Power density at distance $d$ from isotropic radiator of power $P_t$: $$ S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \quad [\text{W/m}^2] $$ ### Step 2 — Effective Aperture A receiving antenna of gain $G_r$ has effective aperture: $$ A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi} $$ ### Step 3 — Received Power $$ P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2} $$ ### Step 4 — Convert to Decibels Substitute $\lambda = c/f$ and take $10 \log_{10}(\cdot)$: $$ P_r[dBm] = P_t[dBm] + G_t[dBi] + G_r[dBi] - 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(f) - 32.44 $$ where **d is in km, f in MHz**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Numerical example (5G mmWave at 28 GHz, d = 1 km):** > $$\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(1) + 20\log_{10}(28000) + 32.44 = 0 + 88.94 + 32.44 = \mathbf{121.4 \text{ dB}}$$ > This is the *reason* 5G mmWave cells are **micro/picocells** with ranges of **\textless 500 m**, not the 10 km cells of 4G sub-3 GHz. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Drivers Behind Generational Transitions | Driver | 1G → 2G | 2G → 3G | 3G → 4G | 4G → 5G | |--------|---------|---------|---------|---------| | Primary Need | Voice security & capacity | Mobile internet | Broadband data | IoT + low latency | | Key Limitation Overcome | Analog noise, no encryption | 64 kbps circuit-switch cap | 2 Mbps not enough for video | 10 ms latency too high for V2X | | Spectral Efficiency Gain | ~2× | ~5× | ~10× | ~3× (with 10× bandwidth) | | Switched Domain | Analog → Digital | Circuit → Packet (partial) | Circuit → All-IP | Hardware-centric → Software-defined | | Enabling Tech | TDMA, GMSK | WCDMA | OFDM, MIMO | mmWave, Massive MIMO, Network Slicing | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generational Evolution Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A1G["1G ANALOG 1980s
AMPS FM FDMA
Voice Only 9.6 kbps"] A2G["2G DIGITAL 1990s
GSM GMSK TDMA
SMS 64 kbps"] A2_5G["2.5G GPRS EDGE
Packet Data 384 kbps"] A3G["3G MOBILE WEB 2000s
WCDMA UMTS
Video Call 2 Mbps"] A3_5G["3.5G HSPA PLUS
14.4 Mbps"] A4G["4G ALL IP 2010s
LTE OFDMA MIMO
100 Mbps 10 ms"] A4_5G["4.5G LTE ADVANCED PRO
NB IoT LTE M"] A5G["5G eMBB URLLC mMTC
NR Massive MIMO mmWave
20 Gbps 1 ms"] A5GAdv["5G ADVANCED 2025
AI Native Integrated Sensing"] A6G["6G RESEARCH 2030 PLUS
Sub THz RIS UM MIMO
1 Tbps 0.1 ms"] A1G --> A2G --> A2_5G --> A3G --> A3_5G --> A4G --> A4_5G --> A5G --> A5GAdv --> A6G B1["Voice Service"] -.-> A1G B2["SMS Service"] -.-> A2G B3["Mobile Internet"] -.-> A3G B4["Video Streaming"] -.-> A4G B5["IoT V2X AR VR"] -.-> A5G B6["Holographic Comms Brain Computer Interface"] -.-> A6G C1["Spectrum 800 900 MHz"] -.-> A1G C2["Spectrum 900 1800 MHz"] -.-> A2G C3["Spectrum 2 GHz UMTS bands"] -.-> A3G C4["Spectrum 700 MHz to 2.6 GHz"] -.-> A4G C5["Spectrum 3.5 GHz 28 GHz 39 GHz"] -.-> A5G C6["Spectrum 100 GHz to 1 THz VLC"] -.-> A6G ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture — 5G Use-Case Triangle (ITU-R IMT-2020) ```mermaid flowchart TD IMT["5G IMT 2020 VISION
UNIFIED FRAMEWORK"] --> EMBB["eMBB

77 EB per month by 2025"] DRIVERS --> D2["Spectrum Scarcity
Sub 6 GHz Congested"] DRIVERS --> D3["Vertical Specific QoS
URLLC eMBB mMTC"] DRIVERS --> D4["Energy and Cost
5G Energy per bit down 90 percent vs 4G"] D1 --> SOL1["mmWave and Carrier Aggregation"] D2 --> SOL2["Cognitive Radio and Dynamic Spectrum Sharing"] D3 --> SOL3["Network Slicing and SDN NFV"] D4 --> SOL4["AI Native Network Optimisation"] SOL1 --> OUTCOME["5G NR DEPLOYMENT"] SOL2 --> OUTCOME SOL3 --> OUTCOME SOL4 --> OUTCOME OUTCOME --> FUTURE["6G RESEARCH HORIZON
Terahertz RIS Holographic"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper] **"Briefly explain the evolution of mobile communication from 1G to 5G highlighting the modulation and multiple access techniques used in each generation."** **Model Answer (3 Marks — 60–80 words):** | Generation | Period | Modulation | Multiple Access | Peak Rate | |------------|--------|------------|------------------|-----------| | 1G | 1980s | FM | FDMA | \textasciitilde 9.6 kbps | | 2G | 1990s | GMSK, 8-PSK | TDMA / CDMA | 64 kbps – 384 kbps | | 3G | 2000s | QPSK, 16-QAM | WCDMA | 2 Mbps – 14.4 Mbps | | 4G | 2010s | QPSK – 64-QAM | OFDMA + MIMO | 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps | | 5G | 2020s | Up to 256-QAM | OFDMA + Massive MIMO + mmWave | 20 Gbps | The transition from analog to digital (2G), packet-switching (3G), all-IP OFDM (4G), and finally flexible-numerology NR with beamforming (5G) was driven by the demand for higher data rates, lower latency, and massive device connectivity. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **"List and define the three primary 5G use-case categories as defined by ITU-R IMT-2020."** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **eMBB (Enhanced Mobile Broadband):** Provides very high peak data rates (up to 20 Gbps downlink) and uniform coverage for bandwidth-hungry applications such as 4K/8K video, VR/AR, and FWA. 2. **URLLC (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication):** Targets **< 1 ms** air-interface latency with **99.999%** reliability for mission-critical applications like autonomous driving, industrial automation, and remote surgery. 3. **mMTC (Massive Machine-Type Communication):** Supports up to **10⁶ devices per km²** with low data rate and deep coverage for IoT sensors, smart metering, and smart cities. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **"With the help of a comparative table, discuss the evolution of mobile communication systems from 1G to 5G. Explain the three IMT-2020 usage scenarios and the key enabling technologies that make 5G NR a paradigm shift over 4G LTE."** #### Part (a) — Evolution 1G to 5G (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** The evolution of cellular systems can be understood as a series of engineering responses to **three simultaneous pressures**: (i) demand for higher data rate, (ii) demand for more simultaneous users, and (iii) demand for new services (voice → SMS → internet → IoT). **1G (1980s — Analog Voice):** The Advanced Mobile Phone System (AMPS) used **30 kHz** FDMA channels with analog FM. It suffered from crosstalk, eavesdropping, and limited capacity (\textasciitilde 0.1 bps/Hz). **2G (1990s — Digital Voice + SMS):** GSM introduced **GMSK modulation** with **TDMA**, supporting 8 users per 200 kHz carrier. It added **digital encryption (A5/1)** and the **SIM card** concept. GPRS (2.5G) introduced packet switching on the radio interface, achieving **\textasciitilde 114 kbps**. **3G (2000s — Mobile Internet):** UMTS adopted **WCDMA** with **5 MHz** carrier bandwidth, supporting **2 Mbps** peak rate. **Soft handover** and **spread-spectrum processing** improved capacity and link quality. HSPA+ pushed rates to **14.4 Mbps**. **4G (2010s — All-IP Broadband):** LTE abandoned circuit switching entirely. **OFDMA** downlink with **SC-FDMA** uplink, combined with **MIMO (up to 4×4)**, delivered **1 Gbps** theoretical and **\textasciitilde 100 Mbps** practical rate at **\textasciitilde 10 ms** latency. The **flat SAE (System Architecture Evolution)** removed the legacy SS7 core. **5G (2020s — Connected Everything):** 5G NR introduces **flexible OFDM numerology** (15/30/60/120/240 kHz subcarrier spacing), **massive MIMO (up to 256 antennas)**, **mmWave bands (24–52 GHz)**, and **network slicing**. Peak rate **20 Gbps**, latency **< 1 ms**, and density **10⁶/km²** are the ITU targets. #### Part (b) — IMT-2020 Use-Cases & 5G Enabling Technologies (7 Marks) **Three IMT-2020 Usage Scenarios:** 1. **eMBB** — bandwidth-hungry broadband (4K/8K video, VR/AR, FWA). 2. **URLLC** — mission-critical (autonomous vehicles V2X, industrial robotics, remote surgery) with **< 1 ms** latency and **99.999%** reliability. 3. **mMTC** — massive IoT deployments (smart sensors, asset tracking) with **10⁶ devices/km²** and **15-year battery life**. **Key 5G Enabling Technologies:** - **mmWave Communication:** Unlocks 10× bandwidth in 24–52 GHz band; requires dense small cells and beamforming. - **Massive MIMO:** 64–256 element antenna arrays at the base station create highly directive beams, improving SNR by **20–30 dB** and enabling spatial multiplexing of many users on the same time-frequency resource. - **Flexible OFDM Numerology:** Subcarrier spacings of 15, 30, 60, 120, 240 kHz allow 5G NR to efficiently serve diverse latency/coverage profiles (e.g., 240 kHz for low-latency mmWave, 15 kHz for sub-6 GHz wide-area). - **Network Slicing:** A single physical 5G network is partitioned into multiple logical networks (eMBB slice, URLLC slice, mMTC slice) using **SDN and NFV**, each with isolated QoS. - **Edge Computing (MEC):** Computation moves to the radio access network edge, reducing backhaul latency and enabling URLLC applications. - **Beam Management & Beamforming:** Both analog and digital beamforming focus energy into narrow beams, overcoming the high **path loss** of mmWave (e.g., **\textasciitilde 121 dB** at 28 GHz over 1 km, as derived in Section 3.3). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. **Do not** confuse **4G LTE** and **5G NR** numerology. 4G is *fixed* at 15 kHz subcarrier spacing; 5G NR is *flexible*. Examiners specifically test this distinction for 1–2 marks. > 2. **Do not** claim 5G is "100× faster than 4G" without quoting the **same deployment scenario**. The 20 Gbps peak is achieved *only* in mmWave FR2 with carrier aggregation. In sub-6 GHz FR1, 5G peak rate is closer to **1.7 Gbps**. > 3. **Do not** omit **massive MIMO antenna count** when describing 5G; merely saying "MIMO" earns partial credit. Specify **64T64R, 128T128R, 256T256R** arrays. --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks) **"Explain the current scenario of global mobile data growth. Discuss the spectrum scarcity problem and the engineering solutions adopted in 5G and emerging 6G systems to address it. Include Shannon's capacity equation and the FSPL formula in your answer."** #### Part (a) — Mobile Data Growth & Spectrum Scarcity (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** The **current scenario** of global telecommunications is dominated by an exponential surge in mobile data demand. According to **Cisco's Visual Networking Index**, global mobile data traffic grew from **\textasciitilde 12 EB/month in 2017** to **\textasciitilde 77 EB/month by 2025**, driven by: - Smartphone penetration (\textgreater 80% in developed nations). - Video content accounting for **\textgreater 75%** of mobile traffic. - IoT device proliferation (projected **75 billion** connected devices by 2030). **The Spectrum Scarcity Problem:** The radio spectrum below 6 GHz is *fully allocated* on a primary-user basis in most countries. Traditional sub-3 GHz cellular bands (700–2700 MHz) are congested, with limited room for new wideband carriers. Shannon's theorem quantifies the engineering dilemma: $$ C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) $$ For a fixed SNR, the only way to increase $C$ is to increase $B$ — but $B$ is *scarce* below 6 GHz. This motivated the search for higher-frequency spectrum. **Engineering Solutions:** 1. **mmWave Bands (24–52 GHz):** Offers **\textgreater 10 GHz** of contiguous bandwidth, enabling multi-Gbps throughput. Free-space path loss is, however, severe: $$ \text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44 $$ At 28 GHz, 1 km link → **121.4 dB loss** (versus \textasciitilde 100 dB at 2.6 GHz). This forces 5G mmWave to deploy **dense small cells** every **100–200 m** in urban areas. 2. **Sub-6 GHz Refarming (3.5 GHz, CBRS, C-band):** Re-allocates mid-band spectrum previously used by satellites and military radar. #### Part (b) — 6G Horizons and Path Beyond Shannon (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** Even with mmWave, Shannon's limit at $\text{SNR} = 20$ dB and $B = 800$ MHz yields: $$ C = 800 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1 + 100) = 800 \times 10^6 \cdot 6.66 \approx 5.33 \text{ Gbps} $$ To surpass this in 6G, three frontier research directions are pursued: 1. **Terahertz (THz) Communications (100 GHz – 1 THz):** Provides **tens of GHz** contiguous bandwidth, supporting projected **1 Tbps** peak rates. Challenges include molecular absorption, ultra-massive MIMO, and novel transceiver materials (graphene, plasmonic antennas). 2. **Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS):** Software-controlled meta-material panels that *passively reflect* signals with adjustable phase, creating a *smart radio environment* that effectively boosts SNR without additional transmit power. 3. **AI-Native Air Interface:** Machine learning models replace traditional protocol stacks for **channel estimation, beam management, and resource allocation**, reducing processing latency and improving spectral efficiency by **15–30%** in simulations. 4. **Visible Light Communication (VLC) & Optical Wireless:** Uses **400–800 THz** LED light spectrum, providing massive bandwidth and EM-interference-free indoor communications. 5. **Non-Orthogonal Multiple Access (NOMA) & Cell-Free Massive MIMO:** NOMA superposes multiple users in the power domain; cell-free MIMO distributes antennas geographically, eliminating cell-edge bottlenecks. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > 1. **Memorize** the numerical value of FSPL constant **+32.44 dB** *and* its units dependence (d in km, f in MHz). Mixing km with GHz, or m with MHz, will lose full marks. > 2. **Distinguish** spectral efficiency (bps/Hz) from peak data rate (bps). A 5G cell with 100 MHz bandwidth and 30 bps/Hz efficiency has 3 Gbps aggregate — *not* 30 Gbps. > 3. **6G question caution:** Examiners expect *research-horizon awareness* but penalize *unsubstantiated claims*. Always tie 6G targets to **ITU-R IMT-2030 framework** (currently in development) rather than vague "1 Tbps" statements. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Background and Current Scenario" (Module 2)** - [x] **Six generations:** 1G analog FM/FDMA → 2G GSM GMSK/TDMA → 3G UMTS WCDMA → 4G LTE OFDMA/MIMO → 5G NR flexible numerology + mmWave + massive MIMO → 6G THz + RIS + AI. - [x] **Shannon's Capacity Equation:** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ — the absolute physical limit on data rate. - [x] **Spectral Efficiency ($\eta = R/B$):** 1G ≈ 0.1 bps/Hz; 4G ≈ 30 bps/Hz; 5G peak \textasciitilde 30 bps/Hz with much wider bandwidth. - [x] **FSPL Formula:** $\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ dB (d in km, f in MHz). - [x] **5G ITU-R IMT-2020 KPIs:** Peak rate **20 Gbps** DL / **10 Gbps** UL, latency **< 1 ms**, density **10⁶ devices/km²**, mobility up to **500 km/h**. - [x] **Three 5G Use-Cases:** eMBB, URLLC, mMTC. - [x] **5G NR Frequency Ranges:** FR1 (sub-6 GHz) and FR2 (mmWave 24.25–52.6 GHz). - [x] **5G NR Subcarrier Spacings:** 15, 30, 60, 120, 240 kHz (vs. fixed 15 kHz in 4G LTE). - [x] **6G Frontier Tech:** Terahertz, RIS, UM-MIMO, AI-native air interface, optical wireless, LEO satellite mega-constellations. - [x] **Current Scenario Drivers:** (i) Mobile data explosion (77 EB/month by 2025), (ii) Spectrum scarcity, (iii) Vertical-specific QoS, (iv) Energy and cost optimization. - [x] **6G KPIs:** **1 Tbps** peak, **< 0.1 ms** latency, **AI-driven** network orchestration. - [x] **Remember:** Higher carrier frequency → larger bandwidth → higher path loss → smaller cells → more beamforming gain required. - [x] **Engineering mantra:** "Approach Shannon's limit, exploit spectrum, minimise latency, scale density." > [!TIP] > **One-line Memory Aid:** > *"From analog voice (1G) to digital data (2G), mobile internet (3G), broadband IP (4G), connected intelligence (5G), and ambient holographic intelligence (6G) — each generation conquered one barrier of Shannon's equation: bandwidth, power, latency, or density."*

topics.title: Future Wireless Network Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Future Wireless Network Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Future Wireless Network Requirements** refer to the comprehensive set of Key Performance Indicators (KPIs), architectural constraints, spectrum needs, and service-level specifications mandated by international standardisation bodies (ITU-R, 3GPP) for next-generation mobile communication systems — specifically **5G (IMT-2020)**, its evolution (**5G-Advanced / IMT-2020 Extension**), and the research frontier **6G (IMT-2030)**.

These requirements are formally codified in the ITU-R **M.2410** document for 5G and the **IMT-2030 Framework Recommendation** for 6G, and are organised into three primary usage scenarios for 5G and four expanded usage scenarios for 6G.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> In Module 2 of PERAT753, this topic bridges the gap between legacy 4G LTE capabilities and emerging 5G/6G architectures. Students must internalise the *quantitative* performance targets (Gbps data rates, sub-millisecond latency, 10⁶ devices/km² density) rather than treat them as vague "faster, better" slogans.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of 5G (IMT-2020)

The ITU-R specification groups future wireless requirements into three foundational usage scenarios:

1. **eMBB (enhanced Mobile BroadBand)** — Extreme data rates, high capacity, and seamless user experience for applications like 4K/8K video streaming, VR/AR, and fixed wireless access.
2. **URLLC (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communications)** — Sub-millisecond latency with 99.999% (five-nines) reliability for mission-critical applications such as autonomous driving, industrial automation, and remote surgery.
3. **mMTC (massive Machine-Type Communications)** — Support for at least 10⁶ devices per km² for IoT, smart cities, and massive sensor deployments.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Highway Analogy**
> 
> Imagine a city's road network:
> - **4G** is a 4-lane highway built for passenger cars moving at moderate speeds. It works well for commuters (smartphones browsing, video calls).
> - **Future networks (5G/6G)** must simultaneously serve three radically different travellers on the same road infrastructure:
>   1. **Formula-1 race cars** (eMBB) — needing 20 Gbps lanes with zero packet loss.
>   2. **Ambulances carrying organs for transplant** (URLLC) — needing 1 ms response time and absolute reliability, even if it means fewer lanes.
>   3. **Millions of tiny delivery robots** (mMTC) — each moving slowly, but the sheer quantity of robots (10⁶/km²) means we need a fundamentally different signalling system.
> 
> Building one road for all three is the **defining engineering challenge** of future wireless systems.

## 1.4 Standardised Quantitative Targets

| Metric | 4G (LTE-A) Baseline | 5G Target (IMT-2020) | 6G Vision (IMT-2030) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Peak Data Rate | **1 Gbps** | **20 Gbps** (DL), 10 Gbps (UL) | **1 Tbps** |
| User-Experienced Rate | 10 Mbps | **100 Mbps** (DL), 50 Mbps (UL) | **1 Gbps** |
| Latency (User Plane) | 50 ms | **1 ms** (URLLC target) | **0.1 ms** (sub-millisecond) |
| Device Density | 10⁵/km² | **10⁶/km²** | **10⁷–10⁸/km²** |
| Mobility Support | 350 km/h | **500 km/h** | **1000 km/h** (high-speed trains/aerospace) |
| Spectrum Efficiency | 1× baseline | **3×** 4G | **5–10×** 5G |
| Reliability | 99.9% (three-nines) | **99.999%** (five-nines) | **99.99999%** (seven-nines) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant to Remember:** The light travel time through **1 km of optical fibre** is approximately **5 µs** (microseconds). Wireless sub-millisecond latency targets are therefore *tighter* than the propagation delay in a short fibre link — a profound engineering constraint that drives edge-computing architectures.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Each Requirement

### 2.1.1 Peak Data Rate (Why 20 Gbps?)

The peak data rate is the maximum achievable data rate under ideal conditions (single user, all radio resources allocated). It is derived from Shannon's capacity bound with practical enhancements:

$$C_{peak} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} B_i \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SINR}_i) \cdot \nu_i$$

where $N$ is the number of aggregated component carriers, $B_i$ is the bandwidth of carrier $i$, $\text{SINR}_i$ is the signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio, and $\nu_i$ is the spectral efficiency.

> **Why 20 Gbps?** 8K video streaming at 120 fps requires ~80 Mbps; immersive VR/AR (180° field-of-view) requires ~1 Gbps per eye (i.e., 2 Gbps per user). With carrier aggregation across mmWave (28 GHz, 26 GHz), sub-6 GHz, and low-band spectrum, the aggregated peak rate reaches the 20 Gbps target.

### 2.1.2 Latency (Why 1 ms?)

User-plane latency is the time from when a source sends a packet via the radio protocol stack until the destination receives it. It comprises:

$$T_{latency} = T_{TX} + T_{propagation} + T_{processing} + T_{queuing} + T_{HARQ}$$

For 5G URLLC, the target of **1 ms one-way** implies:

- Transmission Time Interval (TTI) reduced from 1 ms (LTE) to **0.125 ms** (1 OFDM symbol at 15 kHz subcarrier spacing, scaled numerology).
- Mini-slot scheduling (2, 4, or 7 OFDM symbols) to bypass waiting for a full subframe.
- Flexible numerology: subcarrier spacing (SCS) of 30 kHz, 60 kHz, 120 kHz, or 240 kHz for shorter symbol durations.

> **Why 1 ms?** Tactile Internet applications (haptic feedback, tele-surgery) require round-trip latencies below ~10 ms for stable control loops. With 5 ms allocated to the network core and 5 ms to the radio interface, the **one-way user-plane latency of 1 ms** is the remaining budget.

### 2.1.3 Device Density (Why 10⁶/km²?)

Massive IoT scenarios (smart cities, asset tracking, environmental sensors) require connection densities orders of magnitude higher than 4G. The link budget analysis for mMTC uses:

$$\text{Devices per km}^2 = \frac{\text{Available Resource Elements}}{\text{Resource Elements per device per hour}}$$

Access schemes such as **grant-free random access** (pre-configured resource elements) and **NOMA (Non-Orthogonal Multiple Access)** enable this density without exhausting control signalling.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Units |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| 1 | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SINR})$ | Shannon channel capacity | bits/s |
| 2 | $\text{Spectral Efficiency} = \frac{C}{B}$ | Bits per Hz per second | bps/Hz |
| 3 | $T_{symbol} = \frac{1}{\Delta f}$ | OFDM symbol duration = inverse of subcarrier spacing | seconds |
| 4 | $N_{subcarriers} = \frac{B}{\Delta f}$ | Number of usable subcarriers in bandwidth $B$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{Path Loss (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.45$ | Free-space path loss (Friis) | dB |
| 6 | $\text{Device Density} = \frac{N_{devices}}{A_{coverage}}$ | Devices per unit area | devices/km² |
| 7 | $E_b/N_0 = \text{SNR} \cdot \frac{B}{R_b}$ | Energy per bit to noise spectral density | dB |
| 8 | $T_{latency} \geq \frac{2d}{c}$ | Light-speed propagation lower bound for distance $d$ | seconds |
| 9 | $N_{antennas} \approx \frac{2d}{\lambda}$ | Half-wavelength spaced array aperture for length $d$ | count |
| 10 | $\text{QoS Class Identifier (QCI)} \rightarrow$ 5QI mapping | Latency / reliability / priority mapping for bearers | table-driven |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pitfall:** In KTU exams, students often write "5G has low latency" without specifying that the 1 ms target applies *only* to the **URLLC slice* and is the *one-way user-plane* delay, not the round-trip time.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These requirements directly translate to design choices in:

- **Radio Access Network (RAN):** Massive MIMO (64–256 antenna elements), beamforming, millimetre-wave (mmWave) deployment.
- **Core Network:** Service-Based Architecture (SBA), Network Slicing, Multi-access Edge Computing (MEC) for sub-millisecond processing.
- **Spectrum Policy:** Re-farming of sub-3 GHz bands, opening of mmWave (24–52 GHz), exploration of sub-THz (100–300 GHz) and THz for 6G.
- **AI/ML Integration:** Self-organising networks (SON), predictive handover, AI-driven beam management.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Peak Data Rate from 5G Numerology

**Problem (KTU Typical):** Calculate the theoretical peak downlink data rate for a 5G NR system using:
- Bandwidth: 100 MHz (single component carrier)
- Subcarrier spacing (SCS): 30 kHz
- Modulation: 64-QAM (6 bits/symbol)
- MIMO layers: 4×4 (4 spatial streams)
- Overhead: 25% (control, pilots, guard bands)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Determine OFDM symbol duration.**

The OFDM symbol duration is the inverse of the subcarrier spacing. With 30 kHz SCS:

$$T_{symbol} = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{30 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz}}$$

$$T_{symbol} = 33.33 \ \mu s$$

Note: A subframe of 1 ms contains $\frac{10^{-3}}{33.33 \times 10^{-6}} = 30$ OFDM symbols.

**Step 2: Compute the number of usable subcarriers per 100 MHz.**

For LTE/5G NR, the total number of Resource Elements (REs) in 100 MHz is the bandwidth divided by the subcarrier spacing:

$$N_{subcarriers} = \frac{B}{\Delta f} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{30 \times 10^3}$$

$$N_{subcarriers} = 3333.33 \approx 3333 \ \text{subcarriers}$$

**Step 3: Compute Resource Elements per subframe.**

A Resource Block (RB) in 5G NR occupies 12 subcarriers × 1 slot (14 symbols for normal CP). Each subframe has 2 slots:

$$N_{RE}^{subframe} = N_{subcarriers} \times 14 = 3333 \times 14$$

$$N_{RE}^{subframe} = 46{,}662 \ \text{REs per subframe}$$

**Step 4: Subtract overhead (control, reference signals, guard).**

With 25% overhead, usable REs are:

$$N_{RE}^{usable} = N_{RE}^{subframe} \times (1 - 0.25)$$

$$N_{RE}^{usable} = 46{,}662 \times 0.75 = 34{,}996.5 \ \text{REs}$$

**Step 5: Compute the bit rate per subframe.**

With 64-QAM (6 bits/RE) and 4 MIMO layers:

$$R_{subframe} = N_{RE}^{usable} \times \log_2(M) \times N_{layers}$$

$$R_{subframe} = 34{,}996.5 \times 6 \times 4$$

$$R_{subframe} = 839{,}916 \ \text{bits per subframe}$$

**Step 6: Compute bits per second (1000 subframes per second).**

$$R_{peak} = R_{subframe} \times 1000$$

$$R_{peak} = 839{,}916 \times 1000 = 8.399 \times 10^8 \ \text{bps}$$

$$R_{peak} \approx 840 \ \text{Mbps}$$

> **Interpretation:** A single 100 MHz carrier at 30 kHz SCS with 64-QAM 4×4 MIMO yields ~840 Mbps. To reach 20 Gbps peak, **carrier aggregation of ~24 such carriers** is required, which is achievable in mmWave (28 GHz) bands where 800 MHz aggregated bandwidths are practical.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Achievable Latency Bound

**Problem:** A 5G URLLC packet must travel from a sensor in a factory to an edge server located 2 km away. Determine whether the 1 ms latency target is feasible, ignoring processing and queuing delays.

**Step 1: Calculate free-space propagation delay.**

Radio waves travel at the speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. The one-way propagation delay for 2 km is:

$$T_{prop} = \frac{d}{c} = \frac{2000 \text{ m}}{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$$

$$T_{prop} = 6.67 \ \mu s$$

**Step 2: Add the OFDM symbol transmission time.**

For 30 kHz SCS, one symbol is 33.33 µs. Even with mini-slot of 2 symbols, the transmission duration is:

$$T_{TX} = 2 \times 33.33 = 66.67 \ \mu s$$

**Step 3: Sum one-way latency components.**

$$T_{one-way} = T_{prop} + T_{TX} = 6.67 + 66.67 = 73.34 \ \mu s$$

**Step 4: Compare to 1 ms budget.**

$$\text{Used: } 73.34 \ \mu s \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{Budget: } 1000 \ \mu s$$

$$\text{Margin: } 1000 - 73.34 = 926.66 \ \mu s$$

> **Conclusion:** The 1 ms target is *physically feasible* with ample margin for processing and queuing. The remaining ~926 µs can be allocated to gNB/DU processing (typically 100–200 µs), fronthaul/midhaul transport, and edge server application processing.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: 4G vs 5G vs 6G

| Parameter | 4G LTE-A | 5G eMBB | 5G URLLC | 5G mMTC | 6G Vision |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| Peak Rate | 1 Gbps | 20 Gbps | 1 Gbps | 100 kbps | 1 Tbps |
| Latency | 50 ms | 4 ms | **1 ms** | 10 s (tolerable) | **0.1 ms** |
| Reliability | 99.9% | 99.9% | **99.999%** | 99.9% | **99.99999%** |
| Density | 10⁵/km² | 10⁵/km² | 10⁵/km² | **10⁶/km²** | 10⁷–10⁸/km² |
| Spectrum | < 6 GHz | sub-6 + mmWave | sub-6 + mmWave | sub-6 GHz | sub-6 + mmWave + **THz** |
| Mobility | 350 km/h | 500 km/h | 500 km/h | stationary | 1000 km/h |
| AI Native | No | Partial | Partial | No | **Yes (AI-Native)** |
| Energy Efficiency | 1× | 3× | 3× | 10× | **100×** |
