# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define mMTC and list two of its use cases. **[3 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**Model Answer:**

**mMTC (massive Machine-Type Communications)** is one of the three primary usage scenarios defined by ITU-R for 5G (IMT-2020). It targets the connection of a very large number of low-cost, low-power devices that transmit small volumes of data sporadically. The key KPI is **device density of 10⁶ devices per km²** with battery life of up to 10 years.

**Two Use Cases:**
1. **Smart City Sensing** — Streetlight controllers, air-quality monitors, and waste-bin level sensors distributed across urban areas.
2. **Asset Tracking in Logistics** — GPS-equipped low-power trackers on shipping containers and parcels for supply-chain visibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Point:** mMTC prioritises *scale and energy efficiency* over data rate. Per-device throughput is often < 100 kbps, but the network must support **a million concurrent connections per square kilometre**.

---

### Question 2: State the URLLC latency and reliability targets for 5G. **[3 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**Model Answer:**

The ITU-R M.2410 specification defines URLLC targets as follows:

- **User-plane latency (one-way):** **1 ms** for the packet to traverse the radio access network from UE to the application server.
- **Reliability:** **99.999%** (i.e., five-nines) success probability for transmitting a 32-byte packet within 1 ms.

**Engineering Implication:** These targets cannot be met by simply reducing frame duration; they require **mini-slot scheduling, grant-free access, multi-connectivity redundancy, and edge computing** to co-locate compute with the radio.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A: 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Style]**

#### (a) Explain the three primary 5G usage scenarios defined by ITU-R. Compare their KPIs. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The ITU-R IMT-2020 vision defines three usage scenarios to unify diverse service requirements under a single 5G standard:

**1. enhanced Mobile BroadBand (eMBB):**

eMBB extends 4G mobile broadband to support data-intensive applications. KPIs include:
- Peak data rate: **20 Gbps downlink**, 10 Gbps uplink
- User-experienced data rate: 100 Mbps DL
- Area traffic capacity: 10 Mbps/m²
- Spectral efficiency: 3× that of 4G

**Use cases:** 4K/8K streaming, VR/AR, fixed wireless access (FWA), stadium/crowd hotspots.

**2. Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communications (URLLC):**

URLLC supports mission-critical applications requiring deterministic, near-instantaneous response. KPIs include:
- **User-plane latency: 1 ms (one-way)**
- **Reliability: 99.999%** for 32-byte packet in 1 ms
- High availability and connection setup in < 10 ms

**Use cases:** Autonomous vehicles (V2X), industrial robotics, remote surgery, smart-grid protection.

**3. massive Machine-Type Communications (mMTC):**

mMTC enables massive-scale IoT deployments. KPIs include:
- **Connection density: 10⁶ devices/km²**
- Low-cost devices (< €5 module cost)
- Long battery life (up to 10 years)
- Coverage enhancement: 164 dB Maximum Coupling Loss (MCL)

**Use cases:** Smart metering, environmental monitoring, asset tracking, wearables.

**Comparative KPI Table:**

| KPI | eMBB | URLLC | mMTC |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| Peak Rate | **20 Gbps** | 1 Gbps | 100 kbps |
| Latency | 4 ms | **1 ms** | 10 s (relaxed) |
| Reliability | 99.9% | **99.999%** | 99.9% |
| Density | 10⁵/km² | 10⁵/km² | **10⁶/km²** |
| Mobility | 500 km/h | 500 km/h | Low (stationary) |

---

#### (b) Derive the peak downlink data rate for a 5G NR system with the following parameters: Bandwidth = 50 MHz, SCS = 60 kHz, modulation = 256-QAM, MIMO layers = 8, overhead = 20%. Comment on how this can be scaled to 20 Gbps. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Symbol duration.**

$$T_{symbol} = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{60 \times 10^3} = 16.67 \ \mu s$$

**Step 2: Number of subcarriers.**

$$N_{subcarriers} = \frac{B}{\Delta f} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{60 \times 10^3} = 833.33 \approx 833$$

**Step 3: Resource Elements per subframe.**

For 60 kHz SCS, a subframe is 1 ms, with each slot containing 14 OFDM symbols. A subframe has 2 slots = 28 symbols:

$$N_{RE}^{subframe} = 833 \times 14 \times 2 = 23{,}324 \ \text{REs}$$

(For simplicity using one slot of 14 symbols: $833 \times 14 = 11{,}662$ REs/slot.)

**Step 4: Apply 20% overhead.**

$$N_{RE}^{usable} = 23{,}324 \times 0.80 = 18{,}659.2 \ \text{REs}$$

**Step 5: Bit rate per subframe.**

256-QAM gives 8 bits/RE. With 8 MIMO layers:

$$R_{subframe} = 18{,}659.2 \times 8 \times 8 = 1{,}194{,}189 \ \text{bits}$$

**Step 6: Bits per second.**

$$R_{peak} = 1{,}194{,}189 \times 1000 = 1.194 \ \text{Gbps}$$

**Comment on scaling to 20 Gbps:**

A single 50 MHz carrier with the given parameters yields ~1.2 Gbps. To reach 20 Gbps, the system employs:

- **Carrier Aggregation (CA):** Combining up to 16 component carriers across sub-6 GHz and mmWave bands. With 800 MHz aggregated bandwidth (16 × 50 MHz) in mmWave, the same scaling gives ~19.1 Gbps.
- **Higher-order modulation:** Pushing to 1024-QAM (10 bits/RE) in good channel conditions.
- **Massive MIMO:** Expanding to 64 layers in mid-band (sub-6 GHz) and even 256 layers in mmWave.
- **Dual-Connectivity:** Simultaneously using 4G LTE as an anchor plus 5G NR for additional throughput.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

#### (a) Describe the spectrum requirements for 5G and 6G. Explain why sub-THz and THz bands are being explored. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**5G Spectrum Bands:**

5G NR operates across three frequency ranges (FR):

1. **FR1 (Sub-6 GHz):** 410 MHz – 7.125 GHz — Provides wide-area coverage and penetration through walls. Bands n78 (3.5 GHz) and n77 are workhorses for capacity.
2. **FR2 (mmWave):** 24.25 – 52.6 GHz — Provides massive bandwidth (up to 800 MHz per carrier) for hotspots and FWA. Path loss is severe, mitigated by beamforming.
3. **Unlicensed/shared:** 5 GHz, 6 GHz (LAA), and 60 GHz (802.11ay).

**6G Spectrum Vision:**

6G research targets frequencies **above 100 GHz**, specifically:
- **Sub-THz (100 – 300 GHz):** Window of 140–160 GHz is favoured due to lower atmospheric attenuation. Promises 10–100 GHz contiguous bandwidths.
- **THz (0.3 – 10 THz):** Frontier research for ultra-high-capacity backhaul and chip-to-chip communications.

**Why explore sub-THz/THz?**

1. **Bandwidth scarcity:** Sub-6 GHz bands are nearly exhausted. Each new generation needs **10× the spectrum** to support higher data rates.
2. **Shannon capacity scaling:** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ — Capacity scales *linearly* with bandwidth. Going from 100 MHz (sub-6) to 10 GHz (sub-THz) provides a 100× bandwidth multiplier.
3. **Miniaturised antennas:** At 300 GHz, $\lambda \approx 1$ mm, allowing thousands of antenna elements in a smartphone-sized array for extreme beamforming gain.
4. **Deterministic propagation:** THz signals are highly directional, reducing interference and enabling secure, localised communications.

**Challenges:** Atmospheric absorption (especially at 60 GHz, 180 GHz, 325 GHz due to water-vapour resonances), poor diffraction, very high path loss, and immature RF-front-end technology.

---

#### (b) With reference to URLLC, explain the 1 ms latency budget allocation. List three techniques used in 5G NR to achieve this target. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Latency Budget Allocation for 1 ms URLLC:**

| Component | Typical Allocation | Description |
| :-- | :-- | :-- |
| UE TX processing | 0.05 ms | Encoding, modulation, AGC settling |
| Radio TX time | 0.067 ms | 2 OFDM symbols at 30 kHz SCS |
| Air-interface propagation | 0.007 ms | ~2 km at speed of light |
| gNB RX processing | 0.05 ms | Decoding, CRC check |
| Fronthaul/midhaul | 0.1 ms | eCPRI fibre transport to CU |
| Edge compute | 0.5 ms | Application-level processing |
| Return path | 0.226 ms | Symmetric budget for response |
| **Total** | **1.0 ms** | End-to-end one-way |

**Three Techniques to Achieve 1 ms in 5G NR:**

**1. Flexible Numerology and Mini-Slots:**

5G NR supports subcarrier spacings of 15, 30, 60, 120, and 240 kHz. Higher SCS shortens symbol duration. A **mini-slot of 2 OFDM symbols** at 60 kHz SCS occupies only ~35 µs, compared to a 1 ms LTE subframe. This eliminates the "wait for the next frame" latency.

**2. Grant-Free Access:**

In LTE/traditional 5G, devices must first request scheduling grants (Scheduling Request → Buffer Status Report → UL Grant → Data Tx) before transmitting, which can take 8–12 ms. In URLLC, devices use **pre-configured or contention-based resources** (Configured Grant Type 1/2) to transmit immediately, saving ~4–6 ms.

**3. Multi-Connectivity and Packet Duplication:**

To meet 99.999% reliability, the same packet is duplicated across two radio links (e.g., 5G + 4G or dual 5G carriers). The receiver discards duplicates and uses the first to arrive, simultaneously improving latency and reliability.

> **Supporting Techniques (Bonus):** Pre-emptive scheduling, TTI bundling with HARQ-ACK feedback within 4 slots, and Multi-access Edge Computing (MEC) to place application servers physically near the gNB.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Lose 2–3 Marks Each):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "one-way" and "round-trip" latency:** URLLC's 1 ms is *one-way* user-plane latency. Many students write "round-trip" and lose marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units in derivations:** Always state the units (bps, Hz, ms) at each step. KTU examiners explicitly check for this.
> 3. **Citing "5G is faster" without numbers:** Generic qualitative answers are marked low. Always quantify (e.g., "20 Gbps peak", "10⁶ devices/km²").
> 4. **Mixing eMBB/URLLC/mMTC KPIs:** Each scenario has *distinct* targets. Confusing them indicates lack of clarity.
> 5. **Omitting the 25% overhead factor in peak-rate calculations:** This is a frequent silent deduction in numerical questions.
> 6. **Not mentioning the ITU-R M.2410 reference:** Examiners award bonus marks for citing the official document.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Memorise Before Exam):**

- **Three 5G Pillars:** eMBB (speed), URLLC (latency + reliability), mMTC (density).
- **5G Targets:** 20 Gbps peak, 100 Mbps user rate, 1 ms URLLC latency, 10⁶/km² mMTC density, 500 km/h mobility, 99.999% reliability.
- **6G Targets:** 1 Tbps peak, 0.1 ms latency, 10⁷–10⁸/km² density, 1000 km/h mobility, sub-THz/THz spectrum, AI-native architecture.
- **Key Documents:** ITU-R M.2410 (5G requirements), ITU-R M.2160 (5G framework), ITU-R IMT-2030 Framework (6G vision).
- **Shannon Capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ — bandwidth scaling is the primary lever for higher data rates.
- **OFDM Symbol Duration:** $T_{symbol} = 1/\Delta f$ — higher SCS yields shorter symbols and lower latency.
- **5G Spectrum:** FR1 (sub-6 GHz) for coverage; FR2 (24–52 GHz mmWave) for capacity; future sub-THz (100–300 GHz) for 6G.
- **Network Slicing:** Enables eMBB, URLLC, and mMTC to coexist on shared physical infrastructure with isolated resources.
- **Edge Computing (MEC):** Co-locates compute with radio access to keep total latency under 1 ms.
- **Mini-slot:** 2/4/7 OFDM symbols — bypasses subframe waiting time.
- **Grant-Free Access:** Eliminates scheduling-request latency for URLLC and mMTC.
- **Three Reliability Classes:** 99.9% (eMBB), 99.999% (URLLC), 99.99999% (6G URLLC extreme).
- **Path-loss baseline:** Free-space PL = $32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$ (d, f in km and MHz) — fundamental to coverage planning.
- **Massive MIMO principle:** $N_{antennas} \approx 2d/\lambda$ to fully sample an aperture of length $d$.
- **Half-wavelength antenna spacing:** Maximum spatial multiplexing without grating lobes.
- **Energy Efficiency:** 6G targets 100× the energy efficiency of 5G, driven by AI-driven sleep modes and renewable-powered base stations.
- **Three Frequency Ranges of 5G NR:** FR1 (< 7.125 GHz), FR2 (24–52 GHz), and FR3 (7–24 GHz) being studied in 3GPP Release 18+.
- **Six 6G Usage Scenarios:** Immersive, Hyper-reliable, Massive, Ubiquitous, AI-native, Integrated Sensing and Communication.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi) Standards and Applications (IEEE 802.11a/b/g/n/ac/ax)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi) Standards and Applications (IEEE 802.11a/b/g/n/ac/ax)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **IEEE 802.11** standard, commercially branded as **Wi-Fi (Wireless Fidelity)**, is a family of wireless local area network (WLAN) communication protocols defined by the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) LAN/MAN Standards Committee (IEEE 802). The standard specifies the **Physical (PHY) layer** and the **Medium Access Control (MAC) sublayer** of the Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) model for implementing wireless connectivity in the **2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, and 6 GHz** unlicensed industrial, scientific, and medical (ISM) radio bands.

The standard has evolved through multiple amendments — **802.11a, 802.11b, 802.11g, 802.11n, 802.11ac, and 802.11ax** — each introducing higher data rates, better spectral efficiency, improved modulation schemes (DSSS, OFDM, OFDMA), and advanced antenna techniques (MIMO, MU-MIMO, beamforming).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERAT753):**
> The student must be able to compare and contrast the **PHY layer parameters** (frequency band, modulation, channel bandwidth, maximum data rate, MIMO configuration) of all six amendments and identify their **real-world deployment scenarios**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway System

Imagine a city highway as the wireless spectrum:

- The **frequency band (2.4/5/6 GHz)** is the *type of road surface* (concrete/asphalt) — different surfaces allow different vehicle speeds.
- The **channel bandwidth (20/40/80/160 MHz)** is the *number of lanes* on the highway — wider channels carry more "data cars" simultaneously.
- The **modulation scheme (BPSK → 1024-QAM)** is the *vehicle's aerodynamics* — more aerodynamic vehicles (higher-order QAM) carry more cargo per trip but need smoother roads (higher SNR).
- The **MIMO streams** are *multiple parallel highways* stacked on top of each other — increasing throughput without expanding spectrum.
- The **MAC protocol (CSMA/CA)** is the *traffic police* — ensuring no two cars crash into each other on the shared air medium.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU 2024):**
> - **AP (Access Point):** Central hub that broadcasts the SSID and bridges wireless clients to the wired network.
> - **SSID (Service Set Identifier):** The human-readable network name (e.g., "KTU_WiFi").
> - **STA (Station):** Any Wi-Fi client device (laptop, phone, IoT sensor).
> - **BSS (Basic Service Set):** A single AP and its associated stations form a BSS.
> - **ESS (Extended Service Set):** A collection of BSSs interconnected via a Distribution System (DS), enabling roaming.
> - **DSSS / OFDM / OFDMA:** Physical layer multiple-access/multiplexing techniques.

### 1.3 Standardization Bodies and Branding

The **Wi-Fi Alliance** is the global non-profit organization that owns the "Wi-Fi" trademark. It certifies devices for interoperability and assigns consumer-friendly generation numbers (Wi-Fi 4, 5, 6, 6E, 7). While IEEE writes the technical standard, the Wi-Fi Alliance ensures that a phone from Brand X can talk to a router from Brand Y seamlessly.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Evolution of IEEE 802.11 Amendments

The evolution can be understood through **four engineering levers** that every amendment pulls to improve performance:

1. **Lever 1 — Frequency Band Migration:** From the congested 2.4 GHz ISM band to the cleaner 5 GHz U-NII band, and later to 6 GHz (Wi-Fi 6E/7).
2. **Lever 2 — Wider Channels:** From 20 MHz → 40 MHz → 80 MHz → 160 MHz → 320 MHz, multiplying spectral capacity.
3. **Lever 3 — Higher-Order Modulation:** From DBPSK (1 bit/symbol) → DQPSK (2 bits) → 16-QAM (4 bits) → 64-QAM (6 bits) → 256-QAM (8 bits) → 1024-QAM (10 bits) → 4096-QAM (12 bits).
4. **Lever 4 — Spatial Multiplexing (MIMO/MU-MIMO):** Using multiple antennas to send independent data streams in parallel, theoretically scaling throughput linearly with the number of antennas.

### 2.2 Detailed PHY Layer Comparison

| Amendment | Year | Band (GHz) | Max Channel BW | Modulation | Max Spatial Streams | Max Data Rate | Multi-User |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 802.11 (legacy) | 1997 | 2.4 | 20 MHz | DSSS/DBPSK, DQPSK | 1×1 SISO | **2 Mbps** | No |
| 802.11a | 1999 | 5 | 20 MHz | OFDM, BPSK to 64-QAM | 1×1 SISO | **54 Mbps** | No |
| 802.11b | 1999 | 2.4 | 20 MHz | DSSS/CCK, DQPSK | 1×1 SISO | **11 Mbps** | No |
| 802.11g | 2003 | 2.4 | 20 MHz | OFDM, BPSK to 64-QAM | 1×1 SISO | **54 Mbps** | No |
| 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4) | 2009 | 2.4 / 5 | 20/40 MHz | OFDM, BPSK to 64-QAM | 4×4 MIMO | **600 Mbps** | No (SU-MIMO only) |
| 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) | 2013 | 5 | 20/40/80/160 MHz | OFDM, BPSK to 256-QAM | 8×8 MU-MIMO (DL) | **6.93 Gbps** | Yes (DL MU-MIMO) |
| 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) | 2019 | 2.4 / 5 / 6 | 20/40/80/160 MHz | OFDM/OFDMA, BPSK to 1024-QAM | 8×8 MU-MIMO (UL+DL) | **9.6 Gbps** | Yes (UL+DL OFDMA + MU-MIMO) |

### 2.3 Core Mathematical Foundations (KTU Formula Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet — Must-Memorize Equations**

| Concept | Equation | Variable Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | $C$=Capacity, $B$=Bandwidth, SNR = $S/N$ | bits/s |
| MIMO Capacity (Eigenmode) | $C = \sum_{i=1}^{\min(N_t, N_r)} B \cdot \log_2(1 + \lambda_i \cdot \tfrac{\text{SNR}}{N_t})$ | $\lambda_i$=Eigenvalues of $\mathbf{H}\mathbf{H}^{H}$ | bits/s |
| OFDM Symbol Duration | $T_{\text{sym}} = T_{\text{FFT}} + T_{\text{CP}}$ | $T_{\text{FFT}}$=Useful IFFT period, $T_{\text{CP}}$=Cyclic Prefix | seconds |
| OFDM Subcarrier Spacing | $\Delta f = \tfrac{1}{T_{\text{FFT}}} = \tfrac{N}{T_{\text{sym}}}$ | $N$=FFT size (e.g., 64) | Hz |
| PHY Data Rate (General) | $R = N_{ss} \cdot N_{bpsc} \cdot R_c \cdot \tfrac{N_{sc}}{T_{\text{sym}}}$ | $N_{ss}$=Spatial streams, $N_{bpsc}$=coded bits/subcarrier, $R_c$=coding rate, $N_{sc}$=data subcarriers | bits/s |
| 802.11a/g OFDM Rate (e.g., 64-QAM 3/4) | $R = 48 \cdot 6 \cdot \tfrac{3}{4} \cdot \tfrac{1}{4\,\mu s}$ | 48 data subcarriers, 6 bits/symbol, 3/4 rate | **= 54 Mbps** |
| Free-Space Path Loss (Friis) | $\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\tfrac{4\pi}{c}\right)$ | $d$=distance (m), $f$=frequency (Hz), $c$=3×10⁸ m/s | dB |
| Received Power (Friis) | $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\tfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | $G_t, G_r$=antenna gains, $\lambda$=wavelength | Watts |
| Net Throughput | $\eta = R \cdot (1 - \text{overhead factor})$ | Overhead = MAC headers, ACKs, DIFS, backoff | bits/s |
| Maximum Achievable Throughput (802.11ax) | $R_{\max} = \tfrac{9.6 \text{ Gbps}}{\text{(PHY rate)}}$ | Effective MAC efficiency ≈ 70–75% of PHY rate | bits/s |

### 2.4 Why Each Step Matters — The "How & Why" Engineering Logic

- **Why DSSS in 802.11b?** The 2.4 GHz band is shared with microwave ovens, Bluetooth, and ZigBee. **Spread spectrum** spreads the signal over a wider bandwidth, providing **processing gain** that resists narrowband interference. CCK (Complementary Code Keying) was then introduced to push 802.11b to 11 Mbps.
- **Why OFDM in 802.11a/g?** OFDM splits a wideband channel (20 MHz) into **52 orthogonal narrowband subcarriers** (48 data + 4 pilots). Each subcarrier experiences **flat fading**, eliminating intersymbol interference (ISI) without complex equalizers. This was the breakthrough that made 54 Mbps possible.
- **Why MIMO in 802.11n?** At high SNR, Shannon's law says **capacity grows linearly with bandwidth, not with power**. The only way to scale throughput further (without buying more spectrum) is **spatial multiplexing** — sending different data on different antennas in the same frequency at the same time, exploiting multipath as a resource instead of a foe.
- **Why MU-MIMO + OFDMA in 802.11ax?** In dense deployments (stadiums, airports, offices), most users send tiny packets. Traditional OFDM wastes bandwidth because one AP talks to one user per slot. **OFDMA** divides subcarriers into **Resource Units (RUs)** allocated to different users simultaneously. **MU-MIMO** further stacks spatial streams onto RUs. This is the **multi-user efficiency revolution** of Wi-Fi 6.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **802.11a/b/g/n:** Home/office routers, legacy IoT devices.
- **802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5):** HD/4K video streaming, video conferencing, smart TVs.
- **802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6/6E):** Dense urban deployments, Industry 4.0, AR/VR, telehealth, university campuses.
- **Enterprise/Mesh:** Used in campus-wide backhaul (e.g., Eduroam), municipal Wi-Fi, in-flight connectivity.
- **IoT:** 802.11ax includes **Target Wake Time (TWT)** to schedule client wakeups, saving battery for sensor networks — critical for smart agriculture and asset tracking.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum PHY Data Rate of 802.11a/g at 64-QAM with Coding Rate 3/4

The OFDM PHY of 802.11a/g divides the 20 MHz channel into 64 subcarriers (52 used, of which 48 carry data and 4 are pilots). Each OFDM symbol consists of an IFFT period of 3.2 µs plus a cyclic prefix of 0.8 µs.

**Step 1 — Identify the OFDM symbol duration.**

We sum the useful IFFT window and the cyclic prefix guard interval:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{FFT}} &= 3.2 \,\mu s \quad (\text{from } 64 \text{-point IFFT at } 20 \text{ MHz sample rate}) \\
T_{\text{CP}} &= 0.8 \,\mu s \quad (\text{guard interval, } 25\% \text{ of } T_{\text{FFT}}) \\
T_{\text{sym}} &= T_{\text{FFT}} + T_{\text{CP}} = 3.2 \,\mu s + 0.8 \,\mu s = 4.0 \,\mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine subcarrier symbol rate.**

The 48 data subcarriers each carry one symbol per OFDM symbol. Therefore, the number of OFDM symbols per second is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{sym}} &= \frac{1}{T_{\text{sym}}} = \frac{1}{4.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 250{,}000 \text{ symbols/s} \\
&= 250 \text{ kOFDM-symbols/s per subcarrier}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute raw bits per OFDM symbol using 64-QAM and 3/4 coding rate.**

64-QAM encodes $\log_2(64) = 6$ bits per subcarrier symbol. With a convolutional coding rate of $R_c = 3/4$, only 75% of these bits are payload:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{bits/sym}} &= N_{\text{sc}} \times N_{\text{bpsc}} \times R_c \\
&= 48 \times 6 \times \frac{3}{4} \\
&= 48 \times 4.5 \\
&= 216 \text{ payload bits per OFDM symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the final PHY data rate.**

Multiply the payload bits per symbol by the symbol rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{PHY}} &= \frac{N_{\text{bits/sym}}}{T_{\text{sym}}} = 216 \times 250{,}000 \\
&= 54{,}000{,}000 \text{ bits/s} \\
&= \boxed{54 \text{ Mbps}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the 802.11a/g theoretical peak PHY rate of **54 Mbps**. The same 48×6×3/4 architecture with 64-QAM is reused across 802.11n (per spatial stream) and 802.11ac/ax (at higher MCS indices).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum PHY Data Rate of 802.11n (4×4 MIMO, 40 MHz, 64-QAM, Short GI)

**Step 1 — Determine the new OFDM symbol parameters for 40 MHz channel.**

For a 40 MHz channel, the subcarrier spacing doubles to keep OFDM robust. Hence $N_{\text{FFT}} = 128$ and the useful IFFT window halves:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{FFT}} &= \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{312.5 \text{ kHz}} = 3.2 \,\mu s \\
T_{\text{CP,short}} &= 0.4 \,\mu s \quad (\text{Short Guard Interval, SGI}) \\
T_{\text{sym, SGI}} &= 3.2 + 0.4 = 3.6 \,\mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Count the data subcarriers.**

For 40 MHz, 802.11n uses **108 data subcarriers** (4× the 20 MHz count, minus pilot overhead):

$$
N_{\text{sc}} = 108
$$

**Step 3 — Compute raw bits per OFDM symbol (64-QAM, rate 5/6).**

64-QAM carries 6 bits, and the highest 802.11n code rate is $R_c = 5/6$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{bits/sym/stream}} &= 108 \times 6 \times \frac{5}{6} = 540 \text{ bits} \\
R_{\text{per stream}} &= \frac{540}{3.6 \,\mu s} = 150 \text{ Mbps per spatial stream}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 4 spatial streams.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{PHY, max}} &= 4 \times 150 \text{ Mbps} = 600 \text{ Mbps} \\
&= \boxed{600 \text{ Mbps}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the marketed peak rate of 802.11n.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shannon Capacity Bound vs. 802.11ac Realized Rate

Consider a 160 MHz channel at 5 GHz with an SNR of 35 dB (a typical close-range laptop connection).

**Step 1 — Convert SNR to linear scale.**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{lin}} = 10^{35/10} = 3162.3
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon-Hartley theorem.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \\
&= 160 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1 + 3162.3) \\
&= 160 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(3163.3) \\
&= 160 \times 10^6 \cdot 11.627 \\
&\approx 1.86 \text{ Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to 802.11ac single-stream, 160 MHz, 256-QAM (rate 5/6).**

256-QAM carries 8 bits/symbol. With 234 data subcarriers (160 MHz) and a 4 µs symbol:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{stream}} &= 234 \times 8 \times \frac{5}{6} \times \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 234 \times 6.667 \times 250{,}000 \\
&\approx 390 \text{ Mbps per stream}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — With 8 spatial streams (theoretical 8×8 MIMO), peak PHY rate is:**

$$
R_{\text{max}} = 8 \times 390 \approx 3.12 \text{ Gbps}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> The marketed "6.93 Gbps" of 802.11ac is achieved with 160 MHz, 256-QAM, 8×8 MIMO, short GI, and 5/6 coding. Shannon's law at 35 dB SNR predicts only **1.86 Gbps** for a SISO link, proving the massive spectral efficiency gains from MIMO spatial multiplexing. Real-world throughput is lower (50–70%) due to MAC overhead, contention, and imperfect channel conditions.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — MCS Index Table (802.11ac, excerpt)

| MCS Index | Modulation | Coding Rate | Spatial Streams | Channel BW | Data Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| MCS 0 | BPSK | 1/2 | 1 | 20 MHz | 6.5 Mbps |
| MCS 7 | 64-QAM | 5/6 | 1 | 20 MHz | 65 Mbps |
| MCS 9 | 256-QAM | 5/6 | 1 | 80 MHz | 433.3 Mbps |
| MCS 9 | 256-QAM | 5/6 | 2 | 160 MHz | 866.7 Mbps |
| MCS 9 | 256-QAM | 5/6 | 8 | 160 MHz | **6.93 Gbps** |

The AP and client dynamically negotiate the MCS based on **Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI)**, **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**, and **packet error rate** — this is called **link adaptation**.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IEEE 802.11 Protocol Stack and OSI Mapping

This block diagram maps the IEEE 802.11 architecture into the OSI model and shows how the 802.2 Logical Link Control (LLC) layer bridges the WLAN to upper-layer protocols (IP, etc.).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Upper Layers - IP - TCP - UDP - HTTP] --> node2[OSI Layer 3 - Network]
    node2 --> node3[IEEE 802.2 - Logical Link Control - LLC]
    node3 --> node4[IEEE 802.11 - MAC Sublayer - CSMA CA - ACK - RTS CTS]
    node4 --> node5[IEEE 802.11 - PHY Layer - DSSS - OFDM - OFDMA - MIMO]
    node5 --> node6[Wireless Medium - 2.4 GHz - 5 GHz - 6 GHz RF Channel]

    node4A[MAC Functions - Beacon - Probe - Auth - Assoc - Power Save] -.-> node4
    node5A[PHY Functions - Scrambling - Coding - Interleaving - Mapping - IFFT] -.-> node5
```

### 4.2 Evolution Roadmap of IEEE 802.11 Amendments

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[802.11 legacy - 1997 - 2 Mbps - DSSS FHSS] --> nodeB[802.11a - 1999 - 54 Mbps - 5 GHz - OFDM]
    nodeA --> nodeC[802.11b - 1999 - 11 Mbps - 2.4 GHz - DSSS CCK]
    nodeB --> nodeD[802.11g - 2003 - 54 Mbps - 2.4 GHz - OFDM]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[802.11n Wi-Fi 4 - 2009 - 600 Mbps - MIMO OFDM]
    nodeE --> nodeF[802.11ac Wi-Fi 5 - 2013 - 6.93 Gbps - MU-MIMO]
    nodeF --> nodeG[802.11ax Wi-Fi 6 6E - 2019 - 9.6 Gbps - OFDMA MU-MIMO]
    nodeG --> nodeH[802.11be Wi-Fi 7 - 2024 - 46 Gbps - 320 MHz - 4096-QAM]
```

### 4.3 General 802.11 MAC Frame Format (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Frame Control - 2 bytes] --> node2[Duration ID - 2 bytes]
    node2 --> node3[Address 1 - 6 bytes]
    node3 --> node4[Address 2 - 6 bytes]
    node4 --> node5[Address 3 - 6 bytes]
    node5 --> node6[Seq Control - 2 bytes]
    node6 --> node7[Address 4 - 6 bytes optional]
    node7 --> node8[QoS Control - 2 bytes optional]
    node8 --> node9[Frame Body - 0 to 2312 bytes]
    node9 --> node10[FCS - 4 bytes CRC32]
```

### 4.4 CSMA/CA Channel Access Flowchart (Block-Level Function)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeS[Station has data to send] --> nodeDIFS[Wait DIFS - DCF Inter-Frame Space]
    nodeDIFS --> nodeBACK[Start Backoff Timer - Random Slot 0 to CW]
    nodeBACK --> nodeCHK{Slot Idle?}
    nodeCHK -- No - busy medium --> nodeFREEZE[Freeze Timer and Wait]
    nodeFREEZE --> nodeBACK
    nodeCHK -- Yes - slot decremented to zero --> nodeTX[Transmit RTS or Data Frame]
    nodeTX --> nodeACK{ACK Received within Timeout?}
    nodeACK -- Yes --> nodeNEXT[Frame Delivered - Send Next]
    nodeACK -- No --> nodeRETRY[Double CW and Retry]
    nodeRETRY --> nodeBACK
```

### 4.5 802.11ax OFDMA Resource Unit Allocation (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeCH[20 MHz Channel - 256 Subcarriers] --> nodeRU1[RU1 - 26 Subcarriers - User A]
    nodeCH --> nodeRU2[RU2 - 26 Subcarriers - User B]
    nodeCH --> nodeRU3[RU3 - 52 Subcarriers - User C]
    nodeCH --> nodeRU4[RU4 - 106 Subcarriers - User D]
    nodeCH --> nodeRU5[RU5 - 26 Subcarriers - User E]
    nodeCH --> nodeRU6[RU6 - 19 Subcarriers - Pilot and Guard]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Interpretation Tip:**
> In 802.11ax, a single 20 MHz OFDMA transmission can serve up to **9 simultaneous users** (each with a 26-tone RU). This is the **multi-user parallelism** that gives Wi-Fi 6 its 4× capacity gain in dense scenarios over Wi-Fi 5.

### 4.6 ESS Roaming Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeDS[Distribution System - Switched Ethernet Backbone] --> nodeAP1[AP1 - BSS1 - Channel 36]
    nodeDS --> nodeAP2[AP2 - BSS2 - Channel 44]
    nodeDS --> nodeAP3[AP3 - BSS3 - Channel 48]
    nodeSTA1[Laptop STA] -.handoff.-> nodeAP1
    nodeSTA1 -.moves.-> nodeAP2
    nodeSTA1 -.moves.-> nodeAP3
```

The **ESS (Extended Service Set)** allows seamless roaming as the client performs **re-association** with stronger APs while maintaining IP connectivity through the Distribution System.
