
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
Define the **3GPP-LTE standard**. State **any four** key design goals of the LTE standard as defined in 3GPP Release 8.

**Model Answer:**
The 3GPP-LTE (Long Term Evolution) standard, formalized in 3GPP Release 8, is a broadband cellular radio standard specifying the Evolved Packet System (EPS), comprising the E-UTRAN (Evolved UMTS Terrestrial Radio Access Network) and the EPC (Evolved Packet Core). It is an all-IP, flat-architecture system designed to succeed UMTS/HSPA+ and serve as the foundation for 4G mobile broadband.

Four key design goals:
1. **Peak data rate** of 100 Mbps (DL) and 50 Mbps (UL) in a 20 MHz channel.
2. **User-plane latency** below 5 ms (one-way, unloaded).
3. **Scalable channel bandwidth** of 1.4, 3, 5, 10, 15, and 20 MHz.
4. **DL spectral efficiency** of 5 bit/s/Hz (4×4 MIMO) and **UL** of 2.5 bit/s/Hz.

> *(Any four from the set: peak rate, low latency, scalable BW, spectral efficiency, mobility up to 350 km/h, all-IP, flexible spectrum, reduced CAPEX/OPEX.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
Differentiate between **OFDMA** (used in LTE downlink) and **SC-FDMA** (used in LTE uplink) in **three** aspects.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | OFDMA (DL) | SC-FDMA (UL) |
|---|---|---|
| PAPR | High (~10–12 dB) | Low (~5–7 dB) |
| Signal generation | IFFT at the transmitter | DFT-precoded IFFT |
| Suitability | eNodeB (no PA constraint) | UE (battery-powered, low-PAPR requirement) |

> *(Equivalent valid points accepted.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Each 14-mark question carries *two sub-parts*, each 7 marks, typically (a) descriptive and (b) numerical/comparative. An **internal choice** means the student may answer either **Question A or Question B** on the same module.

---

### 5.2.1 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q. A) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of the LTE network**. Clearly differentiate between E-UTRAN and EPC, and explain the function of **eNodeB, MME, S-GW, P-GW, and HSS.**  *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **LTE radio frame structure** for **Frequency Division Duplex (FDD)** mode. Show the mapping from **frames → subframes → slots → OFDM symbols → Resource Elements → Resource Blocks** and compute the number of **Resource Elements per RB per subframe** in normal cyclic prefix.  *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture of LTE Network (7 marks):**

The LTE network follows a **flat, all-IP architecture** with two major domains:

- **E-UTRAN (Evolved UMTS Terrestrial Radio Access Network):** Contains only one element — the **eNodeB (eNodeB)**, which absorbs the radio functions of the legacy NodeB + RNC. The **X2 interface** connects peer eNodeBs for inter-cell handover and ICIC (Inter-Cell Interference Coordination).
- **EPC (Evolved Packet Core):** The packet-only core network.

Functions of each node:

| Node | Function |
|---|---|
| **eNodeB** | Radio resource management, scheduling, HARQ, RRC, inter-cell RRM, IP header compression, encryption. Connects to UE via the **Uu** interface. |
| **MME** | Pure control-plane node: handles attach/detach, tracking area updates, bearer establishment, and authentication. Speaks to HSS via **S6a**. |
| **S-GW** | User-plane anchor for intra-LTE and inter-3GPP handover. Routes/forwards packets between eNodeB and P-GW. |
| **P-GW** | Edge node to the internet; allocates UE IP address, applies QoS, performs deep-packet inspection and lawful intercept. |
| **HSS** | Master subscriber database — stores IMSI, Ki (auth key), and service profile. |

A neat block diagram must show UE ↔ eNodeB ↔ (MME, S-GW) ↔ P-GW ↔ Internet, with HSS and PCRF connected to MME and P-GW respectively.

**(b) LTE FDD Radio Frame Structure (7 marks):**

A **10 ms radio frame** is divided into **10 subframes (1 ms each)**. Each subframe contains **2 slots of 0.5 ms**. With **normal cyclic prefix**, each slot has **7 OFDM symbols**. Therefore:

$$
N_{sym}^{subframe} = 2 \times 7 = 14\ \text{symbols}
$$

A **Resource Block (RB)** is defined as **12 consecutive subcarriers × 1 slot (0.5 ms)**, i.e., 12 × 7 = 84 Resource Elements. In one subframe, one RB spans 2 slots, so:

$$
\boxed{N_{RE}^{RB, subframe} = 12 \times 14 = 168\ \text{Resource Elements}}
$$

The **Resource Element (RE)** is the smallest unit: **1 subcarrier × 1 OFDM symbol**.

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| 1 Frame | 10 ms |
| 1 Subframe | 1 ms (2 slots) |
| 1 Slot | 0.5 ms (7 symbols, normal CP) |
| 1 RB | 12 subcarriers × 7 symbols = 84 REs (per slot) |
| 1 RB / subframe | 168 REs |
| 20 MHz → RBs | 100 |
| 20 MHz / frame | 100 × 1680 = 168 000 REs |

---

### 5.2.2 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q. B) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**(a)** Explain the **multiple-access techniques** used in LTE: **OFDMA on the downlink and SC-FDMA on the uplink.** Justify **why** OFDMA was chosen for DL and SC-FDMA for UL, focusing on PAPR, scheduler flexibility, and UE power-amplifier efficiency.  *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Calculate the **theoretical peak downlink data rate** of an LTE system operating with a **20 MHz** channel bandwidth, **64-QAM** modulation, **normal cyclic prefix**, and **SISO** antenna configuration. Also determine the corresponding **downlink spectral efficiency**. Show every step.  *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) OFDMA in DL and SC-FDMA in UL (7 marks):**

- **Downlink (OFDMA):** The eNodeB is not power-constrained (it has mains supply). It benefits from OFDMA's **high spectral efficiency**, **frequency-domain scheduling flexibility** (per-RB allocation across users every TTI = 1 ms), and **simple receiver equalization** in frequency-selective channels. The high PAPR of OFDM (~10–12 dB) is acceptable at the eNodeB.
- **Uplink (SC-FDMA):** The UE is **battery-powered** and must use a **linear, power-efficient PA**. OFDM's high PAPR is unsuitable. SC-FDMA (a single-carrier variant implemented as DFT-precoded OFDM) achieves **PAPR ~ 5–7 dB**, prolonging battery life and improving cell-edge coverage. The cost: SC-FDMA requires **contiguous RB allocation** for a single user (FDMA-like), reducing scheduling flexibility — but this is acceptable because **per-UE allocation** in UL is anyway localized in practice.
- **Summary:** OFDMA maximizes **spectrum efficiency** on the DL where spectrum is *shared*; SC-FDMA maximizes **PA efficiency** on the UL where *battery* is shared.

**(b) Peak DL Data Rate Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Map bandwidth to RBs:**

$$
B = 20\ \text{MHz} \;\Rightarrow\; N_{RB} = 100
$$

**Step 2 — Subframe structure (normal CP):**
- Subframe = 2 slots, each with 7 OFDM symbols → **14 OFDM symbols per subframe**.

**Step 3 — Modulation bits per RE:**
- 64-QAM ⇒ $m = \log_2(64) = 6$ bits/symbol.

**Step 4 — Theoretical peak rate (no overhead, code rate 1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{peak,DL} &= N_{RB} \times N_{subcarriers}^{RB} \times N_{sym}^{subframe} \times m \times 10^{3}\ \text{subframes/s} \\
&= 100 \times 12 \times 14 \times 6 \times 10^{3} \\
&= 100{,}800{,}000\ \text{bps} \\
&= \boxed{100.8\ \text{Mbps} \;\approx\; 100\ \text{Mbps (standard peak)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Downlink spectral efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{DL} = \frac{R_{peak,DL}}{B} = \frac{100\ \text{Mbps}}{20\ \text{MHz}} = \boxed{5\ \text{bit/s/Hz}}
$$

This matches the 3GPP Release 8 design target exactly.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (EARN your 14 marks!):**
> 1. **Forgetting the 14-symbol structure.** Students often write $100 \times 12 \times 7 \times 6$ and divide by 2 — this is *per slot*, not per subframe. *Always multiply by 14 (symbols/subframe) for a 1-ms computation.*
> 2. **Mixing subframes with frames.** A subframe is 1 ms; a frame is 10 ms. If the question says "per frame," use **140 symbols**, not 14.
> 3. **Wrong modulation lookup.** Students confuse $m=4$ (16-QAM) with $m=6$ (64-QAM). For *peak* rate, the standard assumes **64-QAM**.
> 4. **Confusing OFDMA with OFDM.** OFDM is the *modulation*; OFDMA is the *multiple-access* (resource-allocation) scheme. Examiners dock 1 mark for this.
> 5. **Missing the 20 MHz → 100 RB mapping.** This is the single most-tested fact in Module 2.
> 6. **Saying "EPC = MME" or "EPC = S-GW".** EPC is the *whole* core (MME + S-GW + P-GW + HSS + PCRF). Examiners expect the components listed, not the abbreviation.
> 7. **Conflating SC-FDMA with OFDMA.** SC-FDMA is *not* a separate air interface; it is a DFT-precoded OFDM signal — the same OFDM numerology, just with a *per-UE DFT* pre-spreader at the transmitter.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **LTE = Release 8 (Dec 2008), 3GPP, EPS = E-UTRAN + EPC, all-IP, flat architecture.**
- **20 MHz → 100 RBs → 1200 subcarriers → 100.8 Mbps peak DL (64-QAM, SISO).**
- **50 MHz is the bandwidth choice; 6 bandwidths total: 1.4 / 3 / 5 / 10 / 15 / 20 MHz.**
- **Frame → Subframe → Slot → Symbol → Resource Element → Resource Block** is the strict hierarchy: 10 ms / 1 ms / 0.5 ms / 66.67 µs / 1 RE / 12×7 REs.
- **1 RB = 180 kHz (12 subcarriers × 15 kHz) × 0.5 ms = 84 REs per slot; 168 REs per subframe.**
- **Downlink = OFDMA; Uplink = SC-FDMA (DFTS-OFDM).** Reason: PAPR & PA efficiency.
- **Modulation ladder: QPSK → 16-QAM → 64-QAM** (256-QAM only in LTE-A R12+).
- **MIMO: 1×2, 2×2, 4×4** standardized; up to 8×8 in LTE-Advanced.
- **Latency target: < 5 ms one-way, unloaded.**
- **Spectral efficiency target: 5 bit/s/Hz DL; 2.5 bit/s/Hz UL (SISO).**
- **Core nodes: MME (signalling), S-GW (user-plane anchor), P-GW (internet edge + IP allocator), HSS (subscriber DB), PCRF (policy/charging).**
- **eNodeB = eNodeB; replaced NodeB + RNC; X2 interface connects eNodeBs; S1-MME / S1-U connect eNodeB to EPC.**
- **TTI = 1 ms (subframe); HARQ round-trip ≈ 8 ms (synchronous, 8 TTIs).**
- **CP variants: Normal CP (7 symbols/slot, 4.69 µs CP) for typical cells; Extended CP (6 symbols/slot, 16.67 µs CP) for large cells / MBSFN.**
- **5G NR inherits LTE's OFDM foundation but makes subcarrier spacing scalable (15 → 30 → 60 → 120 kHz)** — mastering LTE numerology = half the battle in 5G.

> **Final One-Liner to remember:** *"LTE = flat, all-IP, OFDMA-down, SC-FDMA-up, 100 Mbps peak in 20 MHz, frame = 10 ms, RB = 180 kHz × 0.5 ms."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Millimeter Wave Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Millimeter Wave Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — MILLIMETER WAVE CHARACTERISTICS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Millimeter Waves (mmWaves)** are electromagnetic waves occupying the **Extremely High Frequency (EHF)** band of the radio spectrum, with **frequencies between 30 GHz and 300 GHz**, corresponding to **wavelengths from 10 mm down to 1 mm**. They form the upper edge of the microwave region and the lower edge of the far-infrared (terahertz) region.

The mmWave spectrum is broadly classified by the **IEEE** into four primary bands:

| Band Designation | Frequency Range (GHz) | Wavelength Range (mm) | Primary Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Ka-band (V-band lower edge)** | 26.5 – 40 | 7.5 – 11.3 | Satellite, 5G FR2-lower |
| **V-band** | 40 – 75 | 4 – 7.5 | 5G FR2, 60 GHz WLAN/WiGig |
| **E-band (W-band lower edge)** | 60 – 90 | 3.3 – 5 | Point-to-point backhaul |
| **W-band** | 75 – 110 | 2.7 – 4 | Automotive radar, imaging |
| **D-band (mmWave upper)** | 110 – 170 | 1.7 – 2.7 | 6G research, short-range links |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Laser-Flashlight" Intuition

Imagine you are in a dark room with two light sources:

- A **flashlight (microwave, 2.4 GHz)** spreads its beam broadly; it can pass through walls, bounce around furniture, and still illuminate the entire room.
- A **laser pointer (mmWave, 60 GHz)** produces a razor-thin, almost perfectly straight beam. It cannot bend around obstacles, cannot pass through walls, and reaches much farther only when aimed directly at the target.

This is the essence of mmWaves: **highly directional, short-range, blocked by obstacles, but capable of enormous bandwidth**. The same physics that makes laser pointers precise also makes mmWaves suitable for **5G/6G hotspots, automotive radar, and high-capacity backhaul links**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal constants used in mmWave analysis:**
> - Speed of light, **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - Impedance of free space, **η₀ = 377 Ω (≈ 120π Ω)**
> - Boltzmann constant, **k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
> - Permittivity of free space, **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
> - Permeability of free space, **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**

The relationship between frequency and wavelength is governed by:

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f}
$$

For example, a 60 GHz signal has a wavelength of **λ = 3 × 10⁸ ⁄ 60 × 10⁹ = 5 mm**, while a 28 GHz 5G signal has a wavelength of **λ ≈ 10.7 mm**.

### 1.4 Syllabus-Mandated Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme (PERAT753, Module 2):**
> Students must be able to describe the **propagation characteristics, channel models, atmospheric effects, and hardware implications** of mmWaves. The following seven characteristics are explicitly assessed:
> 1. **High Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)**
> 2. **Atmospheric & Molecular Absorption**
> 3. **Rain Attenuation & Weather Sensitivity**
> 4. **Poor Diffraction & Penetration (Blockage)**
> 5. **High Directionality & Antenna Gain**
> 6. **Small Antenna & Device Footprint**
> 7. **Wide Bandwidth & High Data Capacity**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Characteristic 1 — High Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

The free-space path loss follows directly from the **Friis transmission equation**. Since FSPL scales with the **square of frequency**, mmWaves suffer significantly higher path loss than sub-6 GHz microwaves.

- **Why it matters:** Doubling frequency quadruples the loss.
- **KTU implication:** A 60 GHz link suffers **~28 dB more loss** than a 2.4 GHz link at the same distance.
- **Engineering mitigation:** Use **high-gain, steerable phased-array antennas** to recover the link budget.

### 2.2 Characteristic 2 — Atmospheric & Molecular Absorption

Specific molecules in the atmosphere (oxygen O₂ and water vapor H₂O) resonate at mmWave frequencies and absorb energy.

- **Why it matters:** Certain frequencies become **"rain-forbidden"** for long-range use.
- **Key absorption peaks:** **22 GHz (H₂O)**, **60 GHz (O₂)**, **120 GHz (O₂)**, **183 GHz (H₂O)**.
- **Engineering window:** The 28 GHz, 38 GHz, and E-band (71–76 / 81–86 GHz) regions are chosen for 5G/backhaul because they lie in **"low-loss windows"** between peaks.

### 2.3 Characteristic 3 — Rain Attenuation

Rain droplets (radius 0.1–3 mm) are comparable in size to mmWavelengths, causing **scattering (Mie scattering)** and **absorption**.

- **Why it matters:** A heavy rainstorm (50 mm/hr) can attenuate a 60 GHz signal by **10–15 dB/km**.
- **Link-budget impact:** Cell radius in heavy rain may shrink to **<200 m**.
- **Mitigation:** Adaptive coding, link margin, and site diversity.

### 2.4 Characteristic 4 — Poor Diffraction & Penetration

Diffraction loss depends on the **Fresnel-Kirchhoff parameter** and worsens as wavelength decreases.

- **Why it matters:** A human body attenuates 60 GHz by **20–30 dB**; a brick wall by **40–60 dB**.
- **Engineering consequence:** **Non-Line-of-Sight (NLOS) coverage** is extremely limited; networks must rely on **dense small cells, reflectors, and beam-steering**.

### 2.5 Characteristic 5 — High Directionality & Antenna Gain

For a fixed antenna **aperture area A**, gain scales with frequency squared:

$$
G = \frac{4\pi A}{\lambda^{2}} = \frac{4\pi A f^{2}}{c^{2}}
$$

- **Why it matters:** A 10 cm × 10 cm antenna at 60 GHz has gain **~32 dBi**, versus only **~6 dBi** at 2.4 GHz.
- **Engineering consequence:** **Beamforming** becomes essential.

### 2.6 Characteristic 6 — Small Antenna & Device Footprint

Half-wave dipole length is **λ/2**, so a 60 GHz antenna is only **2.5 mm** long.

- **Why it matters:** Enables **on-chip / in-package antenna arrays** for smartphones and IoT devices.
- **Engineering consequence:** Suitable for **massive MIMO** and **beam-steered arrays** in a compact form factor.

### 2.7 Characteristic 7 — Wide Bandwidth & High Data Capacity

Available contiguous spectrum in mmWave bands is **hundreds of MHz to GHz**, versus only ~20 MHz in sub-6 GHz cellular.

- **Why it matters:** Per Shannon-Hartley theorem, capacity scales linearly with bandwidth:

$$
C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

- **Engineering consequence:** Multi-Gbps and future Tbps links.

---

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~80% of mmWave numerical problems in KTU exams.**

| S.No. | Formula (LaTeX) | Physical Meaning | Units / Variables | Typical KTU Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength | $\lambda$ in m, $c=3\times 10^{8}$ m/s, $f$ in Hz | Find wavelength from frequency |
| 2 | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | Free-Space Path Loss | $d$ in **km**, $f$ in **MHz**, FSPL in dB | Most common KTU numerical |
| 3 | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$ | FSPL (SI form) | $d$ in m, $\lambda$ in m | Derivation-based question |
| 4 | $\dfrac{P_{r}}{P_{t}} = G_{t} G_{r}\!\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | Friis Power Ratio | Dimensionless; $G$ dimensionless | Link budget |
| 5 | $G = \dfrac{4\pi A_{e}}{\lambda^{2}} = \dfrac{4\pi A_{e} f^{2}}{c^{2}}$ | Antenna Gain | $A_{e}$ effective aperture in m², $G$ dimensionless | Antenna design |
| 6 | $C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \text{SNR}\right)$ | Shannon Capacity | $B$ in Hz, $C$ in bps | Bandwidth advantage |
| 7 | $\theta_{3\text{dB}} \approx \dfrac{0.886 \,\lambda}{D}$ | Half-Power Beamwidth | $\theta$ in rad, $D$ antenna diameter in m | Directionality proof |
| 8 | $A_{\text{rain}} = a R^{b}$ (in dB/km) | Specific Rain Attenuation | $R$ rain rate in mm/hr, $a,b$ ITU constants | Rain-loss numerical |
| 9 | $\gamma_{\text{atm}} = \gamma_{o} + \gamma_{w}$ (dB/km) | Total Atmospheric Attenuation | Sum of dry-air + vapor contributions | Spectrum window |
| 10 | $P_{r}(\text{dBm}) = P_{t} + G_{t} + G_{r} - FSPL - L_{\text{other}}$ | Received Power (dB) | All in dB/dBi/dBm | Full link budget |
| 11 | $\text{PL}_{\text{NLOS}} = \text{FSPL} + N(0,\sigma^{2})$ | NLOS Path Loss Model | Random shadow fading term | Channel modelling |
| 12 | $EIRP = P_{t} + G_{t}$ (dB) | Effective Isotropic Radiated Power | dBm + dBi | Regulatory compliance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall:** In KTU exam answers, always state **units** explicitly. Saying "FSPL = 88 dB" without "for d = 50 m and f = 60 GHz" loses 1 mark.

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Why mmWaves Are Used | Frequency Band |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **5G NR FR2** | Multi-Gbps hotspot, stadium, dense urban | 24–40 GHz |
| **6G Research** | Terahertz frontier, sub-THz sensing | 100–300 GHz |
| **Automotive Radar** | High resolution for collision avoidance | 76–81 GHz |
| **Satellite / Backhaul** | Wide-bandwidth E-band links | 71–76 / 81–86 GHz |
| **WiGig / IEEE 802.11ad/ay** | Wireless docking, AR/VR, short-range | 60 GHz |
| **Security Imaging** | Sees through clothing (privacy concerns) | 94 GHz |
| **Medical / Spectroscopy** | Rotational spectra of molecules | 100–300 GHz |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) from Friis Equation

**Starting Point:** The Friis transmission equation states that the power received by an isotropic receive antenna from an isotropic transmit antenna, separated by distance $d$ in free space, is:

$$
P_{r} = P_{t}\, G_{t}\, G_{r}\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 1 — Define Free-Space Path Loss as the ratio $P_{t}/P_{r}$ with unity gains:**

$$
FSPL = \frac{P_{t}}{P_{r}} \bigg|_{G_{t}=G_{r}=1} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to decibels by taking $10\log_{10}$:**

$$
FSPL_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the logarithm:**

$$
FSPL_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(4\pi) + 20 \log_{10}(d) - 20 \log_{10}(\lambda)
$$

**Step 4 — Numerically evaluate the constant $20\log_{10}(4\pi) \approx 21.98$ dB and substitute $\lambda = c/f$:**

$$
FSPL_{\text{dB}} = 21.98 + 20 \log_{10}(d) - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{c}{f}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s and rearrange into KTU's standard form (with $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz):**

$$
FSPL_{\text{dB}} = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})
$$

This is the **KTU exam-friendly FSPL formula** that you will use in 95% of problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — FSPL Difference Between Microwave and mmWave

**Given:** Two systems, one at 2.4 GHz and another at 60 GHz, both at the same distance $d$.

**Step 1 — Compute FSPL for 2.4 GHz link:**

$$
FSPL_{2.4} = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(2400)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute FSPL for 60 GHz link:**

$$
FSPL_{60} = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(60000)
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the two equations; the $d$-dependent term cancels, and the constant $32.44$ cancels:**

$$
\Delta FSPL = FSPL_{60} - FSPL_{2.4} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{60000}{2400}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(25)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerically:**

$$
\Delta FSPL = 20 \times \log_{10}(25) = 20 \times 1.39794 \approx 27.96 \text{ dB}
$$

**Conclusion:** The 60 GHz link suffers **~28 dB more path loss** than the 2.4 GHz link at the same range. This is why 5G mmWave cells are small (~200 m) while 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi cells are large (~100 m).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Antenna Gain from Physical Aperture

**Starting Point:** The effective aperture $A_{e}$ of an antenna is related to its gain $G$ by:

$$
A_{e} = \frac{G \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}
$$

**Step 1 — Solve for gain:**

$$
G = \frac{4\pi A_{e}}{\lambda^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\lambda = c/f$:**

$$
G = \frac{4\pi A_{e} f^{2}}{c^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to decibels:**

$$
G_{\text{dBi}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi A_{e} f^{2}}{c^{2}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Example:** For a square patch of side $a = 5$ cm at $f = 60$ GHz:

$$
A_{e} = (0.05)^{2} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
G = \frac{4\pi (2.5 \times 10^{-3})(60 \times 10^{9})^{2}}{(3 \times 10^{8})^{2}}
$$

$$
G = \frac{4\pi (2.5 \times 10^{-3})(3.6 \times 10^{21})}{9 \times 10^{16}}
$$

$$
G = \frac{4\pi (9.0 \times 10^{18})}{9 \times 10^{16}} = 4\pi \times 100 = 400\pi \approx 1257
$$

$$
G_{\text{dBi}} = 10 \log_{10}(1257) \approx 31 \text{ dBi}
$$

This is a **massive gain** — the reason mmWave systems can overcome high FSPL with physically small antennas.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Received Power in a mmWave Link Budget

**Problem:** A 60 GHz transmitter delivers $P_{t} = 10$ dBm into a 25 dBi horn antenna. The receiver has a 25 dBi lens antenna. Distance $d = 1$ km. Atmospheric loss $L_{atm} = 15$ dB. Rain loss $L_{rain} = 5$ dB. Compute $P_{r}$.

**Step 1 — Compute FSPL using the KTU formula:**

$$
FSPL = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(1) + 20 \log_{10}(60000)
$$

$$
FSPL = 32.44 + 0 + 20 \log_{10}(6 \times 10^{4})
$$

$$
FSPL = 32.44 + 20 (4.778) = 32.44 + 95.56 = 128 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the link-budget equation:**

$$
P_{r} = P_{t} + G_{t} + G_{r} - FSPL - L_{atm} - L_{rain}
$$

$$
P_{r} = 10 + 25 + 25 - 128 - 15 - 5
$$

$$
P_{r} = 60 - 148 = -88 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** $-88$ dBm equals approximately $1.6 \times 10^{-12}$ W. For a 1 GHz channel bandwidth at room temperature, thermal noise is roughly $-84$ dBm, so SNR $\approx 4$ dB — barely enough for a low-order modulation. The link would require **lower modulation, coding, or higher gain antennas** to be reliable.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Bandwidth Advantage (Shannon Capacity)

**Given:** Sub-6 GHz system with $B_{1} = 20$ MHz, mmWave system with $B_{2} = 800$ MHz, both at SNR = 10 dB (linear ratio = 10).

**Step 1 — Compute sub-6 capacity:**

$$
C_{1} = 20 \times 10^{6} \log_{2}(1 + 10) = 20 \times 10^{6} \times 3.46 \approx 69.2 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute mmWave capacity:**

$$
C_{2} = 800 \times 10^{6} \times 3.46 \approx 2.77 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Ratio:**

$$
\frac{C_{2}}{C_{1}} = \frac{800}{20} = 40
$$

The mmWave link is **40× faster**, purely because of bandwidth, not SNR. This is the central motivation for 5G/6G mmWave.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Atmospheric Attenuation vs Frequency

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **specific attenuation (dB/km) vs frequency (GHz)** for sea-level horizontal propagation, showing all major oxygen and water-vapor absorption peaks and the engineering "windows" used by 5G/6G.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FREQUENCY START 0 GHz"] --> B["0 to 10 GHz LOW LOSS BELOW 0.1 dB per km"]
    B --> C["22 GHz WATER VAPOR PEAK ABOUT 0.2 dB per km"]
    C --> D["28 GHz 5G WINDOW 0.07 dB per km"]
    D --> E["38 GHz 5G WINDOW 0.09 dB per km"]
    E --> F["60 GHz OXYGEN PEAK 15 dB per km FORBIDDEN"]
    F --> G["71 to 76 GHz E BAND WINDOW 0.4 dB per km"]
    G --> H["81 to 86 GHz E BAND WINDOW 0.6 dB per km"]
    H --> I["94 GHz IMAGING 0.8 dB per km"]
    I --> J["120 GHz OXYGEN PEAK 1.4 dB per km"]
    J --> K["140 GHz 6G WINDOW 1.6 dB per km"]
    K --> L["183 GHz WATER VAPOR PEAK 30 dB per km"]
    L --> M["220 to 300 GHz 6G RESEARCH ABOVE 10 dB per km"]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — mmWave Communication System Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    src1["DATA SOURCE 1 Gbps VIDEO"] --> enc1["CHANNEL ENCODER LDPC OR POLAR"]
    enc1 --> mod1["DIGITAL MODULATOR QAM 64 OR 256"]
    mod1 --> if1["IF STAGE 5 GHz"]
    if1 --> upconv1["UP CONVERTER MIXER 60 GHz LO"]
    upconv1 --> hpa1["HPA HIGH POWER AMPLIFIER 10 dBm"]
    hpa1 --> ant1["TX ANTENNA ARRAY GAIN 25 dBi"]
    ant1 --> chan1["WIRELESS CHANNEL FSPL PLUS ATM PLUS RAIN PLUS BLOCKAGE"]
    chan1 --> ant2["RX ANTENNA ARRAY GAIN 25 dBi"]
    ant2 --> lna1["LNA LOW NOISE AMPLIFIER NOISE FIGURE 5 dB"]
    lna1 --> downc1["DOWN CONVERTER MIXER 60 GHz LO"]
    downc1 --> bpf1["BASEBAND FILTER"]
    bpf1 --> demod1["DEMODULATOR QAM DECISION"]
    demod1 --> dec1["CHANNEL DECODER LDPC DECODER"]
    dec1 --> dst1["DATA SINK RECONSTRUCTED BITSTREAM"]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Seven mmWave Characteristics (Causal Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["MILLIMETER WAVE PROPERTIES"] --> n1["CHARACTERISTIC 1 SHORT WAVELENGTH 1 to 10 mm"]
    root --> n2["CHARACTERISTIC 2 HIGH FREE SPACE PATH LOSS"]
    root --> n3["CHARACTERISTIC 3 MOLECULAR ABSORPTION PEAKS"]
    root --> n4["CHARACTERISTIC 4 RAIN ATTENUATION"]
    root --> n5["CHARACTERISTIC 5 POOR DIFFRACTION AND PENETRATION"]
    root --> n6["CHARACTERISTIC 6 SMALL ANTENNA FOOTPRINT"]
    root --> n7["CHARACTERISTIC 7 WIDE BANDWIDTH"]
    n1 --> e1["ENABLES COMPACT ANTENNA ARRAYS"]
    n2 --> e2["REQUIRES HIGH GAIN BEAMFORMING"]
    n3 --> e3["CREATES FREQUENCY WINDOWS 28 38 60 94 E BAND"]
    n4 --> e4["LIMITS CELL RADIUS IN MONSOON CLIMATES"]
    n5 --> e5["NEEDS DENSE SMALL CELLS AND NLOS REFLECTORS"]
    n6 --> e6["SUPPORTS MASSIVE MIMO IN MOBILE HANDSET"]

```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — KTU mmWave Link-Budget Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1["STEP 1 TX POWER Pt dBm"] --> p2["STEP 2 ADD TX ANTENNA GAIN Gt dBi"]
    p2 --> p3["STEP 3 COMPUTE FSPL dB FROM 32.44 PLUS 20 LOG d PLUS 20 LOG f"]
    p3 --> p4["STEP 4 SUBTRACT RAIN ATTENUATION Lrain dB"]
    p4 --> p5["STEP 5 SUBTRACT ATMOSPHERIC ABSORPTION Latm dB"]
    p5 --> p6["STEP 6 SUBTRACT BLOCKAGE AND FADING MARGIN Lmargin dB"]
    p6 --> p7["STEP 7 ADD RX ANTENNA GAIN Gr dBi"]
    p7 --> p8["STEP 8 RECEIVED POWER Pr dBm RESULT"]
    p8 --> p9["STEP 9 COMPARE WITH THERMAL NOISE kTB dBm"]

```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERAT753]**
*List any three characteristics of millimeter waves that distinguish them from conventional microwave signals.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**

The three principal distinguishing characteristics of mmWaves are:

1. **Higher free-space path loss:** Since $FSPL \propto f^{2}$, mmWaves at 60 GHz suffer ~28 dB more loss than 2.4 GHz microwaves at the same distance, requiring high-gain antennas.

2. **Severe atmospheric and rain attenuation:** Specific absorption peaks at **22 GHz (water vapor)**, **60 GHz (oxygen)**, and **183 GHz (water vapor)** limit propagation; heavy rain introduces additional scattering loss that can shrink the cell radius to a few hundred meters.

3. **Very short wavelength enabling compact antenna arrays:** Wavelengths of 1–10 mm permit physically small antennas, allowing **on-chip phased arrays and massive MIMO** to be integrated into handsets and base stations — something impossible at sub-6 GHz.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PERAT753]**
*What is the significance of the 60 GHz band in mmWave communication? Why is it preferred for short-range indoor links despite its high oxygen absorption?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 60 GHz band lies at the centre of a **strong oxygen (O₂) molecular absorption peak** of approximately **15 dB/km**, which would normally disqualify it for long-range use. However, this is precisely the reason it is **preferred for short-range indoor links (1–10 m)** such as **WiGig (IEEE 802.11ad/ay)**, wireless docking, and AR/VR:

- The 60 GHz band is **license-free worldwide** in most jurisdictions (e.g., 57–64 GHz in the US, 57–66 GHz in Europe), so no spectrum auction cost is incurred.
- The high atmospheric attenuation acts as a **natural security/shielding mechanism**, preventing interference between adjacent rooms and minimising co-channel reuse issues in dense deployments.
- The short wavelength enables **multi-Gbps links** over ~5 GHz of contiguous bandwidth.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part-B question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Internal choice is given between **Question A** and **Question B** with comparable cognitive level. Both options below are designed at **Apply / Analyse (Bloom Level 3–4)**.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the free-space path loss expression $FSPL_{\text{dB}} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$, clearly stating the units of $d$ and $f$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5G base station transmits at $f = 28$ GHz with $P_{t} = 30$ dBm, antenna gain $G_{t} = G_{r} = 20$ dBi, distance $d = 500$ m, atmospheric loss $L_{atm} = 1.5$ dB, rain loss $L_{rain} = 2$ dB, and implementation margin $L_{impl} = 3$ dB. Compute the received power $P_{r}$ in dBm. Is the link viable if the receiver noise floor is $-95$ dBm at 100 MHz bandwidth? **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Start with the Friis equation:

$$
P_{r} = P_{t}\, G_{t}\, G_{r}\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Define FSPL for isotropic antennas ($G_{t}=G_{r}=1$):

$$
FSPL = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Take $10\log_{10}$ and substitute $\lambda = c/f$:

$$
FSPL_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{c/f}\right) = 20\log_{10}(4\pi) + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) - 20\log_{10}(c)
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Plug in $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s and convert $d$ to km, $f$ to MHz; the constant evaluates to **32.44**, yielding:

$$
FSPL_{\text{dB}} = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})
$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute FSPL with $d = 0.5$ km and $f = 28000$ MHz:

$$
FSPL = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(0.5) + 20 \log_{10}(28000)
$$

$$
FSPL = 32.44 + 20(-0.301) + 20(4.447)
$$

$$
FSPL = 32.44 - 6.02 + 88.94 = 115.36 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Sum total losses:

$$
L_{\text{total}} = FSPL + L_{atm} + L_{rain} + L_{impl} = 115.36 + 1.5 + 2 + 3 = 121.86 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply link budget:

$$
P_{r} = P_{t} + G_{t} + G_{r} - L_{\text{total}}
$$

$$
P_{r} = 30 + 20 + 20 - 121.86 = -51.86 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Viability check: $P_{r} = -51.86$ dBm is **43 dB above** the $-95$ dBm noise floor, so SNR $\approx 43$ dB. The link is **highly viable** with margin for higher-order modulation (e.g., 64-QAM or 256-QAM).

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **rain attenuation** in mmWave propagation. Using the ITU-R power-law $A = aR^{b}$ (dB/km), calculate the specific attenuation at 60 GHz for a rain rate of 25 mm/hr, given $a = 0.187$, $b = 1.04$. If a link is 3 km long, what is the total rain-induced path loss? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **free-space path loss** at 2.4 GHz, 28 GHz, and 60 GHz for a fixed distance of 100 m. Comment on the engineering implications for cell radius. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Rain attenuation arises because raindrop diameters (0.1–3 mm) are comparable to mmWavelengths, causing **Mie scattering** and **absorption**. The ITU-R model $A = aR^{b}$ (dB/km) gives the specific attenuation as a power law of the rainfall rate $R$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute $R = 25$ mm/hr, $a = 0.187$, $b = 1.04$:

$$
A = 0.187 \times (25)^{1.04}
$$

$$
A = 0.187 \times 28.84 = 5.39 \text{ dB/km}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** For a 3 km link, total rain loss:

$$
L_{\text{rain,total}} = 5.39 \times 3 = 16.17 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Implication: the link budget must reserve at least 16 dB margin, or use adaptive modulation, to maintain availability during moderate rain.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (3 marks):** Use $FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$ with $d = 0.1$ km:

- **2.4 GHz:** $FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(0.1) + 20\log_{10}(2400) = 32.44 - 20 + 67.6 = 80.04$ dB
- **28 GHz:** $FSPL = 32.44 - 20 + 20\log_{10}(28000) = 32.44 - 20 + 88.94 = 101.38$ dB
- **60 GHz:** $FSPL = 32.44 - 20 + 20\log_{10}(60000) = 32.44 - 20 + 95.56 = 108.00$ dB

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Loss difference: $108 - 80 = 28$ dB between 2.4 GHz and 60 GHz — a **600× power penalty**.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **Engineering implications:**

- The 60 GHz cell radius (assuming same EIRP, same noise floor) is **~25× smaller** than 2.4 GHz for identical SNR.
- Practical mmWave cell radii in urban 5G deployments are **100–300 m** versus **1–3 km** for sub-6 GHz macro cells.
- This drives the requirement for **ultra-dense small-cell networks** and **high-gain beamforming** to make mmWave economically viable.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost 2–4 marks in KTU 2024 mmWave questions:**
> 1. **Unit confusion in FSPL:** Forgetting to convert $d$ to **km** and $f$ to **MHz** before applying the constant 32.44. Always write the unit in brackets next to the value.
> 2. **Adding instead of subtracting losses:** A frequent error is computing $P_{r} = P_{t} + G_{t} + G_{r} + FSPL + L_{\text{rain}}$ instead of subtracting. **Losses are subtracted, gains are added.**
> 3. **Forgetting the conversion factor 21.98 vs 32.44:** The 32.44 constant already includes the conversion from $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz; do not double-apply it.
> 4. **Mixing dBi with dBd:** Antenna gain in KTU answers should be in **dBi** unless explicitly stated otherwise. 1 dBd = 2.15 dBi.
> 5. **Treating FSPL as frequency-independent:** Many students write a single FSPL value for "all microwave frequencies." Show explicitly how loss scales as $20\log_{10}(f)$.
> 6. **Skipping the Friis derivation step:** Part (a) of 14-mark questions almost always requires derivation, not just substitution. Start from the Friis equation every time.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU revision — read this twice before the exam.**

- ☐ mmWaves = **30–300 GHz** = **1–10 mm** wavelength.
- ☐ Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s; relation $\lambda = c/f$ must be on your fingertip.
- ☐ **FSPL formula (KTU form):** $FSPL_{\text{dB}} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$.
- ☐ Doubling $f$ adds **6 dB** of FSPL; doubling $d$ adds **6 dB** of FSPL.
- ☐ Friis equation: $P_{r} = P_{t} G_{t} G_{r} (\lambda / 4\pi d)^{2}$.
- ☐ **Atmospheric peaks to memorise:** 22 GHz (H₂O), 60 GHz (O₂ — 15 dB/km), 120 GHz (O₂), 183 GHz (H₂O).
- ☐ **5G FR2 windows:** 28 GHz, 38 GHz, and the E-band (71–76 / 81–86 GHz).
- ☐ **Rain attenuation ITU model:** $A = aR^{b}$ dB/km — values of $a$, $b$ are frequency-dependent.
- ☐ **Antenna gain:** $G = 4\pi A_{e} f^{2} / c^{2}$ — gain scales with $f^{2}$, mitigating FSPL.
- ☐ **Beamwidth:** $\theta_{3\text{dB}} \approx 0.886 \lambda / D$ — higher $f$ → narrower beam.
- ☐ **Shannon capacity:** $C = B\log_{2}(1+\text{SNR})$ — mmWave wins because $B$ is huge.
- ☐ mmWave key impairments: **high path loss, atmospheric absorption, rain loss, blockage, NLOS difficulty**.
- ☐ mmWave key advantages: **massive bandwidth, multi-Gbps, compact antennas, secure cells, frequency reuse**.
- ☐ Link budget order: $P_{r} = P_{t} + G_{t} + G_{r} - FSPL - L_{atm} - L_{rain} - L_{margin}$.
- ☐ **Common KTU pitfall:** never confuse $G_{t} + G_{r}$ with antenna gain of a single antenna.
- ☐ **Indoor vs outdoor:** 60 GHz works well indoors (short range, security); 28/38 GHz preferred for outdoor 5G.
- ☐ Remember: mmWaves are **quasi-optical**; treat them like light beams, not radio waves.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Channel Performance at 60 GHz

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Channel Performance at 60 GHz — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **60 GHz band** (spanning roughly **57–64 GHz**, falling in the V-band of the millimeter-wave spectrum) is a section of the electromagnetic spectrum characterized by a carrier wavelength of approximately **λ = 5 mm**. Channel performance at 60 GHz describes the combined effect of **free-space path loss (FSPL)**, **molecular (atmospheric) absorption**, **precipitation (rain) attenuation**, **multipath fading**, and **penetration loss** on a radio signal propagating through this band. The unique oxygen-absorption resonance near 60 GHz makes this band one of the most lossy portions of the RF spectrum, restricting its use primarily to **short-range, high-capacity wireless systems** (e.g., IEEE 802.11ad/ay WiGig, 5G NR FR2+, vehicular radar, point-to-point backhaul).

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value at 60 GHz |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Carrier Frequency | $f_c$ | **60 GHz** |
| Wavelength | $\lambda$ | **5 mm** |
| Bandwidth (unlicensed) | $BW$ | **≈ 7 GHz** (57–64 GHz) |
| Oxygen Absorption Peak | $\alpha_{O_2}$ | **≈ 15 dB/km** (at sea level) |
| Free-Space Path Loss @ 1 m | $FSPL$ | **≈ 88 dB** |
| Range (typical) | $d$ | **< 200 m** (indoor), up to ~1 km (LOS) |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine shouting to a friend across a wide open field — your voice travels easily. Now imagine shouting through a thick fog — the sound gets absorbed, scattered, and loses strength quickly. **The 60 GHz band behaves exactly like this fog scenario for radio waves.**

- **Air molecules (especially oxygen)** resonate naturally at 60 GHz, "drinking up" the energy from the radio wave as it travels.
- The **very short wavelength (5 mm)** means waves cannot bend easily around obstacles — they act almost like light rays (line-of-sight behavior).
- **Raindrops** are physically large compared to the 5 mm wavelength, scattering the energy aggressively (think of headlights in heavy rain).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 60 GHz is still useful despite being "lossy":** The huge available bandwidth (≈ 7 GHz continuous, license-free in most jurisdictions) compensates for the severe path loss by allowing **ultra-wideband signaling**, which translates into multi-Gbps data rates. Engineers "fight" the loss with **high-gain directional antennas**, **beamforming**, and **small cell topologies**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key constants used throughout this module:**
> - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s**
> - Boltzmann constant: $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
> - Thermal noise floor at 290 K: $N_0 = -174$ **dBm/Hz**
> - 60 GHz oxygen absorption peak: **15 dB/km** (at sea level, standard pressure)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the 60 GHz Channel

The total loss experienced by a 60 GHz signal can be written as a sum of physically distinct mechanisms:

### Step 1 — Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)
The **Friis transmission equation** gives the received power in an ideal, unobstructed isotropic environment. The FSPL is the loss component assuming perfect spherical spreading in vacuum.

### Step 2 — Atmospheric (Gaseous) Absorption
Oxygen ($O_2$) and water vapor ($H_2O$) molecules exhibit rotational absorption lines that peak near 60 GHz. This is modeled as a **distance-dependent attenuation** with a specific attenuation coefficient $\alpha_{atm}$ measured in **dB/km**.

### Step 3 — Rain Attenuation
Raindrops cause both **scattering** (Mie regime — drops are larger than $\lambda$) and **absorption**. Specific rain attenuation is modeled using the **ITU-R P.838** power law: $\gamma_R = k \cdot R^{\alpha}$, where $R$ is the rain rate in mm/hr.

### Step 4 — Multipath and Fading
Due to the small wavelength, **diffuse scattering** is weak, and the channel behaves more like **quasi-optical** propagation. The RMS delay spread is typically **< 10 ns** indoors, but severe **blockage** occurs when a human body crosses the link (≈ 20–30 dB loss).

### Step 5 — Penetration and Diffraction Loss
Common building materials cause extreme loss:
- **Drywall / plaster:** ≈ 3–6 dB
- **Glass (clear):** ≈ 3–10 dB
- **Concrete:** ≈ 30–40 dB
- **Human body (blockage):** ≈ 20–30 dB

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $FSPL(d) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$ | Free-space path loss (linear distance $d$) | dB |
| 2 | $FSPL(d) = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$ | FSPL in dB (f in MHz, d in km) | dB |
| 3 | $L_{atm}(d) = \alpha_{O_2} \cdot d$ | Atmospheric (oxygen) attenuation; $\alpha_{O_2} \approx 15$ dB/km at 60 GHz | dB |
| 4 | $\gamma_R = k \cdot R^{\alpha}$ | Specific rain attenuation (ITU-R P.838) | dB/km |
| 5 | $A_{rain}(d) = \gamma_R \cdot d_{eff}$ | Rain attenuation over effective path length | dB |
| 6 | $L_{total}(d) = FSPL(d) + A_{atm}(d) + A_{rain}(d) + L_{misc}$ | Total propagation loss | dB |
| 7 | $P_r(d) = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{total}(d)$ | Received power (link budget) | dBm |
| 8 | $N = k \cdot T \cdot BW$ | Thermal noise power (W) | W |
| 9 | $SNR = P_r / N$ | Signal-to-noise ratio (linear) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C = BW \cdot \log_{2}(1 + SNR)$ | Shannon channel capacity (Shannon-Hartley) | bits/s |
| 11 | $E_b/N_0 = \dfrac{SNR}{R_b / BW}$ | Energy-per-bit to noise spectral density ratio | dB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb (60 GHz only):** $FSPL(d) \approx 68 + 20\log_{10}(d_{m})$ dB, where $d$ is in meters. Add **15 dB per km** for oxygen, plus rain attenuation.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **5G NR FR2+ & IEEE 802.11ay Backhaul:** Designers use these loss models to size link budgets and determine **maximum cell radius** (typically 100–200 m in dense urban deployment).
- **Automotive Radar (76–81 GHz, adjacent band):** Uses similar atmospheric models for collision-avoidance sensors.
- **Wireless Personal Area Networks (WPAN):** WiGig and Virtual Reality (VR) tethering rely on these channel characteristics.
- **Point-to-Point Backhaul Links:** Use highly directional dish antennas (gain ≈ 30–45 dBi) to overcome 60 GHz path loss for distances up to 1 km.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The severe path loss of 60 GHz is actually a *feature* in dense deployments — it enables **high frequency reuse** because signals do not leak far from their intended cell, reducing inter-cell interference.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Free-Space Path Loss at 60 GHz

We begin from the **Friis transmission equation** for isotropic antennas:

$$
\dfrac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2
$$

For **isotropic antennas** ($G_t = G_r = 1$), the ratio becomes:

$$
\dfrac{P_r}{P_t} = \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2
$$

By definition, the **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** is the reciprocal (loss, not gain), expressed in decibels:

$$
FSPL(d) = \left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 \quad \text{(linear)}
$$

$$
FSPL_{dB}(d) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)
$$

### Substituting $\lambda = c / f$

Replace the wavelength with the carrier frequency:

$$
FSPL_{dB}(d) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d \cdot f}{c}\right)
$$

For engineering convenience, evaluate the constant term in **dB**:

$$
20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^8}\right)
$$

We compute this numerically step-by-step:

$$
\dfrac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^8} = \dfrac{12.566}{3 \times 10^8} = 4.1888 \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(4.1888 \times 10^{-8}) = 20\bigl[\log_{10}(4.1888) + \log_{10}(10^{-8})\bigr]
$$

$$
= 20 \cdot (0.6222 - 8) = 20 \cdot (-7.3778) = -147.55 \text{ dB}
$$

Therefore the general form becomes:

$$
FSPL_{dB}(d) = -147.55 + 20\log_{10}(f_{Hz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{m})
$$

Converting $f$ from Hz to **MHz** (multiply by $10^6$, adds $120$ dB) and $d$ from m to **km** (divide by $10^3$, adds $-60$ dB):

$$
FSPL_{dB}(d) = -147.55 + 120 - 60 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})
$$

$$
\boxed{FSPL_{dB}(d) = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})}
$$

### Numerical Evaluation at $f = 60$ GHz, $d = 10$ m

Convert units: $f_{MHz} = 60{,}000$ and $d_{km} = 0.01$.

$$
20\log_{10}(60{,}000) = 20 \cdot (4.7782) = 95.56 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(0.01) = 20 \cdot (-2) = -40 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
FSPL = 32.45 + 95.56 - 40 = 88.01 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** At 10 m, the FSPL at 60 GHz is **≈ 88 dB**, compared to ≈ **62 dB at 2.4 GHz** and ≈ **68 dB at 5 GHz**. The 20–26 dB extra loss at 60 GHz is the first major system design challenge.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Link-Budget at 60 GHz

The **link-budget equation** in dB form is:

$$
P_r(d) = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{total}(d)
$$

where the total loss is:

$$
L_{total}(d) = FSPL(d) + A_{atm}(d) + A_{rain}(d) + L_{misc}
$$

### Worked Example
**System parameters:**
- Transmit power: $P_t = +10$ dBm
- Tx antenna gain: $G_t = 25$ dBi (directional horn)
- Rx antenna gain: $G_r = 25$ dBi
- Distance: $d = 50$ m
- Frequency: $f = 60$ GHz
- Oxygen absorption: $\alpha_{O_2} = 15$ dB/km
- Rain rate: $R = 25$ mm/hr (heavy rain), with $k = 0.2301$, $\alpha = 0.9229$ (ITU-R P.838 vertical, 60 GHz)
- Miscellaneous loss: $L_{misc} = 3$ dB (cables + connectors)

### Step 1: Compute FSPL at 50 m

$$
FSPL(50\text{ m}) = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(60{,}000) + 20\log_{10}(0.05)
$$

$$
= 32.45 + 95.56 + 20\log_{10}(0.05)
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(0.05) = 20 \cdot (-1.301) = -26.02 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
FSPL = 32.45 + 95.56 - 26.02 = 101.99 \text{ dB}
$$

### Step 2: Atmospheric Attenuation at 50 m

Convert distance to km: $d = 0.05$ km.

$$
A_{atm} = 15 \text{ dB/km} \times 0.05 \text{ km} = 0.75 \text{ dB}
$$

### Step 3: Rain Attenuation at 50 m

$$
\gamma_R = k \cdot R^{\alpha} = 0.2301 \cdot (25)^{0.9229}
$$

$$
(25)^{0.9229} = e^{0.9229 \cdot \ln(25)} = e^{0.9229 \cdot 3.2189} = e^{2.971} = 19.51
$$

$$
\gamma_R = 0.2301 \cdot 19.51 = 4.49 \text{ dB/km}
$$

$$
A_{rain} = 4.49 \times 0.05 = 0.225 \text{ dB}
$$

### Step 4: Total Loss

$$
L_{total} = 101.99 + 0.75 + 0.225 + 3 = 105.97 \text{ dB}
$$

### Step 5: Received Power

$$
P_r = 10 + 25 + 25 - 105.97 = -45.97 \text{ dBm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The receiver sees approximately **−46 dBm** of signal power at 50 m. Whether this is usable depends on the **noise floor** and **required SNR** for the chosen modulation (typically −68 to −74 dBm for 64-QAM at 1 Gbps).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Shannon Capacity at 60 GHz

Assume the system uses $BW = 1.76$ GHz (a typical 802.11ad channel bandwidth). The noise floor is:

$$
N = -174 \text{ dBm/Hz} + 10\log_{10}(1.76 \times 10^9) + NF
$$

With a noise figure $NF = 8$ dB:

$$
N = -174 + 92.45 + 8 = -73.55 \text{ dBm}
$$

From Section 3.2, $P_r = -45.97$ dBm, so:

$$
SNR_{dB} = -45.97 - (-73.55) = 27.58 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
SNR_{linear} = 10^{27.58/10} = 10^{2.758} = 572.8
$$

Shannon capacity:

$$
C = BW \cdot \log_2(1 + SNR) = 1.76 \times 10^9 \cdot \log_2(573.8)
$$

$$
\log_2(573.8) = \dfrac{\ln(573.8)}{\ln(2)} = \dfrac{6.352}{0.693} = 9.165 \text{ bits/s/Hz}
$$

$$
\boxed{C = 1.76 \times 10^9 \times 9.165 \approx 16.13 \text{ Gbps}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** Practical 802.11ad systems achieve ≈ **4.6 Gbps** at 50 m because real implementations use lower-order constellations, pilots, and coding — but Shannon gives the hard theoretical ceiling.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q1.** State and explain the **Friis free-space transmission equation**. Why is the free-space path loss at 60 GHz significantly higher than at 2.4 GHz for the same distance?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Friis equation gives the ratio of received to transmitted power in free space for isotropic antennas:
> $$\dfrac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$
> In dB: $FSPL = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$.
> At the same distance, doubling the frequency increases FSPL by 6 dB. Going from 2.4 GHz to 60 GHz is a **factor of 25 increase in frequency**, giving an extra $20\log_{10}(25) = 27.96$ dB of FSPL. Hence, 60 GHz suffers ≈ 28 dB more path loss than 2.4 GHz. **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q2.** What is the **oxygen absorption peak**, and how does it influence the choice of carrier frequency for short-range communication?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Atmospheric oxygen molecules exhibit a rotational absorption line near **60 GHz**, with a peak specific attenuation of approximately **15 dB/km** at sea level. This resonance makes 60 GHz signals attenuate rapidly in air, limiting outdoor range to a few hundred meters. However, this same property is exploited to enable **high frequency reuse** in dense deployments and to confine signals within rooms (security benefit), making the band ideal for indoor short-range high-data-rate systems like WiGig and 5G hotspots. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B1]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the **free-space path loss formula** in dB from the Friis transmission equation. Compute the FSPL at **60 GHz** for a distance of **20 m**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 60 GHz link has the following parameters: $P_t = 0$ dBm, $G_t = 30$ dBi, $G_r = 30$ dBi, distance $d = 30$ m, oxygen attenuation $\alpha_{O_2} = 15$ dB/km, rain rate $R = 10$ mm/hr with $k = 0.2301$, $\alpha = 0.9229$, and miscellaneous loss $L_{misc} = 2$ dB. Calculate the **received power** and **Shannon capacity** for a system bandwidth of 1 GHz and noise figure 7 dB. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — FSPL Derivation
Start with Friis equation (isotropic case, $G_t = G_r = 1$):

$$
\dfrac{P_r}{P_t} = \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2
$$

The **path loss** is the inverse of this ratio:

$$
PL = \left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2
$$

Convert to dB:

$$
PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)
$$

Substitute $\lambda = c / f$:

$$
PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d f}{c}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right) + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)
$$

Evaluating the constant $\,20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^8}\right) = -147.55\,$ dB, and converting to MHz and km:

$$
\boxed{PL_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})}
$$

**Numerical substitution** at $f = 60$ GHz, $d = 20$ m = 0.02 km:

$$
PL_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(60{,}000) + 20\log_{10}(0.02)
$$

$$
= 32.45 + 95.56 + (-33.98) = 94.03 \text{ dB}
$$

> **Part (a) Final Answer:** $FSPL(20\text{ m}, 60\text{ GHz}) = \mathbf{94.03 \text{ dB}}$ **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Link Budget and Capacity

**Step 1: FSPL at 30 m**

Convert: $d = 0.03$ km.

$$
FSPL = 32.45 + 95.56 + 20\log_{10}(0.03) = 32.45 + 95.56 - 30.46 = 97.55 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2: Atmospheric Loss**

$$
A_{atm} = 15 \times 0.03 = 0.45 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3: Rain Attenuation**

$$
\gamma_R = 0.2301 \cdot (10)^{0.9229} = 0.2301 \cdot 8.376 = 1.927 \text{ dB/km}
$$

$$
A_{rain} = 1.927 \times 0.03 = 0.058 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4: Total Loss**

$$
L_{total} = 97.55 + 0.45 + 0.058 + 2 = 100.06 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 5: Received Power**

$$
P_r = 0 + 30 + 30 - 100.06 = -40.06 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Step 6: Noise Floor**

$$
N = -174 + 10\log_{10}(10^9) + 7 = -174 + 90 + 7 = -77 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Step 7: SNR**

$$
SNR = -40.06 - (-77) = 36.94 \text{ dB} \quad \Rightarrow \quad SNR_{lin} = 10^{3.694} = 4943
$$

**Step 8: Shannon Capacity**

$$
C = 10^9 \cdot \log_2(4944) = 10^9 \cdot 12.27 = 12.27 \text{ Gbps}
$$

> **Part (b) Final Answer:** $P_r = -40.06$ dBm, $C = 12.27$ Gbps. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B2]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **oxygen absorption mechanism** near 60 GHz. With the help of the **ITU-R rain attenuation model**, derive the expression for rain-induced specific attenuation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 60 GHz point-to-point link is designed for a target received power of −60 dBm at 100 m. The transmitter delivers $P_t = 20$ dBm, with $G_t = 35$ dBi and $G_r = 35$ dBi. Compute the **maximum allowable total path loss** and the corresponding **oxygen plus rain attenuation budget** if FSPL is 108 dB. Neglect miscellaneous loss. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Absorption and Rain Models
**Oxygen Absorption:** The oxygen molecule O₂ has a permanent magnetic dipole moment due to its two unpaired electrons. At frequencies near 60 GHz, the rotational energy transitions of the molecule resonate with the incoming electromagnetic field, converting EM energy into kinetic (heat) energy. The peak occurs at **60 GHz with ≈ 15 dB/km** at sea level. As altitude increases, atmospheric pressure drops and the peak broadens and weakens. The absorption coefficient depends on **pressure, temperature, and humidity** and is given by the Van Vleck–Weisskopf line shape summed over the relevant resonance frequencies.

**Rain Attenuation Model (ITU-R P.838):** For a given frequency and polarization, the specific rain attenuation is:

$$
\gamma_R = k \cdot R^{\alpha} \quad \text{[dB/km]}
$$

where $R$ is the rain rate in **mm/hr** and $k$, $\alpha$ are frequency- and polarization-dependent coefficients (tabulated in ITU-R P.838). For 60 GHz, vertical polarization, $k \approx 0.2301$ and $\alpha \approx 0.9229$.

**Total rain attenuation** over an effective path length $d_{eff}$ is:

$$
A_{rain} = \gamma_R \cdot d_{eff} = k \cdot R^{\alpha} \cdot d_{eff}
$$

**(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Link-Budget Reverse Calculation

**Step 1: Maximum Allowable Total Loss**

$$
L_{max} = P_t + G_t + G_r - P_{r,min}
$$

$$
L_{max} = 20 + 35 + 35 - (-60) = 150 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2: Reserved Loss for Atmospheric + Rain**

$$
A_{atm} + A_{rain} = L_{max} - FSPL = 150 - 108 = 42 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3: Validation**
At 100 m, oxygen absorption contributes only $15 \times 0.1 = 1.5$ dB. Heavy rain ($R = 100$ mm/hr) contributes approximately $\gamma_R = 0.2301 \cdot 100^{0.9229} = 17.4$ dB/km, giving 1.74 dB over 100 m. Total atmospheric+rain ≈ 3.24 dB, far below the 42 dB budget — meaning the design has a **safe margin of 38.76 dB** for additional implementation losses.

> **Part (b) Final Answer:** $L_{max} = 150$ dB; $A_{atm} + A_{rain}$ budget = 42 dB. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (PERAT753 Examiners' Notes):**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** The most frequent error is mixing units in the FSPL formula (using $f$ in GHz but $d$ in m, or vice versa). The formula $32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$ is **strict** — mixing units costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting Oxygen Loss:** Examiners specifically look for the **15 dB/km oxygen absorption** term. Ignoring it loses 2 marks in link-budget problems.
> 3. **Sign Errors in dB Addition:** Remember that all gains and the transmit power are **added**, and all losses are **subtracted**. A common sign flip costs a full 7-mark sub-question.
> 4. **Confusing $E_b/N_0$ with SNR:** $SNR = E_b/N_0 \cdot (R_b/BW)$. Students often use the wrong ratio. Show the conversion explicitly.
> 5. **Neglecting the Noise Figure:** $NF$ must be **added** to the thermal noise floor when computing $N$ at the receiver input. Forgetting $NF$ inflates the SNR falsely.
> 6. **Wrong Shannon Formula:** Use $C = BW \cdot \log_2(1 + SNR)$, **not** $\log_{10}$. This single error has caused many full-mark deductions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Channel Performance at 60 GHz:**
> - **Carrier:** 60 GHz lies in the V-band; wavelength ≈ 5 mm; bandwidth ≈ 7 GHz.
> - **Dominant Loss 1 — FSPL:** $32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$; ≈ 88 dB at 10 m, ≈ 128 dB at 1 km.
> - **Dominant Loss 2 — Oxygen Absorption:** Peak **15 dB/km** at 60 GHz sea level.
> - **Dominant Loss 3 — Rain:** Specific attenuation $\gamma_R = k R^{\alpha}$ (ITU-R P.838).
> - **Multipath:** Weak diffuse scattering due to small $\lambda$; **blockage by human body = 20–30 dB**.
> - **Penetration Loss:** Drywall (3–6 dB), glass (3–10 dB), concrete (30–40 dB).
> - **Link Budget:** $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - (FSPL + A_{atm} + A_{rain} + L_{misc})$.
> - **Thermal Noise:** $N = kTB$; in dBm: $N_{dBm} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(BW) + NF$.
> - **Shannon Limit:** $C = BW \cdot \log_2(1 + SNR)$; practical 802.11ad systems achieve ~5 Gbps; theoretical limit > 10 Gbps in many scenarios.
> - **Engineering Strategies:** Use **high-gain directional antennas**, **beamforming**, and **small cells** to overcome the severe path loss.
> - **Memorize these key numbers:** 88 dB @ 10 m, 15 dB/km O₂, 7 GHz bandwidth, 5 mm wavelength.
> - **Exam Tip:** Always draw a block diagram of the link budget for full marks on 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Development of Millimeter Wave Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Development of Millimeter Wave Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Millimeter Wave (mmWave) Standards** are a class of communication standards formally developed by international standards bodies (IEEE, 3GPP, ITU-R, ECMA) that govern the physical layer (PHY) and medium access control (MAC) operation of wireless systems operating in the frequency range **30 GHz to 300 GHz** (corresponding wavelengths of **1 mm to 10 mm**).

For the KTU 2024 PERAT753 syllabus, the *development* of these standards is studied as a chronological and architectural process in which channel measurements, regulatory spectrum allocation, antenna/MIMO research, and application requirements (5G, WLAN, WPAN) converge into ratified specifications such as **IEEE 802.15.3c (2009)**, **IEEE 802.11ad (2012)**, **IEEE 802.11ay (2021)** and **3GPP 5G NR FR2 (Release 15 onward)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Snapshot:** *A millimeter wave standard is the codified set of PHY/MAC rules — frequency band, channelization, modulation/coding, beamforming, frame structure and coexistence — that allows interoperable deployment of radios in the 30–300 GHz spectrum.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a **highway system**. Low-frequency bands (sub-6 GHz) are like broad 6-lane national highways: they carry traffic far, but become congested because every vehicle (cellphone, TV, Wi-Fi, satellite) competes for the same road. mmWave bands are like a brand-new, **multi-tier elevated expressway** built specifically above 30 GHz: it is mostly empty, offers enormous bandwidth, and supports very high speeds, *but* the road surface is sensitive to rain, fog, and buildings, and the on-ramps (antennas) must be precisely aimed.

**Standards are the *traffic laws* of that expressway** — they define the lane width (channel bandwidth), speed limit (modulation order), vehicle type (frame format), and how on-ramp signals are steered (beamforming protocol). Without them, devices from different vendors would crash into each other instead of cooperating.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s**
- Wavelength–frequency relation: $\lambda = c / f$
- Standard mmWave anchor frequencies: **28 GHz**, **38 GHz**, **60 GHz**, **73 GHz**, **140 GHz**
- **60 GHz band**: 57–66 GHz (a *radiation hazard* band per ITU-R, with **~15 dB/km oxygen absorption peak**)
- **5G NR FR2 bands**: n257 (26.5–29.5 GHz), n258 (24.25–27.5 GHz), n259 (39.5–43.5 GHz), n260 (37–40 GHz), n261 (27.5–28.35 GHz)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Development" matters in the KTU Module 2 context:** The question is not just *what* the standards are, but *how* channel-sounder campaigns (e.g., NYU WIRELESS at 28/38/60/73 GHz, NIST 83.5/140 GHz), regulatory work in WRC-15/WRC-19, and consensus in IEEE/3GPP transformed raw spectrum into a deployable technology. The narrative has four pillars: **(i) spectrum allocation, (ii) channel modeling, (iii) PHY design, (iv) MAC/beam-management protocols.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Stage Architecture of mmWave Standardization

The development of any mmWave standard follows a deterministic four-stage pipeline, mapped to the KTU module learning outcome **CO2 (Analyze mmWave physical layer and channel characteristics)**.

1. **Stage 1 – Spectrum Regulation (ITU-R / FCC / ETSI)**
   - Identify usable bands. The **60 GHz band** has a globally harmonized ~7 GHz unlicensed window (57–64 GHz in US, 57–66 GHz in EU/JP).
   - **5G mmWave** bands were first identified at **WRC-15 (Geneva, November 2015)** for IMT-2020.

2. **Stage 2 – Channel Measurement & Modeling**
   - Statistical channel models (S-V, NYUSIM, 3GPP TR 38.901) replace deterministic ray-tracing for system-level simulation.
   - Key parameters: path loss exponents ($\alpha$), shadow fading variance ($\sigma_{SF}$), RMS delay spread ($\tau_{rms}$), angular spread (ASA, ASD, ZSA, ZSD), and cluster parameters.

3. **Stage 3 – PHY Specification (IEEE / 3GPP)**
   - Modulation/coding (MCS) tables, OFDM/SC numerologies, pilot patterns, beamforming reference signals.

4. **Stage 4 – MAC & Higher Layers (Multi-Gigabit, Beam Management)**
   - Beam training, beam tracking, sector sweep, association, and channel access in directional networks.

### 2.2 Major Standards — Engineering "Why" and "How"

#### 2.2.1 IEEE 802.15.3c (2009) — First 60 GHz WPAN Standard

- **Why**: Provide *short-range, point-to-point* multi-Gbps connectivity for cable replacement (USB 3.0, HDMI).
- **How**: Defined an mmWave PHY using **OFDM** (672 MHz × 4 mandatory) and a **single-carrier (SC)** mode; supports **4 modulation modes** (BPSK, QPSK, 16-QAM with 2/3 LDPC) and a directional MAC.

#### 2.2.2 IEEE 802.11ad (WiGig, 2012) — 60 GHz WLAN

- **Why**: Extend Wi-Fi to 7 Gbps in the 60 GHz band while coexisting with 2.4/5 GHz via *Fast Session Transfer*.
- **How**: Defines 3 PHY modes — **Control PHY (BPSK, MCS0)**, **SC PHY (π/2-BPSK to π/2-64-QAM, MCS1–12)** for single-carrier operation, and **OFDM PHY (BPSK to 64-QAM, MCS13–24)**. Channel bandwidth: **2160 MHz**; total throughput **up to 6.75 Gbps** (later 7 Gbps).

#### 2.2.3 IEEE 802.11ay (2021) — Next-Generation 60 GHz

- **Why**: Push 802.11ad beyond 100 Gbps, support **channel bonding (2.16 + 2.16 = 4.32 GHz, up to 8.64 GHz)** and **MIMO up to 8 streams**, while reusing the 11ad MAC framework.
- **How**: Introduces **EDMG (Enhanced Directional Multi-Gigabit) PHY** with **MCS-25 to MCS-32** using π/2-64-QAM + LDPC rate 13/16, channel bonding, MU-MIMO, and trigger-based beamforming enhancements.

#### 2.2.4 3GPP 5G NR FR2 (Release 15, June 2018)

- **Why**: Provide a *globally coordinated* cellular mmWave framework supporting eMBB, URLLC, mMTC.
- **How**: Introduces **scalable numerology** with subcarrier spacings of **60 kHz, 120 kHz, 240 kHz**, max bandwidth **400 MHz per carrier (up to 8 CC carrier aggregation = 3.2 GHz)**, hybrid beamforming (analog + digital), and a **three-stage beam management** procedure (P1: beam sweeping; P2: beam refinement; P3: beam tracking).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Mnemonic — "S-C-O-M"** to remember the development stages: **S**pectrum → **C**hannel → **O**FDM/Modulation → **M**AC/Beamforming.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Wavelength | $\lambda = c / f$ | $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s |
| 2 | Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) | $FSPL(\text{dB}) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz |
| 3 | FSPL (d, f in SI) | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d \cdot f) - 147.55$ | $d$ in m, $f$ in Hz |
| 4 | Log-distance path loss | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10\alpha \log_{10}(d/d_0) + X_{\sigma}$ | $\alpha \approx 2$ LOS, 3–5 NLOS; $X_\sigma \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$ |
| 5 | 3GPP TR 38.901 UMi-LOS mmWave PL | $PL = 32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f_c)$ | $d$ in m, $f_c$ in GHz; 0.5 < d < 100 m |
| 6 | Oxygen absorption (peak) | $\gamma_{O_2} \approx 15$ dB/km @ 60 GHz | Causes 60 GHz "resonance" |
| 7 | Specific rain attenuation | $\gamma_R = k R^{\alpha}$ | $R$ in mm/hr, ITU-R P.838 coefficients |
| 8 | Shannon capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | bps, with $B$ in Hz |
| 9 | Array factor (N-element ULA) | $AF(\theta) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} w_n e^{j n \psi}$ | $\psi = \frac{2\pi d}{\lambda}\sin\theta$ |
| 10 | Half-power beamwidth (ULA) | $\theta_{3\text{dB}} \approx 0.886 \frac{\lambda}{N d}$ | rad, for uniform weighting |
| 11 | Array gain | $G_{array} = 10\log_{10}(N)$ | dBi, ideal broadside |
| 12 | Effective SNR with beamforming gain | $SNR_{eff} = SNR_{elem} \cdot N$ | Linear scale |
| 13 | Noise figure (NF) | $NF = 10\log_{10}(F)$ | $F = SNR_{in}/SNR_{out}$ |
| 14 | Thermal noise | $N_0 = kT$ | $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| 15 | EIRP | $EIRP = P_t + G_t - L_f$ | dBW + dBi − dB |
| 16 | 802.11ad symbol duration | $T_{sym} = 0.463$ ns × $N_{FFT}$ | 512-pt SC; 64-QAM OFDM |
| 17 | NR FR2 numerology SCS | $\Delta f = 2^{\mu} \cdot 15$ kHz | $\mu \in \{2,3,4\}$ |
| 18 | NR FR2 max BW / CC | $BW_{CC,max} = 400$ MHz | 8 CC → 3.2 GHz aggregated |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pitfall Alert (KTU answer scripts):** When writing FSPL in a KTU answer, always declare the units of $d$ and $f$ explicitly. Most of the 1-mark errors in past valuation sheets come from omitting units.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **5G small cells (FR2)** use 28 GHz mmWave for stadium, hotspot, and FWA (Fixed Wireless Access) deployments.
- **802.11ay** powers wireless backhaul for AR/VR, intra-rack data center links, and 8K video streaming.
- **802.15.3c** continues to serve as the silicon-PHY basis for mmWave contactless connectors and short-range kiosk downloads.
- Channel models developed for these standards are reused in **6G research** at sub-THz (140, 220, 300 GHz) — *same development methodology, higher frequency*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Free-Space Path Loss at mmWave Anchor Frequencies

We start from the Friis transmission equation in linear form and convert it to dB.

$$
P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 1.** Substitute $\lambda = c / f$.

$$
P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{c}{4\pi d f}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 2.** Take $10 \log_{10}$ of both sides.

$$
P_r(\text{dB}) = P_t(\text{dB}) + G_t + G_r - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)
$$

**Step 3.** Group the negative term as the free-space path loss $FSPL$.

$$
FSPL(\text{dB}) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)
$$

**Step 4.** Evaluate the constant $20\log_{10}(4\pi/c) = 20\log_{10}(4\pi / 3\times 10^{8}) = 20\log_{10}(4.18879 \times 10^{-8}) \approx -147.55$ dB.

$$
FSPL(\text{dB}) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) - 147.55 \quad (\text{d in m, f in Hz})
$$

**Step 5.** Apply to mmWave anchor frequencies at $d = 10$ m.

For $f = 28$ GHz:

$$
FSPL_{28} = 20\log_{10}(10) + 20\log_{10}(28 \times 10^{9}) - 147.55 = 20 + 20(10.4472) - 147.55
$$

$$
FSPL_{28} \approx 20 + 208.94 - 147.55 = 81.39 \text{ dB}
$$

For $f = 38$ GHz:

$$
FSPL_{38} = 20 + 20\log_{10}(38 \times 10^{9}) - 147.55 = 20 + 20(10.5798) - 147.55 = 84.04 \text{ dB}
$$

For $f = 60$ GHz:

$$
FSPL_{60} = 20 + 20\log_{10}(60 \times 10^{9}) - 147.55 = 20 + 20(10.7782) - 147.55 = 88.01 \text{ dB}
$$

For $f = 73$ GHz:

$$
FSPL_{73} = 20 + 20\log_{10}(73 \times 10^{9}) - 147.55 = 20 + 20(10.8633) - 147.55 = 89.71 \text{ dB}
$$

**Analytical interpretation:** Each doubling of frequency adds exactly **6 dB** of free-space loss. From 28 GHz to 60 GHz the loss grows by **~6.6 dB**, and from 28 GHz to 73 GHz by **~8.3 dB**, mandating directional high-gain antennas to close the link budget.

### 3.2 Derivation B — 60 GHz Atmospheric Attenuation and Effective Range

**Step 1.** The 60 GHz band suffers a molecular oxygen absorption peak: $\gamma_{O_2} \approx 15$ dB/km at sea level (clear air). Add this as a multiplicative factor to FSPL over distance $d$ in km.

$$
PL_{total}(d) = FSPL(d) + \gamma_{O_2} \cdot d
$$

**Step 2.** For $f = 60$ GHz, $d = 1$ km:

$$
FSPL_{60,1\text{km}} = 20\log_{10}(1000) + 20\log_{10}(60\times 10^{9}) - 147.55 = 60 + 109.54 - 147.55 = 21.99 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
PL_{total}(1\text{ km}) = 21.99 + 15 \cdot 1 = 36.99 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3.** For $d = 100$ m $= 0.1$ km:

$$
PL_{total}(0.1\text{ km}) = 20\log_{10}(100) + 20\log_{10}(60\times 10^{9}) - 147.55 + 15 \cdot 0.1
$$

$$
PL_{total}(0.1\text{ km}) = 40 + 109.54 - 147.55 + 1.5 = 3.49 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4.** Interpretation: at 60 GHz, atmospheric absorption is negligible below **~50 m**, but at **1 km** it adds **15 dB** of extra loss, bounding the practical outdoor range to *tens of meters without very high gain arrays*. This is *the* reason 802.11ad and 802.11ay mandate directional beamforming.

### 3.3 Derivation C — Beamforming Array Gain for Beam Management

A uniform linear array (ULA) with $N$ elements coherently combined yields an array gain of:

$$
G_{array}(\text{linear}) = N \quad \Rightarrow \quad G_{array}(\text{dBi}) = 10\log_{10}(N)
$$

**Step 1.** For $N = 8$ (typical handset phased array):

$$
G_{8} = 10\log_{10}(8) = 9.03 \text{ dBi}
$$

**Step 2.** For $N = 64$ (typical gNB at 28 GHz):

$$
G_{64} = 10\log_{10}(64) = 18.06 \text{ dBi}
$$

**Step 3.** Net path loss at 28 GHz, $d = 200$ m, with $N = 64$ ULA at TX and RX (assuming unity element gain):

$$
PL_{net} = FSPL_{28}(200\text{m}) - G_{TX} - G_{RX} = 81.39 + 20\log_{10}(20) - 18.06 - 18.06
$$

$$
PL_{net} = 81.39 + 26.02 - 36.12 = 71.29 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4.** Without beamforming (single isotropic antenna, $N = 1$): $PL_{net} = 107.41$ dB — a **36 dB gap** that must be closed by beamforming alone. This is the analytical basis for 3GPP NR's mandatory **multi-stage beam management (P1/P2/P3)**.

### 3.4 Derivation D — Throughput Bound from 3GPP FR2

For 5G NR FR2 with $B = 400$ MHz, $S/N = 10$ dB (linear $= 10$):

$$
C = B \log_2(1 + S/N) = 400 \times 10^{6} \times \log_2(11) \approx 400 \times 10^{6} \times 3.459 \approx 1.384 \text{ Gbps}
$$

With **8-component carrier aggregation** ($B_{agg} = 3.2$ GHz), and with beamforming gain effectively increasing the operating SNR to ~25 dB:

$$
C_{agg} = 3.2 \times 10^{9} \times \log_2(1 + 10^{2.5}) = 3.2 \times 10^{9} \times 8.21 \approx 26.3 \text{ Gbps}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This bound is the *theoretical* Shannon limit; commercial mmWave systems reach **~3–5 Gbps** in practice due to MCS roll-off, beam-tracking latency, and front-end impairments — a fact the KTU examiner commonly tests.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Standard Comparison

| Parameter | IEEE 802.15.3c | IEEE 802.11ad | IEEE 802.11ay | 3GPP NR FR2 |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|----------------|-------------|
| **Release / Ratification** | 2009 | 2012 | 2021 | 2018 (Rel-15) |
| **Frequency band** | 57–66 GHz | 57–66 GHz | 57–71 GHz (incl. China) | 24.25–52.6 GHz |
| **Channel BW** | 2160 MHz | 2160 MHz | 4320 / 6480 / 8640 MHz | 50/100/200/400 MHz (CA up to 8 CC) |
| **Peak data rate** | ~5 Gbps | 6.75–7 Gbps | ~100+ Gbps | ~5 Gbps per UE (Rel-15), up to 25 Gbps+ with CA |
| **PHY modes** | SC + OFDM | Control, SC, OFDM | EDMG SC + OFDM | OFDM with scalable numerology |
| **Modulation (max)** | 16-QAM | 64-QAM | 64-QAM | 256-QAM (DL, Rel-15) |
| **MIMO** | No | No | Up to 8×8 MU-MIMO | Massive MIMO (hybrid beamforming) |
| **Beamforming** | Directional | Sector sweep + beam tracking | MU-MIMO beamforming | 3-stage beam management (P1/P2/P3) |
| **Channel model** | IEEE 802.15.3c UWB-ext | 60 GHz TGad | TGay (NYUSIM-based) | 3GPP TR 38.901 (UMi/UMa/InH) |
| **Use case** | Cable replacement | WLAN/WiGig | Next-gen WLAN, backhaul | 5G cellular eMBB/FWA |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — mmWave Standard Development Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Spectrum Regulation] --> B[ITU-R WRC Identification]
    A --> C[FCC / ETSI Allocation]
    B --> D[Channel Sounding Campaigns]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Statistical Channel Models]
    E --> F[3GPP TR 38.901]
    E --> G[IEEE TGad TGay Models]
    F --> H[PHY Specification]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Modulation Coding MCS Tables]
    H --> J[Numerology Frame Structure]
    I --> K[MAC and Beam Management]
    J --> K
    K --> L[802.15.3c 2009]
    K --> M[802.11ad 2012 WiGig]
    K --> N[802.11ay 2021 EDMG]
    K --> O[3GPP NR FR2 Rel 15 Onwards]
    L --> P[Interoperable mmWave Ecosystem]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — 3GPP NR FR2 Three-Stage Beam Management

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stage P1 Beam Sweeping] --> P2[Stage P2 Beam Refinement]
    P2 --> P3[Stage P3 Beam Tracking]
    P3 --> P2
    P1 --> UE[UE Synchronization and SSB Detection]
    P2 --> UE
    P3 --> UE
    UE --> QPSK[CSI RS for Refinement]
    UE --> PTRS[Tracking Reference Signals]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — IEEE 802.11ad/ay Beam Training Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sector Level Sweep SLS] --> B[Beam Refinement Protocol BRP]
    B --> C[Beam Tracking BT]
    C --> D[Data Transmission in Quasi Omni Mode]
    A --> E[Initiator TXSS]
    A --> F[Responder TXSS]
    E --> B
    F --> B
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — 5G NR FR2 Numerology Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NR FR2 Scalable Numerology] --> B[Mu 2 SCS 60 kHz]
    A --> C[Mu 3 SCS 120 kHz]
    A --> D[Mu 4 SCS 240 kHz]
    B --> E[Cyclic Prefix Normal Extended]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Slot Symbols 14 or 12]
    F --> G[Mini Slot for Low Latency]
    G --> H[Carrier Aggregation up to 8 CC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above are deliberately structured as **functional architecture flows** to remain compatible with the KTU 2024 scheme's recommended use of Mermaid for answer scripts in modules that require schematic support.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERAT753]**
*List the four major mmWave communication standards ratified by IEEE and 3GPP, and specify the frequency band of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **IEEE 802.15.3c (2009):** operates in the **57–66 GHz** band for 60 GHz WPAN. (1 mark)
2. **IEEE 802.11ad (WiGig, 2012):** operates in the **57–66 GHz** band for 60 GHz WLAN with up to 6.75 Gbps. (1 mark)
3. **IEEE 802.11ay (EDMG, 2021):** operates in the **57–71 GHz** band (extended) with channel bonding up to 8.64 GHz and >100 Gbps. (0.5 mark)
4. **3GPP 5G NR FR2 (Rel-15 onward, 2018):** operates in **24.25–52.6 GHz** (n257/n258/n259/n260/n261). (0.5 mark)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PERAT753]**
*Why is the 60 GHz band attractive for short-range multi-Gbps communication? Mention one drawback.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The 60 GHz band is attractive because (i) it offers a **globally harmonized ~7–9 GHz unlicensed bandwidth**, supporting very wide channels (2160 MHz in 802.11ad, 8640 MHz in 802.11ay); (ii) oxygen absorption of **~15 dB/km** creates a natural interference boundary between rooms/links, allowing aggressive frequency reuse; (iii) small wavelength (~5 mm) enables compact **phased-array antennas** with many elements for high beamforming gain. (2 marks)
**Drawback:** High atmospheric/rain attenuation, severe path loss (~88 dB at 10 m), and the need for line-of-sight with precise beam alignment make 60 GHz unsuitable for long-range or NLPC mobile use. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the four-stage development pipeline of mmWave communication standards. Mention the role of ITU-R, IEEE, 3GPP, and channel measurement campaigns. **[7 marks]**

**Q. (b)** Compare IEEE 802.11ad and IEEE 802.11ay in terms of channel bandwidth, modulation, MIMO support, peak throughput, and beamforming protocol. Comment on why 11ay requires channel bonding. **[7 marks]**
