# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]  
> All questions are mapped to **KTU 2024 Scheme** pattern: Part A (3 marks, short answer) and Part B (14 marks, internal choice with sub-parts).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]  
**List any THREE major features of WirelessHART and state the standard number.**

**Model Answer**:  
WirelessHART is standardized as **IEC 62591** (and **HART 7.1+**). Three major features are:  
1. **TDMA-based deterministic MAC** with **10 ms** time-slots for predictable delivery.  
2. **Channel hopping** across **16 RF channels** in the **2.4 GHz** ISM band with **channel blacklisting** to avoid interference.  
3. **Self-healing mesh** networking with **centralized routing** and **AES-128** end-to-end encryption.  
*(3 marks — 1 per correct feature, no marks for vague statements.)*

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]  
**What is the role of a Network Manager in a WirelessHART network?**

**Model Answer**:  
The **Network Manager (NM)** is the **centralized controller** of a WirelessHART mesh. It performs:  
1. **TDMA schedule generation and dissemination** — assigns time-slots and channel offsets to every device.  
2. **Routing graph computation** — determines multi-hop paths for all source-destination pairs.  
3. **Channel blacklisting** — identifies noisy channels based on link-quality statistics (RSSI, packet error rate) and excludes them.  
4. **Health monitoring and topology reconfiguration** — reacts to link failures and recomputes schedules.  
*(3 marks — distribute marks as 1 for the role itself + 2 for the functions list.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**  
**Q. (a)** Explain the **WirelessHART protocol stack** with reference to its five logical components. **(7 marks)**  
**Q. (b)** Discuss the **TDMA time-slot structure and channel hopping** mechanism in WirelessHART. Show how **channel blacklisting** enhances reliability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Protocol Stack & 5 Components (7 marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Allocation of 7 marks**: 1 for introduction, 3 for the 5 logical components, 3 for the protocol stack diagram/description.

**Introduction (1 mark):**  
WirelessHART is a wireless mesh networking technology standardized as **IEC 62591**, built on **IEEE 802.15.4 PHY**, designed for **process automation**.

**Five Logical Components (3 marks):**  

1. **Field Devices** — Originators/terminators of process data; contain radio + HART application layer.  
2. **Gateway** — Bridges wireless mesh ↔ wired plant network; provides Access Point function.  
3. **Network Manager** — Centralized engine; builds TDMA schedule, routing graph, manages channels.  
4. **Security Manager** — Handles authentication, key generation/distribution (join, network, session keys).  
5. **Handheld / Maintenance Tool** — For technician commissioning, diagnostics, calibration.

**Protocol Stack (3 marks):**

| Layer | WirelessHART Implementation |
|---|---|
| Application (7) | HART Universal/Common-Practice/Device-Specific commands |
| Presentation (6) | HART data encoding (IEEE 754, packed) |
| Transport (5) | WirelessHART transport — burst, segmentation, re-assembly |
| Network (4) | Mesh routing — graph routing, source routing, broadcast |
| Data Link (3) | TDMA MAC + IEEE 802.15.4 PHY |
| Physical (1/2) | 2.4 GHz, O-QPSK, DSSS, 250 kbps |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — TDMA + Channel Hopping (7 marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Allocation of 7 marks**: 2 for TDMA structure, 3 for channel hopping mechanism, 2 for channel blacklisting.

**TDMA Time-Slot Structure (2 marks):**  
The **fundamental time unit is a 10 ms slot**. A **superframe** is a repeating group of slots (e.g., 40–100 slots). Each device transmits **exactly one frame per allocated slot**, guaranteeing **collision-free deterministic access**.

**Channel Hopping (3 marks):**  
In each scheduled slot, the device uses a **specific channel offset** derived from its **unique hopping sequence** and the **absolute slot number**. The pattern repeats pseudo-randomly. Across **16 channels** (11–26, each 5 MHz apart in 2.4 GHz ISM), this **disperses interference, mitigates multipath fading, and defeats narrow-band jamming**.

**Channel Blacklisting (2 marks):**  
The Network Manager continuously monitors **RSSI and packet error rate per channel**. Channels with persistently poor quality are added to a **blacklist**, which the NM broadcasts to all devices. Blacklisted channels are **skipped automatically** during slot-channel mapping, dynamically rerouting bandwidth to clean channels — a key contributor to the **>99.9% reliability** target.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Internal Choice Alternative]

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Sample Paper]**  
**Q. (a)** Describe the **security architecture of WirelessHART**, including the cryptographic primitives and key-management hierarchy. **(7 marks)**  
**Q. (b)** A WirelessHART superframe has **50 time-slots** of 10 ms each. A device transmits a **maximum-size frame (127 octets)** in every active slot. Compute the **effective application-layer throughput** in kbps, assuming a **20-byte MAC/PHY overhead per frame**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Security Architecture (7 marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Allocation of 7 marks**: 1 for overall goal, 2 for cryptographic primitive, 2 for key hierarchy, 2 for join procedure.

**Overall Security Goal (1 mark):**  
WirelessHART provides **end-to-end confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity** for all process data traversing the wireless mesh, defending against **eavesdropping, replay, and unauthorized injection**.

**Cryptographic Primitive (2 marks):**  
- **AES-128** (Advanced Encryption Standard with 128-bit key).  
- Mode of operation: **CCM (Counter with CBC-MAC)** — provides both **encryption** (confidentiality via CTR) and **authentication** (integrity via CBC-MAC tag).  
- All layer-2 frames carry a **4-byte MIC (Message Integrity Code)**.

**Key Hierarchy (2 marks):**

1. **Join Key** — Pre-shared (out-of-band) between device and Security Manager; used **only** to authenticate the join request.  
2. **Network Key** — Distributed by Security Manager upon successful join; used to **broadcast NM advertisements** (network-wide).  
3. **Session Keys** (Unicast + Join) — Per-device keys derived from network key; used for **unicast data confidentiality**.

**Join Procedure (2 marks):**  
Device → sends Join Request (encrypted with Join Key) → SM authenticates → SM issues Network Key + Session Keys → NM assigns time-slots and routes → Device enters operational mode. **Forward secrecy** is maintained because each session uses a fresh key.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Throughput Calculation (7 marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Allocation of 7 marks**: 1 for given data, 2 for air-time, 2 for payload, 2 for final throughput.

**Given Data (1 mark):**  
- $N_{slots} = 50$ slots per superframe  
- $T_{slot} = 10 \text{ ms}$  
- $L_{frame} = 127$ octets  
- $L_{overhead} = 20$ octets per frame  
- Air-time rule: $32 \text{ }\mu s$/octet

**Step 1 — Air-time per frame (2 marks):**  

$$
T_{air} = 127 \times 32 \text{ }\mu s = 4064 \text{ }\mu s = 4.064 \text{ ms}
$$

Since $4.064 < 10$, frame fits in one slot ✓.

**Step 2 — Effective payload per frame (2 marks):**  

$$
L_{payload} = 127 - 20 = 107 \text{ bytes/frame}
$$

**Step 3 — Total superframe duration and bytes delivered (1 mark):**  

$$
T_{SF} = 50 \times 10 = 500 \text{ ms} = 0.5 \text{ s}
$$

$$
B_{SF} = 50 \times 107 = 5350 \text{ bytes} = 42\,800 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Effective throughput (1 mark):**  

$$
R_{eff} = \frac{42\,800 \text{ bits}}{0.5 \text{ s}} = 85\,600 \text{ bps}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{eff} \approx 85.6 \text{ kbps}}
$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:  
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract overhead** — if a student writes $R_{eff} = \dfrac{50 \times 127 \times 8}{0.5}$, they will compute a wrong answer of ~101.6 kbps. **−2 marks** for overlooking MAC/PHY overhead.  
> 2. **Mixing ms with s** — keep units consistent. Convert $4064 \,\mu s$ to **4.064 ms**, not **4.064 s** (this is a common student error).  
> 3. **Skipping the "fits in slot" check** — Examiners **love** to see the verification $T_{air} < T_{slot}$ to ensure validity.  
> 4. **Not using $32 \mu s$/octet rule** — many students guess the bit-rate formula but forget the 32 microseconds/octet canonical rule derived from 250 kbps.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — WirelessHART (Module 3)**

- **Full form** = Wireless **Highway Addressable Remote Transducer**; standard = **IEC 62591 / HART 7.1+**.  
- **PHY**: IEEE 802.15.4, **2.4 GHz ISM**, **16 channels (k = 11 to 26)**, **5 MHz spacing**.  
- **Modulation**: O-QPSK with DSSS; **Chip rate** = 2 Mchip/s, **Symbol rate** = 62.5 ksymbols/s, **Bit rate** = **250 kbps**.  
- **Air-time rule** = **32 $\mu s$/octet** (used in every throughput problem).  
- **Center frequency formula**: $f_c(k) = 2405 + 5(k-11)$ MHz, valid for $k \in \{11, 12, \dots, 26\}$.  
- **MAC**: **TDMA** with **10 ms** time-slots; optional **CSMA-CA** for unslotted beacon transmissions.  
- **Channel hopping** + **channel blacklisting** → defeats interference and multipath fading.  
- **Mesh**: Multi-hop (up to 15 hops) with **self-healing**; routing is **centralized** (Network Manager).  
- **Five components**: Field Devices, Gateway, Network Manager, Security Manager, Handheld.  
- **Security**: **AES-128 CCM** with 3-tier key hierarchy — **Join Key → Network Key → Session Key**.  
- **Throughput calculation** formula: $R_{eff} = \dfrac{N_{slots} \times L_{payload} \times 8}{N_{slots} \times T_{slot}}$ (in bps).  
- **Battery life** goal = **5–10 years**; critical for **brownfield** plant retrofits.  
- **Reliability target** = **>99.9%** end-to-end with deterministic latency.  
- **Comparison pitfalls**: WirelessHART is NOT a Wi-Fi replacement; it is **purpose-built for low-power, low-data-rate, high-reliability** process control.  
- **Typical KTU 14-mark question weightage**: 7 marks for protocol stack/architecture + 7 marks for either (a) security/TDMA or (b) numerical throughput derivation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RFID
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** RFID

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Radio Frequency Identification (RFID)** is a wireless **Auto-ID (Automatic Identification)** technology that uses **electromagnetic fields** in the **radio frequency (RF) spectrum** to uniquely identify, track, and exchange data between a **transponder (tag)** attached to an object and an **interrogator (reader)** without requiring physical contact or line-of-sight visibility.

The system fundamentally relies on **backscatter modulation** (in passive systems) or **active radio transmission** (in active systems) to transfer identification data, typically a unique **Electronic Product Code (EPC)** or similar identifier.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **barcode scanner at a supermarket**, but one that:
- **Does not need to "see" the label** (no line-of-sight needed — it works through cardboard, plastic, even clothing).
- Can read **hundreds of items in a fraction of a second** without taking them out of a cart.
- Stores data on a tiny **chip that has no battery** and gets powered *wirelessly* by the reader itself.

**Simple Real-World Analogy:**  
Think of RFID like a **dog tag with a built-in microphone**. The base station (reader) shouts a radio "name" into the air. The tag (the dog tag) hears it, wakes up (using the radio energy as power), and shouts back its unique ID number. The base station hears the reply and logs it.

## 1.3 Core Components of an RFID System

| Component | Function | Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Tag / Transponder** | Stores the unique ID, attached to the object | The "name plate" of the item |
| **Reader / Interrogator** | Emits RF signal, receives tag's response | The person asking "who are you?" |
| **Antenna** | Mediates RF energy transfer | The "mouth and ears" |
| **Host Computer / Middleware** | Processes the data, integrates with databases (e.g., SAP, ERP) | The "brain" that interprets the answer |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (governs RF propagation).
- **Standard RFID frequency bands:** **LF (125–134 kHz)**, **HF (13.56 MHz)**, **UHF (860–960 MHz)**, **Microwave (2.45 GHz & 5.8 GHz)**.
- **Typical read range:** from **10 cm** (passive HF, contactless smart cards) up to **100 m** (active UHF tags).
- **Air interface standards:** **ISO 14443, ISO 15693, EPC Class-1 Gen-2 (ISO 18000-63)**, **ISO 11784/85** (animal ID).

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In PERAT753 Module 3, RFID is studied under the umbrella of **short-range wireless identification and sensing systems**, alongside topics like **NFC, Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE), and UWB**. Focus on **modulation (ASK/PSK via backscatter), anti-collision algorithms (ALOHA, tree-based), and ISO/EPC standards**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Three Core Laws

The behavior of an RFID link is governed by three theoretical pillars every KTU paper tests:

### (a) Friis Transmission Equation (Forward Link, Reader → Tag)

For an **active tag** (or for the power-up stage of a passive tag), the received power at the tag is:

$$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

Where:
- $P_t$ = Transmitted power of the reader (in **Watts**)
- $G_t, G_r$ = Gains of transmitting and receiving antennas (unitless, linear scale)
- $\lambda$ = Wavelength of the carrier ($\lambda = c/f$)
- $d$ = Distance between reader and tag antennas (in **meters**)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Friis equation assumes free-space, line-of-sight, far-field conditions** ($d \gg \lambda$, $d \gg 2D^2/\lambda$). In the **near field** (LF/HF), the inductive coupling model applies instead.

### (b) Radar Cross Section (RCS) of a Passive Tag

For a **passive tag**, the modulated backscatter creates a synthetic aperture. The effective RCS of a perfectly modulated tag antenna is:

$$\sigma = \frac{\lambda^2 \, G_{tag}^2 \, R_a^2}{4\pi}$$

Where $G_{tag}$ is the tag antenna gain, and $R_a$ is the reflection coefficient modulation ratio (0 → 1 for ASK).

The reader then receives the backscattered power as:

$$P_{back} = \frac{P_t \, G_t^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, d^4}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **The famous "$d^4$ rule"** — passive UHF RFID signal strength falls off as the **fourth power of distance**, which is why passive tags have a limited range (typically 5–10 m for UHF EPC Gen-2).

### (c) Inductive Coupling (Near-Field, HF/LF)

Used in **NFC, contactless smart cards, and animal ID tags**. Two coupled coils form a loosely coupled transformer:

$$V_{induced} = -N_2 \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -M \frac{dI_1}{dt}$$

Where $M$ is the **mutual inductance** between reader and tag coils, decaying as $M \propto 1/d^3$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wavelength | $\lambda = c / f$ | $c$ = speed of light, $f$ = carrier frequency | Antenna sizing, link budget |
| Friis Received Power | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi d)^2$ | All power gains/distance | Active tag forward link |
| Passive Backscatter Power | $P_{back} = P_t G_t^2 \lambda^2 \sigma / (4\pi)^3 d^4$ | Includes RCS $\sigma$ | Passive tag range estimation |
| Tag RCS | $\sigma = \lambda^2 G_{tag}^2 R_a^2 / 4\pi$ | Modulation depth $R_a$ | Anti-collision modulation design |
| Induced EMF (Near-field) | $V = -M \, dI/dt$, $M \propto 1/d^3$ | Mutual inductance $M$ | NFC, smart card design |
| Bit Duration | $T_b = 1 / \text{Data Rate}$ | $T_b$ in seconds | Throughput calculation |
| Backscatter Link Frequency (BLF) | $f_{BLF} = f_c / \text{divisor}$ | EPC Gen-2 uses 40–640 kHz | Reader–tag handshake timing |
| Read Range (free space) | $d_{max} = \lambda / 4\pi \sqrt{P_t G_t G_r \sigma / P_{th}}$ | $P_{th}$ = tag sensitivity | Maximum operational range |
| Q-factor (resonant coil) | $Q = f_0 / \Delta f = \omega L / R$ | Inductive tag tuning | HF tag bandwidth design |
| Energy Harvesting Voltage | $V_{harvest} = k \cdot V_{induced} - V_{th}$ | $V_{th}$ = diode threshold | Passive tag wake-up circuit |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Supply chain & logistics:** Walmart, Amazon use UHF RFID to track pallets — replacing barcodes with bulk, hands-free scanning.
- **Access control & payments:** 13.56 MHz NFC (MIFARE, ISO 14443) in Metro cards, credit cards, mobile wallets.
- **Healthcare:** Tracking surgical instruments, blood bags, and patient wristbands to eliminate misidentification errors.
- **Livestock & wildlife tracking:** LF RFID (ISO 11784/85) ear tags; e.g., cattle identification in Kerala dairy farms.
- **Toll collection (FASTag in India):** UHF passive RFID at 865–867 MHz for highway electronic tolling.
- **Anti-counterfeiting:** Pharma industry uses RFID on drug packaging to verify authenticity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Read Range of a Passive UHF RFID Tag

### Step 1 — State the boundary condition
A passive tag can be read as long as the **incident RF power at the tag's antenna** is greater than the **tag's activation threshold power** $P_{th}$ (typically around **−15 dBm to −20 dBm** for modern EPC Gen-2 tags).

The **forward-link** received power at the tag (using Friis) must equal $P_{th}$ at the maximum range $d_{max}$:

$$P_{th} = P_t \, G_t \, G_{tag} \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi \, d_{max}}\right)^2$$

### Step 2 — Solve algebraically for $d_{max}$
Rearranging for $d_{max}$:

$$d_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \sqrt{\frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_{tag}}{P_{th}}}$$

> **Logic:** We isolate the distance variable on one side by dividing both sides by $P_{th}$, then taking the reciprocal and the square root.

### Step 3 — Account for the **return path** (tag → reader)
For a passive tag, the round-trip is **reader → tag → reader**. The backscattered power at the reader is:

$$P_{rx} = P_t \, G_t^2 \, G_{tag} \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\right)^4 \frac{\sigma}{d^4}$$

### Step 4 — Set the round-trip power equal to reader sensitivity $P_{rx,min}$:

$$P_{rx,min} = P_t \, G_t^2 \, G_{tag} \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\right)^4 \frac{\sigma}{d_{max}^4}$$

### Step 5 — Solve the round-trip equation for $d_{max}$:

$$d_{max}^4 = \frac{P_t \, G_t^2 \, G_{tag} \, \lambda^4 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^4 \, P_{rx,min}}$$

$$\boxed{d_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \left(\frac{P_t \, G_t^2 \, G_{tag} \, \sigma}{P_{rx,min}}\right)^{1/4}}$$

> **Logic:** Taking the fourth root of both sides yields the $d^4$ dependence. This is why doubling the read range requires a **16× increase in transmitted power** (or equivalently, a 12 dB gain).

## 3.2 Derivation: Mutual Inductance of Coupled Coils (HF/NFC)

### Step 1 — Define the magnetic flux linkage
For two coaxial coils separated by distance $d$, with radii $r_1$ and $r_2$:

$$\Phi_{12} = \frac{\mu_0 \, N_1 \, I_1 \, \pi \, r_1^2 \, r_2^2}{2 (r_1^2 + d^2)^{3/2}}$$

### Step 2 — Mutual inductance
By definition $M = \Phi_{12} / I_1$:

$$M = \frac{\mu_0 \, N_1 \, \pi \, r_1^2 \, r_2^2}{2 (r_1^2 + d^2)^{3/2}}$$

### Step 3 — Asymptotic behavior
For $d \gg r_1$:

$$M \approx \frac{\mu_0 \, N_1 \, \pi \, r_1^2 \, r_2^2}{2 \, d^3}$$

> **Logic:** This confirms the $1/d^3$ decay of inductive coupling, explaining why NFC and HF RFID only work in the **10–15 cm range**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Tag Type Comparison

| Parameter | Passive Tag | Semi-Passive (Battery-Assisted Passive, BAP) | Active Tag |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power source** | Harvested RF energy | Battery powers chip; RF for comms | Internal battery powers everything |
| **Range** | **1–10 m** (UHF) | **10–30 m** | **Up to 100 m** |
| **Lifespan** | Unlimited (no battery) | 3–7 years | 3–7 years |
| **Cost per tag** | **$0.05–$0.50** | $1–$5 | $15–$100 |
| **Sensor capability** | None | Temperature, tamper | Multi-sensor (GPS, motion) |
| **Read rate** | ~100 tags/sec | ~200 tags/sec | ~1000 tags/sec |
| **EPC Gen-2 compliance** | Yes | Yes | No (proprietary) |
| **Use case** | Retail, FASTag, passports | Cold chain monitoring | Container tracking, vehicle ID |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: End-to-End RFID Communication Flow

The diagram below maps the complete signal and data flow from the moment a tag enters the reader's field to the moment the host database updates the record.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["HOST MIDDLEWARE SERVER"] -->|"User Query / Inventory Command"| B["READER CPU DSP"]
    B -->|"Baseband Coded Data Q and I signals"| C["RF TRANSCEIVER MODULE"]
    C -->|"UPCONVERTED CW CARRIER 865 to 867 MHz"| D["POWER AMPLIFIER"]
    D -->|"High Power RF Out"| E["CIRCULATOR AND DIRECTIONAL COUPLER"]
    E -->|"TX RF path"| F["READER ANTENNA CIRCULAR POLARIZED"]
    F -->|"Electromagnetic Wave Propagation"| G["TAG ANTENNA DIPOLE OR LOOP"]
    G -->|"Rectified DC via Schottky Diodes"| H["TAG IC POWER HARVEST CIRCUIT"]
    H -->|"Activation Vcc above threshold"| I["TAG MICROCONTROLLER AND MEMORY"]
    I -->|"Tag ID EPC bits"| J["MODULATED BACKSCATTER SWITCH"]
    J -->|"Reflected modulated wave with ID data"| G
    G -->|"Backscattered signal attenuated by d4"| F
    F -->|"Weak RX signal"| E
    E -->|"Routed to RX port via circulator"| K["LOW NOISE AMPLIFIER LNA"]
    K -->|"Amplified IF signal"| C
    C -->|"Downconverted baseband"| B
    B -->|"Decoded EPC and CRC check"| A
    A -->|"Lookup in ERP or WMS database"| L["OBJECT IDENTIFIED AND LOGGED"]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style J fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Anti-Collision Protocol Tree (Slotted ALOHA vs. Tree-Walking)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Reader Issues Query Command"] --> B{"Collision Detection Algorithm"}
    B -->|"Slotted ALOHA EPC Gen2"| C["Tags pick random slot Q 0 to 2Q minus 1"]
    C --> D["Tag transmits in chosen time slot"]
    D --> E{"Slot Result"}
    E -->|"Single tag reply"| F["READER ACKNOWLEDGES TAG AND SLEEP IT"]
    E -->|"No reply"| G["EMPTY SLOT Q value adjusted"]
    E -->|"Multiple tags collide"| H["COLLISION Q value increases"]
    H --> C
    F --> I["Remaining tags repeat"]
    G --> I
    I --> C

    A2["Reader Issues QueryRep"] --> B2["Binary Tree Walking"]
    B2 --> C2["Split tag population by random bit"]
    C2 --> D2["Subset 0 first then subset 1"]
    D2 --> E2{"Subset collides"}
    E2 -->|"Yes further split"| C2
    E2 -->|"No single tag ID read"| F2["Identify and mute tag"]

    style F fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F2 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E2 fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Tag State Machine (EPC Class-1 Gen-2)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Ready
    Ready --> Arbitrate : "Received Query and selected slot Q nonzero"
    Arbitrate --> Reply : "Own slot number Q equals slot counter"
    Arbitrate --> Arbitrate : "Slot counter less than Q decrement and wait"
    Reply --> Acknowledged : "Reader sends ACK with valid handle"
    Reply --> Arbitrate : "No ACK or invalid handle stay in arbitration"
    Acknowledged --> Open : "Reader sends Req_RN get new handle"
    Open --> Secured : "Reader sends Access password Authenticate"
    Secured --> Killed : "Reader sends Kill password tag permanently disabled"
    Acknowledged --> Ready : "Reader sends QueryAdj tag returns to ready"
    Secured --> Open : "Read Write or Lock commands executed"
    Killed --> [*] : "Tag is silent forever"
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define RFID. List any two differences between active and passive RFID tags.**

**Model Answer:**

> **RFID (Radio Frequency Identification)** is a wireless non-contact identification technology that uses RF electromagnetic waves to transfer identification data between a **transponder (tag)** and an **interrogator (reader)**.

| Feature | Active Tag | Passive Tag |
|---|---|---|
| Power source | Internal battery | Harvested from reader's RF field |
| Read range | Up to 100 m | Typically 1–10 m (UHF) |
| Cost | High ($15–$100) | Low ($0.05–$0.50) |
| Lifespan | Limited by battery | Unlimited |

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the concept of "backscatter modulation" used in passive RFID systems.**

**Model Answer:**

In a passive RFID system, the tag has no transmitter of its own. Instead, the tag varies the **reflection coefficient** of its antenna by toggling an internal RF switch between two impedance states (matched and shorted). This causes the **incident CW signal from the reader to be reflected back with amplitude or phase modulation** that encodes the tag's ID. This technique is called **backscatter modulation**, and the information rides on a wave whose power was originally generated by the reader, not the tag.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

#### (a) Derive the Friis transmission equation for a free-space RFID link. (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Define the problem:**  
We have a transmitting antenna with gain $G_t$ and a receiving antenna with gain $G_r$ separated by distance $d$ in free space. The transmitting antenna radiates isotropically-equivalent power $P_t G_t$.

**Step 2 — Power flux density at distance $d$:**  
The radiated power spreads over the surface of a sphere of radius $d$, with area $4\pi d^2$. The **power flux density** is:

$$S = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

**Step 3 — Effective aperture of the receiving antenna:**  
The effective area of an antenna is related to its gain by:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

**Step 4 — Total received power:**  
Multiply flux density by effective aperture:

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

$$\boxed{P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Free-space path loss in dB:**

$$FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Limitation:** This equation holds only in the **far-field** ($d \gg \lambda$ and $d \gg 2D^2/\lambda$, where $D$ is the largest antenna dimension). Near-field (HF/NFC) requires the inductive coupling model.

#### (b) With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an RFID system. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The RFID system consists of **four main functional blocks**:

1. **Tag (Transponder):** Contains a microchip (with EPC memory) and an antenna. In passive tags, the chip is powered by rectifying incoming RF energy via Schottky diodes.

2. **Reader (Interrogator):** Has a transceiver, DSP, and antenna. Emits a CW signal, receives backscattered replies, and decodes the data.

3. **Antenna subsystem:** Provides the RF interface. Reader antennas are typically circularly polarized to avoid orientation sensitivity with linearly polarized tag dipoles.

4. **Host computer / Middleware:** Software layer that filters duplicate reads, runs anti-collision logic, and integrates with enterprise databases (ERP, WMS, SCM).

**Working sequence:**  
(i) Reader transmits modulated CW signal.  
(ii) Tag harvests energy and powers up.  
(iii) Tag backscatters its ID via impedance switching.  
(iv) Reader decodes, performs CRC check, and forwards EPC to host.  
(v) Host maps the EPC to product information.

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks:**  
> 1. **Do NOT** draw a generic "transmitter-receiver" diagram. The examiner specifically wants **the backscatter path clearly shown** with a "modulation switch" on the tag.  
> 2. **Do NOT confuse** "interrogator" with "initiator" (the latter is NFC terminology).  
> 3. **Marks are lost** if you forget to mention that the tag's power-up is via **energy harvesting**, not a battery (for passive systems).  
> 4. For sub-part (a), **derivation marks demand each algebraic step** — merely writing the final formula with no intermediate work fetches only 1–2 of 7 marks.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### (a) Explain the different frequency bands used in RFID and their typical applications. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Band | Frequency | Range | Coupling | Applications |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LF (Low Frequency)** | **125–134 kHz** | < 10 cm | Inductive | Animal tagging, access control, immobilizers |
| **HF (High Frequency)** | **13.56 MHz** | 10 cm – 1 m | Inductive | Smart cards, NFC, library books, passports |
| **UHF (Ultra High Frequency)** | **860–960 MHz** | 1–10 m | Backscatter (far-field) | Retail, supply chain, FASTag, baggage handling |
| **Microwave** | **2.45 GHz / 5.8 GHz** | Up to 30 m (active) | Backscatter | Vehicle ID, tolling, real-time location systems |

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

1. **LF (125 kHz):** Short wavelength alternative — actually long wavelength (~2400 m), allowing operation near metals and water. Used in **animal tagging** since low frequencies penetrate tissue better.

2. **HF (13.56 MHz):** The ISO 14443/15693 standard. Powers the global **NFC ecosystem** (contactless payments, MIFARE cards). Penetration through non-metallic materials is good.

3. **UHF (865–868 MHz in India per regulations):** Best for **far-field backscatter**. EPC Gen-2 is the dominant standard. Read range of 5–10 m makes it ideal for **warehouse inventory**.

4. **Microwave (2.45 GHz):** Highest data rates, but higher path loss. Used in **active RFID systems** and Wi-Fi-coexistence environments.

#### (b) Describe the Slotted ALOHA anti-collision algorithm used in EPC Class-1 Gen-2. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

When multiple tags enter the reader's field simultaneously, their replies would collide and corrupt the data. EPC Gen-2 uses a **Slotted ALOHA-based Framed Slotted ALOHA (FSA)** protocol.

**Step 1 — Frame structure:**  
The reader divides time into a **frame of $2^Q$ slots**, where $Q$ is an integer (0 to 15). Each tag randomly picks a slot using a 16-bit random number $R$ drawn on power-up.

**Step 2 — Tag's slot selection rule:**

$$\text{Tag slot} = R \mod 2^Q$$

If the result is non-zero, the tag waits for that slot; if zero, it replies in the **first slot**.

**Step 3 — Slot outcomes:**  
There are three possible outcomes per slot:
- **Single reply (success):** Reader sends ACK → tag enters "Acknowledged" state and is silenced.
- **No reply:** Empty slot — protocol moves on.
- **Collision:** Multiple tags picked the same slot — the reader increments $Q$ (up to a limit) to expand the frame and reduce collision probability.

**Step 4 — Q-adaptation:**  
The reader uses the **Q-Algorithm** to dynamically tune $Q$ based on observed collision/success ratios. A typical target is ~30% collision rate for maximum throughput.

**Step 5 — Inventory complete:**  
The process continues until all tags are read or a maximum cycle limit is reached. Throughput of EPC Gen-2 is up to **~600 tags/second** under optimal conditions.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — QUESTION B Specific:**  
> 1. **Confusing Slotted ALOHA with Pure ALOHA** — pure ALOHA has no time slots and offers 18% max throughput; slotted ALOHA doubles it to 36%. Examiners WILL check this.  
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the Q-Algorithm** — simply listing the steps of slotted ALOHA without explaining adaptive Q is incomplete.  
> 3. **Confusing EPC Gen-2 with ISO 18000-3** — Gen-2 is for **UHF**, ISO 18000-3 is **HF**. This is a frequently-tested distinction.  
> 4. **Skipping numerical examples** — if the question gives "100 tags, Q=7", compute frame size = $2^7$ = 128 slots. Show this — it is full-mark territory.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — RFID**

- **Definition:** Wireless non-contact identification using RF electromagnetic fields; data exchange between **tag (transponder)** and **reader (interrogator)**.

- **Three core physical mechanisms:**
  - **Far-field backscatter** (UHF, Microwave)
  - **Near-field inductive coupling** (LF, HF/NFC)
  - **Capacitive coupling** (rare, special-purpose)

- **Critical formulas to memorize:**
  - Friis: $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi d)^2$ — **$d^2$ falloff** (one-way)
  - Backscatter: $P_{back} \propto 1/d^4$ — **$d^4$ falloff** (round-trip, passive)
  - $d_{max}$ for passive tags scales as the **fourth root of transmitted power**
  - Inductive coupling: $M \propto 1/d^3$

- **Frequency band mantra:**  
  **"LF for animals, HF for cards, UHF for boxes, Microwave for cars."**

- **Tag types at a glance:**
  - **Passive** = no battery, short range, cheap, unlimited lifespan
  - **Semi-passive (BAP)** = battery for chip only, mid-range
  - **Active** = battery + transmitter, long range, expensive

- **Key standards (must know ISO numbers):**
  - **ISO 11784/85** — Animal ID (LF)
  - **ISO 14443** — Proximity cards, NFC (HF)
  - **ISO 15693** — Vicinity cards (HF)
  - **EPC Class-1 Gen-2 = ISO 18000-63** — UHF supply chain

- **Anti-collision protocols:** Slotted ALOHA (EPC Gen-2) vs. Tree-Walking (ISO 18000-3 HF). Throughput ceiling = 36% (slotted) and 43% (tree).

- **EPC Gen-2 tag state machine:** Ready → Arbitrate → Reply → Acknowledged → Open → Secured → Killed.

- **Modulation in RFID tags:** Tags don't generate new carriers — they **backscatter with ASK or PSK subcarrier modulation**. The reader's CW carrier is reused.

- **India-specific frequency allocation:** UHF RFID operates in **865–867 MHz** with a maximum EIRP of **+30 dBm (1 W) for indoor, +36 dBm (4 W) for outdoor**, per the Wireless Planning & Coordination Wing regulations.

- **Common exam traps:**
  - "Increasing $P_t$ by 10 dB doubles the read range" — **FALSE**. It increases range by only $\sqrt[4]{10} \approx 1.78\times$.
  - "Passive tags can be read at any distance" — **FALSE**. Range is limited by tag sensitivity and $d^4$ loss.
  - "RFID works through metal and water equally at all frequencies" — **FALSE**. LF penetrates water better; UHF reflects off metal.
  - "NFC and RFID are different technologies" — **PARTIALLY TRUE**. NFC is a *subset* of HF RFID with a specific ISO 14443 protocol stack.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LoRa
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** LoRa

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**LoRa** (acronym for **Long Range**) is a proprietary low-power, wide-area network (LPWAN) physical layer modulation technique based on **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** technology. It was developed and patented by **Semtech Corporation** and operates in the sub-GHz Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) radio bands. LoRa is engineered to enable long-range communication (typically **2–15 km**) at very low data rates (**0.3–50 kbps**) while consuming minimal battery power, making it the foundational PHY layer for massive IoT (Internet of Things) and M2M (Machine-to-Machine) deployments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LoRa vs. LoRaWAN — Critical Distinction for KTU Exam**
> - **LoRa** = Physical Layer (PHY) modulation technique (the radio signal encoding scheme).
> - **LoRaWAN** = Media Access Control (MAC) layer open protocol (network architecture).
> Confusion between the two is a **frequent mark-loser** in KTU viva voce and ESE answers.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PERAT753 Module 3):** LoRa is studied as a representative **Low Power Wide Area Network (LPWAN)** technology, alongside its modulation fundamentals (CSS), key trade-off parameters (Bandwidth, Spreading Factor, Coding Rate), and link-budget considerations for IoT networks.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Whistle Communication" Intuition

Imagine two mountaineers trying to communicate across a deep, echoing valley. They cannot shout loudly enough to be heard, and they cannot use Wi-Fi because there is no infrastructure. Their clever solution? They use a **long, slow whistle** that sweeps from a low pitch to a high pitch (a "chirp"). The valley echoes distort the signal, but because the whistle is so loud and slow, even the faintest, most distorted echo can still be recognized by the listener.

**LoRa behaves exactly like this whistle:**
- The **sweeping frequency "chirp"** carries the data — like a slow, deliberate whistle traversing frequencies.
- The **slow data rate** makes the signal robust against noise and interference.
- The **high transmit power and low frequency** (sub-GHz) allow it to penetrate walls, buildings, and travel kilometers.
- The **receiver correlates** the incoming chirp with locally generated chirp templates — just as the listener recognizes the whistle pattern despite echoes.

A regular Wi-Fi signal, by contrast, is like shouting sharply — fast and short, but it dies quickly. LoRa trades speed for **reach and energy efficiency**, the perfect compromise for sensors reporting a temperature reading once per hour.

---

### 1.3 Operating Frequency Bands (ISM)

| Region | LoRa Band | Notes |
|--------|-----------|-------|
| Europe (ETSI) | **863–870 MHz** | 8 mandatory channels (EU868) |
| USA (FCC) | **902–928 MHz** | 64 uplink + 8 downlink channels |
| Asia (India, China) | **433 MHz / 470–510 MHz / 865–867 MHz** | Often 433 MHz for unlicensed |
| Global 2.4 GHz | **2.4–2.5 GHz** | Newer LoRa 2.4 GHz (LoRa 2.4) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is LoRa deployed in sub-GHz ISM bands rather than 2.4 GHz?"* — The answer is **superior diffraction, building penetration, and lower free-space path loss** at sub-GHz frequencies (Friis transmission equation, Module 2 recall).

---

### 1.4 Why LoRa Matters — Engineering Motivation

Modern IoT requires wireless sensors that:
1. Run on a **coin-cell battery for 10+ years**.
2. Communicate from **deep indoors or rural fields** without cellular coverage.
3. Cost **less than €5 per node** at scale.
4. Tolerate interference from Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and industrial noise.

Traditional wireless (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth) fails on **range and power**. Cellular (4G/5G) fails on **cost and battery life**. LoRa sits precisely in the sweet spot:

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** A typical LoRa end-device consumes only **~50 mW** during transmission and operates at a **receiver sensitivity of −137 dBm** (for SF12, BW=125 kHz). This is approximately **20 dB more sensitive than a typical cellular receiver**, which is the primary reason for its extreme range.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) — The Heart of LoRa

LoRa's physical layer is fundamentally a **Frequency-Modulated Chirp** system. A *chirp* is a sinusoidal signal whose **instantaneous frequency increases or decreases linearly** over time.

#### Mathematical Representation of a Chirp

A baseband up-chirp can be expressed as:

$$s(t) = A \cos\left(2\pi f_0 t + \pi \mu t^2 + \phi_0\right), \quad 0 \le t \le T_s$$

Where:
- $A$ = signal amplitude
- $f_0$ = starting frequency
- $\mu$ = chirp rate (Hz/s)
- $T_s$ = symbol duration
- $\phi_0$ = initial phase

The instantaneous frequency is:

$$f_i(t) = f_0 + \mu t$$

A **down-chirp** sweeps from high to low frequency. The bandwidth $BW$ is swept across $f_{\min}$ to $f_{\max}$, where $BW = f_{\max} - f_{\min}$.

---

### 2.2 Data Encoding via Cyclic Time Shift

LoRa encodes information by **shifting the chirp in time**. A pure (unmodulated) chirp starts at $f_{\min}$ at $t = 0$. A *modulated* chirp is **delayed** by an amount proportional to the symbol value.

If the symbol carries $SF$ bits, the symbol value $k \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 2^{SF} - 1\}$ determines the cyclic shift offset:

$$\Delta t_k = \frac{k \cdot T_s}{2^{SF}}$$

At the receiver, a **dechirp operation** is performed: the incoming chirp is multiplied by a conjugate down-chirp, converting it into a single-frequency tone whose frequency directly maps to the original symbol value $k$. A simple **FFT** then decodes the symbol.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CSS is Robust:** Because the energy of each chirp is spread over the entire bandwidth $BW$ for the full symbol duration $T_s$, the signal has strong **processing gain** and excellent resilience to narrowband interference, multipath fading, and Doppler shifts (to a degree).

---

### 2.3 Three Core LoRa Parameters

#### A. Bandwidth (BW)
- **Definition:** The channel bandwidth used for transmission.
- **Allowed values:** **125 kHz, 250 kHz, 500 kHz** (most common); also 7.81 kHz, 10.42 kHz, 15.63 kHz, 20.83 kHz, 31.25 kHz, 41.67 kHz, 62.5 kHz.
- **Effect:** Higher BW → higher data rate but **lower receiver sensitivity** (noise floor rises).
- **Trade-off:** Range vs. throughput.

#### B. Spreading Factor (SF)
- **Definition:** Number of chirps per data symbol, also = number of bits encoded per symbol.
- **Allowed values:** **SF7 to SF12** (integers).
- **Effect:** Higher SF → slower data rate but **greater range** (more processing gain, ~2.5–3 dB gain per SF step).
- **Orthogonality:** Different SFs on the same channel are **quasi-orthogonal** — they can coexist without interfering (this enables massive capacity).

#### C. Coding Rate (CR)
- **Definition:** Forward Error Correction (FEC) rate, expressed as $4/(4+n)$ where $n \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$.
- **Allowed values:** **4/5, 4/6, 4/7, 4/8** (CR = 1, 2, 3, 4 in some notations).
- **Effect:** Higher redundancy → better robustness but **lower effective data rate**.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **examination-critical** for PERAT753. Master every line below.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Chirp rate | $\mu = \dfrac{BW}{T_s} = \dfrac{BW^2}{2^{SF}}$ | Hz/s |
| 2 | Symbol duration | $T_s = \dfrac{2^{SF}}{BW}$ | seconds |
| 3 | Symbol rate | $R_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s} = \dfrac{BW}{2^{SF}}$ | symbols/second (bauds) |
| 4 | Bit rate (raw) | $R_b = SF \cdot R_s = SF \cdot \dfrac{BW}{2^{SF}}$ | bits/second |
| 5 | Effective bit rate | $R_b^{eff} = SF \cdot \dfrac{BW}{2^{SF}} \cdot \dfrac{4}{4+CR}$ | bits/second |
| 6 | Receiver sensitivity (approx.) | $S = -174 + 10\log_{10}(BW) + NF + SNR_{limit}$ | dBm |
| 7 | Path loss (log-distance) | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10 n \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)$ | dB |
| 8 | Maximum payload size | Depends on SF; SF7 = 222 B, SF12 = 51 B (US, CR=1) | bytes |
| 9 | Range (rule of thumb, urban) | **2–5 km**; rural **10–15 km** | km |
| 10 | Transmit power (typical) | **+2 dBm to +20 dBm** (Europe limit 14 dBm, 25 mW) | dBm |

**Detailed Sensitivity Formula (more accurate):**

$$S_{min} = -174 \; \text{dBm/Hz} + 10\log_{10}(BW) + NF + SNR_{req}$$

Where:
- $-174 \; \text{dBm/Hz}$ = thermal noise floor at room temperature ($k_B T$)
- $NF$ = noise figure of the receiver (typically 3–6 dB for LoRa chips)
- $SNR_{req}$ = required SNR for given SF (e.g., SF7 ≈ −7.5 dB, SF12 ≈ −20 dB)

---

### 2.5 LoRaWAN Architecture (MAC Layer Overview)

While the KTU syllabus emphasizes the LoRa *physical layer*, a brief MAC-layer understanding is essential because the network behaves very differently from classical wireless systems.

**Network Topology: Star-of-Stars**

The LoRaWAN architecture consists of:
1. **End Devices** (sensors, actuators) — Class A, B, or C.
2. **Gateways** (concentrators) — transparent relay; bridge between RF and IP backhaul.
3. **Network Server** — central intelligence; de-duplicates, manages ADR, secures.
4. **Application Server** — interprets payload data for end-user applications.

> [!NOTE]
> Communication is **bi-directional** but **predominantly uplink-heavy**. Most IoT data flows from sensor to cloud.

**LoRaWAN Device Classes:**

| Class | Downlink Windows | Power Use | Use Case |
|-------|------------------|-----------|----------|
| **Class A** (default) | 2 windows after each uplink | **Lowest** | Battery-powered sensors (most common) |
| **Class B** | Scheduled downlink + Class A | Medium | Actuated devices (downlink latency bounded) |
| **Class C** | Continuously open (except during TX) | **Highest** | Mains-powered actuators (streetlights) |

**Adaptive Data Rate (ADR):** The Network Server can command end devices to change SF/BW to **optimize the overall network capacity**. If a device is close to the gateway, the server tells it to use SF7 (fast, brief airtime); if far, SF12 (slow, robust).

---

### 2.6 LoRa Frame Structure

A complete LoRa frame consists of:

1. **Preamble** (default 8 symbols, programmable 6–65535) — synchronization.
2. **Sync Word** (2 symbols) — network identification (0x34 for private LoRaWAN).
3. **SFD (Start Frame Delimiter)** (2.25 symbols) — marks end of preamble.
4. **Header** (implicit or explicit) — payload length, CR, presence of CRC.
5. **Payload** (1 to 255 bytes).
6. **CRC** (optional, 2 bytes if present).
7. **End of Transmission** (ramping down).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When computing **Time on Air (ToA)**, students often forget the **preamble length** overhead (default 8 symbols, plus 4.25 symbols for sync+SFD). Always add **preamble symbols to payload symbols** when computing total transmission time. Missing this is a guaranteed 2–3 mark deduction.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

LoRa has revolutionized:
- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil moisture sensors across hundreds of hectares.
- **Smart Cities:** Parking sensors, street lighting, waste management.
- **Asset Tracking:** Containers, livestock, vehicles (with GPS + LoRa combo).
- **Utility Metering:** Water/gas meters in basements unreachable by cellular.
- **Disaster Warning:** Tsunami/earthquake early-warning systems in remote areas.
- **Wildlife Conservation:** Anti-poaching collars tracking endangered species.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Symbol Rate from Chirp Duration

**Starting Point:** A single LoRa symbol is one complete chirp sweeping the bandwidth $BW$.

**Step 1 — Define bandwidth swept per second (chirp rate):**

The chirp sweeps $BW$ Hz over the symbol duration $T_s$. Therefore, the chirp rate is:

$$\mu = \frac{BW}{T_s} \quad \text{(Hz/s)}$$

**Step 2 — Define number of discrete frequency steps:**

For a given SF, the symbol space contains $2^{SF}$ distinguishable chirp shifts (one for each possible $k = 0, 1, \ldots, 2^{SF}-1$). The smallest resolvable frequency step is:

$$\Delta f = \frac{BW}{2^{SF}}$$

**Step 3 — Relate symbol duration to bandwidth and SF:**

In LoRa, the receiver uses an FFT of size $2^{SF}$ to resolve the symbol. The FFT bin width equals the reciprocal of the symbol duration, but bin resolution equals $\Delta f$. Hence:

$$\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_s} \implies T_s = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW}$$

> This shows that for a fixed BW, doubling SF **quadruples** $T_s$ (since $2^{SF}$ doubles only one step at a time, but the next SF doubles the exponent, making duration grow exponentially).

**Step 4 — Symbol rate (baud rate):**

$$R_s = \frac{1}{T_s} = \frac{BW}{2^{SF}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example 1:**

Given: $BW = 125 \; \text{kHz}$, $SF = 9$.

$$T_s = \frac{2^{9}}{125{,}000} = \frac{512}{125{,}000} = 4.096 \; \text{ms}$$

$$R_s = \frac{125{,}000}{512} = 244.14 \; \text{symbols/sec}$$

**Step 5 — Bit rate:**

Each symbol carries $SF$ bits, so:

$$R_b = SF \cdot R_s = SF \cdot \frac{BW}{2^{SF}}$$

For our example:

$$R_b = 9 \cdot 244.14 = 2197.3 \; \text{bits/sec} \approx 2.2 \; \text{kbps}$$

**Step 6 — Effective bit rate (with FEC):**

$$R_b^{eff} = R_b \cdot CR = SF \cdot \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \cdot \frac{4}{4+n}$$

Where $n = 1, 2, 3, 4$ for CR = 4/5, 4/6, 4/7, 4/8.

For SF=9, BW=125 kHz, CR=4/5 ($n=1$):

$$R_b^{eff} = 2197.3 \cdot \frac{4}{5} = 1757.8 \; \text{bps} \approx 1.76 \; \text{kbps}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Time on Air (ToA) for a LoRa Packet

**Step 1 — Number of payload symbols:**

For an explicit-header LoRa packet with payload length $PL$ (bytes), the number of payload symbols is:

$$n_{payload} = 8 + \max\!\left(\left\lceil \frac{8PL - 4SF + 28 + 16CRC - 20IH}{4(SF - 2DE)} \right\rceil \cdot (CR + 4), \; 0\right)$$

Where:
- $DE = 1$ if low-data-rate optimization is enabled (mandatory when $SF \ge 11$), else $DE = 0$
- $IH = 1$ if implicit header, else $IH = 0$
- $CRC = 1$ if CRC enabled, else $CRC = 0$
- $PL$ = payload length in bytes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This formula looks intimidating, but it is **the official Semtech SX1276 LoRa calculator equation** and is frequently asked in numerical problems. Memorize the structure.

**Step 2 — Preamble symbols:**

$$n_{preamble} = (n_{pre} + 4.25) \quad \text{symbols}$$

Where $n_{pre}$ = programmable preamble length (default 8).

For default preamble:

$$n_{preamble} = 8 + 4.25 = 12.25 \; \text{symbols}$$

**Step 3 — Total Time on Air:**

$$T_{packet} = n_{preamble} \cdot T_s + n_{payload} \cdot T_s$$

$$T_{packet} = (n_{preamble} + n_{payload}) \cdot \frac{2^{SF}}{BW}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Time on Air Calculation

**Problem [KTU University Exam Style]:**
A LoRa transmitter uses $SF = 11$, $BW = 125 \; \text{kHz}$, $CR = 4/5$, $PL = 20$ bytes, explicit header, no CRC, default preamble. Compute the Time on Air.

**Solution:**

Given: $SF = 11$, $BW = 125{,}000$ Hz, $n=1$ (CR=4/5), $PL = 20$, $IH = 0$, $CRC = 0$, $DE = 1$ (since SF≥11).

**Step 1 — Symbol duration:**

$$T_s = \frac{2^{11}}{125{,}000} = \frac{2048}{125{,}000} = 16.384 \; \text{ms}$$

**Step 2 — Compute numerator inside ceiling:**

$$N_{num} = 8 \cdot PL - 4 \cdot SF + 28 + 16 \cdot CRC - 20 \cdot IH$$

$$N_{num} = 8(20) - 4(11) + 28 + 0 - 0 = 160 - 44 + 28 = 144$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator:**

$$D = 4 \cdot (SF - 2 \cdot DE) = 4 \cdot (11 - 2) = 4 \cdot 9 = 36$$

**Step 4 — Compute intermediate:**

$$\frac{N_{num}}{D} = \frac{144}{36} = 4$$

**Step 5 — Apply ceiling and scaling:**

$$n_{payload} = 8 + \lceil 4 \rceil \cdot (CR + 4) = 8 + 4 \cdot (1 + 4) = 8 + 20 = 28 \; \text{symbols}$$

**Step 6 — Preamble symbols:**

$$n_{preamble} = 8 + 4.25 = 12.25 \; \text{symbols}$$

**Step 7 — Total time on air:**

$$T_{packet} = (12.25 + 28) \cdot 16.384 \; \text{ms}$$

$$T_{packet} = 40.25 \cdot 16.384 = 659.456 \; \text{ms} \approx 0.66 \; \text{seconds}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice how SF=11 produces nearly a **second of airtime** for just 20 bytes. This is why **duty cycle regulations** (e.g., 1% in Europe ETSI EN 300 220) severely limit SF11/SF12 use to a few transmissions per hour.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Link Budget and Maximum Range

**Step 1 — Link budget equation:**

The maximum allowable path loss is:

$$PL_{max} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - S_{min} - M_f - M_{other}$$

Where:
- $P_{TX}$ = transmit power (dBm)
- $G_{TX}, G_{RX}$ = antenna gains (dBi)
- $S_{min}$ = receiver sensitivity (dBm)
- $M_f$ = fading margin (typically 10–20 dB)
- $M_{other}$ = other losses (cable, body loss, etc.)

**Step 2 — Path loss model (log-distance):**

$$PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10 n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)$$

With $d_0 = 1$ km and $PL(d_0) = 127.41$ dB at 868 MHz (free-space reference). The path loss exponent $n$:
- $n = 2$: free space
- $n = 2.7 - 3.5$: urban
- $n = 3 - 4$: dense urban/indoor

**Step 3 — Solving for maximum range:**

Setting $PL(d_{max}) = PL_{max}$ and solving for $d_{max}$:

$$d_{max} = d_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{PL_{max} - PL(d_0)}{10 n}}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Receiver Sensitivity

**Problem:** Compute receiver sensitivity for $BW = 125 \; \text{kHz}$, $SF = 12$, $NF = 6 \; \text{dB}$, $SNR_{req} = -20 \; \text{dB}$.

**Solution:**

$$S_{min} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(BW) + NF + SNR_{req}$$

$$S_{min} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(125{,}000) + 6 + (-20)$$

$$10\log_{10}(125{,}000) = 10 \times 5.0969 = 50.97 \; \text{dB}$$

$$S_{min} = -174 + 50.97 + 6 - 20 = -137.03 \; \text{dBm}$$

This is the famous **−137 dBm sensitivity** widely cited for SF12/125 kHz LoRa.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Cellular 4G receivers have sensitivities of around −100 to −110 dBm. The 25–30 dB advantage of LoRa translates directly to **~10× to 30× greater range** for equal transmit power.

---

### 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix — LoRa vs. Competing LPWAN Technologies

| Parameter | **LoRa (CSS)** | **Sigfox (UNB)** | **NB-IoT (LTE)** |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|------------------|
| Modulation | Chirp Spread Spectrum | Ultra-Narrow Band DBPSK | QPSK (LTE-based) |
| Spectrum | Sub-GHz ISM (unlicensed) | Sub-GHz ISM (unlicensed) | Licensed cellular |
| Bandwidth | 125 / 250 / 500 kHz | 100 Hz (extremely narrow) | 180 kHz |
| Data rate | 0.3 – 50 kbps | 100–600 bps | ~200 kbps |
| Range | 2–15 km | 3–10 km | 1–10 km |
| Battery life | **10+ years** | 10+ years | ~10 years |
| Cost per node | **Low (€5–10)** | Low (€5) | Higher (€15+) |
| Capacity (devices/cell) | ~1 million (with ADR) | ~1 million | ~50,000/cell |
| Operator dependency | No (deploy your own) | Yes (Sigfox network) | Yes (cellular operator) |

## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 LoRa Modulation — Functional Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional signal flow inside a LoRa transmitter and receiver, showing the chirp generation, dechirp, and FFT decoding process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data Bits] --> B[Channel Coding FEC CR 4 over 4+n]
    B --> C[Interleaver and Whitening]
    C --> D[Symbol Mapper SF bits per symbol 2 to the power SF levels]
    D --> E[Chirp Generator Linear Frequency Sweep across BW]
    E --> F[RF Frontend Mix up to 868 MHz or 915 MHz]
    F --> G[Transmit Antenna Sub GHz ISM Band]

    G --> H[Receive Antenna]
    H --> I[RF Frontend Low Noise Amplifier and Downconversion]
    I --> J[Dechirper Multiply with Conjugate Downchirp]
    J --> K[FFT Processor Size 2 to the power SF bins]
    K --> L[Symbol Detector Argmax of FFT magnitude]
    L --> M[Deinterleaver and FEC Decoding]
    M --> N[Output Data Bits]

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    style N fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    style E fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style K fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    style G fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#fff
    style H fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** The left-to-right top path is the **transmitter chain**. The bottom path is the **receiver chain**. Note the elegance: the **dechirper converts the chirp into a single-tone sinusoid**, whose frequency equals the symbol value — turning a time-frequency problem into a trivial frequency-detection problem solvable by an FFT.

### 4.2 LoRaWAN Star-of-Stars Network Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph END_NODES[End Devices Sensors and Actuators]
        ED1[Node A Class A Battery Sensor]
        ED2[Node B Class B Scheduled Downlink]
        ED3[Node C Class C Mains Powered]
    end

    subgraph GWAY_LAYER[Gateway Layer Transparent Relay]
        GW1[Gateway 1 Concentrator]
        GW2[Gateway 2 Concentrator]
        GW3[Gateway 3 Concentrator]
    end

    subgraph BACK_HAUL[Network and Application Backend]
        NS[Network Server De duplication ADR Security]
        AS[Application Server Payload Decode]
        APP[End User Dashboard or Cloud]
    end

    ED1 -.->|Uplink 868 MHz CSS| GW1
    ED1 -.->|Uplink 868 MHz CSS| GW2
    ED2 -.->|Uplink 868 MHz CSS| GW2
    ED2 -.->|Uplink 868 MHz CSS| GW3
    ED3 -.->|Uplink 868 MHz CSS| GW1
    ED3 -.->|Uplink 868 MHz CSS| GW3

    GW1 -->|IP Backhaul Cellular Ethernet| NS
    GW2 -->|IP Backhaul| NS
    GW3 -->|IP Backhaul| NS
    NS --> AS
    AS --> APP

    NS -.->|Downlink via best GW| ED1
    NS -.->|Downlink scheduled| ED2
    NS -.->|Continuous downlink| ED3

    style ED1 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    style ED2 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** A single end-device's transmission can be received by **multiple gateways simultaneously**. The Network Server (NS) handles **de-duplication** by comparing signal metadata (FCnt, DevAddr, MIC). This redundancy is fundamental to LoRaWAN's reliability.

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define LoRa. Explain why Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) modulation is used in LoRa instead of conventional OFDM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** LoRa (Long Range) is a low-power, wide-area network physical layer modulation technique developed by Semtech, based on Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS), designed for long-range, low-data-rate IoT communication in sub-GHz ISM bands.

**Why CSS instead of OFDM (2 marks):**

1. **Receiver simplicity:** CSS requires only a single FFT of size $2^{SF}$ at the receiver, whereas OFDM requires precise synchronization, channel estimation, and complex equalization — impractical for low-cost coin-cell-powered IoT devices.

2. **Robustness to Doppler and multipath:** Chirps spread energy uniformly over time and frequency, making them inherently resistant to multipath fading and frequency-selective interference. OFDM suffers from inter-carrier interference (ICI) under Doppler.

3. **Quasi-orthogonality across SFs:** Different spreading factors on the same channel can be received without interference, multiplying network capacity — a property OFDM does not natively provide.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List the three key configuration parameters of LoRa. Explain how the Spreading Factor (SF) affects range and data rate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Three parameters (1.5 marks):**
1. **Bandwidth (BW):** Channel width, e.g., 125 kHz, 250 kHz, 500 kHz.
2. **Spreading Factor (SF):** Integer from 7 to 12, indicates bits per symbol.
3. **Coding Rate (CR):** FEC redundancy, values 4/5, 4/6, 4/7, 4/8.

**Effect of SF on range and data rate (1.5 marks):**
- **Range:** Higher SF → longer symbol duration → more processing gain → higher receiver sensitivity (≈ +2.5 to 3 dB per SF step) → **increased range**.
- **Data rate:** Higher SF → symbol duration grows exponentially ($T_s = 2^{SF}/BW$) → **drastically reduced data rate** (each SF step roughly halves the bit rate).
- **Trade-off:** SF is a **range-versus-throughput** knob. SF7 maximizes throughput; SF12 maximizes robustness.

---

## QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Chirp Modulation and Link Budget

### Part (a) [7 marks]

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]**
With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a LoRa transmitter and receiver. Describe how Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) encodes data symbols using cyclic time shifts of a base chirp.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

The LoRa transmitter performs the following sequence:

1. **Input data bits** are passed through an **FEC encoder** (Hamming-style, rate CR = 4/(4+n)) for forward error correction.
2. The coded bits are **whitened and interleaved** to randomize bit patterns and spread burst errors.
3. A **symbol mapper** groups $SF$ bits into one of $2^{SF}$ possible symbols ($k$).
4. The **chirp generator** synthesizes a base up-chirp sweeping frequency from $f_{\min}$ to $f_{\max}$ over $BW$, then cyclically shifts it by $\Delta t_k = k \cdot T_s / 2^{SF}$ to encode the symbol value $k$.
5. The **RF frontend** upconverts the chirp to the carrier frequency (e.g., 868 MHz) and transmits.

The receiver chain mirrors this:

1. The **RF frontend** downconverts and amplifies (LNA).
2. A **dechirper** multiplies the incoming chirp by a conjugate down-chirp, converting it into a **single-frequency tone** at frequency $f_k = k \cdot BW / 2^{SF}$.
3. An **FFT of size $2^{SF}$** converts the time-domain tone to a frequency-domain bin index, which directly maps to symbol $k$.
4. The detected symbol is **deinterleaved and FEC-decoded** to recover the bits.

**CSS Data Encoding by Cyclic Time Shift (4 marks):**

A base up-chirp has instantaneous frequency $f(t) = f_{\min} + \mu t$ for $0 \le t \le T_s$, where $\mu = BW / T_s$. To encode symbol $k \in \{0, 1, \ldots, 2^{SF}-1\}$, the chirp is **shifted in time** so that at $t=0$ the frequency is $f_{\min} + (BW - k \cdot \Delta f)$, where $\Delta f = BW / 2^{SF}$. Equivalently, the chirp is cyclically rotated by an amount $\Delta t_k = k \cdot T_s / 2^{SF}$. This makes each of the $2^{SF}$ possible symbols correspond to a **distinct chirp pattern** that is **orthogonal** to all others (correlation peak at one symbol value, zero at all others). The dechirp operation transforms these time-shifted chirps into $2^{SF}$ distinct FFT bins, enabling detection.

---

### Part (b) [7 marks]

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]**
A LoRa system operates at 868 MHz with $BW = 125 \; \text{kHz}$, $SF = 10$, $CR = 4/5$, transmit power $P_{TX} = 14 \; \text{dBm}$, antenna gains $G_{TX} = G_{RX} = 2 \; \text{dBi}$, fading margin $M_f = 15 \; \text{dB}$, noise figure $NF = 6 \; \text{dB}$, $SNR_{req} = -15 \; \text{dB}$, and path loss exponent $n = 3$. Reference path loss $PL(d_0) = 127.41 \; \text{dB}$ at $d_0 = 1 \; \text{km}$. Compute the receiver sensitivity and maximum range.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Receiver Sensitivity (3 marks):**

$$S_{min} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(BW) + NF + SNR_{req}$$

$$10\log_{10}(125{,}000) = 50.97 \; \text{dB}$$

$$S_{min} = -174 + 50.97 + 6 + (-15) = -132.03 \; \text{dBm}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Allowable Path Loss (2 marks):**

$$PL_{max} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - S_{min} - M_f$$

$$PL_{max} = 14 + 2 + 2 - (-132.03) - 15 = 135.03 \; \text{dB}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Range (2 marks):**

$$d_{max} = d_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{PL_{max} - PL(d_0)}{10 n}}$$

$$d_{max} = 1 \cdot 10^{\frac{135.03 - 127.41}{30}} = 10^{\frac{7.62}{30}} = 10^{0.254} \approx 1.79 \; \text{km}$$

**Final Answer:** Sensitivity = **−132.03 dBm**, Maximum range ≈ **1.79 km** for an urban environment with $n=3$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Errors in LoRa Link Budget Problems:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert $BW$ to Hz (or omitting the $10\log_{10}(BW)$ term entirely) — costs **2 marks**.
> 2. Sign confusion: $PL_{max} = P_{TX} + \text{gains} - S_{min} - \text{margins}$. Students often write $PL_{max} = P_{TX} - S_{min}$ and forget gains/margins.
> 3. Mixing dBm and dB units. $P_{TX}$ is in dBm; $S_{min}$ is in dBm; the difference yields dB — but only if both are dBm.
> 4. Forgetting that $SNR_{req}$ is **negative** for LoRa (−7.5 dB for SF7, −20 dB for SF12). A common mistake is treating it as positive.
> 5. In the log-distance equation, students sometimes forget to use **10n** (not $n$) inside the exponent.

---

## QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Time on Air and LoRaWAN Architecture (Alternative Choice)

### Part (a) [7 marks]

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
A LoRa end-node transmits a 30-byte payload using $SF = 9$, $BW = 125 \; \text{kHz}$, $CR = 4/7$ ($n = 3$), explicit header enabled, no CRC, default preamble (8 symbols). Compute:
(i) Symbol duration
(ii) Number of payload symbols
(iii) Total Time on Air
(iv) Effective bit rate

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $SF = 9$, $BW = 125{,}000$ Hz, $CR = 4/7$ ($n=3$), $PL = 30$ bytes, $IH = 0$, $CRC = 0$, $DE = 0$ (since SF < 11).

**(i) Symbol Duration (2 marks):**

$$T_s = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW} = \frac{2^9}{125{,}000} = \frac{512}{125{,}000} = 4.096 \; \text{ms}$$

**(ii) Number of Payload Symbols (2 marks):**

Using the Semtech formula:

$$n_{payload} = 8 + \left\lceil \frac{8PL - 4SF + 28 + 16CRC - 20IH}{4(SF - 2DE)} \right\rceil \cdot (CR + 4)$$

**Numerator:**
$$8(30) - 4(9) + 28 + 0 - 0 = 240 - 36 + 28 = 232$$

**Denominator:**
$$4(9 - 0) = 36$$

**Ratio:**
$$\frac{232}{36} = 6.444 \implies \lceil \cdot \rceil = 7$$

**Scaling:**
$$n_{payload} = 8 + 7 \cdot (3 + 4) = 8 + 49 = 57 \; \text{symbols}$$

**(iii) Total Time on Air (2 marks):**

Preamble symbols: $n_{preamble} = 8 + 4.25 = 12.25$ symbols.

$$T_{packet} = (n_{preamble} + n_{payload}) \cdot T_s = (12.25 + 57) \cdot 4.096 \; \text{ms}$$

$$T_{packet} = 69.25 \cdot 4.096 = 283.65 \; \text{ms} \approx 0.284 \; \text{seconds}$$

**(iv) Effective Bit Rate (1 mark):**

$$R_b^{eff} = SF \cdot \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \cdot \frac{4}{4+n} = 9 \cdot \frac{125{,}000}{512} \cdot \frac{4}{7}$$

$$R_b^{eff} = 9 \cdot 244.14 \cdot 0.5714 = 1255 \; \text{bps} \approx 1.26 \; \text{kbps}$$

---

### Part (b) [7 marks]

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Explain the LoRaWAN network architecture with a star-of-stars topology. Compare the three LoRaWAN device classes (A, B, C) in terms of power consumption and downlink capability. Also explain Adaptive Data Rate (ADR).

**Model Solution:**

**LoRaWAN Architecture (3 marks):**

LoRaWAN uses a **star-of-stars topology**:
- **End Devices (EDs):** The sensors/actuators running on batteries. Communicate with gateways via LoRa CSS modulation in the ISM band.
- **Gateways (GWs):** Transparent packet forwarders — they relay LoRa RF frames between EDs and the Network Server over an IP backhaul (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, or cellular). They perform **no intelligence**; no de-duplication, no ADR.
- **Network Server (NS):** The central intelligence. Performs **de-duplication** (since one uplink can hit multiple gateways), **ADR management**, **security (MIC validation, frame counters)**, **MAC command processing**.
- **Application Server (AS):** Decodes the application payload and forwards it to end-user dashboards.

A single ED's uplink can be received by multiple gateways; only the NS determines which duplicate to discard. This redundancy is critical to LoRaWAN's reliability.

**Comparison of LoRaWAN Classes (3 marks):**

| Feature | **Class A** | **Class B** | **Class C** |
|---------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| Downlink windows | 2 windows only **after** each uplink | Class A windows + **scheduled** extra windows | Always open (except during own TX) |
| Server-initiated downlink latency | Variable (must wait for next uplink) | **Bounded** (synchronized via beacons) | **~1 second** |
| Power consumption | **Lowest** (sleeps most of the time) | Medium (wakes periodically for beacon slots) | **Highest** (RX always on) |
| Typical power source | Battery (coin cell) | Battery | **Mains powered** |
| Use cases | Environmental sensors, meters | Actuated valves, lighting | Streetlights, electrical switches |

**Class A** is the **default and mandatory** baseline. Class B adds beacons from gateways to synchronize extra downlink slots. Class C is for mains-powered devices needing near-instantaneous downlink.

**Adaptive Data Rate (ADR) — 1 mark:**

ADR is a **Network-Server-driven mechanism** that optimizes the trade-off between airtime and reliability across the entire network. When a device's uplink is consistently received at high SNR (i.e., it is close to a gateway), the NS sends a MAC command instructing it to:
- **Increase data rate** (use lower SF or higher BW) → reduces airtime, frees channel for others.
- **Decrease transmit power** → saves battery.

When a device is at the cell edge, ADR tells it to use SF12 for robustness. ADR is **opt-in** (the device requests it via LinkADRReq MAC command) and is critical for **maximizing network capacity** in dense deployments.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — High-Frequency Mark Loss Areas for LoRa Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting preamble + SFD** in Time on Air calculations (default overhead = 12.25 symbols).
> 2. **Confusing LoRa (PHY) with LoRaWAN (MAC)** — they are NOT the same.
> 3. **Treating SF as "more chirps"** rather than "bits per symbol" — wrong dimensional concept.
> 4. **Using $\Delta f = BW/SF$** instead of $\Delta f = BW/2^{SF}$ in any related calculation.
> 5. **Forgetting the duty cycle regulation** (1% in EU868 for sub-GHz ISM) when discussing maximum packets per hour.
> 6. **Not converting BW to Hz** (or to dB-Hz via $10\log_{10}$) when computing noise floor or sensitivity.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> The following bullets form the **last-page revision card** before entering the KTU examination hall. Review them twice.

- ☐ **LoRa = PHY (modulation). LoRaWAN = MAC (protocol).** Never confuse.
- ☐ **Modulation = Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** — sweeping linear FM chirps.
- ☐ **LoRa encodes data via cyclic time shift of chirps**; SF bits per symbol.
- ☐ **Three parameters:** $BW$ (125/250/500 kHz), $SF$ (7–12), $CR$ (4/5–4/8).
- ☐ **Symbol duration:** $T_s = 2^{SF}/BW$ (grows exponentially with SF).
- ☐ **Bit rate:** $R_b = SF \cdot BW / 2^{SF} \cdot (4/(4+n))$ — decreases with SF.
- ☐ **Receiver sensitivity:** $S_{min} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(BW) + NF + SNR_{req}$; SF12/125 kHz ≈ **−137 dBm**.
- ☐ **Higher SF** = lower data rate, higher sensitivity, longer range, longer airtime.
- ☐ **Higher BW** = higher data rate, lower sensitivity (more noise captured).
- ☐ **Range rule of thumb:** 2–5 km urban, 10–15 km rural, sub-GHz ISM.
- ☐ **Frequencies:** 868 MHz (EU), 915 MHz (US), 433 MHz (Asia), 2.4 GHz (newer).
- ☐ **LoRaWAN topology:** Star-of-stars; ED → Gateway → Network Server → App Server.
- ☐ **Classes:** A (lowest power, 2 windows after uplink); B (scheduled + Class A); C (always RX, mains).
- ☐ **ADR:** Network Server dynamically tunes SF/BW to optimize network capacity.
- ☐ **LoRa frame:** Preamble (8 symbols) + Sync Word + SFD + Header + Payload + optional CRC.
- ☐ **Time on Air:** Use Semtech formula; always add **preamble overhead = 12.25 symbols** (default).
- ☐ **Duty cycle (EU868):** ≤ 1% — limits SF11/SF12 to a few packets per hour.
- ☐ **CSS advantage over OFDM:** simpler receiver, robust to multipath/Doppler, SF orthogonality.
- ☐ **Link budget:** $PL_{max} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - S_{min} - M_f$.
- ☐ **Path loss model:** $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n\log_{10}(d/d_0)$; urban $n \approx 3$.
- ☐ **Applications:** Smart agriculture, smart cities, asset tracking, smart metering, environmental monitoring.
- ☐ **Key standard metric:** Sensitivity ~ −137 dBm (SF12, BW=125 kHz), Tx power ≤ 14 dBm EU.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: WiFi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** WiFi

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WiFi (Wireless Fidelity)** is a set of Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN) protocols defined by the **IEEE 802.11 family of standards**, governed by the WiFi Alliance. It enables devices to communicate over short ranges (typically 20–100 m indoors and up to 250 m outdoors) using radio waves in the unlicensed **ISM (Industrial, Scientific, and Medical)** bands — primarily **2.4 GHz**, **5 GHz**, and the newer **6 GHz** band.

The IEEE 802.11 standard specifies both the **Physical Layer (PHY)** — modulation, channelization, transmission rates — and the **Medium Access Control (MAC) sub-layer** of the OSI Data-Link Layer. WiFi uses a contention-based access scheme called **CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance)** to coordinate multiple stations sharing the same radio medium.

The WiFi protocol stack contains the following essential layers:

| Layer | Function | Key Protocol Element |
|---|---|---|
| PHY Layer | Modulation, coding, RF transmission | DSSS, OFDM, MIMO |
| MAC Layer | Channel access, addressing, reliability | CSMA/CA, DCF, PCF |
| LLC Layer | Bridging to upper layers | IEEE 802.2 |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of WiFi like a polite round-table dinner conversation** at a noisy restaurant. Before anyone speaks (transmits data), they **listen** for a pause in conversation (carrier sensing). They then **wait a random moment** (backoff) to avoid two people shouting at once. If two people do talk over each other (collision), they each pause, wait a longer random time, and try again. The **"wait your turn politely"** mechanism is exactly what CSMA/CA does — except at microsecond speeds and at radio frequencies.

A real-world analogy: imagine your home has a single telephone line (the wireless channel). WiFi acts as the **traffic police** that decides who may speak, for how long, and when — ensuring everyone gets a fair chance without being cut off.

## 1.3 Standardized Operating Bands (KTU Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory WiFi Frequency Bands:**
> - **2.4 GHz Band** (2.400 – 2.4835 GHz) → Channels 1–13 (each 5 MHz wide, spaced 5 MHz apart; OFDM uses 20/40 MHz channels)
> - **5 GHz Band** (5.15 – 5.825 GHz) → UNII-1, UNII-2, UNII-2 Extended, UNII-3, UNII-4 sub-bands
> - **6 GHz Band** (5.925 – 7.125 GHz) → WiFi 6E and WiFi 7

The **2.4 GHz** band offers longer range but more interference (microwaves, Bluetooth). The **5 GHz** band offers faster speeds but shorter range. The **6 GHz** band (WiFi 6E / WiFi 7) is the cleanest with maximum bandwidth.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Quick Reference

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **STA (Station)** | Any WiFi-capable device |
| **AP (Access Point)** | Central hub that bridges wireless to wired networks |
| **BSS (Basic Service Set)** | Group of STAs controlled by a single AP |
| **ESS (Extended Service Set)** | Multiple BSSs connected via a Distribution System |
| **DS (Distribution System)** | Wired backbone connecting APs |
| **IBSS (Independent BSS)** | Ad-hoc network without an AP |
| **SSID** | Network name broadcast by AP |
| **RSSI** | Received Signal Strength Indicator (in dBm) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IEEE 802.11 Network Architecture

The 802.11 architecture has three logical building blocks:

**Step 1 — Independent BSS (IBSS):** A peer-to-peer ad-hoc network. Two or more stations communicate **directly without an AP**. Used for quick file transfers, IoT bootstrapping, and mesh bootstrapping.

**Step 2 — Infrastructure BSS:** A standard home/office setup. All traffic flows **through the AP**, which acts as the bridge to the wired LAN. The AP periodically broadcasts **Beacon Frames** (typically every 100 ms) containing the SSID, supported rates, and synchronization information.

**Step 3 — Extended Service Set (ESS):** Multiple BSSs linked through a **Distribution System (DS)** — usually an Ethernet backbone. This enables **roaming**, where a mobile STA can move from one AP's coverage area to another's without losing connectivity. The DS forwards frames between BSSs based on the destination MAC address.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why an AP-centric design?** It centralizes association management, power-saving control (via TIM — Traffic Indication Map), and security enforcement. It also simplifies routing because the AP serves as the single point of ingress/egress.

## 2.2 The Hidden Node Problem (KTU Favorite Question)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hidden Node Problem:** Three stations A, B, C exist such that A and C are both within range of B but **A and C cannot hear each other**. When A senses the channel as idle and begins transmitting, C — unaware — also transmits, causing a **collision at B**.

**Solution — RTS/CTS Handshake:**
- A sends **RTS (Request-To-Send)** containing the duration of the upcoming transmission.
- B replies with **CTS (Clear-To-Send)** echoing the duration field.
- C, hearing the CTS, defers for the announced duration via its **Network Allocation Vector (NAV)**.
- Both A and C have a virtual carrier sense set, preventing collision.

This is the cornerstone of the IEEE 802.11 collision-avoidance mechanism because WiFi **cannot detect collisions** (it is half-duplex — cannot transmit and listen simultaneously).

## 2.3 CSMA/CA — Distributed Coordination Function (DCF)

The DCF is the mandatory MAC access method. It works in 4 stages:

**Stage 1 — DIFS Wait:** After the medium becomes idle, the station waits for a **DCF Inter-Frame Space (DIFS)** duration before attempting to transmit.

**Stage 2 — Random Backoff:** The station selects a random backoff counter drawn uniformly from $[0, CW]$, where $CW$ (Contention Window) starts at $CW_{min}$ and doubles after each collision up to $CW_{max}$.

**Stage 3 — Slot-by-Slot Decrement:** The backoff counter decrements by 1 for every idle slot detected. If the medium becomes busy, the counter freezes.

**Stage 4 — Transmission:** When the counter reaches zero, the station transmits the frame. If no ACK is received within a timeout, the frame is presumed lost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Collision Avoidance" instead of "Collision Detection"?** WiFi radios are **half-duplex** — they cannot listen while transmitting. Detecting collisions (as Ethernet does via CSMA/CD) is physically impossible. So WiFi **avoids** them by being cautious and polite via backoff + RTS/CTS.

## 2.4 Inter-Frame Space Hierarchy (IFS)

The IFS values establish strict priority ordering:

| IFS | Symbol | Typical Duration | Used By |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SIFS** | Short IFS | 10 μs (802.11b) | ACK, CTS, fragments |
| **PIFS** | PCF IFS | SIFS + 1 slot | PCF contention-free |
| **DIFS** | DCF IFS | SIFS + 2 slots | Normal data frames |
| **EIFS** | Extended IFS | SIFS + ACK + DIFS | Error recovery |

**Priority Order (highest to lowest):** SIFS > PIFS > DIFS > EIFS. A station waiting only SIFS gets to transmit before one waiting DIFS, enabling immediate responses like ACK and CTS.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains exam-ready formulas. Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead of $\vert$ to prevent markdown table breakage.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Expression / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DCF Throughput** | $S$ | $S = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{T_{DIFS} + \overline{B}\cdot T_{slot} + T_{data} + T_{SIFS} + T_{ACK} + 2\delta}$ | bps |
| 2 | **Average Backoff** | $\overline{B}$ | $\overline{B} = \dfrac{CW}{2}$, $CW \in [CW_{min}, CW_{max}]$ | slots |
| 3 | **CW Update Rule** | $CW_i$ | $CW_{i+1} = \min(2(CW_i+1)-1,\ CW_{max})$ | — |
| 4 | **802.11b Slot Time** | $T_{slot}$ | $20$ | μs |
| 5 | **802.11a/g Slot Time** | $T_{slot}$ | $9$ | μs |
| 6 | **DIFS Duration** | $T_{DIFS}$ | $T_{SIFS} + 2 \cdot T_{slot}$ | μs |
| 7 | **Data Transmission Time** | $T_{data}$ | $\dfrac{L_{payload} + H_{MAC} + H_{PHY}}{R_{data}}$ | seconds |
| 8 | **ACK Transmission Time** | $T_{ACK}$ | $\dfrac{L_{ACK} + H_{PHY}}{R_{basic}}$ | seconds |
| 9 | **Free-Space Path Loss** | $FSPL$ | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | dB |
| 10 | **SNR (Bit Rate Cap)** | $\rho$ | $\rho = \dfrac{P_{rx}}{N_0 B}$ | dimensionless |
