- **Application Layer:** Safety applications (forward collision warning, emergency electronic brake light), traffic efficiency (green wave, platooning), and infotainment.
- **Networking & Transport Layer:** Routing protocols (GeoNetworking, AODV), transport (UDP for safety, TCP for non-safety), and congestion control.
- **Access Layer:** MAC and PHY—either IEEE 802.11p (DSRC) or 3GPP sidelink (C-V2X). Operates in the **5.9 GHz** band.
- **Sensing / Perception Layer:** On-board sensors (cameras, radar, LiDAR) and GNSS (GPS / Galileo) feeding data into the cooperative awareness engine.

## 2.3 Communication Type Taxonomy (V2X)

The taxonomy of V2X is the single most heavily tested KTU topic. Memorize the five-element classification:

| Mode | Full Form | Counterpart | Range | Latency Budget | Example Use Case |
|:-----|:----------|:------------|:------|:---------------|:-----------------|
| V2V | Vehicle-to-Vehicle | Other cars | 50–300 m | < 100 ms | Forward collision warning |
| V2I | Vehicle-to-Infrastructure | RSU / traffic light | 100–1000 m | < 100 ms | Signal phase timing, curve speed warning |
| V2P | Vehicle-to-Pedestrian | Smartphone / cyclist | 50–100 m | < 100 ms | Pedestrian crossing alert |
| V2N | Vehicle-to-Network | Cellular tower / cloud | km-scale | 100 ms – 1 s | HD map download, software OTA update |
| V2X | Vehicle-to-Everything | All of the above | Variable | Variable | Cooperative automated driving |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently interchange V2X and V2V. **V2X is the umbrella term**; V2V is one specific mode *within* the V2X family. KTU examiners specifically mark this distinction.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Design Decision

- **Why 5.9 GHz?** This band balances propagation range (line-of-sight dominant, ~300 m) with antenna size ($\lambda \approx 5$ cm fits inside a vehicle's roof rack) and is lightly licensed.
- **Why 10 MHz channel (not 20 MHz)?** A narrower channel gives better robustness against delay spread in high-mobility channels (coherence bandwidth constraints).
- **Why 10 Hz CAM rate?** It balances channel load with positional accuracy: a vehicle traveling at 30 m/s covers only 3 m between beacons, well within GPS uncertainty.
- **Why sidelink (PC5) in C-V2X?** It enables direct V2V without a cellular base station—critical in rural areas with no coverage.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are exhaustively required for KTU ESE numericals. Do **not** skip any.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:--------------|
| 1 | $f_d = \dfrac{v_{rel} \cdot f_c}{c}$ | Doppler frequency shift | Hz; $v_{rel}$ in m/s |
| 2 | $T_c \approx \dfrac{0.423}{f_d}$ | Coherence time (Jakes) | seconds |
| 3 | $PL_{FS}(d) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f_c) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right)$ | Free-space path loss (Friis) | dB; $d$ in m |
| 4 | $PL_{ITSU}(d) = PL_0 + 10 n \log_{10}(d) + X_\sigma$ | Log-distance path loss model | dB; $n \in [1.6, 2.1]$ for LOS |
| 5 | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Shannon channel capacity | bits/s |
| 6 | $R_{max} = \sqrt[\ ]{\dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{P_{min} (4\pi)^2}}$ | Max free-space range | m |
| 7 | $D_{rel} = v_{rel} \cdot \tau$ | Distance covered during transmission | m; $\tau$ = packet airtime |
| 8 | $PLR = 1 - (1 - p)^n$ | Packet Loss Ratio over $n$ transmissions | dimensionless |
| 9 | $D_c \approx \dfrac{0.5}{\sigma_\tau}$ | Coherence bandwidth | Hz; $\sigma_\tau$ = RMS delay spread |
| 10 | $f_{beacon} = \dfrac{v}{d_{res}}$ | Beacon frequency for resolution $d_{res}$ | Hz; $v$ in m/s |
| 11 | $N_{nodes} = \rho \cdot \pi R^2$ | Average neighbors in range | nodes; $\rho$ = density |
| 12 | $E[P] = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy at impact (Joules) | J; $m$ in kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Formula 3:** The free-space path loss is often written in simplified form as $PL_{FS}(d) = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ dB. KTU examiners often expect this form for 5.9 GHz calculations.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles covered here underpin four major industries:

1. **Automotive OEMs (Tesla, Toyota, BMW):** Integrate C-V2X modems into 2025+ model year vehicles.
2. **Smart City Infrastructure:** Municipalities deploy RSUs at intersections for V2I cooperative signal phase timing.
3. **Cellular Network Operators (Ericsson, Nokia, Huawei):** Monetize V2N services via 5G slicing for automotive verticals.
4. **Logistics & Fleet Management:** Truck platooning using V2V saves 10–15% fuel via aerodynamic drafting.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents the most important derivations a KTU 2024 student is expected to reproduce on paper. Each derivation is fully expanded with the reasoning behind every algebraic step.

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Doppler Shift in a V2V Channel

The Doppler effect describes the apparent shift in the received carrier frequency when a transmitter and receiver are in relative motion. For a vehicle moving with velocity $v$ at carrier frequency $f_c$, the maximum Doppler shift is given by the projection of velocity onto the line-of-sight direction.

**Given:**
- Carrier frequency: $f_c = 5.9$ GHz $= 5.9 \times 10^9$ Hz
- Relative velocity: $v_{rel} = 70$ m/s (≈ 252 km/h, typical highway scenario)
- Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s

**Step 1 — Definition of Doppler shift for a moving receiver:**

The classical Doppler formula for a wave received by a moving observer is:

$$
f_r = f_c \left( \frac{c + v_{obs}}{c} \right)
$$

where $v_{obs}$ is the velocity component of the observer along the direction of wave propagation. For a vehicle moving **toward** the transmitter (worst case), $v_{obs}$ is positive.

**Step 2 — Calculate the absolute Doppler shift:**

$$
f_d = f_r - f_c = f_c \cdot \frac{v_{rel}}{c}
$$

Substitute the given values:

$$
f_d = (5.9 \times 10^9) \times \frac{70}{3 \times 10^8}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the numerical evaluation:**

$$
f_d = 5.9 \times 10^9 \times 2.3333 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
f_d = 5.9 \times 2.3333 \times 10^{2} = 13.7667 \times 10^2
$$

$$
\boxed{f_d \approx 1376.7 \text{ Hz}}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation:**

This Doppler shift is small in absolute terms (~1.4 kHz on a 5.9 GHz carrier, a fractional shift of $2.33 \times 10^{-7}$) but it determines the channel coherence time, which in turn dictates the maximum allowable packet airtime.

## 3.2 Derivation: Channel Coherence Time (Jakes' Model)

The coherence time $T_c$ is the duration over which the channel impulse response can be considered statistically invariant. Jakes' classical formula for a Rayleigh-fading mobile channel is:

$$
T_c \approx \frac{0.423}{f_d}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply the formula using the Doppler shift from §3.1:**

$$
T_c = \frac{0.423}{1376.7} \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the result:**

$$
T_c = 3.073 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.3073 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_c \approx 307 \text{ μs}}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering implication:**

For the channel to behave as time-invariant, a packet's airtime $\tau$ must satisfy $\tau \ll T_c$. For 10 MHz bandwidth, a 100-byte packet has airtime:

$$
\tau = \frac{100 \times 8}{10 \times 10^6} = 80 \text{ μs}
$$

Since $80\,\mu s < 307\,\mu s$, the channel is approximately time-invariant over a single packet transmission. The ratio $\tau / T_c \approx 0.26$ indicates moderate—but acceptable—fading variation.

## 3.3 Derivation: Free-Space Path Loss for a V2I Link

**Given:**
- Distance: $d = 300$ m (typical V2I safety range)
- Frequency: $f_c = 5.9$ GHz $= 5900$ MHz

The free-space path loss formula (logarithmic form) is:

$$
PL_{FS}(d) = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})
$$

**Step 1 — Convert distance to km:**

$$
d_{km} = 0.3 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the logarithmic terms:**

$$
20\log_{10}(0.3) = 20 \times (-0.5229) = -10.458 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(5900) = 20 \times 3.7709 = 75.418 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the terms:**

$$
PL_{FS} = 32.45 - 10.458 + 75.418
$$

$$
PL_{FS} = 97.41 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
\boxed{PL_{FS}(300 \text{ m, 5.9 GHz}) \approx 97.4 \text{ dB}}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify using the linear Friis equation:**

$$
PL_{FS} = \left(\frac{4\pi d f_c}{c}\right)^2
$$

$$
PL_{FS} = \left(\frac{4 \pi \times 300 \times 5.9 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8}\right)^2
$$

$$
PL_{FS} = \left(4\pi \times 5900\right)^2 = \left(74141\right)^2 \approx 5.498 \times 10^9
$$

Converting to dB:

$$
PL_{FS,dB} = 10 \log_{10}(5.498 \times 10^9) = 97.40 \text{ dB} \;\checkmark
$$

The two forms agree, confirming the result.

## 3.4 Derivation: Maximum Communication Range (Link Budget Closure)

**Given:**
- Transmit power: $P_t = 20$ dBm $= 100$ mW
- Tx/Rx antenna gains: $G_t = G_r = 3$ dBi (typical vehicular patch antenna)
- Receiver sensitivity: $P_{min} = -90$ dBm
- Frequency: $f_c = 5.9$ GHz, $\lambda = 0.0508$ m

The Friis transmission equation solved for range is:

$$
R_{max} = \sqrt[ ]{\frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{P_{min} (4\pi)^2}}
$$

**Step 1 — Convert all quantities to linear scale:**

- $P_t = 10^{20/10} \text{ mW} = 100$ mW
- $G_t = G_r = 10^{3/10} = 1.995 \approx 2$
- $P_{min} = 10^{-90/10} \text{ mW} = 10^{-9}$ mW

**Step 2 — Substitute into the range equation:**

$$
R_{max} = \sqrt[ ]{\frac{100 \times 2 \times 2 \times (0.0508)^2}{10^{-9} \times (4\pi)^2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 400 \times 0.002581 = 1.0324
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$
(4\pi)^2 = 157.91
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 157.91 \times 10^{-9} = 1.5791 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the ratio and the square root:**

$$
\frac{1.0324}{1.5791 \times 10^{-7}} = 6.538 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
R_{max} = \sqrt[ ]{6.538 \times 10^6} = 2557 \text{ m} \approx 2.56 \text{ km}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{max} \approx 2.56 \text{ km (free-space, line-of-sight)}}
$$

**Step 6 — Real-world correction factor:**

In practice, urban non-line-of-sight conditions add 15–25 dB of additional path loss, reducing the effective range to **300–800 m**, consistent with the 300 m safety-range design target.

## 3.5 Derivation: Shannon Channel Capacity for the V2V Link

**Given:**
- Bandwidth: $B = 10$ MHz
- Received SNR: $S/N = 10$ dB $= 10$ (linear)

**Step 1 — Apply Shannon's theorem:**

$$
C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
C = 10 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(1 + 10) = 10^7 \times \log_2(11)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the logarithm:**

$$
\log_2(11) = \frac{\ln(11)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{2.3979}{0.6931} = 3.459
$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$
C = 10^7 \times 3.459 = 3.459 \times 10^7 \text{ bits/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{C \approx 34.6 \text{ Mbit/s}}
$$

This is the absolute upper bound; practical C-V2X achieves 30–50% of this value due to coding overhead and pilot symbols.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Common Numerical Templates

The following matrix summarizes the typical problem types and the formula/path to apply:

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Formula |
|:-------------|:------|:-----|:------------|
| Doppler shift | $v, f_c$ | $f_d$ | Formula 1 |
| Coherence time | $f_d$ | $T_c$ | Formula 2 |
| Free-space path loss | $d, f_c$ | $PL$ (dB) | Formula 3 |
| Channel capacity | $B, S/N$ | $C$ | Formula 5 |
| Max range | $P_t, G, \lambda, P_{min}$ | $R_{max}$ | Formula 6 |
| Beacon rate | $v, d_{res}$ | $f_{beacon}$ | Formula 10 |
| Neighbor count | $\rho, R$ | $N$ | Formula 11 |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram: ITS Four-Layer Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the canonical four-layer architecture of a vehicular communication node, including all internal data flows between sensing, access, networking, and application layers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appL["Application Layer: Safety Efficiency Infotainment"]
    netL["Networking and Transport Layer: GeoNetworking UDP TCP"]
    accL["Access Layer: IEEE 802.11p or C-V2X 3GPP Sidelink"]
    senL["Sensing and Perception Layer: GNSS Camera Radar LiDAR"]
    rsu1["RSU: Road-Side Unit"]
    rsu2["Cellular Base Station gNB"]
    cloud["Cloud Server and HD Map Provider"]
    ped1["Pedestrian Smartphone V2P"]
    vehA["Neighbour Vehicle V2V"]
    ftx1["Beacon CAM 10 Hz"]
    ftx2["Service Message DENM"]
    ftx3["Sidelink PC5 5.9 GHz"]
    ftx4["Cellular Uu 4G or 5G"]
    ftx5["Sensor Fusion Input"]

    senL --> ftx5
    ftx5 --> accL
    accL --> ftx3
    ftx3 <--> rsu1
    ftx3 <--> ped1
    ftx3 <--> vehA
    accL --> ftx4
    ftx4 <--> rsu2
    ftx4 <--> cloud
    accL --> netL
    netL --> ftx1
    ftx1 --> appL
    ftx2 --> appL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Sensor data from GNSS, cameras, radar, and LiDAR feed the Access Layer, which implements the physical layer using either IEEE 802.11p (DSRC) or 3GPP sidelink (C-V2X). The PC5 interface at 5.9 GHz handles direct peer-to-peer communication with RSU, pedestrians, and neighbour vehicles, while the Uu interface to the cellular base station extends range to cloud services. The Networking Layer formats and routes CAM and DENM messages, which the Application Layer consumes for safety and infotainment decisions.

## 4.2 Diagram: Three-Era Evolution of Vehicular Communication

This timeline Mermaid diagram maps the historical evolution of intelligent vehicular communication, identifying the key enabling technology and the dominant communication paradigm of each era.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    era1["Era 1 Pre 1990s: Standalone Vehicles, AM FM Radio"]
    era2["Era 2 1990s 2000s: Telematics, GPS, eCall One Way"]

    e1goal["Metric: Local Awareness"]
    e2goal["Metric: Fleet Monitoring"]
    e3goal["Metric: Collective Perception"]
    driver1["Driver: No external comms"]
    driver2["Driver: FCC 5.9 GHz Allocation 1999"]
    driver3["Driver: 3GPP Rel 14, 16, 5G NR V2X"]
    ieee11p["IEEE 802.11p Standard 2010"]
    cv2xrel14["3GPP Release 14 C-V2X 2017"]
    cv2xrel16["3GPP Release 16 5G NR V2X 2020"]

    era1 --- e1tech
    era1 --- e1goal
    era1 --- driver1
    era2 --- e2tech
    era2 --- e2goal
    era2 --- driver2
    era3 --- e3tech
    era3 --- e3goal
    era3 --- driver3
    era3 --> ieee11p
    era3 --> cv2xrel14
    era3 --> cv2xrel16
    era1 ==> era2
    era2 ==> era3
```

## 4.3 Diagram: V2X Communication Mode Taxonomy

This Mermaid diagram shows the five-element taxonomy of Vehicle-to-Everything communication, with each mode's typical range and primary application.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    v2x["V2X Vehicle to Everything: Umbrella Term"]

    v2x --> v2v
    v2x --> v2i
```

## 4.4 Diagram: DSRC vs C-V2X Protocol Stack Comparison

This block-level functional diagram contrasts the layered protocol stacks of DSRC (IEEE 802.11p / IEEE 1609) and C-V2X (3GPP). It maps each OSI-equivalent layer to the corresponding standards body, making the technology comparison explicit.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stack1["DSRC Stack: North American Origin"]
    stack2["C-V2X Stack: 3GPP Global Origin"]
    dsrcl7["Application: SAE J2735 J2945"]
    dsrcl6["Presentation: ASN.1 UPER"]
    dsrcl5["Session: IEEE 1609 WSA"]
    dsrcl4["Transport: UDP TCP"]
    dsrcl3["Network: WSMP GeoNetworking IPv6"]
    dsrcl2["MAC: IEEE 802.11p CSMA CA"]
    dsrcl1["PHY: OFDM 5.9 GHz 10 MHz"]

    cvl7["Application: ETSI ITS G5 SAE J2735"]
    cvl6["Presentation: ASN.1 UPER"]
    cvl5["Session: Facilities Layer"]
    cvl4["Transport: UDP TCP"]
    cvl3["Network: IP Non IP Sidelink"]
    cvl2["MAC: 3GPP Sidelink PC5 Mode 4"]
    cvl1["PHY: LTE or 5G NR Sidelink 5.9 GHz"]

    stack1 --> dsrcl7
    dsrcl7 --> dsrcl6
    dsrcl6 --> dsrcl5
    dsrcl5 --> dsrcl4
    dsrcl4 --> dsrcl3
    dsrcl3 --> dsrcl2
    dsrcl2 --> dsrcl1

    stack2 --> cvl7
    cvl7 --> cvl6
    cvl6 --> cvl5
    cvl5 --> cvl4
    cvl4 --> cvl3
    cvl3 --> cvl2
    cvl2 --> cvl1
```

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Aid:** Both stacks share the upper layers (Application/Presentation/Transport); the key divergence is at **MAC and PHY**, where DSRC uses contention-based CSMA/CA (IEEE 802.11p) and C-V2X uses scheduled sidelink resources (3GPP Mode 4). This explains why C-V2X is generally considered more robust in high-density traffic.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Part A: 2 × 3 = 6 marks. Part B: 1 × 14 = 14 marks (Module Internal Choice). Total per question paper module: 20 marks. The questions below are calibrated to this pattern.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Intelligent Transport System (ITS) and list its three evolutionary eras. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

Intelligent Transport System (ITS) is the integrated application of information and communication technologies, sensor systems, and control strategies into the transportation infrastructure to improve safety, mobility, efficiency, and environmental sustainability. The three evolutionary eras are:

1. **Standalone Era (pre-1990s):** Vehicles functioned as isolated mechanical systems with no external communication beyond analog radio broadcast. The dominant metric was *local awareness*.
2. **Telematics Era (1990s – early 2000s):** GPS-based navigation, satellite fleet tracking, and one-way eCall systems enabled centralized monitoring. The dominant metric was *fleet monitoring*.
3. **Cooperative / Connected Era (mid-2000s – present):** Bidirectional, low-latency V2V/V2I/V2P/V2N communication became possible through DSRC and C-V2X. The dominant metric is *collective perception*.

### Question 2: Differentiate between DSRC and C-V2X in terms of MAC and PHY layers. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DSRC (IEEE 802.11p) | C-V2X (3GPP) |
|:----------|:-------------------|:-------------|
| Origin | North American (IEEE) | Global (3GPP) |
| MAC | CSMA/CA contention-based | Sidelink scheduled resources (Mode 4) |
| PHY | OFDM, 10 MHz channel | LTE/5G NR sidelink, 10/20 MHz |
| Frequency | 5.9 GHz | 5.9 GHz (PC5) and licensed cellular (Uu) |
| Latency | ~4 ms typical | ~3–5 ms typical |
| Congestion behavior | Degrades sharply under high load | More robust via scheduling |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each. KTU provides an internal choice: **you must answer either Question A or Question B**, not both.

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, describe the three evolutionary eras of Intelligent Transport System. Identify the key enabling technology and the dominant communication metric of each era. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Solution:**

**Era 1 — Standalone Era (Pre-1990s):**
- *Technology:* Mechanical systems with analog AM/FM radio broadcast.
- *Communication:* No external data exchange; one-way driver-to-driver auditory signals (horn, indicators).
- *Metric:* *Local awareness* — each vehicle senses only its own state and the immediate line-of-sight environment.
- *Limitation:* Cannot detect accidents or hazards beyond line-of-sight.

**Era 2 — Telematics Era (1990s – Early 2000s):**
- *Technology:* GPS (1995 fully operational), OnStar-type eCall systems, GSM/satellite uplink.
- *Communication:* One-way uplink only; vehicles broadcast their position to a central server.
- *Metric:* *Fleet monitoring* — a centralized dispatcher can track vehicle kinematics.
- *Regulatory milestone:* FCC's 1999 allocation of 75 MHz in the 5.9 GHz band for ITS.
- *Limitation:* Asymmetric bandwidth, no peer-to-peer messaging between vehicles.

**Era 3 — Cooperative / Connected Era (Mid-2000s – Present):**
- *Technology:* DSRC (IEEE 802.11p, 2010) and C-V2X (3GPP Rel 14 in 2017, Rel 16 in 2020).
- *Communication:* Bidirectional V2V/V2I/V2P/V2N with sub-100 ms latency.
- *Metric:* *Collective perception* — vehicles build a shared 360° awareness map by fusing on-board sensor data with cooperative messages.
- *Application:* Forward collision warning, platooning, cooperative adaptive cruise control (C-ACC), vulnerable road user (VRU) protection.

The block diagram (Mermaid in §4.2) maps the transitions and key drivers.

#### (b) [7 Marks] A vehicle travels at 30 m/s on a highway. The carrier frequency of a V2V link is 5.9 GHz. Calculate (i) the maximum Doppler shift, (ii) the channel coherence time using Jakes' formula, and (iii) the maximum allowable packet airtime for the channel to be treated as time-invariant. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Velocity: $v = 30$ m/s
- Carrier frequency: $f_c = 5.9 \text{ GHz} = 5.9 \times 10^9$ Hz
- Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- Jakes' constant: 0.423

**(i) Maximum Doppler shift:**

$$
f_d = \frac{v \cdot f_c}{c} = \frac{30 \times 5.9 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8}
$$

$$
f_d = \frac{1.77 \times 10^{11}}{3 \times 10^8} = 590 \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_d = 590 \text{ Hz}}
$$

**(ii) Coherence time (Jakes' formula):**

$$
T_c = \frac{0.423}{f_d} = \frac{0.423}{590} = 7.169 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_c \approx 717 \text{ μs}}
$$

**(iii) Maximum packet airtime:**

For the channel to be time-invariant, the packet airtime $\tau$ must satisfy $\tau \leq T_c / 10$ (engineering rule of thumb):

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{T_c}{10} = \frac{717 \text{ μs}}{10} = 71.7 \text{ μs}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{max} \approx 71.7 \text{ μs}}
$$

**Verification:** For a 10 MHz channel, this corresponds to a maximum payload of:

$$
N_{max} = B \cdot \tau_{max} = 10 \times 10^6 \times 71.7 \times 10^{-6} = 717 \text{ bits} \approx 89 \text{ bytes}
$$

This is consistent with typical CAM packet sizes of 100–200 bytes when additional coding overhead is included.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the V2X communication taxonomy. With neat diagrams, describe V2V, V2I, V2P, and V2N modes, including their typical range and one application each. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Model Solution:**

V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) is the umbrella term for all vehicular communication modes. The taxonomy comprises:

**1. V2V — Vehicle-to-Vehicle:**
- *Definition:* Direct, peer-to-peer data exchange between nearby vehicles.
- *Communication range:* 50–300 m.
- *Latency budget:* < 100 ms.
- *Application:* Forward collision warning (FCW) — when the leader vehicle suddenly brakes, the follower receives a CAM within 50 ms and pre-charges its brakes.
- *PHY/MAC:* IEEE 802.11p or 3GPP sidelink (PC5 mode 4) at 5.9 GHz.

**2. V2I — Vehicle-to-Infrastructure:**
- *Definition:* Communication between vehicles and fixed roadside units (RSUs) such as traffic signals, lamp posts, or toll gates.
- *Communication range:* 100–1000 m.
- *Latency budget:* < 100 ms.
- *Application:* Signal Phase and Timing (SPaT) — vehicles approaching an intersection receive the current green-time remaining, enabling green-wave driving.
- *PHY/MAC:* IEEE 802.11p or C-V2X sidelink/uplink.

**3. V2P — Vehicle-to-Pedestrian:**
- *Definition:* Communication between vehicles and vulnerable road users (VRUs) such as pedestrians, cyclists, or wheelchair users carrying smartphones.
- *Communication range:* 50–100 m.
- *Latency budget:* < 100 ms.
- *Application:* Pedestrian crossing alert — a vehicle's blind-spot detection system warns both the driver and the pedestrian's phone of imminent collision.
- *PHY/MAC:* C-V2X sidelink (PC5) at 5.9 GHz; smartphones broadcast their GPS location periodically.

**4. V2N — Vehicle-to-Network:**
- *Definition:* Communication between vehicles and cellular base stations (gNB/eNB) that act as gateways to cloud servers.
- *Communication range:* km-scale (bounded by cellular coverage).
- *Latency budget:* 100 ms – 1 s (non-safety critical).
- *Application:* HD map download for autonomous driving; software over-the-air (OTA) updates; real-time traffic routing.
- *PHY/MAC:* LTE/5G NR Uu interface on licensed cellular bands.

**5. V2X — Vehicle-to-Everything (umbrella):**
- Combines all the above into a unified, cooperative perception framework.

The taxonomy is illustrated in the Mermaid diagram of §4.3.

#### (b) [7 Marks] A V2I link operates at 5.9 GHz. The transmit power is 20 dBm, antenna gains are 3 dBi each, and the receiver sensitivity is –90 dBm. Calculate (i) the maximum line-of-sight communication range assuming free-space propagation, and (ii) the free-space path loss at 300 m. Compare the result with a typical urban non-line-of-sight path loss and explain the discrepancy. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $P_t = 20$ dBm $= 100$ mW
- $G_t = G_r = 3$ dBi
- $P_{min} = -90$ dBm $= 10^{-9}$ mW
- $f_c = 5.9$ GHz
- $\lambda = c / f_c = (3 \times 10^8) / (5.9 \times 10^9) = 0.0508$ m

**(i) Maximum free-space range:**

From the Friis equation:

$$
R_{max} = \sqrt[ ]{\frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{P_{min} (4\pi)^2}}
$$

Substitute linear values:

$$
P_t = 100 \text{ mW}, \quad G_t = G_r = 10^{0.3} = 1.995, \quad P_{min} = 10^{-9} \text{ mW}
$$

$$
R_{max} = \sqrt[ ]{\frac{100 \times 1.995 \times 1.995 \times (0.0508)^2}{10^{-9} \times (4\pi)^2}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
100 \times 1.995 \times 1.995 \times 0.002581 = 1.0274
$$

Denominator:

$$
(4\pi)^2 = 157.91, \quad 157.91 \times 10^{-9} = 1.5791 \times 10^{-7}
$$

Ratio:

$$
\frac{1.0274}{1.5791 \times 10^{-7}} = 6.506 \times 10^6
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{max} = \sqrt[ ]{6.506 \times 10^6} \approx 2551 \text{ m} \approx 2.55 \text{ km}}
$$

**(ii) Free-space path loss at 300 m:**

Using the simplified formula:

$$
PL_{FS}(d) = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})
$$

$$
PL_{FS} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(0.3) + 20\log_{10}(5900)
$$

$$
PL_{FS} = 32.45 - 10.46 + 75.42 = 97.41 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
\boxed{PL_{FS}(300 \text{ m}) \approx 97.4 \text{ dB}}
$$

**(iii) Comparison with urban NLOS:**

In a typical urban environment, additional losses include:
- *Shadow fading margin:* 8–10 dB
- *Building penetration / diffraction loss:* 10–15 dB
- *Vegetation / clutter loss:* 3–5 dB

Total urban NLOS path loss at 300 m:

$$
PL_{urban} = 97.4 + 8 + 12 + 4 \approx 121 \text{ dB}
$$

**Discrepancy:** The urban NLOS loss is approximately **24 dB higher** than free-space. This explains why the practical V2I safety range is limited to **300–500 m** in cities, even though the free-space range is **2.55 km**. Designers must add a 20–30 dB link margin to convert free-space specifications into real-world performance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing V2X with V2V (–1 mark):** V2X is the umbrella, V2V is a sub-mode.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert dBm to mW in the Friis range formula (–2 marks):** The Friis equation in its standard form uses linear power in watts or milliwatts, not dBm.
> 3. **Using $20 \log_{10}$ for path-loss exponent instead of $10 n \log_{10}$ (–1 mark):** The free-space formula uses $20 \log_{10}(d)$, but the log-distance model uses $10 n \log_{10}(d)$.
> 4. **Skipping units in Doppler shift calculations (–1 mark):** Always state $f_d$ in Hz.
> 5. **Omitting the 0.423 constant in Jakes' coherence-time formula (–1 mark):** Writing $T_c = 1/f_d$ loses a mark; the correct form is $T_c = 0.423/f_d$.
> 6. **Stating the wrong carrier frequency (–1 mark):** V2X operates at **5.9 GHz**, not 2.4 GHz (WiFi) or 5.0 GHz (UNII). This is a *favorite* KTU examiner trap.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-Second Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This and Tape to Your Wall**

### Core Definitions
- **ITS** = ICT + sensing + control integrated into transportation infrastructure.
- **V2X** = Vehicle-to-Everything (umbrella term).
- **V2V, V2I, V2P, V2N, V2G** = the five-element taxonomy.
- **DSRC** = IEEE 802.11p / IEEE 1609 family, US-origin.
- **C-V2X** = 3GPP Releases 14/15/16, global cellular-origin.
- **CAM** = Cooperative Awareness Message (10 Hz beacon).
- **DENM** = Decentralized Environmental Notification Message (event-driven).
- **RSU** = Road-Side Unit.
- **LDM** = Local Dynamic Map.

### Three Evolutionary Eras
1. **Standalone (pre-1990s):** No comms, local awareness.
2. **Telematics (1990s–2000s):** One-way GPS/GSM uplink, fleet monitoring.
3. **Cooperative (2000s–today):** Bidirectional V2X, collective perception.

### Critical Numbers (Memorize Verbatim)
- Carrier frequency: **5.9 GHz**
- Channel bandwidth: **10 MHz**
- Beacon rate: **10 Hz**
- Max safety range: **~300 m**
- Max Doppler shift (highway): **~1377 Hz** at $v_{rel} = 70$ m/s
- Coherence time (at 1377 Hz): **~307 μs**
- Max free-space range (20 dBm, 3 dBi, –90 dBm): **~2.55 km**
- Free-space path loss at 300 m, 5.9 GHz: **~97.4 dB**
- Shannon capacity (10 MHz, 10 dB SNR): **~34.6 Mbit/s**

### Key Formulas (Top 5 for KTU)
1. $f_d = v_{rel} f_c / c$
2. $T_c = 0.423 / f_d$
3. $PL_{FS} = 32.45 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{MHz})$
4. $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$
5. $R_{max} = \sqrt[\ ]{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 / (P_{min} (4\pi)^2)}$

### Standards to Know
- **IEEE 802.11p** — DSRC PHY/MAC (approved 2010).
- **IEEE 1609** — DSRC upper layers (WSA, security).
- **SAE J2735 / J2945** — DSRC message dictionary.
- **3GPP Release 14 (2017)** — C-V2X sidelink (PC5 mode 4).
- **3GPP Release 16 (2020)** — 5G NR-V2X sidelink.
- **ETSI ITS-G5** — European profile of 802.11p.

### Common KTU Question Triggers
- "Discuss the evolution of vehicular communication" → Three eras.
- "Compare DSRC and C-V2X" → MAC/PHY table.
- "Calculate Doppler shift / coherence time" → Formulas 1, 2.
- "Calculate maximum range / path loss" → Formulas 3, 6.
- "Explain V2X taxonomy" → V2V, V2I, V2P, V2N.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Good Luck on the KTU ESE!** Remember: the examiner awards marks for *clear, structured, equation-supported* answers. Always state assumptions, show units, and label every variable. A well-labeled diagram earns 1–2 free marks even when the textual explanation is incomplete.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicular Networks and ITS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vehicular Networks and ITS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🚗 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Vehicular Ad-hoc Networks (VANETs)

A **Vehicular Ad-hoc Network (VANET)** is a self-organising, distributed communication network formed between moving vehicles and roadside infrastructure, where every node (vehicle or RSU) acts both as a transmitter and a router. It is a specialised sub-class of **Mobile Ad-hoc Networks (MANETs)** characterised by high mobility, predictable trajectories (constrained to roads), and energy-abundant nodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (PERAT753):** VANET is a spontaneous network of vehicles equipped with short-range wireless communication capability (DSRC / C-V2X) that enables Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V), Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I), and Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication, supporting road safety, traffic efficiency, and infotainment applications.

## 1.2 Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS)

**Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS)** integrate information and communication technologies (ICT) into transportation infrastructure to deliver innovative services relating to traffic management, road safety, and infotainment. ITS is the *application umbrella* under which VANETs operate as a core enabling technology.

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of ITS as the "Brain" and VANET as the "Nervous System":** ITS decides *what* information drivers and traffic authorities need; VANETs physically *transport* that information between cars, signals, and cloud servers.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🚌🚌

Imagine a busy Indian city junction during a thunderstorm. One driver ahead suddenly brakes hard on a slippery road. Without technology, the car behind learns this only when it *sees* the brake lights — often too late.

- **V2V Analogy:** The first car *whispers* a message ("I'm braking hard, road slippery, my GPS is at lat X, lon Y") to every car within **300 m** — 10 times per second.
- **V2I Analogy:** The same car *posts* this message to the traffic signal pole, which forwards it to a city traffic server that updates Google Maps and the next signal's green-wave timing.
- **V2P / VRU Analogy:** A pedestrian's phone receives a vibration: *"Car approaching from behind at 60 km/h, 40 m away — watch out!"*

This is the *promise* of Vehicular Networks and ITS.

## 1.4 Key Technical Metrics (Board-Favourite Constants)

| Parameter | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Frequency Band** | **5.85–5.925 GHz** (5.9 GHz band) | Allocated globally for ITS / DSRC |
| **Channel Bandwidth** | **10 MHz** (DSRC) / **20 MHz** (C-V2X) | Sub-channels of 10 MHz |
| **Data Rate** | **3–27 Mbps** (DSRC 802.11p) | Up to **27 Mbps** for safety + infotainment |
| **Communication Range** | **100–1000 m** | Short to medium range |
| **Beacon / BSM Rate** | **10 Hz** (typical) | Periodic safety message broadcast |
| **Maximum Relative Velocity** | **~500 km/h** (highway scenarios) | Worst-case Doppler in spec |
| **Latency Target (Safety)** | **< 100 ms** (E2E) | Hard real-time bound |
| **Reliability Target** | **> 99.999 %** | For pre-crash sensing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners **love** asking for the operating frequency (**5.9 GHz**) and data rate (**up to 27 Mbps**) — memorise these.

## 1.5 V2X — The Five Communication Modes

| Acronym | Meaning | Counterpart |
|---|---|---|
| **V2V** | Vehicle-to-Vehicle | Car ↔ Car |
| **V2I** | Vehicle-to-Infrastructure | Car ↔ RSU (Roadside Unit) |
| **V2N** | Vehicle-to-Network | Car ↔ Cellular base station / Cloud |
| **V2P** | Vehicle-to-Pedestrian | Car ↔ Phone / VRU (Vulnerable Road User) |
| **V2X** | Vehicle-to-Everything (umbrella term) | All of the above |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic: "V2-NIP-X"** — N(etwork), I(nfrastructure), P(erson), X(everything umbrella).

## 1.6 Two Major Technology Stacks

1. **DSRC / IEEE 802.11p / WAVE** — Wi-Fi-derived, US/Japan standard.
2. **C-V2X (Cellular V2X)** — 4G LTE (3GPP Rel-14) and 5G NR (Rel-16) based, adopted by most global OEMs and cellular operators.
3. **ETSI ITS-G5** — European variant of 802.11p, profile for cooperative awareness.

Both operate in the **5.9 GHz** band and are designed to coexist with regulatory spectrum sharing rules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Distinctive Characteristics of VANETs

VANETs differ from generic MANETs in several fundamental ways that drive their protocol design:

- **High Node Mobility:** Speeds 0–200 km/h produce rapidly changing topologies (link lifetimes often < 1 s).
- **Predictable Mobility Patterns:** Vehicles move along road topology → constrained graph mobility models.
- **Bounded Network Density:** From sparse rural (a few vehicles/km) to ultra-dense urban gridlock (hundreds of vehicles/km).
- **Abundant Power & Computation:** Cars can host powerful OBUs (On-Board Units); battery is not a constraint.
- **Hard Real-Time Constraints:** Safety messages demand bounded latency (≤ 100 ms) and high reliability (≥ 99 %).
- **Heterogeneous Communication:** Mix of V2V, V2I, V2N, and short/long range radios simultaneously active.
- **Geographically Relevant Information:** Messages are valid only within a specific region (e.g., accident location).

## 2.2 The IEEE 802.11p / WAVE Protocol Stack

The US standard for DSRC is built on the **WAVE (Wireless Access in Vehicular Environments)** stack, standardised under IEEE 1609 family and IEEE 802.11p.

| Layer | Standard | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Application** | SAE J3061 / J2945 | Safety apps, signal phase timing |
| **Message Sub-layer** | SAE J2735 | BSM, MAP, SPaT message dictionaries |
| **Networking** | IEEE 1609.3 (WAVE Short Message Protocol — WSMP) | IPv6 + UDP/TCP + WSMP dual-stack |
| **Multi-channel** | IEEE 1609.4 | Alternating Control (CCH) / Service (SCH) channels |
| **MAC** | IEEE 802.11p (amended from 802.11a) | CSMA/CA with Enhanced Distributed Channel Access (EDCA) |
| **PHY** | OFDM, 10 MHz channels, 5.9 GHz | BPSK → 64-QAM, 3–27 Mbps |

### 2.2.1 WAVE Channel Structure

The 5.9 GHz band is split into **7 channels of 10 MHz** each:

- **Channel 178** — Control Channel (CCH) — mandatory safety messages.
- **Channel 172** — Service Channel (SCH) — V2V safety / high-power.
- **Channel 184** — Service Channel (SCH) — public safety.
- **Channels 174, 176, 180, 182** — Service Channels (SCH) — general use.
- **Channel 5 MHz Guard band** at edges.

IEEE 1609.4 mandates that radios alternate every **50 ms** between CCH and SCH, giving **100 ms** cycle latency — a known weakness addressed in C-V2X.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EDCA (Enhanced Distributed Channel Access):** Adds 4 Access Categories (AC_VO, AC_VI, AC_BE, AC_BK) with different AIFS and CW values, prioritising safety messages.

## 2.3 C-V2X (Cellular V2X)

C-V2X was introduced in 3GPP **Release 14** (2017) and evolved through Rel-15/16/17. It defines **two complementary operating modes**:

### 2.3.1 Mode 3 (Network-scheduled, "Uu" interface)
- Communication goes through the cellular base station (eNodeB / gNodeB).
- Suited for **V2N** (Telematics, HD map download, software updates).
- Latency: **20–100 ms** (4G) / **1–10 ms** (5G URLLC).

### 2.3.2 Mode 4 (Autonomous / Sidelink, "PC5" interface)
- Direct **device-to-device** communication without cellular coverage.
- Uses **sensing-based semi-persistent scheduling (SPS)** — vehicles sense channel and reserve slots autonomously.
- Designed for **V2V, V2I, V2P** safety messages.
- Latency: **< 20 ms** at 10 Hz message rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **5G NR-V2X (Rel-16)** adds advanced features: **groupcast**, **unicast**, **sidelink CSI feedback**, **HARQ on sidelink**, and supports **higher-order modulation (256-QAM)** for V2N infotainment.

## 2.4 ETSI ITS-G5 Stack (European Profile)

ETSI ITS-G5 is a profile of IEEE 802.11p with European-specific amendments:

- **Access Layer:** ETSI EN 302 636 (802.11p at 5.9 GHz).
- **Networking & Transport:** ETSI EN 302 636-4 (GeoNetworking), GeoBroadcast.
- **Facilities Layer:** ETSI TS 102 894 (CAM, DENM message handling).
- **Security:** ETSI TS 102 940 (PKI, certificate management).
- **Applications:** ETSI TS 101 539 (Road Hazard Signalling, Cooperative ACC).

## 2.5 Standard Cooperative Awareness Messages

| Message | Origin | Size | Frequency | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BSM** (Basic Safety Message) | SAE J2735 | ~250 bytes | 10 Hz | Vehicle state: position, speed, heading, accel, brake status |
| **CAM** (Cooperative Awareness Message) | ETSI EN 302 637-2 | ~100–800 bytes | 1–10 Hz | Periodic status broadcast |
| **DENM** (Decentralised Env. Notification Message) | ETSI EN 302 637-3 | Variable | Event-driven | Hazard warning (accident, ice, road works) |
| **SPaT** (Signal Phase and Timing) | SAE J2735 | Variable | 10 Hz | Traffic signal state, time-to-green |
| **MAP** (Map Data) | SAE J2735 | Variable | 1 Hz | Intersection geometry, lane topology |

## 2.6 ITS Architecture (Three-Tier)

A complete ITS deployment is structured as:

1. **Field Layer (Perception):** OBU, RSU, cameras, loop detectors, roadside sensors.
2. **Communication Layer:** DSRC / C-V2X / 5G / 4G LTE / 802.11ac.
3. **Central / Service Layer:** Traffic Management Centre (TMC), cloud, edge compute, HD map servers.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | Free-space path loss (Friis) | d in km, f in MHz, dB |
| 2 | $f_d = \dfrac{v \cdot f_c}{c} \cos\theta$ | Doppler shift | Hz |
| 3 | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Shannon channel capacity | bits/s |
| 4 | $T_{\text{E2E}} = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{tx}} + T_{\text{proc}}$ | End-to-end latency | s |
| 5 | $T_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{d}{c}$ | Propagation delay (speed of light) | s |
| 6 | $L_{\text{total}} = L_{FSPL} + L_{\text{shadow}} + L_{\text{fade}}$ | Total vehicular path loss | dB |
| 7 | $q = k \cdot \bar{v}_s$ | Traffic flow = density × space-mean speed | vehicles/h |
| 8 | $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^2 / ((4\pi)^2 d^2)$ | Received power (Friis) | W |
| 9 | $T_{slot}^{V2V} = \dfrac{1}{N_{slots}\cdot f_{BSM}}$ | Slot duration with N parallel slots | s |
| 10 | $\text{SNR} = P_r / (N_0 B)$ | Receiver SNR | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Pitfall:** Use the **Friis constant 32.44** *only* when d is in **km** and f is in **MHz**. With d in metres and f in Hz the constant changes. KTU questions frequently set d=100 m, f=5.9 GHz — beware unit conversion.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive OEMs (Tesla, GM, Toyota, Hyundai, Tata Motors):** Embed C-V2X modules for ADAS and eventual autonomous platooning.
- **Smart Cities (Kerala, Bengaluru, Pune pilots):** Adaptive traffic signal control using SPaT/MAP.
- **Insurance & Tolling (FASTag, NH-44 corridors):** V2I for cashless tolling and pay-as-you-drive insurance.
- **Logistics (Tata, Ashok Leyland, Volvo trucks):** Platoon-based fuel savings (5–15 %).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section builds the three most-asked KTU numericals from this topic: **Free Space Path Loss**, **Doppler shift**, and **E2E latency** for V2V.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Free Space Path Loss (Friis Transmission Equation)

### Problem Setup
A vehicle OBU transmits at $P_t = 20$ dBm on $f_c = 5.9$ GHz with isotropic antennas ($G_t = G_r = 1$). The receiving vehicle is $d = 500$ m away. Find:
(a) the free-space path loss in dB,  
(b) the received power in dBm.

### Step 1 — Convert to linear / consistent SI units
- Frequency: $f_c = 5.9 \text{ GHz} = 5.9 \times 10^9$ Hz
- Distance: $d = 500 \text{ m} = 0.5$ km
- Transmit power (linear): $P_t = 20 \text{ dBm} = 10^{20/10} = 100$ mW $= 0.1$ W

### Step 2 — Apply Friis Free-Space Path Loss equation

$$
\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 32.44
$$

### Step 3 — Plug numerical values

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL} &= 20\log_{10}(0.5) + 20\log_{10}(5900) + 32.44 \\
&= 20 \times (-0.3010) + 20 \times (3.771) + 32.44 \\
&= -6.020 + 75.42 + 32.44 \\
&= 101.84 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute received power
The Friis received-power equation in dB form:

$$
P_r \;(\text{dBm}) = P_t \;(\text{dBm}) + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= 20 + 0 + 0 - 101.84 \\
&= -81.84 \text{ dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert back to linear (optional)

$$
P_r = 10^{-81.84/10} = 10^{-8.184} = 6.54 \times 10^{-9} \text{ W} = 6.54 \text{ nW}
$$

### Verification by Sanity Check
- 5.9 GHz over 500 m should give ~100 dB loss ✅
- Receiver noise floor in a 10 MHz channel is ~ -104 dBm, leaving a ~22 dB SNR margin — acceptable for short-range V2V.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Trick:** The 32.44 constant disappears if you keep everything in SI (metres & Hz). Use $ \text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) - 147.55$ (with d in m, f in Hz). Either is acceptable if stated upfront.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Doppler Shift in V2V Highway Scenario

### Problem Setup
A car moving at $v = 108$ km/h (30 m/s) approaches a stationary Roadside Unit (RSU) head-on along the line of sight. The carrier frequency is $f_c = 5.9$ GHz. Calculate the maximum Doppler shift experienced by the received signal.

### Step 1 — Convert speed to m/s

$$
v = 108 \;\text{km/h} = \frac{108 \times 1000}{3600} = 30 \;\text{m/s}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Doppler equation

$$
f_d = \frac{v \cdot f_c}{c} \cdot \cos\theta
$$

where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s and $\theta$ is the angle between the velocity vector and the line-of-sight direction to the receiver.

### Step 3 — Worst-case geometry ($\theta = 0$, head-on)

$$
\cos\theta = \cos(0) = 1
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute numerical values

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_d &= \frac{30 \times 5.9 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}} \times 1 \\
&= \frac{1.77 \times 10^{11}}{3 \times 10^{8}} \\
&= 590 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpret in the 10 MHz channel

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Normalised Doppler } f_d / B &= 590 / 10^{7} = 5.9 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

A 590 Hz Doppler on a 10 MHz carrier corresponds to a coherence time of approximately:

$$
T_c \approx \frac{0.423}{f_d} = \frac{0.423}{590} \approx 0.72 \text{ ms}
$$

The channel is *fast-fading* — the OFDM symbol duration (8 µs in 802.11p) is far shorter than $T_c$, so the channel is approximately **constant within one symbol** ✅.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Hint:** Always mention the angle $\theta$ in the Doppler derivation. The *maximum* Doppler occurs at $\theta = 0$ (closing directly), and the *minimum* (zero) occurs at $\theta = 90°$ (perpendicular motion).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — End-to-End Latency Budget for a BSM

### Problem Setup
A vehicle broadcasts a BSM of $L = 250$ bytes over a 6 Mbps C-V2X sidelink (PC5) to a vehicle 200 m away. BSM periodicity is 10 Hz. Compute the worst-case E2E latency.

### Step 1 — Transmission time

$$
T_{tx} = \frac{L \times 8}{R} = \frac{250 \times 8}{6 \times 10^{6}} = 3.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.333 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 2 — Propagation time

$$
T_{prop} = \frac{d}{c} = \frac{200}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 6.67 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} \approx 0.0007 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 3 — Queueing / access delay
C-V2X Mode 4 uses sensing-based SPS. Average access delay ≈ one sensing window of 100 ms in the worst case; typical average ≈ 20 ms.

$$
T_{queue} = 20 \text{ ms (average)}
$$

### Step 4 — Processing delay (Modem + application)
Assuming $T_{proc} = 5$ ms (typical modem stack).

### Step 5 — Sum all components

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{E2E} &= T_{prop} + T_{queue} + T_{tx} + T_{proc} \\
&= 0.0007 + 20 + 0.333 + 5 \\
&\approx 25.33 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Verify against 100 ms safety budget

$$
T_{E2E} = 25.33 \text{ ms} \;\ll\; 100 \text{ ms} \quad ✅
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** C-V2X Mode 4 with 6 Mbps easily meets the 100 ms safety latency target with ~75 ms of margin. DSRC at 27 Mbps gives $T_{tx} \approx 0.074$ ms — even tighter, but the MAC contention and CCH/SCH alternation can push DSRC latency close to 100 ms in dense scenarios.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Recommended Technology | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Highway V2V safety, no coverage | C-V2X Mode 4 (PC5) | Lowest latency, autonomous |
| Urban V2I + traffic light (SPaT) | DSRC / ITS-G5 | Mature, low cost, low latency |
| HD map download, infotainment | C-V2X Mode 3 (Uu) or 5G | High throughput |
| Mixed fleet (legacy + 5G) | Hybrid DSRC + C-V2X | Backward compatibility |
| Platooning (low latency needed) | 5G NR-V2X (URLLC) | < 5 ms achievable |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid block diagrams capture the architecture of vehicular networks. All node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text, as per the V10 engine safeguards.

## 4.1 V2X Communication Ecosystem

```mermaid
graph TD
    veh1[VEHICLE A OBU]
    veh2[VEHICLE B OBU]
    veh3[VEHICLE C OBU]
    rsu1[ROADSIDE UNIT RSU1]
    rsu2[ROADSIDE UNIT RSU2]
    bs1[CELLULAR BASE STATION 5G GNB]
    ped1[PEDESTRIAN SMARTPHONE V2P]
    cloud[ITS CLOUD TMC HD MAP SERVER]
    tmc[TRAFFIC MANAGEMENT CENTRE]
    sema[SIGNAL CONTROLLER]

    veh1 ---|V2V PC5 5.9 GHZ| veh2
    veh1 ---|V2V PC5 5.9 GHZ| veh3
    veh2 ---|V2I DSRC| rsu1
    veh3 ---|V2I C V2X UU| bs1
    rsu1 ---|FIBER BACKHAUL| tmc
    bs1 ---|4G 5G CORE| cloud
    rsu2 ---|FIBER| sema
    veh1 ---|V2P PC5| ped1
    tmc ---|ITS API| cloud
```

## 4.2 WAVE Protocol Stack (IEEE 802.11p / 1609.x)

```mermaid
graph TD
    a1[APPLICATION SAE J3061 J2945]
    a2[MESSAGE LAYER SAE J2735 BSM MAP SPAT]

    a4[MULTICHANNEL IEEE 1609 DOT 4 CCH SCH ALTERNATION]
    a5[MAC IEEE 802 DOT 11P EDCA QOS 4 AC]
    a6[PHY OFDM 10 MHZ 5 DOT 9 GHZ BPSK TO 64 QAM]
    a7[FREQUENCY 5 DOT 85 TO 5 DOT 925 GHZ BAND]

    a1 --> a2
    a2 --> a3
    a3 --> a4
    a4 --> a5
    a5 --> a6
    a6 --> a7
```

## 4.3 ITS Three-Tier Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    t1[TIER 1 FIELD LAYER SENSING]
    t2[TIER 2 COMMUNICATION LAYER TRANSPORT]

    s2[ROADSIDE UNIT RSU]
    s3[INDUCTION LOOPS CAMERAS LIDAR]

    c3[ETSI ITS G5]
    c4[FIBER BACKHAUL]

    d2[EDGE COMPUTE MEC]
    d3[CLOUD HD MAP OTA SERVERS]
    d4[EMERGENCY DISPATCH POLICE AMBULANCE]

    s1 --> c1
    s2 --> c1
    s3 --> c1
    s1 --> c2
    s2 --> c2
    s1 --> c3
    s2 --> c3
    s1 --> c4
    s2 --> c4
    c1 --> d1
    c2 --> d2
    c2 --> d3
    c4 --> d1
    d1 --> d4
    d3 --> d4
```

## 4.4 C-V2X Mode 3 vs Mode 4 Operating Modes

```mermaid
graph LR
    mode3[MODE 3 NETWORK SCHEDULED UU]
    mode4[MODE 4 AUTONOMOUS SIDELINK PC5]
    enb[E NODE B G NODE B BASE STATION]

    car2[VEHICLE B OBU]
    spec[SPS SENSING BASED SEMI PERSISTENT SCHEDULER]

    mode3 --> sched
    mode4 --> spec
    enb --> sched
    car1 --> mode3
    car2 --> mode3
    car1 ---|PC5 DIRECT| car2
    car1 --> mode4
    car2 --> mode4
```

## 4.5 Cooperative Awareness Message Flow

```mermaid
    participant V2 as VEHICLE B
    participant RSU as RSU
    participant TMC as TRAFFIC MGMT CENTRE

    V1->>V2: BSM BROADCAST 10 HZ PC5 5.9 GHZ
    V1->>RSU: BSM UPLINK UU
    RSU->>TMC: BSM FORWARD FIBER
    V2->>V2: DECODE BSM APPLY SAFETY LOGIC
    RSU->>TMC: AGGREGATE MULTIPLE BSM
    TMC-->>RSU: SPAT MAP GREEN WAVE OPTIMISATION
    RSU-->>V1: UPDATED SIGNAL TIMING
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section follows the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks with internal choice, split as 7+7).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
**Define VANET. List any four characteristics that distinguish it from a generic MANET.**

**Model Answer:**
A Vehicular Ad-hoc Network (VANET) is a self-organising, distributed wireless network formed among vehicles and roadside units (RSUs) using short-range communication (DSRC/C-V2X) in the 5.9 GHz band to support road safety, traffic efficiency, and infotainment.

**Four distinguishing characteristics:**

1. **High node mobility** — speeds up to 200 km/h cause link lifetimes < 1 second.
2. **Constrained/predictable mobility** — vehicles follow road topology, unlike random-waypoint MANETs.
3. **No energy constraint** — OBUs are powered by the car battery.
4. **Hard real-time safety requirements** — latency < 100 ms, reliability > 99 %.

*(Any 4 valid points = 3 marks; 1 mark for definition + 0.5 each characteristic.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**What is the operating frequency band and channel bandwidth used in DSRC / IEEE 802.11p? State the typical data rate.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Operating frequency band:** **5.85 – 5.925 GHz** (centre **5.9 GHz**).  *(1 mark)*
- **Channel bandwidth:** **10 MHz** (the 75 MHz band is divided into 7 channels of 10 MHz each).  *(1 mark)*
- **Typical data rate:** **3 to 27 Mbps**, depending on modulation (BPSK → 64-QAM).  *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, 7 + 7)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** You must answer either **Q.1 OR Q.2**, but **not both**. Each 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question 1 (A) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Model]**

**(a) Explain the WAVE protocol stack with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the Control Channel (CCH) and Service Channels (SCH) in IEEE 1609.4. (7 marks)**

**(b) Compare DSRC and C-V2X in terms of PHY, MAC, latency, scalability, and deployment status. (7 marks)**

#### Solution 1(a)

The WAVE stack consists of:
- **Application Layer** — SAE J3061 (security), SAE J2945 (use cases), J2735 (message dictionary).
- **Networking Layer** — IEEE 1609.3, supports both WSMP (low-latency, single-hop) and IPv6/UDP/TCP.
- **Multichannel Layer** — IEEE 1609.4 — coordinates CCH/SCH alternation.
- **MAC Layer** — IEEE 802.11p — EDCA with 4 Access Categories.
- **PHY Layer** — OFDM at 5.9 GHz, 10 MHz channels, BPSK to 64-QAM.

**Role of CCH & SCH:**
- The **Control Channel (CCH, Ch 178)** is used for safety-critical WAVE Short Messages (WSM), BSMs, and management frames. Every OBU *must* monitor it.
- **Service Channels (SCH, Ch 172, 174, 176, 180, 182, 184)** carry non-safety traffic — IP-based applications, infotainment, firmware updates.
- IEEE 1609.4 enforces a **synchronised 100 ms cycle** — 50 ms on CCH + 50 ms on SCH, using a common Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) reference (e.g., GPS).
- Drawback: this alternation caps safety message latency at 100 ms even in the best case.

*(Block diagram: see SECTION 4.2 — 3 marks; CCH/SCH explanation: 4 marks.)*

#### Solution 1(b)

| Parameter | DSRC / 802.11p | C-V2X (LTE Rel-14 / 5G NR) |
|---|---|---|
| **PHY base** | Wi-Fi OFDM (802.11a) | LTE SC-FDMA / NR OFDMA |
| **Channel BW** | 10 MHz | 10 / 20 MHz (sidelink) |
| **MAC** | CSMA/CA, contention-based | Scheduled (Mode 3) or SPS (Mode 4) |
| **Latency** | ~ 50–100 ms | ~ 20 ms (LTE) / ~ 5 ms (NR URLLC) |
| **Congestion control** | Reactive (CAM-DCC) | Distributed congestion control (DCC) + sensing-based |
| **Scalability at high density** | Degrades sharply (CSMA collision) | Better — controlled resource allocation |
| **Coverage reliance** | None — works without cellular | Mode 4 works without cellular, Mode 3 needs it |
| **Deployment** | Mature, US/Japan pilots | Most global OEMs (Ford, GM, Audi, Hyundai) |
| **Forward evolution** | Frozen (no 802.11p advance) | Continuous 5G NR evolution |
| **Spectrum coexistence** | 5.9 GHz | 5.9 GHz (Rel-14) + 5G mid-band |

*(7 marks: 1 for PHY, 1 for MAC, 1 for latency, 1 for scalability, 1 for coverage, 1 for evolution, 1 for summary.)*

---

### **Question 2 (B) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Model]**

**(a) Describe the ITS architecture. With a neat diagram, explain the three tiers and the role of the Traffic Management Centre (TMC). (7 marks)**

**(b) An OBU transmits at 20 dBm on 5.9 GHz over a distance of 300 m. Compute: (i) the free-space path loss in dB, (ii) the received power in dBm, and (iii) the maximum Doppler shift for a vehicle approaching at 72 km/h. (7 marks)**

#### Solution 2(a)

**ITS Architecture — Three Tiers:**

1. **Tier 1 — Field / Perception Layer:** OBUs (in vehicles), RSUs (on roadside), traffic detectors (induction loops, cameras, LiDAR, smart traffic lights). This layer senses real-time data: vehicle position, speed, signal state, weather, accidents.

2. **Tier 2 — Communication Layer:** Wireless (DSRC, C-V2X, 4G/5G, ETSI ITS-G5) and wired (fiber, Ethernet) networks that transport data between the field and central layers with low latency.

3. **Tier 3 — Central / Service Layer:** Traffic Management Centre (TMC), edge compute (MEC), cloud servers, HD map database, OTA (over-the-air) servers, and emergency services.

**Role of the TMC:**
- Aggregates BSMs/CAMs from thousands of vehicles.
- Performs **big-data analytics** (traffic density, congestion prediction).
- Computes **adaptive signal timing** (SCATS, SCOOT extensions).
- Disseminates **SPaT/MAP** to vehicles via RSU.
- Coordinates with emergency services during accidents.
- Issues **DENM** warnings for hazards (fog, road works, water-logging).

*(Diagram: see SECTION 4.3 — 3 marks; 3-tier narrative: 2 marks; TMC role: 2 marks.)*

#### Solution 2(b)

**Given:** $P_t = 20$ dBm, $f_c = 5.9$ GHz, $d = 300$ m, $v = 72$ km/h = 20 m/s.

**(i) Free Space Path Loss:**

Using d in km = 0.3, f in MHz = 5900:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL} &= 20\log_{10}(0.3) + 20\log_{10}(5900) + 32.44 \\
&= 20 \times (-0.5229) + 20 \times (3.771) + 32.44 \\
&= -10.46 + 75.42 + 32.44 \\
&= 97.40 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Received Power** (assuming unity gains):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= P_t - \text{FSPL} = 20 - 97.40 \\
&= -77.40 \text{ dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Maximum Doppler shift** ($\theta = 0$, head-on):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_d &= \frac{v \cdot f_c}{c} = \frac{20 \times 5.9 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}} \\
&= 393.3 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Results:**
- FSPL = **97.40 dB**
- $P_r$ = **−77.40 dBm**
- $f_d$ = **393.3 Hz**

*(3 marks: FSPL derivation; 2 marks: received power; 2 marks: Doppler with explicit angle assumption.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common reasons students LOSE marks on Vehicular Networks / ITS questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "−147.55" vs "32.44" Friis constant** — marks deducted if units are inconsistent. *Always state the units of d and f explicitly in the formula.*
> 2. **Omitting $\cos\theta$** in Doppler — examiner expects an explicit mention of geometry (head-on / receding / perpendicular).
> 3. **Confusing CCH vs SCH** — many students write "CCH is for control and SCH is for service" but forget the **50/50 alternation** of IEEE 1609.4.
> 4. **Mixing DSRC and C-V2X terminology** — saying "C-V2X uses CSMA/CA" is wrong. C-V2X uses **scheduled access (Mode 3) or sensing-based SPS (Mode 4)**.
> 5. **Skipping the BSM/CAM/DENM distinction** — KTU questions often expect a tabular comparison; a one-line answer scores low.
> 6. **Not drawing the WAVE stack** — block diagrams carry **2–3 marks** in Part B and many students omit them.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist ✅

- **VANET** = self-organising vehicular ad-hoc network; **5.9 GHz**; **3–27 Mbps**; **< 100 ms** latency target.
- **WAVE** = IEEE 1609.x + 802.11p; **CCH Ch 178** for safety, **SCHs** for service; **100 ms cycle** (50 CCH + 50 SCH).
- **C-V2X** = 3GPP Rel-14/15/16; **Mode 3 (Uu, scheduled)** for V2N; **Mode 4 (PC5, SPS)** for V2V/V2I/V2P.
- **5G NR-V2X** adds **groupcast/unicast/HARQ**; **5G URLLC** enables **< 5 ms** latency for platooning.
- **ETSI ITS-G5** = European 802.11p profile with **CAM** (periodic) and **DENM** (event-driven).
- **SAE J2735** standardises **BSM, SPaT, MAP** messages.
- **Key formulas (memorise):** Friis FSPL, Doppler $f_d = v f_c \cos\theta / c$, Shannon capacity, E2E latency sum.
- **ITS 3-tier architecture:** Field → Communication → Central (TMC + cloud + edge).
- **ITS applications:** safety (forward collision warning, emergency brake light), efficiency (green-wave, ramp metering), infotainment (HD map download, OTA).
- **Challenges:** high mobility, channel congestion, hidden terminal, security (PKI), privacy (pseudonym certificates), cross-border interoperability.
- **KTU must-memorise numbers:** **5.9 GHz**, **10 MHz channel**, **27 Mbps**, **100 ms latency**, **10 Hz BSM**, **5–1000 m range**.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line Board Mantra:** *"VANET is the network, ITS is the system, 5.9 GHz is the band, 802.11p and C-V2X are the two stacks, BSM is the message, TMC is the brain, and 100 ms is the budget."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IEEE 802.11p WAVE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IEEE 802.11p WAVE

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🔷 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**IEEE 802.11p** is an approved amendment to the IEEE 802.11 standard that adds **Wireless Access in Vehicular Environments (WAVE)**. It defines the **Physical (PHY) and Medium Access Control (MAC) layers** of the protocol stack to support communications between **vehicles (V2V)**, **vehicles and roadside infrastructure (V2I)**, and **vehicles and pedestrians/Networks (V2N/V2P)** — collectively known as **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) communications**.

The standard operates in the **5.85 GHz – 5.925 GHz** band, also called the **Dedicated Short-Range Communications (DSRC)** band, and is the foundational layer of the **WAVE architecture** defined jointly with the **IEEE 1609.x family** of standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** IEEE 802.11p is the **PHY/MAC layer** specification. The complete V2X communication suite is built on top of it using **IEEE 1609.1, 1609.2, 1609.3, and 1609.4**. Students must know both layers to score full marks.

## 🔷 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Digital Road Walkie-Talkie"

Imagine a busy highway where every car is fitted with a **super-fast, hands-free walkie-talkie** that:
- **Turns on and connects in milliseconds** (no pairing or Wi-Fi password needed — like a CB radio that auto-connects).
- **Uses a special "vehicle-only" frequency band** so that car messages don't interfere with home Wi-Fi or mobile phones.
- **Broadcasts "Here I Am" messages 10 times per second** to nearby cars, telling them your speed, location, braking, and direction.
- **Works at speeds up to 200 km/h** without losing the signal — unlike normal Wi-Fi that drops out when you drive fast.

> **Analogy Box:** Think of **IEEE 802.11p as the "language and airwaves"** used by autonomous vehicles to constantly chat with each other, while **IEEE 1609.x is the "etiquette and safety rules"** governing that conversation.

## 🔷 1.3 Why IEEE 802.11p Exists — The Core Problem It Solves

Standard **Wi-Fi (802.11a/b/g/n/ac)** is designed for **stationary or low-mobility indoor users** with **tolerant latency** (login pages, video streaming). It fails for vehicular networks because:

| Issue with Standard Wi-Fi | Vehicular Network Requirement |
|---------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Authentication/Association takes **hundreds of ms** | Connection must be established in **< 50 ms** |
| Channel coherence time is **long** (humans walk slowly) | Cars move at **v = 100 km/h** → severe **Doppler shift** |
| Handoff assumes **slow roaming** | Vehicles pass through AP coverage in **seconds** |
| 20 MHz channels have **short symbol time (4 μs)** | **Doppler spread** corrupts short symbols in fast fading |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bolded Key Metrics of IEEE 802.11p:**
> - Operating band: **5.85 GHz – 5.925 GHz** (5.9 GHz DSRC)
> - Channel bandwidth: **10 MHz** (half of 802.11a's 20 MHz)
> - OFDM symbol duration: **8 μs** (double of 802.11a)
> - Preamble duration: **32 μs** (short preamble for fast sync)
> - Data rates: **3, 4.5, 6, 9, 12, 18, 24, 27 Mbps**
> - Maximum vehicle speed supported: **≥ 200 km/h**
> - Maximum communication range: **~1 km (line of sight)**

## 🔷 1.4 Position in the WAVE Protocol Stack

| Layer Number | Protocol/Standard | Function |
|--------------|-------------------|----------|
| 7 (Application) | **IEEE 1609.1** | Resource Manager / Multi-channel operation control |
| 6 (Presentation/Security) | **IEEE 1609.2** | Security Services (signing, encryption) |
| 5 (Transport/Network) | **IEEE 1609.3** | Network & Transport Services (WSMP, IPv6) |
| 4 (Multi-Channel) | **IEEE 1609.4** | Multi-Channel Operation (CCH/SCH switching) |
| 2 (Data Link — MAC) | **IEEE 802.11p** | Modified CSMA/CA MAC (no assoc./auth.) |
| 1 (Physical — PHY) | **IEEE 802.11p** | OFDM PHY in 5.9 GHz DSRC band |

> [!NOTE]
> The full WAVE stack is often called the **"IEEE 802.11p / 1609 stack"** because no single standard defines the entire system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔷 2.1 The Operational Logic — Broken into Step-by-Step Concepts

### **Step 1: Why the 5.9 GHz Band?**

- The **5.85 – 5.925 GHz** spectrum was **allocated globally by the ITU** for **Intelligent Transport Systems (ITS)**.
- It lies in the **Unlicensed National Information Infrastructure (U-NII)** band, with **75 MHz** of spectrum.
- It is subdivided into **7 channels of 10 MHz each**:
  - **1 Control Channel (CCH)** at **5.890 GHz** → used for safety messages.
  - **6 Service Channels (SCH)** → for non-safety infotainment.
- The band is relatively **rain-attenuated** and **line-of-sight** — perfect for short-range road communication.

### **Step 2: Why 10 MHz Channel Bandwidth (Not 20 MHz)?**

This is one of the most tested KTU points. The 10 MHz choice directly addresses **vehicular channel impairments**.

- **Doppler Spread (Bd):** At a vehicle speed of v = 60 m/s (216 km/h) and carrier fc = 5.9 GHz, the Doppler shift is:
  - $f_d = \frac{v \cdot f_c}{c}$
- **Coherence Time (Tc):** Inversely proportional to Doppler spread.
- **Symbol Duration (Ts):** MUST be **less than the coherence time** to avoid inter-symbol distortion.

If we use 20 MHz (as in 802.11a), symbol time is **4 μs**, which is shorter than the coherence time in high-speed scenarios. So **802.11p doubles the symbol time to 8 μs** by halving the bandwidth to 10 MHz.

> **The Why:** Longer symbol duration → more robust against **Doppler-induced fast fading** in vehicular channels.

### **Step 3: PHY Layer Design (OFDM with Modified Parameters)**

The PHY of 802.11p is essentially a **downscaled 802.11a OFDM** with these key modifications:

| Parameter | IEEE 802.11a | IEEE 802.11p | Reason for Change |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|-------------------|
| Bandwidth | 20 MHz | **10 MHz** | Robustness to Doppler |
| Useful symbol time | 3.2 μs | **6.4 μs** | Longer symbol for mobile channel |
| Cyclic prefix (GI) | 0.8 μs | **1.6 μs** | Combat vehicular multipath (larger delay spread) |
| Total symbol time | 4 μs | **8 μs** | Sum of useful + GI |
| Preamble | 20 μs (long) | **32 μs (short)** | Use of short training fields only |
| Subcarriers | 52 (48 data + 4 pilot) | **52 (48 data + 4 pilot)** | Same OFDM structure |
| Subcarrier spacing | 312.5 kHz | **156.25 kHz** | Halved with bandwidth halving |
| Modulation | BPSK to 64-QAM | **BPSK to 64-QAM** | Unchanged |
| Coding rates | 1/2, 2/3, 3/4 | **1/2, 2/3, 3/4** | Unchanged |

### **Step 4: MAC Layer Modifications — The "No-Association" Trick**

The biggest MAC-layer innovation in 802.11p is **eliminating the authentication and association procedures** of normal Wi-Fi. This is called the **"WAVE mode"** of operation.

- **Normal 802.11 MAC:** Beacon scan → Authentication → Association → Data exchange (**300+ ms**).
- **802.11p MAC:** As soon as a vehicle enters the range of an **OBU (On-Board Unit)** or **RSU (Roadside Unit)**, it can **immediately transmit** using a special **BSS (Basic Service Set) type = "WAVE BSS"** with a wildcard **BSSID = FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF**.
- This reduces the **join time to < 100 ms** — critical when a vehicle is in coverage for only a few seconds.

Other MAC changes:
- **No RTS/CTS** for control channel messages.
- **EDCA (Enhanced Distributed Channel Access)** with 4 access categories (Voice, Video, Best Effort, Background) prioritized for safety messages.
- **AIFS (Arbitration Inter-Frame Space)** is shorter for high-priority traffic.
- **Contention window (CW)** is tuned for vehicular density.

### **Step 5: Why the Short Preamble (32 μs)?**

In 802.11a, the preamble has **short training (8 μs) + long training (8 μs) + SIGNAL (4 μs) = 20 μs**.
In 802.11p, the **SIGNAL field is moved into the data OFDM symbol** and only the **short training + long training (16 μs)** + a doubled timing structure → **32 μs** total.

**Why this matters for KTU:** Shorter preambles allow vehicles to **synchronize faster and decode messages within microseconds**, which is essential at highway speeds.

---

## 🔷 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and frequently appear in KTU university exam numerical problems.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Doppler Frequency | $f_d = \dfrac{v \cdot f_c}{c}$ | v = velocity (m/s), fc = carrier (Hz), c = 3×10⁸ m/s | v ≤ 250 km/h for compliance |
| 2 | Maximum Doppler Spread (Jakes) | $B_d = 2 \cdot f_d$ | Bd = Doppler spread (Hz) | For isotropic scattering |
| 3 | Coherence Time (50% corr.) | $T_c \approx \dfrac{0.423}{f_d}$ | Tc = coherence time (s) | Jakes spectrum |
| 4 | Coherence Time (90% corr.) | $T_c \approx \dfrac{9}{16\pi \cdot f_d}$ | Tc = coherence time (s) | Conservative estimate |
| 5 | Coherence Bandwidth | $B_c \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi \cdot \sigma_\tau}$ | στ = RMS delay spread (s) | Frequency-selective above Bc |
| 6 | Symbol Duration (802.11p) | $T_s = T_{useful} + T_{GI} = 6.4\mu s + 1.6\mu s$ | Ts = 8 μs | For 10 MHz BW |
| 7 | Useful Symbol Time | $T_{useful} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta f}$ | Δf = 156.25 kHz | = 6.4 μs |
| 8 | Subcarrier Spacing | $\Delta f = \dfrac{BW}{N_{FFT}} = \dfrac{10 \text{ MHz}}{64}$ | Δf = 156.25 kHz | 64-point FFT |
| 9 | Number of Data Subcarriers | $N_d = 48$ | Constant | 802.11p fixed |
| 10 | Number of Pilot Subcarriers | $N_p = 4$ | Constant | Pilot tones |
| 11 | Data Rate (BPSK, r=1/2) | $R = \dfrac{N_d \cdot \log_2(M)}{T_s} \cdot r$ | R = 3 Mbps | BPSK, code rate ½ |
| 12 | Max Delay Spread Tolerance | $\tau_{max} = T_{GI} = 1.6 \mu s$ | For equalizer-free design | Multipath robustness |
| 13 | Maximum Range (Friis) | $d_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r}{P_{r,min}}}$ | λ ≈ 0.051 m @ 5.9 GHz | LOS, free space |

> **Engineering Utility of IEEE 802.11p:** Enables **collision avoidance systems, cooperative adaptive cruise control (CACC), intersection safety, electronic toll collection (ETC), and platooning of autonomous trucks**. It is the **on-ramp technology for V2X and autonomous driving**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🔷 3.1 Derivation 1: Symbol Time Derivation in IEEE 802.11p

We start with the fundamental OFDM relationship between **bandwidth (BW)**, **FFT size (N_FFT)**, and **useful symbol time (T_useful)**.

**Step 1 — Subcarrier Spacing:**

For an OFDM system, the subcarrier spacing is defined as the bandwidth divided by the number of FFT points:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{BW}{N_{FFT}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the 802.11p values:**

In 802.11p, $BW = 10$ MHz and $N_{FFT} = 64$ points. Substituting:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{10 \times 10^6}{64} = 156{,}250 \text{ Hz} = 156.25 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Useful Symbol Duration:**

The useful OFDM symbol time is the inverse of the subcarrier spacing:

$$
T_{useful} = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{156.25 \times 10^3} = 6.4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 6.4\ \mu s
$$

**Step 4 — Guard Interval (Cyclic Prefix):**

802.11p uses a cyclic prefix of 1.6 μs to combat multipath delay spread. The guard interval is **25%** of the useful symbol time (deliberately larger than 802.11a's 0.8 μs = 25% ratio, but tuned to vehicular channel).

**Step 5 — Total Symbol Duration:**

$$
T_s = T_{useful} + T_{GI} = 6.4\ \mu s + 1.6\ \mu s = 8\ \mu s
$$

> **Conclusion:** The total OFDM symbol duration of 802.11p is **8 μs**, exactly **double** that of 802.11a, providing robust tolerance to vehicular Doppler and multipath channels.

---

## 🔷 3.2 Derivation 2: Data Rate Calculation for 802.11p

**Step 1 — Bits per OFDM Symbol:**

Each data subcarrier carries $\log_2(M)$ bits, where M is the modulation order. With $N_d = 48$ data subcarriers, the bits per OFDM symbol are:

$$
B_{sym} = N_d \times \log_2(M)
$$

**Step 2 — Include Coding Rate (r):**

After channel coding at rate r, the **net** data bits per symbol become:

$$
B_{net} = r \times N_d \times \log_2(M)
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Data Rate:**

The data rate is the net bits per symbol divided by the total symbol time:

$$
R = \frac{r \cdot N_d \cdot \log_2(M)}{T_s}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation for BPSK, r = 1/2:**

For BPSK, $\log_2(2) = 1$. Substituting $r = 0.5$, $N_d = 48$, $T_s = 8 \times 10^{-6}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{3Mbps} &= \frac{0.5 \times 48 \times 1}{8 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{24}{8 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 3 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} = 3\ \text{Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Evaluation for QPSK, r = 1/2:**

For QPSK, $\log_2(4) = 2$. Substituting:

$$
R_{6Mbps} = \frac{0.5 \times 48 \times 2}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{48}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 6\ \text{Mbps}
$$

**Step 6 — General Table (Compute All Rates):**

Applying the same formula, we get the standard 802.11p data rates:

| Modulation | Code Rate (r) | log₂(M) | Data Rate (Mbps) |
|------------|---------------|---------|------------------|
| BPSK | 1/2 | 1 | **3** |
| BPSK | 3/4 | 1 | **4.5** |
| QPSK | 1/2 | 2 | **6** |
| QPSK | 3/4 | 2 | **9** |
| 16-QAM | 1/2 | 4 | **12** |
| 16-QAM | 3/4 | 4 | **18** |
| 64-QAM | 2/3 | 6 | **24** |
| 64-QAM | 3/4 | 6 | **27** |

> **Conclusion:** The 802.11p data rates range from **3 Mbps (most robust, used for safety messages)** to **27 Mbps (highest, used for service channels)**.

---

## 🔷 3.3 Derivation 3: Doppler Shift and Coherence Time for Vehicular Channels

**Step 1 — Doppler Shift Formula:**

When a vehicle moves at velocity v toward a transmitter operating at carrier frequency fc, the received frequency is shifted by:

$$
f_d = \frac{v \cdot f_c}{c}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerical Example:**

Consider a vehicle moving at $v = 108$ km/h (30 m/s) on a highway, with $f_c = 5.9$ GHz and $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

$$
f_d = \frac{30 \times 5.9 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8} = 590 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Doppler Spread:**

For a Jakes (isotropic) scattering environment with arrivals from all directions:

$$
B_d = 2 \cdot f_d = 2 \times 590 = 1180 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Coherence Time (90% correlation):**

$$
T_c = \frac{9}{16\pi \cdot f_d} = \frac{9}{16 \pi \times 590} = \frac{9}{29{,}656.6} \approx 303\ \mu s
$$

**Step 5 — Verify Symbol Duration vs Coherence Time:**

The OFDM symbol duration is $T_s = 8\ \mu s$. The ratio is:

$$
\frac{T_c}{T_s} = \frac{303\ \mu s}{8\ \mu s} \approx 37.9
$$

> **Conclusion:** With a coherence time ≈ 38× the symbol duration, the channel is **slow-fading** within one OFDM symbol, so the system can perform reliable equalization. This is the **mathematical justification** for why 802.11p uses **8 μs symbols**.

---

## 🔷 3.4 Analytical Matrix: 802.11p vs 802.11a Comparison

| Comparison Axis | IEEE 802.11a | IEEE 802.11p | KTU-Exam Weight |
|-----------------|--------------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Frequency Band** | 5 GHz (U-NII) | 5.85–5.925 GHz (DSRC) | High |
| **Channel BW** | 20 MHz | **10 MHz** | Very High |
| **Use Case** | Indoor Wi-Fi | Vehicular (V2V/V2I) | Very High |
| **Max Speed** | Walking pace | ≥ 200 km/h | Medium |
| **Association** | Required | **Not required** (WAVE mode) | Very High |
| **OFDM Symbol** | 4 μs | **8 μs** | Very High |
| **Preamble** | 20 μs | **32 μs (short)** | High |
| **GI Length** | 0.8 μs | **1.6 μs** | High |
| **Default Modulation** | BPSK to 64-QAM | **BPSK to 64-QAM** | Medium |
| **Data Rates** | 6 to 54 Mbps | **3 to 27 Mbps** | High |
| **Subcarriers** | 52 | 52 | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
