# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 🔷 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PERAT753, 3 Marks]`

**Q: Define WAVE and list the IEEE standards that form the complete WAVE protocol stack.**

**Model Answer:**

**WAVE (Wireless Access in Vehicular Environments)** is a communications suite defined for **V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle)** and **V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure)** communication, operating in the **5.9 GHz DSRC band** to support intelligent transportation systems.

The complete WAVE stack consists of the following IEEE standards:

| Standard | Layer/Function |
|----------|----------------|
| **IEEE 802.11p** | PHY and MAC layer (OFDM, modified CSMA/CA) |
| **IEEE 1609.1** | Resource Manager (application layer) |
| **IEEE 1609.2** | Security Services (signing, encryption) |
| **IEEE 1609.3** | Network & Transport Services (WSMP, IPv6) |
| **IEEE 1609.4** | Multi-Channel Operation (CCH/SCH switching) |

> **Key Point:** IEEE 802.11p handles only PHY and MAC, while IEEE 1609.x family handles upper layers and multi-channel coordination.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PERAT753, 3 Marks]`

**Q: Why is the OFDM symbol duration in IEEE 802.11p set to 8 μs (twice that of 802.11a)? Explain the design rationale.**

**Model Answer:**

The **OFDM symbol duration in IEEE 802.11p is 8 μs** because of the following design reasons:

1. **Combating Doppler Spread:** In vehicular environments, vehicles move at high speeds (up to 200 km/h), causing significant **Doppler shift** and **fast fading**. A longer symbol time ensures the channel remains approximately constant within one OFDM symbol (coherence time is greater than symbol duration).

2. **Combating Multipath Delay Spread:** Vehicular channels have higher **RMS delay spread** due to reflections from buildings and vehicles. The longer **cyclic prefix (1.6 μs)** in 802.11p absorbs the multipath without ISI.

3. **Halved Bandwidth:** 802.11p uses **10 MHz channels** (half of 802.11a's 20 MHz). Since symbol time is inversely proportional to bandwidth, halving the bandwidth **doubles the symbol time**: $T_s = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{156.25 \text{ kHz}} = 6.4\ \mu s$ useful, plus 1.6 μs GI = **8 μs total**.

4. **Robustness:** The longer symbol is more **robust against inter-carrier interference (ICI)** caused by Doppler-induced frequency offsets.

---

## 🔷 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse 802.11p OFDM parameters with 802.11a** — students commonly write 20 MHz bandwidth, 4 μs symbol, 0.8 μs GI for 802.11p and lose 3–4 marks.
> - **Always mention the "no association" feature** when discussing 802.11p MAC — this is the single most tested differentiator.
> - **Mention both fc and v in Doppler problems**, not just one — partial marks are deducted.
> - **Show all units in numerical problems** (μs, MHz, Hz, km/h, m/s) — unmarked units cause 0.5–1 mark penalty.
> - **Use proper format for WAVE stack** — list all 5 standards (802.11p + 1609.1, 1609.2, 1609.3, 1609.4).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PERAT753]`

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of the WAVE protocol stack. List the functions of each layer.**

**Model Answer:**

The WAVE (Wireless Access in Vehicular Environments) architecture is a layered communication stack designed for vehicular networks. The complete stack is shown in the diagram below.

```
+-----------------------------------------------+
|         IEEE 1609.1  Resource Manager         |  ← Application
+-----------------------------------------------+
|         IEEE 1609.2  Security Services        |  ← Security
+-----------------------------------------------+
|  IEEE 1609.3  Network Services (WSMP, IPv6)   |  ← Network/Transport
+-----------------------------------------------+
|    IEEE 1609.4  Multi-Channel Operation       |  ← Channel Routing
+-----------------------------------------------+
|    IEEE 802.11p  MAC (Modified CSMA/CA)       |  ← Data Link
+-----------------------------------------------+
|  IEEE 802.11p  PHY (OFDM in 5.9 GHz DSRC)     |  ← Physical
+-----------------------------------------------+
|       RF Air Interface (5.85–5.925 GHz)       |  ← Antenna
+-----------------------------------------------+
```

**Functions of each layer:**

1. **IEEE 1609.1 — Resource Manager:** Provides application-layer services and manages **OBU (On-Board Unit) resources**, enabling remote applications to interact with the in-vehicle network. Supports **WAVE Service Advertisement (WSA)** messages.

2. **IEEE 1609.2 — Security Services:** Defines **authentication, encryption, and digital signing** of WAVE messages. Uses **ECDSA (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm)** for message integrity.

3. **IEEE 1609.3 — Network & Transport Services:** Defines two transport protocols — **WSMP (WAVE Short Message Protocol)** for low-latency safety messages and **IPv6** for general networking. WSMP bypasses the TCP/IP stack for sub-millisecond delivery.

4. **IEEE 1609.4 — Multi-Channel Operation:** Manages the **alternation between CCH (Control Channel)** and **SCH (Service Channel)**. Defines the **channel routing and synchronization** so that safety messages get priority on CCH.

5. **IEEE 802.11p — MAC Layer:** Implements **modified CSMA/CA** with **WAVE mode** (no authentication, no association, wildcard BSSID). Uses **EDCA (Enhanced Distributed Channel Access)** with QoS prioritization for safety messages.

6. **IEEE 802.11p — PHY Layer:** Uses **OFDM modulation in the 5.85–5.925 GHz DSRC band** with **10 MHz channels, 8 μs OFDM symbol, 1.6 μs cyclic prefix, 32 μs short preamble**, and supports data rates from **3 to 27 Mbps**.

> **Conclusion:** The WAVE stack is a **multi-layer cooperative framework** where 802.11p provides the radio access (PHY+MAC) and 1609.x provides the upper-layer services specifically tuned for vehicular safety and infotainment.

---

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks

**Q: A vehicle is moving at a velocity of 162 km/h. The carrier frequency of an IEEE 802.11p system is 5.9 GHz. Calculate (i) the Doppler shift, (ii) the maximum Doppler spread, and (iii) the coherence time (using the 90% correlation formula).**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Velocity, $v = 162$ km/h
- Carrier frequency, $f_c = 5.9$ GHz
- Speed of light, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s

**Step 1: Convert velocity to SI units:**

$$
v = 162 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 45\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Doppler Shift (i):**

Using the Doppler equation:

$$
f_d = \frac{v \cdot f_c}{c}
$$

$$
f_d = \frac{45 \times 5.9 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_d &= \frac{265.5 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8} \\
&= 885\ \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore, the Doppler shift is 885 Hz.**

**Step 3: Calculate Maximum Doppler Spread (ii):**

For a Jakes (isotropic) scattering environment, the maximum Doppler spread is:

$$
B_d = 2 \times f_d = 2 \times 885 = 1770\ \text{Hz}
$$

**Therefore, the maximum Doppler spread is 1770 Hz (1.77 kHz).**

**Step 4: Calculate Coherence Time (iii) using 90% correlation formula:**

$$
T_c = \frac{9}{16\pi \cdot f_d}
$$

$$
T_c = \frac{9}{16 \times \pi \times 885} = \frac{9}{44{,}484.05}
$$

$$
T_c \approx 2.023 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 202.3\ \mu s
$$

**Therefore, the coherence time is approximately 202.3 μs.**

**Step 5: Verification — Is the OFDM symbol time valid?**

802.11p symbol time, $T_s = 8\ \mu s$

Since $T_s (8\ \mu s) \ll T_c (202.3\ \mu s)$, the channel is **slow-fading within one OFDM symbol**, and the system can perform reliable equalization.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PERAT753]`

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks

**Q: Compare the PHY layer parameters of IEEE 802.11a and IEEE 802.11p. Why was IEEE 802.11p designed with 10 MHz channels instead of 20 MHz?**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | IEEE 802.11a | IEEE 802.11p |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| Application | Indoor Wi-Fi | Vehicular (V2X) |
| Frequency Band | 5 GHz (U-NII) | 5.85–5.925 GHz (DSRC) |
| Channel Bandwidth | 20 MHz | **10 MHz** |
| FFT Size | 64 | 64 |
| Subcarrier Spacing | 312.5 kHz | **156.25 kHz** |
| Number of Data Subcarriers | 48 | 48 |
| Number of Pilot Subcarriers | 4 | 4 |
| Useful Symbol Time | 3.2 μs | **6.4 μs** |
| Cyclic Prefix (GI) | 0.8 μs | **1.6 μs** |
| Total OFDM Symbol | 4 μs | **8 μs** |
| Preamble Duration | 20 μs | **32 μs (short)** |
| Data Rates | 6 to 54 Mbps | **3 to 27 Mbps** |
| Modulation | BPSK to 64-QAM | BPSK to 64-QAM |
| Channel Coding | Convolutional (1/2, 2/3, 3/4) | Convolutional (1/2, 2/3, 3/4) |

**Reason for 10 MHz Channel Width (instead of 20 MHz):**

1. **Doppler Spread Robustness:** At vehicular speeds (up to 200 km/h), the Doppler shift at 5.9 GHz is significant (≈ 1.1 kHz at 200 km/h). A 10 MHz channel has **half the subcarrier spacing** (156.25 kHz vs 312.5 kHz), making it more tolerant to **inter-carrier interference (ICI)** caused by Doppler.

2. **Longer Symbol Time:** Halving the bandwidth doubles the OFDM symbol time from 4 μs to **8 μs**. A longer symbol time ensures that the channel coherence time is much greater than the symbol duration, which is essential for high-mobility channels.

3. **Multipath Tolerance:** A longer cyclic prefix (**1.6 μs vs 0.8 μs**) absorbs larger RMS delay spread typical of outdoor vehicular environments with reflections from buildings and surrounding vehicles.

4. **Spectrum Allocation:** The 5.9 GHz DSRC band has only **75 MHz** available, divided into 7 channels of 10 MHz each (1 CCH + 6 SCH). Using 10 MHz allows **more channelization flexibility** for safety and service separation.

> **Conclusion:** The 10 MHz channelization is a deliberate engineering trade-off — sacrificing raw data rate (max 27 Mbps vs 54 Mbps) to gain **Doppler robustness, longer symbol time, and better multipath performance** required for high-speed vehicular communication.

---

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the WAVE mode of operation in IEEE 802.11p. How is the connection setup time reduced compared to traditional Wi-Fi? What is the role of the wildcard BSSID?**

**Model Answer:**

**WAVE Mode of Operation:**

WAVE mode is a special operating mode of IEEE 802.11p that allows vehicles to **communicate without performing the standard 802.11 authentication and association procedures**. This is critical because vehicles only remain in the coverage area of an RSU for a few seconds at highway speeds.

**Traditional Wi-Fi Connection Setup (802.11a/b/g/n):**

In normal Wi-Fi, a station must complete these steps before transmitting data:
1. **Active/Passive Scanning** — Listen for beacon frames or send probe requests (50–500 ms).
2. **Authentication** — Open or shared-key exchange (10–30 ms).
3. **Association** — Request/response with the AP (10–30 ms).
4. **DHCP** — Get IP address (100–500 ms).
5. **Data exchange** begins.

**Total setup time: 300 ms to 1500 ms.** For a vehicle moving at 100 km/h, this is **unacceptable** — the vehicle would have left the coverage area before data exchange begins.

**WAVE Mode Connection Setup (802.11p):**

In 802.11p WAVE mode, the procedure is **simplified drastically**:
1. **No scanning** — Vehicles transmit immediately on the CCH.
2. **No authentication** — Assumes trust via IEEE 1609.2 security layer.
3. **No association** — Uses a **wildcard BSSID = FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF**.
4. **No DHCP** — Uses WSMP for short messages.
5. **Data exchange** starts within **< 50 ms**.

**Role of the Wildcard BSSID (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF):**

- A normal BSSID is a unique 48-bit MAC address identifying an AP.
- The **wildcard BSSID** is a special broadcast identifier that **matches any BSS** in the vicinity.
- An OBU configured for WAVE mode accepts frames from **any RSU** that uses this wildcard BSSID, eliminating the need to know the AP's identity in advance.
- This allows **anonymous, fast, broadcast-style communication** — perfect for safety messages that must reach all nearby vehicles.

**Other MAC Enhancements in 802.11p:**
- **EDCA (Enhanced Distributed Channel Access)** with 4 QoS categories prioritizes safety messages.
- **Shorter AIFS (Arbitration Inter-Frame Space)** for high-priority traffic.
- **RTS/CTS is optional** — disabled for most safety broadcasts to reduce overhead.
- **No fragmentation** in many safety applications (since messages are short, ~100 bytes).

> **Conclusion:** WAVE mode + wildcard BSSID reduces connection setup time from **~1000 ms to <50 ms**, enabling real-time V2X communication in fast-changing vehicular topologies.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**IEEE 1609** is a family of standards developed by the **Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE)** for **Wireless Access in Vehicular Environments (WAVE)**. It defines the architecture, communications model, management structure, security services, and physical interface for short-range (≤ 1000 m) wireless communications in **Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS)**. The standard operates over the licensed ITS band of **5.85–5.925 GHz** in North America and forms the upper-layer protocol stack that works in conjunction with the **IEEE 802.11p** amendment at the lower MAC/PHY layers. Collectively, this stack is popularly known as **DSRC (Dedicated Short-Range Communications)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The IEEE 1609 family is often tested under the umbrella of "Vehicular Ad-Hoc Networks (VANETs)". The four core sub-standards you must remember for KTU 2024 exams are **1609.2, 1609.3, 1609.4**, along with the enabling **IEEE 802.11p** standard.

The complete WAVE protocol stack is layered as follows:

| Layer | Standard | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Application | IEEE 1609.1 | Resource Manager |
| Security | IEEE 1609.2 | Secure message formatting & signing |
| Network/Transport | IEEE 1609.3 | WAVE Short Message Protocol (WSMP), IPv6 |
| MAC Extensions | IEEE 1609.4 | Channel switching & coordination |
| PHY/MAC | IEEE 802.11p | OFDM-based 5.9 GHz radio |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Announcement System"

Imagine you are driving on a busy national highway. Your car has a **megaphone** that can either:
1. **Broadcast** an emergency (e.g., "Crash ahead!") to every other car and roadside pole within **300 meters**, OR
2. **Listen** selectively to specific services being advertised by **digital billboards** (Roadside Units).

**IEEE 1609 is the rulebook for that megaphone.** It defines:
- **What format** the announcement must be in (so any car can understand it),
- **Which channel** to use (so two cars speaking at once don't collide),
- **How to sign** the message digitally (so a hacker cannot fake a "crash" warning), and
- **How to switch** between safety broadcasts and infotainment services efficiently.

The **two key actors** in this analogy are:
- **OBU (On-Board Unit)** — the megaphone inside your car.
- **RSU (Roadside Unit)** — the digital billboard/infrastructure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Memorize:**
> - Frequency Band: **5.85 – 5.925 GHz** (Total **75 MHz**)
> - Number of channels: **7 channels** (1 Control + 6 Service)
> - Channel bandwidth: **10 MHz** (optionally 20 MHz)
> - Maximum communication range: **~1000 m** (typical vehicular range ≈ 300 m)
> - Maximum vehicle velocity supported: **200 km/h** (highway speeds)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The IEEE 1609 Family — Sub-Standard Breakdown

The IEEE 1609 standard is **not a single document** but a coordinated set of sub-standards. Each handles a distinct networking responsibility.

#### 2.1.1 IEEE 1609.0 — Architecture
- Defines the **overall WAVE system architecture**.
- Specifies the **communications model** and reference diagram.
- Identifies roles of **Provider** (service publisher) and **User** (service consumer).

#### 2.1.2 IEEE 1609.1 — Resource Manager (WAVE RM)
- Enables **OBUs to use applications resident on remote RSUs** (or other OBUs).
- Defines a programming interface for **WAVE applications**.
- Allows the WAVE Stack to act as a *service intermediary* (similar to the **OSI Application Layer**).

#### 2.1.3 IEEE 1609.2 — Security Services ⚠️ (High-Yield for KTU)
- Provides **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation** for WAVE messages.
- Uses **ECDSA (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm)** with the **NIST P-256 curve** for signing.
- **Why it matters:** A malicious actor must not be able to broadcast a fake "vehicle braking" alert. 1609.2 is the cryptographic shield.
- Defines two message types: **Signed messages** (unencrypted, fast) and **Signed & Encrypted messages** (secure, slower).

#### 2.1.4 IEEE 1609.3 — Networking Services (WSMP) ⚠️ (High-Yield for KTU)
- Defines the **WAVE Short Message Protocol (WSMP)** — a lightweight, connectionless protocol optimized for vehicular environments.
- WSMP **bypasses the TCP/IP overhead** to deliver safety messages with ultra-low latency.
- **WAVE Service Advertisement (WSA)** is broadcast on the control channel — it tells neighboring OBUs *which services are available* on which service channel.
- The WSA contains:
  - Service Identifier
  - Provider Service Identifier (PSID)
  - Channel number on which the service is hosted
  - MAC address of the provider
  - Repeat rate & transmit power

#### 2.1.5 IEEE 1609.4 — Multi-Channel Operations ⚠️ (High-Yield for KTU)
- Manages **channel coordination and switching** between the **Control Channel (CCH)** and **Service Channels (SCHs)**.
- Time is divided into repeated **Sync Intervals (SI)** of **100 ms** each.
- Each Sync Interval contains:
  - **CCH Interval (50 ms)** → dedicated to safety messages, WSAs, and broadcast frames.
  - **SCH Interval (50 ms)** → dedicated to infotainment, IP-based traffic, and service-specific data.
- A 4 ms **Guard Interval (GI)** separates CCH and SCH to allow radios to retune.
- **Why 50/50 split?** It guarantees every WAVE device a *deterministic* time-slot for safety broadcasts — critical for collision avoidance applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Channel Numbering Convention (from IEEE 802.11p):**
> - Channel **178** → Control Channel (CCH) — the safety priority channel
> - Channels **172, 174, 176, 180, 182, 184** → Service Channels (SCHs)
> - Channel **175** → Special reserved channel (5 MHz spacing)

### 2.2 Why IPv6 is Supported but Not Preferred

Although 1609.3 supports **IPv6** (for general-purpose data like internet access in cars), **safety-critical applications use WSMP** because:
- IPv6 over 802.11p incurs ~40–60 ms latency.
- WSMP over 802.11p incurs ~3–5 ms latency.
- In a crash scenario, **40 ms can be the difference between life and death**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Value / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Frequency Band | **5.85 – 5.925** | GHz |
| Channel Bandwidth | **10** (default) or 20 | MHz |
| Total Number of Channels | **7** (1 CCH + 6 SCH) | — |
| Sync Interval (SI) | **100** | ms |
| CCH Interval | **50** | ms |
| SCH Interval | **50** | ms |
| Guard Interval (GI) | **4** | ms |
| Max PHY Data Rate (10 MHz, 64-QAM) | **27** | Mbps |
| Min PHY Data Rate (10 MHz, BPSK) | **3** | Mbps |
| Default Transmit Power | **23** (Class C) | dBm |
| Max Communication Range | **~1000** | m |
| Max Vehicle Speed | **200** | km/h |
| Time to switch CCH → SCH | **4** | ms |
| Data rate per channel (effective) | **6, 12, 18, 24, 27** | Mbps (802.11p rates) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** IEEE 1609 / WAVE forms the U.S. Department of Transportation's (USDOT) **Connected Vehicle** program backbone. Its European counterpart is **ETSI ITS-G5**, and the cellular alternative is **C-V2X (3GPP Release 14+)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Derivations

### 3.1 Derivation: Time Budget Available for Safety Messages in a Sync Interval

**Problem:** Given the IEEE 1609.4 timing specification, calculate the **net time available for control channel (safety) operations** in a 1-second window.

**Given:**
- Sync Interval ($T_{SI}$) = 100 ms
- CCH Interval ($T_{CCH}$) = 50 ms
- Guard Interval ($T_{GI}$) = 4 ms

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$T_{net\_CCH} = T_{CCH} - 2 \cdot T_{GI}$$

$$T_{net\_CCH} = 50 - 2(4) = 42 \text{ ms}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of SIs in 1 second} &= \frac{1000 \text{ ms}}{T_{SI}} \\
&= \frac{1000}{100} \\
&= 10 \text{ SIs/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total\_CCH\_per\_second} &= 10 \times T_{net\_CCH} \\
&= 10 \times 42 \\
&= 420 \text{ ms/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Out of every 1000 ms, only **420 ms (42%)** is effectively available on the control channel for safety broadcasts. The remaining 580 ms is split between SCH operations and tuning overhead. This is why **WSMP messages must be extremely compact** — they have only a few hundred microseconds to be transmitted and acknowledged.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step: WAVE Connection Setup (How a Service is Discovered)

The WAVE service discovery process follows a strict 4-step sequence:

1. **Provider RSU (or OBU) tunes to CCH (Channel 178)** during the CCH interval.
2. The provider **broadcasts a WAVE Service Advertisement (WSA)** frame. The WSA contains:
   - Header with timestamp
   - Service Info (PSID, service port)
   - Channel Info (target SCH number, data rate, transmit power)
   - RCPI (Received Channel Power Indicator) — optional signal quality hint
3. **User OBUs within range** scan the CCH and parse the WSA.
4. Interested User OBUs **switch to the advertised SCH** during the SCH interval and begin exchanging data using WSMP (or IPv6 if the service is IP-based).

> [!NOTE]
> **Transition logic:** A User OBU is *always* on the CCH. It only temporarily switches to an SCH for the duration of an active service session, returning to CCH at the start of every new CCH interval to receive safety messages.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: WSMP vs. IPv6 over WAVE

| Performance Metric | WSMP (1609.3) | IPv6 (over 1609.3 + UDP/TCP) |
|---|---|---|
| End-to-End Latency | **3–5 ms** | **40–60 ms** |
| Header Overhead | ~5 bytes | ~40 bytes (IPv6) + UDP/TCP |
| Use Case | Safety messages (BSM, CAM, DENM) | Internet, infotainment |
| Connection Setup | Connectionless | Connection-oriented (TCP) |
| Address Resolution | PSID-based | IPv6 address (Neighbor Discovery) |
| Suitability for Safety Apps | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Poor (latency-critical) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BSM (Basic Safety Message)** is the U.S. term for safety messages; **CAM (Cooperative Awareness Message)** and **DENM (Decentralized Environmental Notification Message)** are the European ETSI ITS-G5 equivalents — but they ride on the same 802.11p physical layer.

### 3.4 Derivation: Maximum Number of OBUs Addressable by a Single WSA

**Problem:** A WSA can advertise up to **32 services** per frame. If an RSU runs 4 applications, each requiring 1 service channel, how many OBUs can simultaneously access non-overlapping service channels (given 6 SCHs and assuming all 4 apps use unique SCHs)?

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{SCHs\_used} &= 4 \\
N_{SCHs\_available} &= 6 \\
N_{available} &= N_{SCHs\_available} - N_{SCHs\_used} = 6 - 4 = 2 \text{ SCHs free}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each SCH can theoretically support dozens of OBUs via **CSMA/CA** contention. The exact number depends on:
- Channel capacity $C$ (3–27 Mbps)
- Per-vehicle data rate $r$ (e.g., 1 Mbps for BSM)
- Channel access overhead $\alpha$ (≈ 30%)

$$
N_{users\_per\_SCH} \approx \left\lfloor \frac{C \cdot (1 - \alpha)}{r} \right\rfloor
$$

$$
N_{users\_per\_SCH} \approx \left\lfloor \frac{27 \times 0.7}{1} \right\rfloor = 18 \text{ OBUs/SCH}
$$

This is a **theoretical upper bound**; real-world deployments typically support **~10–12 OBUs/SCH** with full QoS guarantees.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 WAVE Protocol Stack (Layered Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    app1[Application Layer 1609.1 Resource Manager]
    sec[Security Layer 1609.2 ECDSA Signing and Encryption]
    wsmp[Network Transport Layer 1609.3 WSMP and IPv6]
    mac[MAC Extension Layer 1609.4 Channel Switching CCH and SCH]
    phy[PHY and MAC Layer 802.11p OFDM at 5.9 GHz]
    rf[5.85 to 5.925 GHz RF Frontend]

    app1 --> sec
    sec --> wsmp
    wsmp --> mac
    mac --> phy
    phy --> rf
```

### 4.2 WAVE Multi-Channel Time-Slot Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Start of Sync Interval 100 ms] --> B[Guard Interval 4 ms CCH to SCH]
    B --> C[CCH Interval 50 ms Channel 178 Safety Messages and WSAs]
    C --> D[Guard Interval 4 ms SCH to CCH]
    D --> E[SCH Interval 50 ms Channels 172 to 184 Infotainment and Data]
    E --> F[End of Sync Interval Returns to CCH]
    F --> A
```

### 4.3 WAVE Service Discovery Flow (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rsu[RSU Provider] --step 1 broadcast WSA--> cch[Control Channel 178]
    obu1[OBU User A] --step 2 listen--> cch
    obu2[OBU User B] --step 2 listen--> cch
    cch --step 3 WSA parsed--> obu1
    cch --step 3 WSA parsed--> obu2
    obu1 --step 4 switch to SCH--> sch1[Service Channel 172]
    obu2 --step 4 switch to SCH--> sch2[Service Channel 174]
    rsu --step 5 deliver service--> sch1
    rsu --step 5 deliver service--> sch2
```

### 4.4 IEEE 1609 Family — Sub-Standard Functional Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    wave[IEEE 1609 WAVE Family]
    wave --> s0[1609.0 Architecture]
    wave --> s1[1609.1 Resource Manager]
    wave --> s2[1609.2 Security Services]
    wave --> s3[1609.3 Networking WSMP]
    wave --> s4[1609.4 Multi Channel Operations]
    s3 --> wsmp[WSMP Short Messages]
    s3 --> ipv6[IPv6 Support]
    s4 --> cch[Control Channel Switching]
    s4 --> sch[Service Channel Switching]
    s2 --> ecdsa[ECDSA P 256 Signing]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** The arrows represent *data/control flow*, not strict OSI layering. WAVE layers are tightly coupled and do not follow the strict independence of the classical OSI 7-layer model.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define IEEE 1609. List its four main sub-standards and their primary functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
IEEE 1609 is a family of standards for Wireless Access in Vehicular Environments (WAVE), enabling short-range communications in the 5.9 GHz band. The four key sub-standards are:
- **1609.1** → Resource Manager for remote application access.
- **1609.2** → Security services (authentication, integrity, confidentiality using ECDSA).
- **1609.3** → Networking services (WSMP and IPv6).
- **1609.4** → Multi-channel operations (CCH/SCH coordination and switching).
*Each sub-standard carries ≈ 0.5 mark; full definition ≈ 1 mark.*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between WAVE Short Message Protocol (WSMP) and IPv6 in vehicular communication. When is each preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | WSMP | IPv6 |
|---|---|---|
| Latency | **3–5 ms** | **40–60 ms** |
| Overhead | Low (~5 bytes) | High (~40 bytes) |
| Use Case | Safety messages (BSM/CAM) | Internet/infotainment |

WSMP is preferred for **safety-critical, low-latency applications**, while IPv6 is used for **general-purpose IP services** such as web browsing and OTA updates. *(1 mark for differences table, 1 mark for use-case distinction, 1 mark for preference justification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the IEEE 1609 WAVE protocol stack with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of IEEE 1609.3 and IEEE 1609.4 in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the WAVE multi-channel operation. Derive the **net time available for safety messages on the control channel per second**, given that each Sync Interval is 100 ms with 4 ms guard intervals. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) WAVE Protocol Stack (7 marks):**

The WAVE protocol stack is structured as follows (top-down):

1. **Application Layer (1609.1):** Manages WAVE applications and provides a resource manager interface so OBUs can run applications hosted on RSUs. *(1 mark)*

2. **Security Layer (1609.2):** Provides message authenticity and integrity using **ECDSA with the NIST P-256 curve**. Defines two message types: **signed-only** (low overhead) and **signed-and-encrypted** (high security). Prevents fake "accident" warnings from malicious actors. *(1 mark)*

3. **Network/Transport Layer (1609.3):** Defines two transport mechanisms:
   - **WSMP (WAVE Short Message Protocol):** Lightweight, connectionless, PSID-addressed protocol for safety messages.
   - **IPv6 with UDP/TCP:** For general IP-based traffic.
   - Also defines the **WAVE Service Advertisement (WSA)** frame format used on the CCH. *(2 marks)*

4. **MAC Extension Layer (1609.4):** Manages the **alternation between the Control Channel (CCH, Ch 178) and Service Channels (SCHs)** using a synchronized time-slot mechanism. *(1.5 marks)*

5. **PHY/MAC Layer (IEEE 802.11p):** OFDM-based physical layer operating in the 5.85–5.925 GHz band, supporting 3–27 Mbps data rates and 1000 m range. *(1.5 marks)*

**(b) Multi-Channel Operation & Time Derivation (7 marks):**

**Multi-Channel Operation:** *(3 marks for explanation)*
- The 5.9 GHz spectrum is divided into **7 channels of 10 MHz each** (1 CCH + 6 SCHs).
- Time is divided into repeated **Sync Intervals of 100 ms**.
- Each Sync Interval has:
  - **CCH Interval (50 ms):** Safety messages, WSAs.
  - **Guard Interval (4 ms):** Tuning time.
  - **SCH Interval (50 ms):** Service data.
- The alternating pattern ensures that *every* device has a deterministic time-slot for safety broadcasts while still allowing access to non-safety services.

**Derivation:** *(4 marks)*

Given: $T_{SI} = 100$ ms, $T_{CCH} = 50$ ms, $T_{GI} = 4$ ms.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{net\_CCH} &= T_{CCH} - 2 \cdot T_{GI} \\
&= 50 - 2(4) = 42 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Number of SIs per second:

$$N_{SI} = \frac{1000}{100} = 10$$

Total effective CCH time per second:

$$T_{eff} = 10 \times 42 = 420 \text{ ms}$$

**Conclusion:** Only **420 ms per second (42%)** is available for safety-critical CCH operations, which is why WSMP must be lightweight.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the WAVE Service Advertisement (WSA) mechanism. List the fields of a typical WSA frame and explain how service discovery is performed in IEEE 1609. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable timing diagram, explain the **alternating CCH/SCH operation** specified by IEEE 1609.4. How is channel synchronization maintained among OBUs? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) WSA Mechanism (7 marks):**
- The **WSA** is broadcast by an RSU (or provider OBU) on the **Control Channel (178)** during the CCH interval. *(1 mark)*
- It informs nearby OBUs about **which services are available** and **on which service channel** they are hosted. *(1 mark)*
- **WSA Frame Fields:** *(3 marks)*
  - **Header:** Version, timestamp, etc.
  - **Service Info:** PSID, service priority, service port.
  - **Channel Info:** SCH number, data rate, transmit power, link quality (RCPI).
  - **MAC Address:** Provider's address.
  - **Repeat Rate:** How often the WSA is rebroadcast.
- **Service Discovery Process:** *(2 marks)*
  1. Provider broadcasts WSA on CCH.
  2. User OBUs scan CCH and parse WSA frames.
  3. Interested OBUs switch to the advertised SCH during the next SCH interval.
  4. WSMP (or IPv6) session begins; OBU returns to CCH at the next interval.

**(b) CCH/SCH Alternation (7 marks):**
- Refer to the timing diagram in **Section 4.2** of these notes. *(2 marks for diagram explanation)*
- **Sync Interval = 100 ms** is divided into CCH (50 ms) + Guard (4 ms) + SCH (50 ms). *(2 marks)*
- **Channel synchronization** is maintained using **UTC time** (derived from GPS) which is globally available to all OBUs and RSUs. *(1.5 marks)*
- All devices align their **CCH start time** to the same UTC boundary, ensuring simultaneous switching. *(1 mark)*
- **Guard intervals (4 ms)** compensate for oscillator drift and radio tuning latency. *(0.5 mark)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing 1609.3 with 1609.4:** 1609.3 handles *protocols* (WSMP/IPv6); 1609.4 handles *channel switching* (CCH/SCH timing). Examiners love to set trick questions mixing these two.
> 2. **Forgetting the 4 ms guard interval:** Many students derive 50 ms × 10 = 500 ms, ignoring the 4 ms GI. Correct value is **420 ms**. Lose 2 marks instantly.
> 3. **Mixing up 802.11p with 1609.0:** 802.11p is the *physical layer* standard; 1609.0 is the *architecture* standard. They are siblings, not parent-child.
> 4. **Writing "DSRC" without context:** DSRC is a *system* (5.9 GHz ITS), not a *standard*. The standards are 802.11p + 1609.x. Always use the precise standard name.
> 5. **Skipping the WSA frame fields:** If asked about WSA, students often give a vague explanation. Always list **at least 4 fields** (PSID, Channel Number, MAC, Repeat Rate) for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

- ✅ **IEEE 1609** = WAVE family for **vehicular short-range communications**.
- ✅ Operating band: **5.85 – 5.925 GHz** with **7 channels of 10 MHz**.
- ✅ **802.11p** is the PHY/MAC; **1609.x** is the upper-layer suite.
- ✅ **1609.2** = Security (ECDSA P-256); **1609.3** = WSMP/IPv6 + WSA; **1609.4** = CCH/SCH switching.
- ✅ **Sync Interval = 100 ms** → **50 ms CCH + 4 ms GI + 50 ms SCH** (effectively 42 ms usable CCH per interval).
- ✅ **WSMP** is preferred over IPv6 for safety messages due to **~10× lower latency**.
- ✅ **WSA** (WAVE Service Advertisement) is the *control plane* for service discovery, broadcast on **CCH (Channel 178)**.
- ✅ **Two main actors:** **OBU** (vehicle-mounted) and **RSU** (infrastructure-mounted).
- ✅ Max data rate: **27 Mbps** (64-QAM); Min: **3 Mbps** (BPSK, used for long-range safety).
- ✅ European equivalent: **ETSI ITS-G5**; Cellular alternative: **C-V2X (3GPP)**.
- ✅ Always use **UTC time from GPS** to synchronize channel switching across all vehicles.

> **Final Tip:** When answering any KTU question on IEEE 1609, always begin with the **frequency band (5.9 GHz)**, mention the **channel structure (1 CCH + 6 SCH)**, and end with a **timing reference (100 ms Sync Interval)**. This signals to the examiner that you have command over the entire system — a guaranteed full-marks pattern.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IEEE 02.15.7 - Visible Light Communication (VLC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT753 - MODERN COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IEEE 02.15.7 - Visible Light Communication (VLC)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**IEEE 802.15.7** is a short-range **Optical Wireless Communication (OWC)** standard published by the **Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE)** in **2011** (revised in 2018), which specifies the **Physical Layer (PHY)** and **Medium Access Control (MAC)** sublayer for wireless communication using **Visible Light** as the transmission medium.

The standard operates within the **380 nm to 780 nm** wavelength band of the electromagnetic spectrum, exploiting the dual function of **Light Emitting Diodes (LEDs)** — illumination and data transmission simultaneously. This dual-use principle is the cornerstone of **Visible Light Communication (VLC)**, often referred to as **Li-Fi (Light Fidelity)** at the consumer-grade marketing level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Identifier:** IEEE Std 802.15.7-2018 (Revision of IEEE Std 802.15.7-2011).
> **Operating Spectrum:** Visible Light Band (380 nm – 780 nm) — a license-free, unregulated portion of the spectrum under international telecommunications law.

### Taxonomy of Optical Wireless Communication

The IEEE 802.15.7 standard categorizes OWC into three distinct tiers based on the wavelength used:

| Tier | Wavelength Range | Standard Reference | Application Focus |
|------|------------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **VLC** | 380 nm – 780 nm | IEEE 802.15.7 | Indoor/outdoor short range with illumination |
| **IR** | 760 nm – 1 mm | IrDA / IEEE 802.11 variants | Remote controls, point-to-point |
| **UV** | 10 nm – 400 nm | Under research | Non-line-of-sight, scatter-based |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Lighthouse & The Lighthouse Keeper**
> Imagine you are standing in a coastal village where the lighthouse keeper wants to send Morse code to ships — but instead of using a radio, he flashes the lighthouse beam in short and long pulses. A ship with a light sensor decodes these flashes into letters. This is **exactly** how VLC works — the **LED light bulb** is the lighthouse, and a **photodetector (photodiode)** is the ship's light sensor. The twist? You can keep reading your book under the bulb while invisible data streams down to your phone at gigabit speeds!

A more relatable analogy in modern terms:

> **The "Talking Lamppost" Analogy:** Every streetlight in a smart city could be a wireless access point. As you walk under it, your phone (with a photodiode sensor on its screen) receives high-speed data streamed via flicker patterns so fast (millions of times per second) that the human eye perceives no flicker — the light looks perfectly steady while carrying terabytes of data.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics form the foundational vocabulary of IEEE 802.15.7:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Speed of Light in Vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
> **Planck's Constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
> **Photopic Luminous Efficiency Peak:** $V(\lambda) = 1.0$ at $\lambda = 555$ nm (green)
> **Standard Indoor Illumination Level:** $300$ – **$500$ lux** at desk surface (ISO 8995-1)
> **Flicker-Free Threshold:** Modulation frequency **$> 200$ Hz** to avoid perceptual flicker
> **Maximum Permissible Exposure (MPE) Eye Safety:** Compliant with **IEC 60825-1** for extended source viewing

---

## 1.4 Motivation: Why VLC?

The radio frequency (RF) spectrum is **congested, licensed, and expensive**. VLC offers compelling engineering advantages:

- **Bandwidth:** Visible light spectrum offers **~400 THz** of unlicensed bandwidth — about **10,000×** more than the entire RF spectrum.
- **Security:** Light does not penetrate walls, providing inherent physical-layer security.
- **Interference Immunity:** VLC is immune to electromagnetic interference (EMI), making it ideal for hospitals, aircraft, and industrial plants.
- **Dual Functionality:** The same LED provides both illumination and communication — zero additional energy cost for the lighting infrastructure.
- **Safety:** Non-ionizing radiation — completely harmless to humans (unlike X-rays or UV).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **VLC** (the technology) with **IEEE 802.15.7** (the standard). VLC is the broader technology; IEEE 802.15.7 is the **specific protocol stack** that governs VLC device interoperability.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture of an IEEE 802.15.7 Link

An end-to-end VLC link comprises three functional blocks:

1. **Transmitter (TX):** Data bits → Line coder → LED driver → LED (emits modulated optical intensity).
2. **Channel:** Free-space propagation under **Lambertian emission pattern** with possible line-of-sight (LOS) or non-LOS (NLOS) paths.
3. **Receiver (RX):** Photodiode (PD) → Transimpedance Amplifier (TIA) → Equalizer → Decoder → Data bits.

The signal at the photodetector is a current proportional to the **incident optical power**, not voltage. This forces VLC to use **Intensity Modulation with Direct Detection (IM/DD)** — the only practical modulation class due to incoherent LED sources.

---

## 2.2 Lambertian Radiation Model (Order $m$)

A standard LED does not radiate uniformly — its emission pattern follows a **Lambertian** profile characterized by the **Lambertian order** $m$, related to the transmitter's half-angle $\Phi_{1/2}$:

$$ m = \frac{-\ln 2}{\ln(\cos \Phi_{1/2})} $$

The **radiant intensity** (W/steradian) as a function of irradiance angle $\phi$ is:

$$ R(\phi) = \frac{m + 1}{2\pi} \cos^m(\phi), \quad \text{for } 0 \le \phi \le \frac{\pi}{2} $$

The channel **DC gain** for a LOS link is:

$$ H_{LOS}(0) = \frac{(m+1) A}{2\pi d^2} \cos^m(\phi) \, T_s(\psi) \, g(\psi) \, \cos(\psi) $$

where $A$ = photodetector area, $d$ = link distance, $\psi$ = incidence angle, $T_s(\psi)$ = optical filter transmission, $g(\psi)$ = optical concentrator gain.

---

## 2.3 IEEE 802.15.7 Modulation Schemes

The standard defines **three PHY modes** with distinct modulation techniques:

### (a) PHY I — On-Off Keying (OOK) with Manchester / 4B6B Coding
- Simplest scheme: LED **ON** = bit 1, LED **OFF** = bit 0.
- Manchester coding ensures a DC-balanced signal (avoids flicker).
- Data rates: **11.67 kbps to 266.6 kbps**.
- Best for: **Outdoor, dim environments, low-data applications**.

### (b) PHY II — Variable Pulse Position Modulation (VPPM)
- Combines **Pulse Position Modulation (PPM)** with **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)**.
- Provides independent control of **dimming** and **data rate**.
- Data rates: **1.25 Mbps to 96 Mbps**.
- Best for: **Indoor office, dimming-aware applications**.

### (c) PHY III — Color Shift Keying (CSK)
- Uses the **RGB color coordinates** of a multi-chip LED.
- A constellation point maps to a color in the **CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram**.
- Data rates: **12 Mbps to 96 Mbps** (and beyond in extensions).
- Best for: **High-speed indoor, full-color LEDs**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** CSK is a defining feature of IEEE 802.15.7 — other VLC standards (e.g., IEEE 802.11bb "Light Communications") do not natively include CSK. Be prepared to explain the 7-band wavelength plan and constellation mapping.

---

## 2.4 Network Topologies

IEEE 802.15.7 supports **three network topologies**:

| Topology | Description | Coordinator | Use Case |
|----------|-------------|-------------|----------|
| **Star** | All devices communicate via a central coordinator (the "infrastructure" LED luminaire). | Yes | Indoor office, smart building |
| **Peer-to-Peer** | Two devices establish a direct bidirectional link. | No | Ad-hoc data exchange, vehicle-to-vehicle |
| **Broadcast** | One-way communication to all receivers in range. | Optional | Beaconing, location services, advertising |

---

## 2.5 MAC Layer Essentials

The IEEE 802.15.7 MAC is derived from **IEEE 802.15.4** (the ZigBee/WSN standard) with VLC-specific adaptations:

- **Superframe structure** with Contention Access Period (CAP) and Contention-Free Period (CFP).
- **CSMA/CA** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance) — but with a twist: the carrier sense is performed by measuring **ambient light levels** rather than RF energy.
- **GTS (Guaranteed Time Slots)** for QoS-sensitive traffic.
- **Dimming control commands** embedded in the MAC to maintain illumination while transmitting.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates all critical equations for exam problem-solving:

| Parameter | Formula | Boundary / Range | Units |
|-----------|---------|------------------|-------|
| Lambertian order | $m = \frac{-\ln 2}{\ln(\cos \Phi_{1/2})}$ | $m \ge 1$ | dimensionless |
| Channel DC gain (LOS) | $H_{LOS} = \frac{(m+1) A}{2\pi d^2} \cos^m(\phi) \cos(\psi)$ | $0 < \phi, \psi < \pi/2$ | dimensionless |
| Lambertian radiant intensity | $R(\phi) = \frac{m+1}{2\pi} \cos^m(\phi)$ | $0 \le \phi \le \pi/2$ | W/sr |
| Optical concentrator gain | $g(\psi) = \frac{n^2}{\sin^2 \Psi_{FOV}}$ | $\psi \le \Psi_{FOV}$ | dimensionless |
| Receiver optical power | $P_r = P_t \cdot H_{LOS}$ | — | W |
| SNR (electrical domain) | $\text{SNR} = \frac{(R \cdot P_r)^2}{N_0 B}$ | Shot + thermal noise | dimensionless |
| Bandwidth-limited data rate | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Shannon limit | bps |
| OOK bit error rate | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\text{SNR}}\right)$ | $Q$ = Q-function | dimensionless |
| Flicker-free modulation freq. | $f_{mod} \ge 200$ Hz | Human perception | Hz |
| Manchester code rate | $R_{coded} = R_{data} / 2$ | 50% efficiency | bps |
| 4B6B code efficiency | $R_{coded} = (4/6) R_{data}$ | 66.67% efficiency | bps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder:** All optical powers are in **Watts (W)**. The photodiode converts watts to **amperes (A)** via responsivity $R$ (A/W). The receiver therefore outputs a **photocurrent**, not a voltage — this is fundamental to IM/DD.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

IEEE 802.15.7 has enabled transformative applications:

- **Smart Hospitals:** EMI-free wireless connectivity for MRI rooms and surgical theaters.
- **Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V):** Taillights broadcasting brake warnings and speed data to following cars.
- **Aviation:** In-cabin entertainment without RF interference to cockpit avionics.
- **Underwater Communication:** RF signals are severely attenuated in water; blue-green LEDs enable subaquatic sensor networks.
- **Indoor Positioning:** Centimeter-accuracy location services using VLC beacons — far more accurate than Wi-Fi triangulation.
- **Secure Government/Military Communications:** Light does not leak through walls — physically immune to remote eavesdropping.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Lambertian Channel DC Gain $H_{LOS}(0)$

This derivation establishes the fundamental VLC link budget equation used in every IEEE 802.15.7 receiver design.

### Starting Premise

An LED transmits optical power $P_t$ (Watts) with a Lambertian emission pattern. A photodetector of area $A$ (m²) is positioned at distance $d$ (m) with irradiance angle $\phi$ and incidence angle $\psi$.

### Step 1 — Radiant Intensity from the Source

The radiant intensity (power per unit solid angle) emitted by a Lambertian source is:

$$ I(\phi) = I_0 \cos^m(\phi) $$

where $I_0$ is the on-axis intensity. By integrating $I(\phi)$ over a hemisphere and equating to $P_t$:

$$ P_t = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} I(\phi) \sin(\phi) \, d\phi \, d\theta = I_0 \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{1}{m+1} $$

Solving for $I_0$:

$$ I_0 = \frac{(m+1) P_t}{2\pi} $$

### Step 2 — Irradiance at Distance $d$

The irradiance $E$ (W/m²) at distance $d$ along angle $\phi$ is the radiant intensity divided by the projected area at distance $d$:

$$ E(d, \phi) = \frac{I(\phi)}{d^2} = \frac{I_0 \cos^m(\phi)}{d^2} = \frac{(m+1) P_t \cos^m(\phi)}{2\pi d^2} $$

### Step 3 — Optical Power Captured by the Photodetector

The photodetector of area $A$ captures power proportional to $\cos(\psi)$ (projected area) — therefore:

$$ P_r = E(d, \phi) \cdot A \cdot \cos(\psi) = \frac{(m+1) A P_t \cos^m(\phi) \cos(\psi)}{2\pi d^2} $$

### Step 4 — Definition of Channel DC Gain

By definition, $H_{LOS}(0) = P_r / P_t$. Dividing both sides by $P_t$:

$$ H_{LOS}(0) = \frac{(m+1) A \cos^m(\phi) \cos(\psi)}{2\pi d^2} $$

> Including the optical filter transmission $T_s(\psi)$ and concentrator gain $g(\psi)$:

$$ H_{LOS}(0) = \frac{(m+1) A T_s(\psi) g(\psi) \cos^m(\phi) \cos(\psi)}{2\pi d^2} $$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The received power decreases as the **square** of distance and as the **$m$-th power** of the cosine of the irradiance angle. This $m$-th power dependence is unique to VLC — it is steeper than RF free-space loss (which is distance-squared only).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Optical Concentrator Gain $g(\psi)$

### Step 1 — Purpose

A non-imaging concentrator (e.g., a hemispherical lens placed on the photodiode) increases the effective collection area of the detector for rays within its field of view $\Psi_{FOV}$.

### Step 2 — Geometric Concentration Limit

By conservation of étendue (optical invariant), the maximum concentration factor is:

$$ g(\psi) = \frac{n^2}{\sin^2(\Psi_{FOV})}, \quad \text{for } 0 \le \psi \le \Psi_{FOV} $$

where $n$ is the refractive index of the concentrator material (typically $n \approx 1.5$ for glass).

**Limiting Behavior:** As $\Psi_{FOV} \to \pi/2$ (hemispherical FOV), $g(\psi) \to n^2 \approx 2.25$. As $\Psi_{FOV} \to 0$ (highly directional), $g(\psi) \to \infty$, but practical constraints (size, alignment) limit realistic values to $g \le 10$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: SNR and Capacity for an IM/DD Link

### Step 1 — Received Photocurrent

The photodiode converts optical power $P_r$ to current $I_{ph}$ via its responsivity $R$ (A/W):

$$ I_{ph} = R \cdot P_r $$

### Step 2 — Noise Sources

Two dominant noise components are present:

- **Shot noise** (quantum noise): $\sigma_{shot}^2 = 2 q (I_{ph} + I_{bg}) B$
- **Thermal noise** (Johnson noise in the TIA): $\sigma_{thermal}^2 = \frac{4 k_B T B}{R_L}$

where $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C is the electron charge, $I_{bg}$ is the background photocurrent, $B$ is the electrical bandwidth, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K is Boltzmann's constant, $T$ is absolute temperature, and $R_L$ is the load resistance.

### Step 3 — Total Noise Power

$$ N_0 B = 2 q (I_{ph} + I_{bg}) B + \frac{4 k_B T B}{R_L} $$

### Step 4 — Electrical SNR

$$ \text{SNR} = \frac{I_{ph}^2}{N_0 B} = \frac{(R P_r)^2}{2 q (R P_r + R P_{bg}) B + \frac{4 k_B T B}{R_L}} $$

### Step 5 — Shannon Capacity Bound

The maximum achievable data rate under additive Gaussian noise is:

$$ C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \quad \text{bits/second} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** A common derivation question is to compute the **maximum achievable data rate** at a given distance and $\text{SNR}$. Use the Shannon formula above as the upper bound — but remember the **practical** VLC link is limited by the **LED bandwidth** ($f_{-3dB}$), typically $20$ – **$60$ MHz** for commercial white LEDs.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Bit Error Probability for OOK in VLC

### Step 1 — Binary Hypothesis Testing

For OOK with optimal threshold detection:

$$ H_0: \text{bit 0} \Rightarrow I_{rx} = n(t) $$
$$ H_1: \text{bit 1} \Rightarrow I_{rx} = I_{ph} + n(t) $$

where $n(t)$ is Gaussian noise with variance $\sigma_n^2$.

### Step 2 — Decision Rule

The optimal threshold (assuming equal priors) is $I_{th} = I_{ph}/2$. The probability of error is:

$$ P_e = P(1) P(\text{error} \mid 1) + P(0) P(\text{error} \mid 0) $$

### Step 3 — Symmetry

By symmetry of equiprobable bits:

$$ P_e = Q\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}/2}{\sigma_n}\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{2\sigma_n}\right) $$

### Step 4 — Substitute $\text{SNR}$

The electrical SNR is $\text{SNR} = I_{ph}^2 / \sigma_n^2$, so $I_{ph} / \sigma_n = \sqrt{\text{SNR}}$. Therefore:

$$ P_e = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{\text{SNR}}}{2}\right) $$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of $1/2$ inside the $Q$-function arises because the OOK "eye opening" is half the peak signal — this is distinct from bipolar signaling where $P_e = Q(\sqrt{\text{SNR}})$. KTU examiners love this subtlety.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Typical Problem)

**Problem:** A VLC link uses an LED with half-power semi-angle $\Phi_{1/2} = 60°$, transmitting $P_t = 1$ W into a Lambertian pattern. A photodetector with $A = 1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4}$ m², responsivity $R = 0.5$ A/W, and $T_s = 1$ is placed at $d = 3$ m directly below the LED ($\phi = \psi = 0°$). The electrical bandwidth is $B = 50$ MHz and $T = 300$ K, $R_L = 10$ kΩ.

**Step 1 — Compute $m$:**

$$ m = \frac{-\ln 2}{\ln(\cos 60°)} = \frac{-0.6931}{\ln(0.5)} = \frac{-0.6931}{-0.6931} = 1.0 $$

**Step 2 — Compute $H_{LOS}(0)$:**

$$ H_{LOS}(0) = \frac{(1+1) \times 10^{-4} \times \cos^1(0) \times \cos(0)}{2\pi \times (3)^2} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{18\pi} $$

$$ H_{LOS}(0) = 3.537 \times 10^{-6} \quad \text{(dimensionless)} $$

**Step 3 — Compute $P_r$:**

$$ P_r = P_t \cdot H_{LOS} = 1 \times 3.537 \times 10^{-6} = 3.537 \, \mu\text{W} $$

**Step 4 — Compute $I_{ph}$:**

$$ I_{ph} = R \cdot P_r = 0.5 \times 3.537 \times 10^{-6} = 1.7685 \, \mu\text{A} $$

**Step 5 — Compute Thermal Noise:**

$$ \sigma_{th}^2 = \frac{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 50 \times 10^6}{10^4} = 8.28 \times 10^{-18} \, \text{A}^2 $$

$$ \sigma_{th} = 9.10 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{A} = 9.10 \, \text{nA} $$

**Step 6 — Compute SNR:**

$$ \text{SNR} = \frac{(1.7685 \times 10^{-6})^2}{8.28 \times 10^{-18}} = \frac{3.128 \times 10^{-12}}{8.28 \times 10^{-18}} = 3.78 \times 10^{5} $$

**Step 7 — Compute Capacity (Shannon):**

$$ C = 50 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(1 + 3.78 \times 10^5) = 50 \times 10^6 \times 18.53 = 926.5 \, \text{Mbps} $$

**Step 8 — Practical Limit:**

The actual achievable rate is limited by the **LED's modulation bandwidth** (say 30 MHz), giving:

$$ C_{practical} \approx 30 \times 10^6 \times 18.53 \approx 556 \, \text{Mbps} $$

---

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q: List the three PHY modes defined in IEEE 802.15.7 and state the modulation scheme used in each.**

**Model Answer:**
The three PHY modes are:
1. **PHY I** — uses **On-Off Keying (OOK)** combined with Manchester or 4B6B line coding; data rate range $11.67$ – $266.6$ kbps.
2. **PHY II** — uses **Variable Pulse Position Modulation (VPPM)**; data rate range $1.25$ – $96$ Mbps.
3. **PHY III** — uses **Color Shift Keying (CSK)**; data rate range $12$ – $96$ Mbps.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning:** Examiners award full marks only if all three PHY modes are listed with their **specific modulation names**. Writing only "modulation schemes" without naming OOK, VPPM, and CSK results in loss of 2 marks.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q: Explain why VLC systems use Intensity Modulation with Direct Detection (IM/DD) instead of coherent detection.**

**Model Answer:**
VLC uses IM/DD for three reasons:
1. **Incoherent LED Source:** Commercial LEDs emit spontaneous (incoherent) light with random phase. Coherent detection requires a local oscillator phase-locked to the incoming signal — this is impossible with LEDs.
2. **Cost and Complexity:** Coherent receivers (e.g., homodyne/heterodyne) require narrow-linewidth lasers and complex DSP — economically infeasible for LED-based illumination infrastructure.
3. **Power and Complexity:** Direct detection via a photodiode is a simple, low-power square-law detector that converts incident optical power directly into proportional current.
Therefore, information is encoded in the **instantaneous optical intensity** of the LED, and recovered by direct photocurrent measurement at the receiver.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an IEEE 802.15.7 Visible Light Communication system. Describe the function of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the channel DC gain $H_{LOS}(0)$ of a line-of-sight VLC link using the Lambertian emission model. State all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Block Diagram** (see Section 4.1 above).

**Block-wise Function Description:**

1. **Data Source:** Generates the binary information bits to be transmitted (e.g., voice, video, sensor data).
2. **Source Encoder:** Compresses the source data to remove redundancy (e.g., Huffman coding).
3. **Line Coder:** Performs **Manchester** or **4B6B** encoding to ensure DC balance, avoid baseline wander, and provide a clock signal at the receiver. Critical for flicker-free illumination.
4. **LED Driver Circuit:** A current driver that modulates the forward current of the LED at the required data rate. Must support high-frequency switching (tens of MHz).
5. **LED Optical Source (TX):** Converts the electrical current into modulated optical intensity. The Lambertian emission pattern propagates through the channel.
6. **Free-Space VLC Channel:** The atmosphere or indoor space between transmitter and receiver. Subject to path loss following the Lambertian model, plus ambient light interference.
7. **Photodiode Optical Receiver:** A reverse-biased PIN or avalanche photodiode (APD) that converts incident optical power into a proportional photocurrent via the photoelectric effect.
8. **Transimpedance Amplifier (TIA):** Converts the weak photocurrent into a usable voltage signal with low noise and wide bandwidth.
9. **Equalizer:** Compensates for the limited LED bandwidth and multipath-induced intersymbol interference (ISI).
10. **Line Decoder:** Reverses Manchester/4B6B encoding to recover the original bit stream.
11. **Source Decoder:** Decompresses the data to recover the original source information.
12. **Data Sink:** Final destination (e.g., speaker, display, processor).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Assumptions:**
- Single LED transmitter with Lambertian emission pattern.
- Line-of-sight (LOS) path with no multipath.
- Photodetector area $A$, positioned at distance $d$, irradiance angle $\phi$, incidence angle $\psi$.
- Optical filter and concentrator characterized by $T_s(\psi)$ and $g(\psi)$.

**Derivation:** (See Section 3.1 above for the complete step-by-step derivation.)

**Final Result:**

$$ H_{LOS}(0) = \frac{(m+1) A T_s(\psi) g(\psi) \cos^m(\phi) \cos(\psi)}{2\pi d^2} $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q: (a)** Explain the three network topologies supported by IEEE 802.15.7. For each topology, state one practical application scenario. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A VLC link uses an LED with half-power semi-angle $\Phi_{1/2} = 30°$, transmitting optical power $P_t = 500$ mW. The photodetector has area $A = 0.5 \text{ cm}^2$, responsivity $R = 0.6$ A/W, and is located at $d = 2.5$ m directly below the LED (on-axis). Calculate: (i) the Lambertian order $m$, (ii) the channel DC gain, (iii) the received optical power, and (iv) the received photocurrent. Assume $T_s = 1$ and $g = 1$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Topology 1 — Star Topology:**
- All devices communicate through a central coordinator (the LED luminaire acts as an access point).
- The coordinator handles synchronization, beaconing, and GTS allocation.
- **Application:** Indoor office lighting infrastructure providing wireless internet to employee devices.

**Topology 2 — Peer-to-Peer Topology:**
- Two devices establish a direct bidirectional VLC link without a coordinator.
- Suitable for ad-hoc data exchange between two terminals.
- **Application:** Vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) communication where two cars exchange speed/brake data via headlight/taillight.

**Topology 3 — Broadcast Topology:**
- One-way communication from a transmitter to all receivers in its coverage area.
- No acknowledgment required; receivers passively listen.
- **Application:** Indoor positioning beacons that broadcast location IDs to smartphones for navigation.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Lambertian order $m$:**

$$ m = \frac{-\ln 2}{\ln(\cos 30°)} = \frac{-0.6931}{\ln(0.8660)} = \frac{-0.6931}{-0.1447} \approx 4.79 $$

**(ii) Channel DC gain** (on-axis: $\phi = \psi = 0$, so $\cos(0) = 1$):

$$ H_{LOS} = \frac{(m+1) A}{2\pi d^2} = \frac{(4.79 + 1) \times 0.5 \times 10^{-4}}{2\pi \times (2.5)^2} $$

$$ H_{LOS} = \frac{5.79 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-4}}{2\pi \times 6.25} = \frac{2.895 \times 10^{-4}}{39.27} = 7.37 \times 10^{-6} $$

**(iii) Received optical power:**

$$ P_r = P_t \cdot H_{LOS} = 0.5 \times 7.37 \times 10^{-6} = 3.685 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{W} = 3.685 \, \mu\text{W} $$

**(iv) Received photocurrent:**

$$ I_{ph} = R \cdot P_r = 0.6 \times 3.685 \times 10^{-6} = 2.211 \, \mu\text{A} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert cm² to m²** — A common error costs 1 mark. Always convert: $1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$.
> 2. **Degrees vs radians** — Angles in $\cos$ and $\sin$ functions MUST be in **radians**. Converting $30° = \pi/6$ before computing $\cos$ is critical.
> 3. **Sign error in $m$ formula** — The negative signs in numerator and denominator cancel, but only if computed correctly. Mark loss if the absolute value is used.
> 4. **Unit confusion between dBm and dBW** — For exam purposes, present results in **Watts** unless explicitly requested.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — IEEE 802.15.7 VLC**

- **Standard:** IEEE 802.15.7-2018 — VLC PHY and MAC specification.
- **Spectrum:** $380$ – $780$ nm (visible light); license-free.
- **PHY I:** OOK with Manchester/4B6B; rates $11.67$ – $266.6$ kbps.
- **PHY II:** VPPM; rates $1.25$ – $96$ Mbps; supports dimming.
- **PHY III:** CSK; rates $12$ – $96$ Mbps; uses RGB LEDs and CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram.
- **Modulation Class:** IM/DD only (Intensity Modulation, Direct Detection).
- **Flicker Threshold:** $f_{mod} > 200$ Hz to avoid human-perceptible flicker.
- **Channel Model:** Lambertian with order $m = -\ln 2 / \ln(\cos \Phi_{1/2})$.
- **Channel DC Gain:** $H_{LOS} = \frac{(m+1) A T_s g \cos^m(\phi) \cos(\psi)}{2\pi d^2}$.
- **Photocurrent:** $I_{ph} = R P_r$ (responsivity $R$ in A/W).
- **Noise:** Shot + Thermal; $\sigma^2 = 2q(I_{ph} + I_{bg})B + 4k_BTB/R_L$.
- **Capacity:** Shannon-limited: $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$.
- **OOK BER:** $P_e = Q(\sqrt{\text{SNR}}/2)$ — note the factor of $1/2$ for unipolar signaling.
- **Topologies:** Star, Peer-to-Peer, Broadcast.
- **MAC:** CSMA/CA with GTS; ambient-light-based carrier sensing.
- **Top Applications:** Indoor wireless, V2V, hospitals, aviation, underwater, secure comms.
- **Dual Function:** LEDs provide illumination AND data simultaneously.

> **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — IEEE 802.15.7 VLC**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Device-to-Device (D2D) Communication** is a revolutionary paradigm in 4G LTE-Advanced and 5G NR (New Radio) cellular networks where two proximate User Equipments (UEs) communicate **directly with each other** over the licensed cellular spectrum, bypassing the traditional data path through the core network (EPC in 4G / 5GC in 5G). The base station (eNB in 4G / gNB in 5G) retains control-plane supervision while the user-plane traffic flows device-to-device.

In 3GPP terminology (Release 12 onwards), this is standardized as **Proximity Services (ProSe)**, while in 5G NR it is referred to as the **Sidelink (SL)** interface, operating over the **PC5 reference point**.

**Key Terminology Mapping:**

| 3GPP Generation | Official Name | Air Interface | Reference Point |
|-----------------|---------------|---------------|-----------------|
| 4G LTE | ProSe (Proximity Services) | LTE Sidelink | PC5 |
| 5G NR | NR Sidelink (SL) | 5G NR Sidelink | PC5 |
| Public Safety | Mission Critical Push-To-Talk (MCPTT) | LTE/NR Sidelink | PC5 + Uu |

> [!NOTE]
> The licensed cellular spectrum is reused for D2D — this is what distinguishes D2D from Wi-Fi Direct or Bluetooth, which operate in unlicensed ISM bands.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a crowded classroom (a cellular cell). Normally, if Student A wants to share notes with Student B sitting next to him, he must:
1. Hand the note to the teacher (eNB),
2. The teacher walks to Student B,
3. Student B receives it.

This is the **traditional cellular path (Uu interface)** — slow, uses teacher's energy, and wastes his bandwidth if many students want to share notes simultaneously.

With **D2D communication**, the teacher simply **gives permission** to Student A and Student B to **directly exchange notes** while sitting next to each other. The teacher (base station) still supervises the room (control plane), but the actual note-passing (data plane) happens peer-to-peer.

**Why is this brilliant?**
- ⚡ **Latency drops** because the round-trip through the core network is eliminated.
- 📡 **Spectrum is reused** — the same frequency can carry both cellular and D2D traffic.
- 🔋 **Battery savings** — low-power short-range transmission instead of high-power uplink to a distant tower.
- 🚀 **Throughput skyrockets** — for nearby devices, Shannon capacity scales with reduced path loss.

## 1.3 Classification of D2D Communication

D2D can be classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

### Axis 1: Spectrum Usage
- **Inband D2D**: D2D links reuse the **licensed cellular spectrum**. *Subdivided into:*
  - *Underlay*: D2D and cellular share the same frequency band simultaneously (causes interference → requires careful power control).
  - *Overlay*: D2D uses dedicated sub-bands orthogonal to cellular traffic.
- **Outband D2D**: D2D uses **unlicensed spectrum** (e.g., 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi/ISM band). Requires a secondary radio interface (like Wi-Fi Direct or Bluetooth) on the UE.

### Axis 2: Operator Control
- **Network-controlled D2D**: eNB/gNB fully manages radio resources (most common in 4G ProSe).
- **Device-controlled D2D**: UEs autonomously select resources (used in 5G NR V2X Mode 2).

## 1.4 Why D2D Matters — Core Use Cases

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Must-Memorize Use Cases:**
> 1. **Public Safety Communications** — When the cellular infrastructure is destroyed (earthquakes, fires), D2D enables first-responders to form a multi-hop ad-hoc network.
> 2. **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X)** — C-V2X (Cellular V2X) uses 5G NR Sidelink for collision avoidance and autonomous driving at sub-millisecond latencies.
> 3. **Proximity-based Social Applications** — Friend-finder, localized advertising, content-sharing (Spotify Nearby, etc.).
> 4. **IoT / MTC (Massive Machine-Type Communications)** — Thousands of low-power sensors forming mesh networks via D2D without overloading the RAN.
> 5. **Network Offloading & Traffic Densification** — Decongesting macrocells in stadiums, concerts, and urban hotspots.

## 1.5 Historical Evolution Timeline

```
3GPP Release Timeline → D2D / Sidelink Evolution
─────────────────────────────────────────────────
Rel 8  (2008)  → LTE Release, no D2D support
Rel 12 (2014)  → ProSe (D2D Discovery + Communication) introduced
Rel 13 (2015)  → eNB-controlled D2D for Public Safety
Rel 14 (2017)  → C-V2X (Mode 3 & Mode 4) for automotive
Rel 15 (2018)  → 5G NR; sidelink framework introduced
Rel 16 (2020)  → 5G V2X with NR Sidelink, unicast/multicast
Rel 17 (2022)  → Sidelink relaying, UE-to-Network relay, NTN integration
Rel 18 (2024)  → 5G-Advanced: AI/ML-driven sidelink resource allocation
```

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — How D2D Works

A D2D session consists of **three sequential phases**:

### Phase 1: D2D Discovery (ProSe Discovery)
The base station assists two UEs in identifying each other's proximity. There are two flavors:

- **EPC-assisted Discovery (Restricted / Open)**: The UE registers its interest via the cellular network; the EPC matches candidates and informs them.
- **Direct (Radio-Level) Discovery**: UEs broadcast **discovery messages** using D2DSS (D2D Synchronization Signals) and discovery resources; nearby devices decode these messages directly over the PC5 interface.

### Phase 2: Mode Selection (Cellular vs Direct)
The eNB/gNB decides **whether** to use the D2D path or the traditional cellular path. This is a critical optimization — D2D is not always better.

**Decision Criteria (KTU High-Yield):**
- If the **distance between UEs** is below a threshold AND
- The **interference** caused to cellular UEs is manageable AND
- The **QoS** of both links can be satisfied,

then **D2D mode** is selected; otherwise, **cellular (relay) mode** is used.

### Phase 3: Direct Communication
The two UEs establish a sidelink radio bearer, and data flows **peer-to-peer** over PC5. The eNB/gNB may continue to schedule resources (Mode 3 in C-V2X) or UEs may self-schedule (Mode 4 in C-V2X).

## 2.2 Resource Allocation Modes in 5G NR Sidelink

| Mode | Name | Resource Selection Authority | Typical Use Case |
|------|------|------------------------------|------------------|
| **Mode 1** | Network-scheduled | gNB dynamically allocates sidelink resources via DCIs | In-coverage UEs, low latency |
| **Mode 2** | UE-autonomous | UE senses the channel and selects resources from a pre-configured/sensing pool | V2X out-of-coverage |
| **Mode 3** | gNB-scheduled with SPS | gNB schedules via NR sidelink configured grant (Type 1/Type 2) | LTE C-V2X, Rel 14+ |
| **Mode 4** | UE-sensing based | UE performs energy/sensing, picks least-interfered RB | LTE C-V2X |

## 2.3 The Interference Problem — Central Challenge

D2D fundamentally **reuses** cellular spectrum. This creates two critical interference scenarios:

```
Interference Model for Underlay D2D
─────────────────────────────────────
1. D2D TX  →  Cellular UE RX   (cross-tier interference)
2. Cellular eNB/gNB TX  →  D2D RX  (downlink interference)
3. D2D TX  →  D2D RX (own link — desired signal)
4. D2D TX  →  Other D2D RX (D2D-to-D2D interference)
```

The **SINR (Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio)** at a D2D receiver is the central metric:

$$\text{SINR}_{D2D} = \frac{P_{D2D} \cdot G_{D2D}}{I_{\text{cellular}} + \sum_{j \neq i} P_{D2D,j} \cdot G_{j \to i} + N_0}$$

where every term will be formally defined in the formula sheet below.

## 2.4 Power Control Strategy

To mitigate interference, the D2D transmit power is **bounded** using a classical open-loop fractional power control rule (3GPP TS 36.213):

$$P_{D2D} = \min\left(P_{\max},\ P_0 + 10 \cdot \alpha \cdot \log_{10}(d_{D2D})\right)$$

- $P_{\max}$ = **Maximum UE transmit power** (typically **23 dBm** for UE Power Class 3).
- $P_0$ = A cell-specific or D2D-specific baseline power.
- $\alpha$ = Path-loss compensation factor, $0 \le \alpha \le 1$.
- $d_{D2D}$ = Distance between D2D transmitter and receiver.

> [!NOTE]
> When $\alpha = 1$, full path-loss compensation is applied (compensates exactly for distance); when $\alpha = 0$, no compensation (constant transmit power).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Board Exam** — Memorize this entire table.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | D2D SINR | $\text{SINR}_{D2D} = \dfrac{P_{tx} \cdot \vert h\vert^2 \cdot d_{D2D}^{-\beta}}{I_{\text{cellular}} + N_0}$ | $\beta$ = path loss exponent (typically 2–4) |
| 2 | Path Loss (Log-distance) | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10\beta \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)$ | $d_0$ = reference distance (often 1 m indoor, 100 m outdoor) |
| 3 | Shannon Capacity (D2D Link) | $C_{D2D} = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \text{SINR}_{D2D}\right)$ | bits/sec; $B$ = allocated bandwidth |
| 4 | Cellular vs D2D Mode Selection | $\text{If } C_{D2D} > C_{\text{cell}} \Rightarrow \text{use D2D}$ | Mode-switching decision metric |
| 5 | Power Control (Fractional) | $P_{D2D} = \min\!\left(P_{\max},\ P_0 + 10\alpha \log_{10}(d_{D2D})\right)$ | dBm; $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ |
| 6 | Spectral Efficiency (overall cell) | $\eta = \dfrac{\sum_{k} C_k}{B_{\text{total}}}$ | bps/Hz |
| 7 | Outage Probability | $P_{\text{out}} = \Pr\!\left(\text{SINR}_{D2D} < \text{SINR}_{\text{th}}\right)$ | Threshold-based reliability |
| 8 | Energy Efficiency | $EE = \dfrac{\text{Throughput (bits)}}{\text{Energy (Joules)}}$ | bits/Joule |
| 9 | Proximity Distance Threshold | $d_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{d_{\text{cell}}}{\sqrt{2}}$ (heuristic) | Within this → D2D beneficial |
| 10 | Sidelink RB Count (5G NR) | $N_{RB}^{SL} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B_{SL}}{12 \cdot \Delta f_{\text{SCS}}} \right\rfloor$ | SCS = Subcarrier Spacing (15/30/60/120 kHz) |

**Boundary Conditions / Limits:**
- $P_{D2D} \le P_{\max}$ = **23 dBm** (UE Power Class 3, EIRP limit).
- $\text{SINR}_{D2D} \ge \text{SINR}_{\text{th}}$ (typically **0 dB** for QPSK 1/3, up to **22 dB** for 64-QAM).
- $d_{D2D} \le R_{\text{cell}}$ (D2D distance bounded by cell radius).

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in Real-World Deployments

| Industry Sector | D2D Application | Performance Benefit |
|------------------|------------------|----------------------|
| Public Safety (PS-LTE) | Off-network talk-around for fire/police | Operates without cellular infrastructure |
| Automotive (C-V2X) | Cooperative collision avoidance | Sub-10 ms end-to-end latency |
| Smart Factories | Sensor-to-controller mesh | Battery life > 10 years |
| Healthcare | Wearable-to-gateway telemetry | Low-power Body Area Networks |
| Disaster Recovery | Ad-hoc relay chains | Multi-hop range extension |
| Multimedia | Proximity content sharing | Up to 1 Gbps sidelink throughput (Rel 16) |

## 2.7 Advantages and Limitations Summary

**✅ Advantages:**
- Lower latency (no Uu round-trip).
- Higher spectral efficiency via spatial reuse.
- Extended coverage via multi-hop relaying.
- Reduced core-network load (offloading).
- Improved energy efficiency for short links.

**❌ Challenges:**
- **Interference management** (cross-tier interference).
- **Security & privacy** (bypassing core network authentication).
- **Mobility & handover** (rapidly changing topology).
- **Synchronization** (especially in out-of-coverage scenarios).
- **Battery drain** on sidelink reception.
